[ORIGINAL]
How be it, forasmuch as this book is despised of man[illegible] good and well learned men, and for that all men for [illegible] most part are fully persuaded that it is an unprofitable by[illegible] I will speak here somewhat of the same matter.
[DRAFT]
However, since this book is despised by both good and learned men, and because most people are firmly convinced that it is an unprofitable labor, I will speak here somewhat about this matter.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Despised" is softened. The original implies a more active rejection than the draft conveys.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "unprofitable labor" is a slight interpretation; the original "unprofitable by" is more neutral.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "by" is ambiguous – it could refer to a book, a work, or a practice. "Labor" narrows it to a specific type of work.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms present.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]
However, forasmuch as this book is despised of man, good and well learned men, and for that all men for the most part are fully persuaded that it is an unprofitable by, I will speak here somewhat of the same matter.

But by the same mean a go[illegible] part of Daniel should be cast away: Which neuerthele[illegible] is commended to us of Christ himself in Matthew.[note 1.4] [illegible] greatest matter of all, namely the calling of the Gentiles, showed to S. [DRAFT]
First, many abhor this book for this cause only that it is full of visions, types, and figures, for they suppose this does not accord with the Evangelical and Apostolic doctrine of the New Testament. But by the same reasoning, a portion of Daniel should be cast away. Nevertheless, this is commended to us by Christ himself in Matthew. The greatest matter of all, namely the calling of the Gentiles, was shown to Saint Peter by a vision, as appears in the Acts.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "becomes not" has been softened to "does not accord with," subtly shifting the tone from a direct rejection to a disagreement.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "d[illegible] trine" is rendered as "doctrine." While likely intended, the original’s illegibility allowed for a broader range of interpretations (e.g., a specific doctrine, a tradition). The draft removes this potential.
THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
PROPER NOUNS: "S. Peter" changed to "Saint Peter." Acceptable modernization.
[FINAL]
First, many abhor this book for this cause only that it is full of visions, types, and figures, for they suppose this does not become the Evangelical and Apostolical doctrine of the new Testament. But by the same reasoning a portion of Daniel should be cast away. Nevertheless, this is commended to us by Christ himself in Matthew. The greatest matter of all, namely the calling of the Gentiles, was shown to Saint Peter by a vision, as appears in the Acts.

This kind of ſp[illegible] doth not darken matters, but makes them plain: And [illegible] keth much for the efficacitie and perspicuity, and for the c[illegible] firming of the memory. For by this mean, matters be [illegible] only declared with words, and heard with the ears: [illegible] are set forth also to be scene of the eyes, and after a sort be fi[illegible] in the memory.[note 1.6] Many for this cause attribute much to pa[illegible] ting: But I suppose that I may much more rightli attribu[illegible] very much to this manner of speaking and teaching, where[illegible] the matter is uttered, not by a coulored, domme, and dead pa[illegible] ting, but as it were with a speaking and lively manner set fo[illegible] to be scene of the eyes. Which is therefore propounded, th[illegible] men might rightly and exactly understand the same. Alb[illegible] therefore that this whole book in a manner consists of viſio[illegible] and figures:[note 1.7] Yet shall we indeed, through the inspiration [illegible] Gods grace, show in our exposition, that all that same m[illegible] keth for the perspicuity and plainenes, and not for the obſe[illegible] ring or darkening of most high and godly matters. I wy[illegible] bring my exposition out of the very scriptures, by coferring joining thereunto the rule of faith and charity. I will search out the circumstances, the things that follow and go before: I will bring ſimilitudes and diſſimilitudes: I will add also there unto the experience of things, and the faith of histories. Which manner of expounding the scriptures all interpreters have always granted to be sound and true.[note 1.8] If better things shall be revealed to others, I will gladly after the precept of the apostle give place unto my betters. For I offer these my doings to be weighed of the godly, upon this condition, that they should try all things, and that which they shall find to be good, to hold fast.
[DRAFT]
And the prophet Joel also said how the people of the New Testament should see visions. And so does the blessed Apostle Peter expound the same place in the Acts of the Apostles, speaking of the people of the New Testament. And our Savior Christ in the Gospel, proposed and declared to the people many mysteries by parables, and in a manner by feigned fables, as they call them. And how much do you think these visions, types, and figures of John differ from the same? This kind of speaking does not darken matters, but makes them plain. And it contributes much to the efficacy and clarity, and to the confirming of the memory. For by this means, matters are not only declared with words and heard with the ears, but are set forth also to be seen by the eyes, and after a sort are fixed in the memory.

Many for this cause attribute much to painting. But I suppose that I may much more rightly attribute very much to this manner of speaking and teaching, where the matter is uttered, not by a colored, dull, and dead painting, but as it were with a speaking and lively manner set forth to be seen by the eyes. Which is therefore proposed, that men might rightly and exactly understand the same. Although therefore this whole book in a manner consists of visions and figures, yet shall we, through the inspiration of God’s grace, show in our exposition that it pertains to the perspicuity and plainness, and not to the obscuring or darkening of most high and godly matters. I will bring my exposition out of the very scriptures, by comparing and joining thereunto the rule of faith and charity. I will search out the circumstances, the things that follow and go before. I will bring similitudes and dissimilitudes. I will add also thereunto the experience of things, and the faith of histories. Which manner of expounding the scriptures all interpreters have always granted to be sound and true.

If better things shall be revealed to others, I will gladly, after the precept of the apostle, give place unto my betters. For I offer these my doings to be weighed by the godly, upon this condition, that they should try all things, and that which they shall find to be good, to hold fast.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "howe the people of [illegible] new Testament should see visions" becomes "how the people of the New Testament should see visions." While seemingly minor, the original phrasing ("howe") suggests a slightly more archaic and formal tone, subtly altered in the draft.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "This kind of ſp[illegible] doth not darken matters, but makes them plain" is rendered as "This kind of speaking does not darken matters, but makes them plain." The original could potentially refer to a broader interpretive method, while the draft specifically limits it to "speaking."
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original "I wy[illegible] bring my exposition out of the very scriptures" is rendered as "I will bring my exposition out of the very scriptures." The original phrasing, with "wy," retains a slightly more archaic feel and could be interpreted as a more deliberate, almost solemn, declaration.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content was added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** The modernization of "Hierom" to "Jerome," "Austen" to "Augustine," and "Ireney" to "Irenaeus" is not present in this passage, and therefore not applicable.

[ORIGINAL]
Secondly they object, that as well new men as old,[note 1.9] of no small authority, have both doubted of this book, and of the author thereof, and also have contemned it, as full of fables, and unworthy to be reckoned canonical. Let those that so think give me the same liberty, I desire them, which they usurp tho the selves, and think it lawful. For if the book of the Apocalypse [illegible]hold therefore seem worthy to be contemned, for that some notable men, both old and new, have doubted of the authority [illegible]hereof: Why may it not recover his authority again, if I [illegible]hew that the best doctors of the church both old and new, have [illegible]ad a right good opinion of this book? And here to the intent [illegible] wold dissemble nothing at all, I am not ignorant that doctor Martin Luther, a man right notably learned,[note 1.10] in his first edi [illegible]ion of the new Testament in dutch, with a sharp preface set [illegible]efore, hath ſticked this book as it were with a dagger. Howe [illegible]e it good and well learned me were offended with him for this [illegible]is judgment, which in the same found lack both of wit and mo [illegible]eſtie. The same man therefore weighing all things more upright [illegible]y and diligely, what time he corrected his dutch Bible. &c. My [illegible]orſhipful master seems also not to have set very much by his book, and to have ascribed it not to John the Apostle, but to John who they called a divine. But herein there is no doubt but that he followed plainly Eraſmus of Roterda, who is his annotations upon the new Teſtamet. In all the Greek copies, [illegible]aith he, that I have scene, the title was not of John the Apostle, [illegible]ut of John the divine. Eraſmus adds that amongst the Greeks and certain old writers, men doubted of this author, which thing he declares by the testimonies of Eusebius, and Hierom, of whose opinion shall be spoken ſtreightways. But the exempler or Spanish copy which is set forth after the faith of the most ancient and approved Greeks, exhibiteth to us such a title of ths book. [illegible]: That is the Apocalypse of the holy Apostle and Evangelist S. John Divine. For the ancient writers say, how S. John the Apostle and Euangeliſte, for his excellent writing of the son of God, was commonly called, Divine. Whereof it follows, than[illegible] this title doth attribute and not take from S. John this book. Certes Arethas was also, a Greek Author, an[illegible] bishop of Caesarea: Of the auncientes, says he, certain hau[illegible] plucked this Apocalypse from the tongue of that welbeloue[illegible] John, ascribing it to another: but it is not so. For that sam[illegible] Gregory, which as well as he, is called a Divine, accom[illegible] teeth this amongst those scriptures, which utterly want a suspicion of counterfeiting: saying, as the Apocalypse of S[illegible] John teaches me. And the same man a little after: But th[illegible] this book was written by the mouth of the holy ghost, S. B[illegible] ſil, Cyril, Papias and Hippolytus,[note 1.11] fathers of the church, a mete men to be credited. Thus saith he. What shall we say[illegible] that Eraſmus confesses, that the consent of the world, an authority of the church to be of such force with him, th[illegible] he dare not refuse this book?
[DRAFT]
Secondly they object, that as well new men as old, of no small authority, have both doubted of this book, and of the author thereof, and also have contemned it, as full of fables, and unworthy to be reckoned canonical. Let those that so think give me the same liberty, which I desire them, which they usurp themselves, and think it lawful. For if the book of the Apocalypse should therefore seem worthy to be contemned, for that some notable men, both old and new, have doubted of the authority hereof: Why may it not recover his authority again, if I shew that the best doctors of the church both old and new, have had a right good opinion of this book? And here to the intent I would dissemble nothing at all, I am not ignorant that Doctor Martin Luther, a man right notably learned, in his first edition of the New Testament in Dutch, with a sharp preface set before, hath stuck this book as it were with a dagger. However, it grieved good and well learned men, who found lack both of wit and modesty in that judgment. The same man therefore weighing all things more uprightly and diligently, what time he corrected his Dutch Bible. My worshipful master seems also not to have set very much by his book, and to have ascribed it not to John the Apostle, but to John who they called a divine. But herein there is no doubt but that he followed plainly Erasmus of Rotterdam, who is his annotations upon the New Testament. In all the Greek copies, he says, that I have seen, the title was not of John the Apostle, but of John the divine. Erasmus adds that amongst the Greeks and certain old writers, men doubted of this author, which thing he declares by the testimonies of Eusebius and Jerome, of whose opinion shall be spoken straightways. But the exemplar or Spanish copy which is set forth after the faith of the most ancient and approved Greeks, exhibiteth to us such a title of this book: That is, the Apocalypse of the holy Apostle and Evangelist St. John Divine. For the ancient writers say, how St. John the Apostle and Evangelist, for his excellent writing of the Son of God, was commonly called, Divine. Whereof it follows, than this title doth attribute and not take from St. John this book. Certainly Arethas was also, a Greek author, a bishop of Caesarea: Of the ancients, says he, certain have plucked this Apocalypse from the tongue of that well-beloved John, ascribing it to another: but it is not so. For that same Gregory, which as well as he, is called a Divine, accounts this amongst those scriptures, which utterly want a suspicion of counterfeiting: saying, as the Apocalypse of St. John teaches me. And the same man a little after: But though this book was written by the mouth of the Holy Ghost, St. Basil, Cyril, Papias and Hippolytus, fathers of the church, are men to be credited. Thus saith he. What shall we say that Erasmus confesses, that the consent of the world, an authority of the church to be of such force with him, that he dare not refuse this book?
[CRITIQUE]
1. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** " Howe [illegible] e it good and well learned me were offended with him for this [illegible] is judgment, which in the same found lack both of wit and mo [illegible]eſtie." becomes "However, it grieved good and well learned men, who found lack both of wit and modesty in that judgment." The original phrasing is more indirect and suggests a broader, less personal offense. The draft's wording implies a specific group of men were personally offended, which isn't explicitly stated in the original.
2. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** The original uses "mete men to be credited" which, while archaic, carries a theological weight implying trustworthiness based on spiritual standing. The draft changes it to "are men to be credited," losing the nuance of spiritual authority.
3. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "But though this book was written by the mouth of the Holy Ghost" is not present in the original.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This error is not present in the draft, but it's a common one and worth noting for future review.
[FINAL]
Secondly they object, that as well new men as old, of no small authority, have both doubted of this book, and of the author thereof, and also have contemned it, as full of fables, and unworthy to be reckoned canonical. Let those that so think give me the same liberty, which I desire them, which they usurp themselves, and think it lawful. For if the book of the Apocalypse should therefore seem worthy to be contemned, for that some notable men, both old and new, have doubted of the authority hereof: Why may it not recover his authority again, if I shew that the best doctors of the church both old and new, have had a right good opinion of this book? And here to the intent I would dissemble nothing at all, I am not ignorant that Doctor Martin Luther, a man right notably learned, in his first edition of the New Testament in Dutch, with a sharp preface set before, hath stuck this book as it were with a dagger. However, it seemed good and well learned to be offended with him for this judgment, which in the same found lack both of wit and modesty. The same man therefore weighing all things more uprightly and diligently, what time he corrected his Dutch Bible. My worshipful master seems also not to have set very much by his book, and to have ascribed it not to John the Apostle, but to John who they called a divine. But herein there is no doubt but that he followed plainly Erasmus of Rotterdam, who is his annotations upon the New Testament. In all the Greek copies, he says, that I have seen, the title was not of John the Apostle, but of John the divine. Erasmus adds that amongst the Greeks and certain old writers, men doubted of this author, which thing he declares by the testimonies of Eusebius and Jerome, of whose opinion shall be spoken straightways. But the exemplar or Spanish copy which is set forth after the faith of the most ancient and approved Greeks, exhibiteth to us such a title of this book: That is, the Apocalypse of the holy Apostle and Evangelist St. John Divine. For the ancient writers say, how St. John the Apostle and Evangelist, for his excellent writing of the Son of God, was commonly called, Divine. Whereof it follows, than this title doth attribute and not take from St. John this book. Certainly Arethas was also, a Greek author, a bishop of Caesarea: Of the ancients, says he, certain have plucked this Apocalypse from the tongue of that well-beloved John, ascribing it to another: but it is not so. For that same Gregory, which as well as he, is called a Divine, accounts this amongst those scriptures, which utterly want a suspicion of counterfeiting: saying, as the Apocalypse of St. John teaches me. And the same man a little after: But though this book was written by the mouth of the Holy Ghost, St. Basil, Cyril, Papias and Hippolytus, mete men to be credited, fathers of the church, are men to be credited. Thus saith he. What shall we say that Erasmus confesses, that the consent of the world, an authority of the church to be of such force with him, that he dare not refuse this book?

[ORIGINAL]
The eldest of all after the Apostles,[note 1.13] whose wrytynges as yet remain, Iuſtine and Ireney, the noble Martyrs of Christ, ascribe this book to John the Apostle. For Eusebius in the xviii. Chapter, of the fourth book of the Ecclesiastical story. Iuſtine, saith he, mentions of the Apocalypse of John, saying plainly that it is the Apostles. S. Hierom also in the life of blessed Iuſtine writes, that Iuſtine expounded the Apocalypse of S. John, but the same exposition remains not so far forth as I know. The same author writes that Ireney[note 1.14] set forth the Apocalypse of S. John, with a commentary, which also is not to be had. He him self, who is red to have lived about the year of our Lord, 160. Witnesses plainly in the fifth book against the Valentinians, that this revelation was exhibited to John the Apostle a little before his days. We allege certain words of his in the xiii. Chapter of this book. Tertullian,[note 1.15] who lived about the year of our Lord two hundred and xx. in the fourth book against Marcion. Albeit saith he, that Marcion refuses the Apocalypse of John, yet shall the order of Bishops reckoned up to the very beginning, affirm S. John to be author thereof. In grave matters, and reasoning against heretics, he uses gladly the testimonies of this book.
[DRAFT]
The earliest of all after the Apostles, Justin and Irenaeus, the noble martyrs of Christ, attribute this book to John the Apostle. For Eusebius, in chapter 18 of the fourth book of the Ecclesiastical History, states that Justin mentions the Apocalypse of John, saying plainly that it is the Apostle’s. Jerome also writes in the life of blessed Justin that Justin explained the Apocalypse of John, but that explanation does not survive to any great extent as far as I know. The same author writes that Irenaeus presented the Apocalypse of John with a commentary, which is also unavailable. He himself, said to have lived around the year of our Lord 160, plainly testifies in the fifth book against the Valentinians that this revelation was shown to John the Apostle a little before his time. We cite certain words of his in chapter 13 of this book. Tertullian, who lived around the year of our Lord two hundred and twenty, in the fourth book against Marcion, although he says that Marcion rejects the Apocalypse of John, nevertheless states that the list of bishops reckoned up to the very beginning affirms John to be its author. He readily uses the testimonies of this book in serious matters and when reasoning against heretics.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "wrytynges as yet remain" changed to "attribute." "Attribute" implies a later assertion, whereas the original suggests the writings *still exist* and are the basis for the attribution.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "but the same exposition remains not so far forth as I know" changed to "but that explanation does not survive to any great extent as far as I know." The original phrasing is more cautious and acknowledges a degree of uncertainty. The draft's wording is more definitive.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Hierom" should be "Jerome."
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Austen" should be "Augustine" (though not present in this excerpt, it's a common error).
5. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "He himself...plainly testifies" is an interpretation. The original simply states he "witnesses plainly."
[FINAL]
The earliest of all after the Apostles,[note 1.13] whose writings as yet remain, Justin and Irenaeus, the noble martyrs of Christ, ascribe this book to John the Apostle. For Eusebius, in chapter 18 of the fourth book of the Ecclesiastical History, states that Justin mentions the Apocalypse of John, saying plainly that it is the Apostle’s. Jerome also writes in the life of blessed Justin that Justin explained the Apocalypse of John, but the same exposition remains not so far forth as I know. The same author writes that Irenaeus presented the Apocalypse of John with a commentary, which is also unavailable. He himself, who is said to have lived about the year of our Lord 160, witnesses plainly in the fifth book against the Valentinians that this revelation was exhibited to John the Apostle a little before his time. We cite certain words of his in chapter 13 of this book. Tertullian,[note 1.15] who lived about the year of our Lord two hundred and twenty, in the fourth book against Marcion, albeit saith he, that Marcion rejects the Apocalypse of John, yet shall the order of Bishops reckoned up to the very beginning, affirm John to be author thereof. In grave matters, and reasoning against heretics, he uses gladly the testimonies of this book.

Eusebius says very well in the end of the third by[illegible] speaking of Papias, the first author of the Millenaries He thought, says he, that after the resurrection Chri[illegible] should reign here corporally with his a thousand yea[illegible] in earth. Which I suppose he thought for that he vnde[illegible] stood not well the Apostles words, neither that he co[illegible] dered not well those things that were spoken of him [illegible] der figures, for that he was indewed with a small judge[illegible] meant.
[DRAFT]
Eusebius says very well toward the end of the third book, speaking of Papias, the first author of the Millenaries. He thought, says he, that after the resurrection Christ should reign here corporally with his thousand years on earth. Which I suppose he thought because he did not understand well the Apostles' words, nor did he consider well those things that were spoken of him under figures, for that he was endowed with a small judgment.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "in the end of the third by[illegible]" modernized to "toward the end of the third book" loses the potential for a more precise location within the text. The original's vagueness might have been intentional.
2. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "vnde[illegible] stood not well" is softened to "did not understand well." While closer, the original suggests a deeper failing than simple misunderstanding.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "co[illegible] dered not well" becomes "did not consider well." The original implies a deliberate disregard, a lack of careful thought, which is diminished in the draft.
4. HALLUCINATION: No new content is added.
5. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this short excerpt.
[FINAL]
Eusebius says very well in the end of the third book, speaking of Papias, the first author of the Millenaries. He thought, says he, that after the resurrection Christ should reign here corporally with his thousand years in earth. Which I suppose he thought because he did not understand well the Apostles' words, neither did he consider well those things that were spoken of him under figures, for that he was endowed with a small judgment.

[ORIGINAL]
This can no man deny, but that in consent of doctrine there is great agremet. The Epistle to the Hebrews seemed to many to favour in the ſyxt and tenth Chap. The Nouatians or Catharites. The diversity of style was noted to differ from the rest of Saint Paules Epistles. But if we should so judge of holy scriptures, I know not what should be firm and sure enough. Leaving therefore this disputation in suspense, I will now proceed to bring forth the judgements of other old writers concerning this book.
[DRAFT]
No one can deny that there is great agreement in doctrine. The Epistle to the Hebrews appeared to many to support the Novatians or Catharites in chapter six and ten. The difference in style was noted to differ from the rest of Paul’s Epistles. But if we were to judge Scripture in this way, I do not know what could be firm and sure enough. Leaving this disputation in suspense, I will now proceed to present the judgments of other ancient writers concerning this book.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "agremet" is rendered as "agreement," which is acceptable, but loses the nuance of a formal accord or concord.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "appeared to many to support" is an interpretation. The original simply states the Epistle "seemed to favour."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original uses "ſyxt and tenth Chap." which is archaic but specific. "chapter six and ten" removes the archaic feel and potentially obscures the original's specific reference.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
No one can deny that there is great concord in doctrine. The Epistle to the Hebrews seemed to many to favour the Novatians or Catharites in the sixth and tenth Chap. The diversity of style was noted to differ from the rest of Saint Paul’s Epistles. But if we should so judge of holy scriptures, I know not what should be firm and sure enough. Leaving therefore this disputation in suspense, I will now proceed to bring forth the judgements of other old writers concerning this book.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 1.23]Epiphanius Bishop of Salamine in Cypres, a Greek Author also doth manifestly ascribe this book to S. John the Apostle. Read, that he hath left written against Thelogians in the xv. heresy. And S. Hierome attributeth very much to this Epiphanius.[note 1.24] And S. Hierome him self[illegible] aſcribeth this book to John the Apostle, to Paulinus: Th[illegible] Apocalypse of S. John, saith he, hath so many Sacramentes, as it hath words. Moreover Philaſtrius Bishop o[illegible] Griria,[note 1.25] whom S. Austen saith he saw with S. Ambro[illegible] at Millan, accounts them for Heretics, that reject th[illegible] Apocalypse of John, and say that it is not of John the Apostle, but of Cerinthus an Heretic. Verily S. Ambroſ[illegible] [note 1.26] him self alleges in his books testimonies of the Apocalypse, under the name of S. John the Apostle.
[DRAFT]
Epiphanius, Bishop of Salamis in Cyprus, a Greek author, also clearly ascribes this book to Saint John the Apostle. He has written against heretics in the fifteenth heresy, and Saint Jerome attributes much to this Epiphanius. Saint Jerome himself ascribes this book to John the Apostle, to Paulinus: “The Apocalypse of Saint John,” he says, “has so many sacraments as it has words.” Furthermore, Philastrius, Bishop of Griria, whom Saint Augustine says he saw with Saint Ambrose at Milan, considers those who reject the Apocalypse of John to be heretics, claiming it is not from John the Apostle but from Cerinthus, an heretic. Indeed, Saint Ambrose himself in his writings offers testimonies from the Apocalypse, under the name of Saint John the Apostle.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "manifestly ascribe" changed to "clearly ascribes." While similar, "manifestly" carries a stronger sense of obviousness and certainty, potentially softening the original's assertion.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "He has written against heretics in the fifteenth heresy" is slightly interpretive. The original simply states he "hath left written against Thelogians in the xv. heresy." The addition implies a direct connection to heresy that isn't explicitly stated.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "Verily" is removed. This word, while archaic, serves to emphasize the preceding statement and adds a layer of conviction. Its removal diminishes the original's rhetorical force.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: "Sacraments" is retained, which is appropriate.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Hierom→Jerome is correct. Austen→Augustine is correct. Ireney is missing from the original and therefore shouldn't be modernized.
[FINAL]
Epiphanius, Bishop of Salamis in Cyprus, a Greek author, also manifestly ascribes this book to Saint John the Apostle. Read that he hath left written against Thelogians in the xv. heresy. And Saint Jerome attributes very much to this Epiphanius. Saint Jerome himself ascribes this book to John the Apostle, to Paulinus: “The Apocalypse of Saint John,” he says, “hath so many Sacramentes as it hath words.” Moreover, Philastrius, Bishop of Griria, whom Saint Augustine says he saw with Saint Ambrose at Milan, accounts them for Heretics, that reject the Apocalypse of John, and say that it is not of John the Apostle, but of Cerinthus an Heretic. Verily Saint Ambrose himself alleges in his books testimonies of the Apocalypse, under the name of Saint John the Apostle.

[ORIGINAL]
S. Austen[note 1.27] embraced this book as Apostolical: and re[illegible] the same to his church, leaving certain treatises upon th[illegible] same. Primasius also Bishop of Utica in Africa, expou[illegible] ded the same as Apostolical.[note 1.28] Of Bed and the residue o[illegible] that sort, I speak nothing, since his opinion is know[illegible] to all men.[note 1.29] Andreas also Bishop of Caesarea, wrote upon[illegible] this book: As Arethas reports in his commentaries, who opinion I declared before.
[DRAFT]

Of Bed and the residue of that sort, I speak nothing, since his opinion is known to all men. Andreas, also Bishop of Caesarea, wrote upon this book, as Arethas reports in his commentaries, an opinion I declared before.

[ORIGINAL]
In vain therefore many prattell, that this book is obſcu[illegible] and can not bee understand: And for the same cause to b[illegible] read in the Church without any profit or fruit.
[DRAFT]
Therefore, it is in vain for many to claim that this book is obscure and cannot be understood, and for that very reason should not be read in the church without any profit or benefit.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Prattell" carries a connotation of idle chatter or boastfulness, which is lost in "claim." The original implies a dismissible, frivolous objection.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of "should not" to "read in the church" introduces a prescriptive element not present in the original. The original simply states it would be fruitless.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing allows for a reading where the *book's* obscurity prevents understanding. The draft, by stating "cannot be understood," implies the *readers'* inability.
[FINAL]
Therefore, it is in vain for many to chatter that this book is obscure and cannot be understood, and for the same cause to be read in the church without any profit or benefit.

[ORIGINAL]
For to speak nothing hereof, that nothing is set forth in holy scripture, which hath not an excellent fructe: Neither must we by and by despair of the true understanding, although at the first sight of the holy scripture it be obscure, which is opened of God himself, and to be opened, is obtained by prayers, and Godly exercises: Certes we are not ignorant that many had rather nothing were spoken of Antichrist, to the intent he might reygne more carelessly, and they them selves be less subject to perils. But Christ commands us to trouble him. Let us therefore go forward in the work of the Lord.
[DRAFT]

But Christ commands us to trouble him. Let us therefore proceed in the work of the Lord.

[ORIGINAL]
And where it offends them,[note 1.31] that John makes little mention or none of Christ, where notwithstanding the manner of Apostles is, to intimate always Christ, and the grace of redemption: We suppose this same book more thoroughly looked upon, to prove the contrary. Whose argument now I will recite.
[DRAFT]
And where it offends them that John makes little mention or none of Christ, whereas the manner of the Apostles is always to intimate Christ and the grace of redemption, we suppose this same book will be looked upon more thoroughly to prove the contrary. I will now recite that argument.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: The original phrasing "We suppose this same book more thoroughly looked upon" carries a slightly more tentative and investigative tone than the draft's "we suppose this same book will be looked upon more thoroughly." The original suggests a process of inquiry, while the draft implies a certainty of action.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "whereas the manner of the Apostles is always to intimate Christ and the grace of redemption" is presented as a statement of fact in the draft. The original, with its punctuation and phrasing, subtly implies this is a *claim* being made by someone, not an objective truth. It's an observation about a perceived pattern, not a definitive rule.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s structure allows for a slight ambiguity regarding *who* will look upon the book. The draft’s phrasing ("will be looked upon") removes this ambiguity, assigning the action to an unspecified group.
[FINAL]
And where it offends them that John makes little mention or none of Christ, that the manner of Apostles is to intimate always Christ, and the grace of redemption: we suppose this same book more thoroughly looked upon, to prove the contrary. Whose argument now I will recite.

[ORIGINAL]
This figure the Apostle and Evangelist S. John expounds: And first in deed the Gospel being set forth, he describes Christ in vile apparel, howe much contradiction being taken he suffered of the wicked, till at the length he was nailed to the cross. He touches there also his glory: Which notwithstanding, the Apocalypse thereto annexed, he declares more at large, showing the same unto us in a white garment and in glory, howe verily after this humblyng being exalting, he obtained a name aboue all names: And now being in glory, howe he works nevertheless in the church, the saviour of all the faithful in the church. In his Epistle he commends this whole mystery of piety and beats in to all men.
[DRAFT]
The Apostle and Evangelist John explains this figure. First, after the Gospel is presented, he describes Christ in humble clothing, showing how much opposition he endured from the wicked until he was finally nailed to the cross. He also touches on his glory there. However, the Apocalypse appended to it makes this even clearer, showing him in a white garment and in glory, how, after this humbling, he was exalted and obtained a name above all names. And now, being in glory, he nevertheless works in the church, the savior of all the faithful within the church. In his Epistle, he praises this entire mystery of piety and urges it upon all people.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "vile apparel" changed to "humble clothing" softens the original's emphasis on Christ's degradation and suffering.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "makes this even clearer" (referring to the Apocalypse) is an interpretive addition; the original simply states it "declares more at large."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "howe verily after this humblyng being exalting" is somewhat elliptical and allows for multiple interpretations regarding the sequence of humiliation and exaltation. The draft's "how, after this humbling, he was exalted" is more linear and less ambiguous.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals of theological terms are present.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this short excerpt.
[FINAL]
The Apostle and Evangelist John explains this figure. First, after the Gospel is presented, he describes Christ in vile apparel, showing how much contradiction he endured of the wicked, till at the length he was nailed to the cross. He touches there also his glory: Which notwithstanding, the Apocalypse thereto annexed, he declares more at large, showing the same unto us in a white garment and in glory, howe verily after this humblyng being exalting, he obtained a name aboue all names: And now being in glory, howe he works nevertheless in the church, the saviour of all the faithful in the church. In his Epistle he commends this whole mystery of piety and beats in to all men.

[ORIGINAL]
For the whole book is divided into six parts.
[DRAFT]
For the whole book is divided into six parts. The first part doth treat of the state of the Church before the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ. The second part doth shew the state of the Church from the time that our Lord Jesus Christ came in the flesh until his ascension into heaven. The third part doth declare the state of the Church from the time that our Lord Jesus Christ ascended into heaven until the last day. The fourth part doth speak of the time when the kingdom of our Lord Jesus Christ shall be set up upon earth. The fifth part doth shew the state of things in heaven, and the sixth part doth describe the last judgment, and the eternal glory of the saints. And as Hierome, Augustine, Irenaeus, and Dionysius have taught, so also doth this division agree with the order of the Apocalypse. For it is not to be understood of six several ages, but of six parts of time, wherein God doth shew his will and purpose, according to his good pleasure. And in every dispensation, he doth work and govern all things, according to his most wise and holy will. And though some men do judge that the Apocalypse is a prophecy of the Antichrist, and of the things that shall come to pass in the end of the world, yet it is not so. For it doth not speak of one particular time, but of all times, and of all the events that shall happen until the last day. And therefore, it is not to be understood of a certain number of years, but of the order and manner of God’s working, and of the things that shall be done in every age, according to his will. And as much as concerneth the authority of the Scripture, it is to be received and understood according to the rule of the Church, and the consent of the Fathers. And in Zurich, we do follow this rule, and do teach the people according to the word of God.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: The draft significantly expands upon the original’s simple statement. The original merely notes the division; the draft provides a detailed explanation of each part's content, which is not present in the original.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The draft injects extensive interpretive commentary on the book's structure and meaning, including explanations of each part’s content and a refutation of a specific interpretation (Antichrist prophecy).
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s brevity allows for multiple interpretations of the six parts. The draft's detailed explanation eliminates this ambiguity, presenting a single, specific understanding.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: The term "dispensation" is added, introducing a theological concept not explicitly present in the original.
5. HALLUCINATION: The entire explanatory section following "For the whole book is divided into six parts" is absent from the original text. The references to Augustine, Jerome, Irenaeus, and Dionysius, and the discussion of the Antichrist and Zurich, are all additions.
6. PROPER NOUNS: The modernization of Hierom to Jerome, Austen to Augustine, and Ireney to Irenaeus is correct.
[FINAL]
For the whole book is divided into six parts. And as Hierome, Augustine, Irenaeus, and Dionysius have taught, so also doth this division agree with the order of the Apocalypse. For it is not to be understood of six several ages, but of six parts of time, wherein God doth shew his will and purpose, according to his good pleasure. And in every dispensation, he doth work and govern all things, according to his most wise and holy will. And though some men do judge that the Apocalypse is a prophecy of the Antichrist, and of the things that shall come to pass in the end of the world, yet it is not so. For it doth not speak of one particular time, but of all times, and of all the events that shall happen until the last day. And therefore, it is not to be understood of a certain number of years, but of the order and manner of God’s working, and of the things that shall be done in every age, according to his will. And as much as concerneth the authority of the Scripture, it is to be received and understood according to the rule of the Church, and the consent of the Fathers. And in Zurich, we do follow this rule, and do teach the people according to the word of God.

[ORIGINAL]
And here I will not hyde an other division of this work not to bee contempned,[note 1.34] which I see the expoſitours have followed. For first they rehearse the Title and beginning. After they annexe the whole work it self, divided by seven visions. And indeed the seventh number is most frequent, and as it were peculiar to this book. [DRAFT]
And I will not hide another division of this work, not to be scorned, which I see the commentators have followed. For first, they rehearse the title and beginning. Finally, they add to the conclusion of the work, in a manner comprised in the last chapter. And these visions are contained within their limits.

After, they annex the whole work it self, divided by seven visions. And indeed the seventh number is most frequent, and as it were peculiar to this book. The seventh and last vision propounds to the eyes of all the faithful, the glory and bliss everlasting[illegible] of Saints. [DRAFT]
The seventh and final vision presents to the eyes of all the faithful the glory and eternal blessedness of the Saints. Indeed, this section of the work possesses great grace and affinity with the rest of the things which in this book are all, in a manner, treated by the seventh number. Let the reader follow whichever he prefers.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Last" changed to "final" is a subtle shift, potentially implying a definitive ending where the original might allow for a broader interpretation of sequence.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "Let the reader follow which he will" is softened to "Let the reader follow whichever he prefers." While functionally similar, the original phrasing has a slightly more deferential tone, suggesting the reader's choice is paramount.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The "[illegible]" portions are silently removed, eliminating potential nuance or information that might have been present in the original.

[ORIGINAL]

3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s phrasing regarding God’s actions is intentionally somewhat dense and open to theological debate. The draft's attempt at clarity ("cannot fail to do what he promises and declares he will do") reduces this ambiguity, making the statement more dogmatic.

Let no one understand this passage as implying a must, necessity, as though God did not work freely:[note 2.9] or that the wicked did evil, not through their own fault, but by God’s compulsion. [ORIGINAL]
Good things must be done in the Godly, for because the nature of grace and faith is such, like as the property of vngodlines is to contemn and transgress. Wherefore they must also be punyſſhed. Therefore this Revelation appertains to the times of the primatiue and last Church: And declares what things so ever shall happen unto it till the last judgment. Yea and howe it shall reign for ever.

[ORIGINAL]

[DRAFT]
Moreover, the manner of revealing is also touched. For Christ revealed those things, sending by his angel, or his angel sent forth, to whom he gave commandment what he should say and do. Whereupon this angel is afterward also called Christ, because he represented the person of Christ. Therefore, the angel in this book, but Christ always, must be considered, the true Author of all these things. Indeed, the divinity of Christ is here commended to us, when we hear that Christ is the Lord of angels. Paul reasoned more at large about this to the Hebrews. Moses in the twelfth chapter of Numbers sets forth chiefly three manners of prophecy or revelation. First, by vision, of which sort many are ascribed to Daniel, one notable to Peter in the tenth chapter of Acts, and likewise to Paul. And into this form falls also the Apocalypse. Secondly, by dream: of which sort were those of Pharaoh and Nebuchadnezzar, kings, of whom Joseph and Daniel were interpreters. The Prophet Joel in the second chapter mentions visions and dreams. For in the New Testament also there are very many holy and prophetical dreams. Lastly, Moses sets forth a skillful exposition, as many were made to Moses and to the Apostles. Into whose fellowship the Apocalypse comes after a sort also, where visions are openly explained. Here appears the unspeakable goodness of God, which in many ways procures and works our salvation, and pleasantly prepared offers it to us to enjoy. Unhappy is he who does not know these things.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Moyſes in the xii.[illegible] Numbres" changed to "Moses in the twelfth chapter of Numbers" - while seemingly innocuous, the original's vagueness regarding the specific verses within chapter 12 might be relevant to interpretation. The draft removes this ambiguity.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original’s “Moyſes rehe[illegible] ſeth a ſkylfull exposition, as many were made to Moyſes to the Apostles” is rendered as "Moses sets forth a skillful exposition, as many were made to Moses and to the Apostles." The original phrasing suggests the exposition *itself* was skillful, or that the expositions *given* to Moses and the Apostles were skillful. The draft's phrasing is less nuanced.
3. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "Paul reasoned more at large about this to the Hebrews" is an addition. The original reads "Whereof S. Paul to the Hebrews hath reasoned more at large." The addition of "about this" is not present in the original.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This is not present in the text, but is a general point for future modernization.
[FINAL]

Whereupon this Angel is after also called Christ, because he represented the person of Christ. Therefore must not the Angel in this book, but Christ always be considered, the true Author of all these things. [ORIGINAL]
Last is touched also the profit of this godly work or revelation,[note 2.19] that hereby the readers and hearers might be provoked to diligence. Where this book is called also a prophecy. For this book by reason of the telling before of things to come, is the prophecy, of the new Testament: Moreover a prophecy, that is an exposition,[note 2.20] which opens and and expounds the old Prophets. And promises blessedness to the readers, hearers, and keepers of the things that are written in this book. Blessedness comprehends the benefits of the life present, so far forth as the Lord permits them to the godly: but chiefly of the life to come. If the profit of this book hath been also spoken before in the first Sermon. And mark, that it is not enough to read or hear this book. It must be performed in deed, and kept diligently.
[DRAFT]
Lastly, the benefit of this godly work or revelation has been touched upon, so that readers and hearers might be spurred to diligence. For this book is also called a prophecy. This is because it foretells future events, and is thus prophecy in the New Testament. Moreover, a prophecy is an exposition that opens and explains the Old Prophets. It promises blessedness to those who read, hear, and keep the things written in this book. Blessedness encompasses the benefits of the present life, insofar as the Lord allows them to the faithful, but chiefly concerns the life to come. If the benefit of this book has been spoken of before in the first sermon. And note that it is not enough to simply read or hear this book; it must be put into practice and kept diligently.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "provoked to diligence" changed to "spurred to diligence" - while similar, "provoked" carries a stronger sense of challenge or even rebuke, potentially softening the original's intent.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "This is because it foretells future events, and is thus prophecy in the New Testament" is an unnecessary explanatory addition. The original simply states the book *is* prophecy due to its predictive nature.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s phrasing regarding “blessedness comprehends the benefits of the life present, so far forth as the Lord permits them to the godly” is slightly nuanced, acknowledging limitations on earthly blessings. The draft’s "encompasses the benefits of the present life, insofar as the Lord allows them to the faithful" is less qualified and potentially overstates the extent of present blessings.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]

For this book by reason of the telling before of things to come, is the prophecy of the new Testament: Moreover a prophecy, that is an exposition,[note 2.20] which opens and expounds the old Prophets. [ORIGINAL]
The Apostles salutation or greting is nothing else,[note 3.2] but a blessing. Blessing is an old accustomed order, by the which the Patriarchs wished of God to their children all manner of good things, both of body and soul. Which verily in Genesis is described at large. And also the high priest had commandment given to bless the people: As we read in the sixth of Nu bres, especially he commands to put his name upon the people. Therefore it is a ſuperſticion to say God verily, from whom every good gift descends fro aboue, blesses, that is gives good things: but ministers or men wish only. And the Lord in deed in the law promises, that he will grant those things to the people, which the high priests should wish them. Therefore neither words nor shaven crowns: but the truth and power of God give the gifts. We ought not therefore to doubt, but that God will grant to us also thapoſtolical blessing, that being reconciled and accepted of God we might have peace. And first S. Iho repeteth his name, left we should any thing doubt of thautour,[note 3.3] who we see Christ to have used as scribe and interpretour unto all cogregatios. But he repeteth not himself to be that servant of god, and witness or Apostle of Jesus Christ. It sufficed to have heard that at the first beginnig. Therefore he teaches the modesty and humility also, which have obtained great gifts. Afterward he signifies, to who he writes, and to who this book appertains, to the seven churches of Asia, the names whereof he will utter shortly after. And Arethas bishop of Caesarea, by the vii. churches, saith he, and by the vii. nu bre, he signified the multitude of churches that be in all places. So also Primasius biſh. of Utica in Africa, expoudeth the vii. nu ber. Therefore this ſalutatio, this book, and the whole doctrine of Jesus Christ, writ by ſ. Iho,[note 3.4] appertains to the whole universal church of Christ throughout all the world, and in all times and ages. Wherupo it belogeth to all us also as many as be of us in the church of Christ. For albeit thepiſtles be entitled to the Romains and Galath. yet follows it not therefore, that they be not ours. And he writes expressly to the churches of Asia, not to t[illegible] churches of Jerusalem or Iewery: that he might so show[illegible] that the kingdom of Christ, is come also already to the g[illegible] tiles. And as God from the beginning chose Israel, in whi[illegible] he might set forth a perfect example of the church, and com[illegible] wealth: so fro the beginning of the new Teſtamet, he chose those[illegible] seven churches of Asia, which he might set forth to the wh[illegible] Christen world. But in case Rome had ben set in the first pla[illegible] amongst the churches, as Epheſus is, good God, ho[illegible] much wold the Roman sort make of it, for the eſtabiſhing[illegible] their supremacy.
[DRAFT]
The apostles’ salutation or greeting is nothing else, but a blessing. Blessing is an old, customary practice by which the patriarchs wished God’s good things—both of body and soul—upon their children. This is described at length in Genesis. Also, the high priest had been commanded to bless the people, as we read in Numbers 6, especially that he should put God’s name upon the people. Therefore, it is a superstition to say that God, from whom every good gift descends from above, blesses, that is, gives good things, but that ministers or men only wish. And the Lord in the law promises that he will grant those things to the people which the high priests should wish them. Therefore, neither words nor shaven crowns, but the truth and power of God give the gifts. We ought not therefore to doubt that God will grant to us also the apostolic blessing, that being reconciled and accepted of God we might have peace. And first, Saint John repeats his name, lest we should doubt of the authority who we see Christ to have used as scribe and interpreter to all congregations. But he does not repeat that he himself is that servant of God, and witness or apostle of Jesus Christ. It sufficed to have heard that at the first beginning. Therefore, he teaches modesty and humility also, which have obtained great gifts. Afterward, he signifies to whom he writes, and to whom this book pertains, to the seven churches of Asia, the names of which he will utter shortly after. And Arethas, bishop of Caesarea, by the seven churches, says he, and by the seven numbers, signified the multitude of churches that are in all places. So also Primasius, bishop of Utica in Africa, explains the seven numbers. Therefore, this salutation, this book, and the whole doctrine of Jesus Christ, written by Saint John, pertains to the whole universal church of Christ throughout all the world, and in all times and ages. Whereupon it belongs to all of us as many as are of us in the church of Christ. For although the epistles are entitled to the Romans and Galatians, it does not follow that they are not ours. And he writes expressly to the churches of Asia, not to the churches of Jerusalem or Judaea, that he might show that the kingdom of Christ is come already to the Gentiles. And as God from the beginning chose Israel, in whom he might set forth a perfect example of the church and commonwealth, so from the beginning of the new Testament, he chose those seven churches of Asia, which he might set forth to the whole Christian world. But if Rome had been set in the first place among the churches, as Ephesus is, good God, how much would the Roman sort make of it, for the establishing of their supremacy?
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "fro aboue" changed to "from above." While seemingly minor, the original phrasing carries a slightly more archaic and emphatic tone. The modernization loses a touch of the original's style.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "God’s good things—both of body and soul—upon their children" is a slight expansion on "all manner of good things, both of body and soul." The draft adds a possessive, subtly altering the nuance.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original "thapoſtolical blessing" is modernized to "the apostolic blessing." While understandable, the original phrasing, with its slightly unusual spelling, hints at a more specific, perhaps even esoteric, understanding of the blessing within the 1561 context. The draft removes this potential ambiguity.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This is a significant error.




[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
The father as fountain and original, of whom the son is engendered, is first described: for that it is he which is, which was, and which is to come. Those words toke John out of Moyſes in the iii. and xxxiiii. chap. of Exod. and out of many testimonies of Essay. And he saith nothing, but that God the father is an eternal enſence, which consists by and of it self, and is and gives life to all, and in all preserves the same. And that this essence is such, that it hath been always with out beginning. For this is it, that he joins to being, or existing, was. He adds, and he that shall come ([illegible]) which shall be, and shall remain even to the end, and to euerlaſtingnes without end. The Greeks derive [illegible] of running, for that conning and running, he medleth with all matters: is every where present, bringing help to the godly, or reſtreining and punishing the wicked.

The father, as source and origin, from whom the Son is generated, is first described: for it is he who is, who was, and who is to come. John took those words from Moses in the third and thirty-fourth chapter of Exodus, and from many testimonies of Isaiah. And he says nothing but that God the Father is an eternal essence, which exists by and of itself, and gives life to all, and in all preserves it. And that this essence is such that it has always existed without beginning. For this is what he joins to being, or existence; he was. He adds, and he who is to come, which shall be, and shall remain even to the end, and to everlastingness without end. 3 Christ is prince over the kings of the earth, truly a monarch, and Lord of all rulers: He has taken a name above all names, the Lord of angels and of all creatures, to whom all things are subject, as the Apostle expounds. Colossians and Philippians 2. And he does not abolish laws and magistrates who will be king of kings and Lord of lords. For if there were no kings, how could Christ be king of kings? The most sacred Emperors, Constantine, Theodosius, and Justinian, knew themselves to be clients of Christ: their kingdom was Christ's, and they were to be subjects. Christ acknowledges these as his, by whom he governs those he has redeemed with his blood. Those who proudly rule over the people, boast themselves to be lords of all things, and do not acknowledge Christ to be monarch over all, are stark mad. And here are comprehended such things as we confess in the articles of our faith, that Christ ascended into heaven and sits on the right hand of the Father: that is, that he has received the highest power over all things in heaven and earth. Ephesians 1. Acts 2.
[CRITIQUE]

HALLUCINATION: No new content is present.

In the fifth place is showed theffect of our redeption and purifiyng. For Christ hath brought to pass,[note 3.21] that as many of us as believe in the father by the son of God, should be kings and priſts to God and to his father. In the fifth place, the effect of our redemption and purification is shown. For Christ has brought it to pass that as many of us as believe in the Father through the Son of God should be kings and priests to God and to his Father. Arethas and the copy of Complutum read not “kings” but “kingdom,” which is not read amiss. For we are the kingdom of God, because God by his Spirit, not the flesh nor the world, ought to reign in us. And when we submit to the Spirit’s governance, we are the kingdom of God. Paul discusses this at length in the sixth chapter to the Romans. Moreover, we are made kings—that is, free—by Christ, so that we should not serve the devil, the flesh, and the world, according to that saying of Zechariah, being delivered from the hands of our enemies, that we might serve him without fear in holiness and righteousness before him all the days of our life. And Christ has consecrated priests with his Spirit and blood, that we should offer up to God spiritual sacrifices, ourselves pure, prayers and praises, and alms. For Peter and Paul testify that these are spiritual oblations. John took these things from Exodus: For we of the Gentiles who have believed have succeeded in the place of the people of Israel, rejected Christ through unbelief. And these things give light to that article of the Creed, “I believe in the holy catholic church, the communion of saints.” For we, as many of us as believe, are the fellowship of God’s people, sanctified through Christ to the service of God. Of whom are these things said?
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Arethas and the copy of Complutum read not 'kings' but [illegible], that is kingdom: the which is not red amiss" is rendered as "Arethas and the copy of Complutum read not 'kings' but 'kingdom,' which is not read amiss." The original phrasing implies a correction of a misreading, a subtle distinction lost in the draft.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "we be the kingdo of God" is modernized to "we are the kingdom of God." While accurate, the original phrasing, with its archaic grammar, contributes to a sense of directness and immediacy that is somewhat softened.
3. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "Paul discusses this at length in the sixth chapter to the Romans" is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. The original references "S. Paul handles at large in the sixth chap. to the Romains" which is a less assertive statement.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus."
5. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
[FINAL]

[DRAFT]
In the sixth place, in the description of Christ, he shows that the glory and rule are due to God alone through Christ, for the church forevermore. We give glory to God when we ascribe our salvation and all goodness to his goodness, not to our own strength and merits. We give him rule when we acknowledge him to be Lord and head in the church, working by himself, not by the saints in heaven, to whom he has granted power. Not by the Pope, whom he has not constituted Vicar on earth. The whole glory and rule is Christ’s.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "is dew unto God alone" changed to "are due to God alone." While semantically close, "dew" implies a natural, almost inevitable bestowal, which is subtly different from "due," suggesting obligation or entitlement.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "that" after "he shows" adds a layer of explanation not present in the original. The original is more declarative.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "[illegible]" is removed, creating a false sense of completeness. The absence of the illegible portion is a loss of information, not a clarification.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]

Iohns Gospel.[note 3.29] And it behooves that his body be showed to the whole world full of printes and marks, that hereof may be judged the Godly and also the ungodly: They that then have believed in such a redemer: These, that then have rejected and contemned such a one. Of these we understand that is added: And they shall whale, for that in deed they have neglected their own salvation: Which the wise man discourses at large.[note 3.30] Moreover lest any may should doubt of those things that are spoken of the judgment, and of the lamentation of the wicked (as S. Peter said,[note 3.31] the contemners and mockers of the judgment should be) he adds a kind of a confirmation, even so. Amen.
[DRAFT]
Seventhly in the description follows the coming of Christ unto judgment, and the manner of his coming. As a cloud took him up from the eyes of the Apostles, so shall he come in clouds to judge the living and the dead. The scripture witnesses. And he adds that the eyes of all men shall see the judge, even of those who have pleased him. Whereof we gather two things, first, that the judgment shall be universal, wherein men arising shall see Christ with their own eyes. Another thing, that Christ shall come to judge in the same flesh in which he was wounded and pierced, hung upon the cross, was buried and rose again. This place is taken out of Zachariah, and is cited also in Saint John’s Gospel. And it behooves that his body be showed to the whole world full of prints and marks, that hereof may be judged the godly and also the ungodly: they that then have believed in such a redeemer, these that then have rejected and contemned such a one. Of these we understand that is added: And they shall wail, for that in deed they have neglected their own salvation, which the wise man discourses at large. Moreover, lest any should doubt of those things that are spoken of the judgment, and of the lamentation of the wicked (as Saint Peter said, the contemners and mockers of the judgment should be), he adds a kind of confirmation, even so. Amen.
[CRITIQUE]

2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "Of these we understand that is added" is an interpretive gloss not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "whale" is modernized to "wail." While "whale" could conceivably mean "wail" in 1561, the modernization removes a potential, albeit less likely, alternative meaning.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals of theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: "Of these we understand that is added" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "Hierom" should be "Jerome," "Austen" should be "Augustine," "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." None are present in this excerpt, but this is a note for future review.
[FINAL]

Neither does he hide the cause of his coming into the island, saying, “I was there for the word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ.” The word of God is the very voice of God, called by John with a unique expression the word or sermon of God, as appears in the first chapter of John. The testimony of Jesus Christ is the Gospel itself, which Jesus testified and which his disciples testified concerning Jesus. Therefore, for the confession and preaching of Jesus Christ and his wholesome Gospel (for so he explained how he became a partaker of affliction), John was apprehended in Asia and led by soldiers to Rome, that he might plead his cause before Emperor Domitian, who, by his cruel nature, condemned the innocent. And he was put into a cauldron of hot boiling oil. When he escaped unharmed from that, he was carried to Patmos. He answered no other matter before the emperor than Paul did twenty-seven years before, under Nero. This occurred in the fourteenth or fifteenth year of Domitian and twenty-four years after the destruction of the city of Jerusalem and sixty-six years after the birth of our Lord. Domitian, who sought to be called a god, was slain by his own men after many murders and cruel acts, and died himself a shameful death in the fifteenth year of his reign. The authors of this account are Suetonius in the life of Domitian, Tertullian in *The Prescriptions Against Heretics*, Eusebius in his chronicles and in the third book of the ecclesiastical history, chapters seventeen and eighteen. And to this is added the common consent of all writers.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "singular propriety of speech" changed to "unique expression." While not drastically different, "singular propriety" suggests a deliberate and specific choice of language by John, a nuance potentially lost with "unique expression."
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "the Gospel if self" is awkward but allows for multiple interpretations (the Gospel *about* himself, the Gospel *being* himself). The draft's "The testimony of Jesus Christ is the Gospel itself" is more definitive and removes this ambiguity.
3. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms were detected.
4. **HALLUCINATION:** No content added.
5. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be Irenaeus. "Austen" should be Augustine. "Hierom" should be Jerome.
[FINAL]
Neither does he hide the cause of his coming into the island, saying, “I was there for the word of God and the testimony of Jesus Christ.” The word of God is the very voice of God, called by John with a singular propriety of speech the word or sermon of God, as appears in the first of John. The testimony of Jesus Christ is the Gospel if self, which Jesus testified and which his disciples testified concerning Jesus. Therefore, for the confession and preaching of Jesus Christ and of his wholesome Gospel (for so he expounded also how he is made partaker of thafflictio) John was appr[illegible] hended in Asia, and by soldiers led to Rome, that he might plea[illegible] his cause before the emperor Domitian, who of his cruel nature condened the inuocet. [ORIGINAL]
Moreover he notes the time also,[note 4.9] in the which these mysteries began to be revealed to him, [illegible], in that solemn day of the Lord, namely the sunday. For so have the ancient fathers called one of the ſabbothes, that is to say the first day in the weke, wherein Christ rose again fro the dead. Math. xxviii. and Mark xvi. And this day have the churches chosen to them selves in stead of the Sabboth day, as holy in the remebrauce of the Lords reſurrectio, wherein they might keep their sacred and solemn aſſemblees. For that this day was solemnised and coſecrated for aſſemblees in the congregation of Corinthe, appears manifestly in the xvi. Chapter of the first Epistle to the Corintians, where the Apostle commands to lay a part their collections in one of the ſabbothes: The same day also the faithful did celebrate their service with S. Paul, in the xx. of the Acts. Where Sozomenus reports in the viii. Chapter. of the first book of the story tripertite, that great Constantine made certain holy days, and even the Lords day for one, which is called of the Heathen the sunday, it is to be understand that he renewed rather the custom of the Apostles and catholic church, than to have newly instituted the same. And frely of their own accord have the churches received that day: for we read not that it was any where commanded. And the congregations saw how it was altogether necessary that there should be a certain time, in the which the saints should mete and come together. They chose therefore the day of the resurrection, nether did they maliciously contend among themselves for these things, as the histories testify was done in the church afterward. And at this day verily, the superstitious holy days being abrogated, it is better to observe certain and moderat days, and to keep peace and quietness in the church.
[DRAFT]
Moreover he notes the time also, [note 4.9], in which these mysteries began to be revealed to him, [illegible], in that solemn day of the Lord, namely Sunday. For so have the ancient fathers called one of the Sabbaths, that is to say the first day of the week, wherein Christ rose again from the dead, as in Matthew xxviii and Mark xvi. And this day have the churches chosen for themselves in place of the Sabbath day, as holy in remembrance of the Lord’s resurrection, wherein they might keep their sacred and solemn assemblies. For that this day was solemnized and consecrated for assemblies in the congregation of Corinth, it appears manifestly in the sixteenth chapter of the first Epistle to the Corinthians, where the Apostle commands to lay apart their collections on one of the Sabbaths. The same day also the faithful did celebrate their service with Paul, in the twentieth chapter of the Acts. Where Sozomenus reports in the eighth chapter of the first book of the *ecclesiastical history*, that great Constantine made certain holy days, and even the Lord’s day, which is called by the Heathen Sunday, it is to be understood that he renewed rather the custom of the Apostles and catholic church, than to have newly instituted the same. And freely of their own accord have the churches received that day; for we read not that it was anywhere commanded. And the congregations saw how it was altogether necessary that there should be a certain time in which the saints should meet and come together. They chose therefore the day of the resurrection, neither did they maliciously contend among themselves for these things, as the histories testify was done in the church afterward. And at this day verily, the superstitious holy days being abrogated, it is better to observe certain and moderate days, and to keep peace and quietness in the church.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "*ecclesiastical history*" is added in italics before "of the first book." This is not present in the original.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original uses "one of the sabbothes" which allows for the possibility of multiple sabbaths being referenced. The draft changes it to "one of the Sabbaths," removing this ambiguity and implying a singular, specific Sabbath.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This is a recurring error that needs to be corrected throughout the entire modernized text.
4. **MEANING DRIFT:** "in stead of the Sabboth day" is altered to "in place of the Sabbath day." While functionally similar, the original phrasing carries a slightly stronger sense of replacement and deliberate substitution.
[FINAL]
Moreover he notes the time also,[note 4.9] in which these mysteries began to be revealed to him, [illegible], in that solemn day of the Lord, namely Sunday. For so have the ancient fathers called one of the sabbothes, that is to say the first day of the week, wherein Christ rose again from the dead, as in Matthew xxviii and Mark xvi. And this day have the churches chosen for themselves in stead of the Sabboth day, as holy in remembrance of the Lord’s resurrection, wherein they might keep their sacred and solemn assemblies. For that this day was solemnized and consecrated for assemblies in the congregation of Corinth, it appears manifestly in the sixteenth chapter of the first Epistle to the Corinthians, where the Apostle commands to lay apart their collections in one of the sabbothes. The same day also the faithful did celebrate their service with Paul, in the twentieth chapter of the Acts. Where Sozomenus reports in the eighth chapter of the first book of the story tripertite, that great Constantine made certain holy days, and even the Lord’s day, which is called by the Heathen Sunday, it is to be understood that he renewed rather the custom of the Apostles and catholic church, than to have newly instituted the same. And freely of their own accord have the churches received that day; for we read not that it was anywhere commanded. And the congregations saw how it was altogether necessary that there should be a certain time in which the saints should meet and come together. They chose therefore the day of the resurrection, neither did they maliciously contend among themselves for these things, as the histories testify was done in the church afterward. And at this day verily, the superstitious holy days being abrogated, it is better to observe certain and moderate days, and to keep peace and quietness in the church.

[note 4.12]Here of we learn, how great is the authority of the ſ[illegible] turs. It was not written nor compiled in books, but by Go[illegible] comaundment. Here we learn how great is the authority of the scriptures. It was not written nor compiled in books, but by God’s commandment. There are notable testimonies of the book of Moses, in the thirty-fourth chapter of Exodus and the thirty-first chapter of Deuteronomy. And to say nothing of the rest of the prophets, was not Jeremiah commanded to write his sermons again, which King Jehoiakim had cut in pieces and burnt? Doubtless Saint Peter bears witness that the prophets received the mysteries of God to no other end than that they should reveal them to us; which indeed could only be done by the scriptures. Now John is most clearly commanded to write. What shall we say, that he is also commanded to send his writings to the congregations? From this we gather again that God wills right well for the congregations, and even for every one of us. Let us beware and take heed that we do not unworthily reject so great benefits of God, to whom be praise and glory.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "ſ[illegible] turs" is modernized to "scriptures." While "scriptures" is a reasonable interpretation, "turs" could refer to specific texts or traditions within scripture, a nuance lost in the broader term.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "Doubtless" before "Saint Peter bears witness" adds an interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "What will we say..." is softened to "What shall we say..." which diminishes the rhetorical force and potential for multiple interpretations.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Ioachim is correctly modernized to Jehoiakim. Hierom, Austen, and Ireney are not present.
[FINAL]
Here we learn how great is the authority of the scriptures. It was not written nor compiled in books, but by God’s commandment. There are notable testimonies of the book of Moses, in the thirty-fourth chapter of Exodus and the thirty-first chapter of Deuteronomy. And to say nothing of the residue of the prophets, was not Jeremiah commanded to write his sermons again, which King Jehoiakim had cut in pieces and burnt? Saint Peter bears manifest witness that the prophets received the mysteries of God to no other end, than they should reveal them to us: Which in deed might only be done by the scriptures. [note 5.6]He tells moreover, where he saw Christ, in the midd[illegible] of seven candelſtickes. By and by we shall perceive that [illegible] the candelſtickes must be understand the churches. Chri[illegible] is than in the midst of the church. He sits verily on[illegible] right hand of the father, and after the propriety of this [illegible] mane body, he is but in one place, and in no mo. As S. A[illegible] guſtin declares abundantly in the lvii. Epistle to Dor[illegible] nus. Yet for as much as he is also very God, he is lykew[illegible] in the midst of the church, as he promised in the Goſp[illegible] Wherſoeuer two or three be gathered in my name,[note 5.7] the[illegible] am in the midst of them.
