(Leave therefore to inquire, what Christ doth [illegible] the right hand of his father, whether he sit continually?) And he is verily in the midst of the churches, fixed to [illegible] place, but showing him self indifferently to all eagle as helpefull. For he neither accepts persons, nor sleep[illegible] He is not painted, he is not Idle, nothing regarding matters of the church: But is chiefly and only attentive to the salvation of the same. Such a one he promised him self to be in the.14.15. and.16. of John. And saying Christ is in the midst of the church, what Vicar moreover shall he have? Shall he have that ennemy which is directly against him?[note 5.8] For a Vicar is in stead of one absent: But Christ is in the middell of the church, present, not absent.
[DRAFT]
He tells moreover, where he saw Christ in the midst of seven candlesticks. By and by we shall perceive that the candlesticks must be understood as the churches. Christ is then in the midst of the church. He sits verily on the right hand of the Father, and according to the propriety of his divine body, he is but in one place, and in no more. As Saint Augustine declares abundantly in the 57th Epistle to Dionysius. Yet for as much as he is also very God, he is likewise in the midst of the church, as he promised in the Gospel: Wherever two or three are gathered in my name, I am in the midst of them. And again: Behold, I am with you unto the world’s end. Therefore, by his divine power, Christ remains and works in the present church, and is not absent. (Leave therefore to inquire, what Christ does on the right hand of his Father, whether he sits continually?) And he is verily in the midst of the churches, fixed to a place, but showing himself indifferently to all, as helpful. For he neither accepts persons, nor sleeps. He is not painted, he is not idle, nothing regarding matters of the church: but is chiefly and only attentive to the salvation of the same. Such a one he promised himself to be in the 14th, 15th, and 16th chapters of John. And saying Christ is in the midst of the church, what Vicar moreover shall he have? Shall he have that enemy which is directly against him? For a Vicar is in the stead of one absent: but Christ is in the middle of the church, present, not absent.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "propriety of this mane body" changed to "propriety of his divine body." The original phrasing, while awkward, refers to Christ's human body. The draft's alteration shifts the focus to his divinity, potentially altering the intended nuance about his dual nature.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "after the propriety of this mane body" is vague and allows for multiple interpretations regarding Christ's presence. The draft's "according to the propriety of his divine body" removes this ambiguity, imposing a specific theological understanding.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "S. A[illegible] guſtin" modernized to "Saint Augustine" is correct.
4. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is added.
5. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms are changed or removed.
[FINAL]
He tells moreover, where he saw Christ in the midst of seven candlesticks. By and by we shall perceive that the candlesticks must be understood as the churches. Christ is then in the midst of the church. He sits verily on the right hand of the Father, and after the propriety of this mane body, he is but in one place, and in no more. As Saint Augustine declares abundantly in the 57th Epistle to Dionysius. Yet for as much as he is also very God, he is likewise in the midst of the church, as he promised in the Gospel: Wherever two or three are gathered in my name, I am in the midst of them. And again: Behold, I am with you unto the world’s end. Therefore, by his divine power, Christ remains and works in the present church, and is not absent. (Leave therefore to inquire, what Christ does on the right hand of his Father, whether he sits continually?) And he is verily in the midst of the churches, fixed to a place, but showing himself indifferently to all, as helpful. For he neither accepts persons, nor sleeps. He is not painted, he is not idle, nothing regarding matters of the church: but is chiefly and only attentive to the salvation of the same. Such a one he promised himself to be in the 14th, 15th, and 16th chapters of John. And saying Christ is in the midst of the church, what Vicar moreover shall he have? Shall he have that enemy which is directly against him? For a Vicar is in the stead of one absent: but Christ is in the middle of the church, present, not absent.

[ORIGINAL]
In the text following Christ is described most plentifully, many things are ascribed unto him: And is declared in what sort Christ is in the midst of the church. And first indeed is showed, what garment he hath on: To wit both priestly and princely. By the which thing is figured, what manner of one Christ is in heaven and in earth: To wit bishop and king, intercessor, mediator, and sacrifice, a most perfect sanctification and justification, a redemer and deliverer of the faithful to his father, evermore working the salvation of his faithful. As S. Paul teaches, Roma. 8. Hebr. 7. Poderes[note 5.9] is found amongst the apparel of Aaron, and it is a priestly garment. Whereof S. Hierome writes to Fabiola, of the priestly garment. The second veſture of linen is a coat down to the foot, of double launde: Which Ioſephus calls Biſsina. And it is called in Hebrew ketheneth, in Greek, [illegible]. This cleaves just to the body, and is so narrow and ſtrayte ſleued, that there is no wryncle at all in the garment, and came down to the legs. This was verily white and clean.[note 5.10] For the Lord Christ is an undefiled Priest. Hebre. 7. Neither wears he again a foul veſture, as he did. Zachar. 3. Nor a purple, as in the.19. of John. But a bright one, as he that hath obtained a name aboue all names. But his gyrdell or belte is worn of Soldiers and triumphaunt persons: And it signifies in Christ the dignity royal. For Christ is king, delyuerer and redemer of the faithful. His victory is ours. He hath overcome Satan, Hell, sin and death.

And so hath our Lord Christ exhibited him self to us to be scene unto salvation, and hath opened him self wholly unto us, as he is, what he doth for us, and howe he is in his church. [ORIGINAL]
Finally he adds,[note 6.11] and I have the keys of Hell and of death. By the which words again he comfort exceedingly, and expresses his power: and declares howe great he is, and what we have of him. Here must we speak by the way of the keep. The ordinary gloss says very well: he that hath the keys of any house, says he, lets in whom he will, and kepeth back whom he will from entering in. Therefore Christ possesses the keys of death and hell, for that whom he will, he delivers from perpetual codemnation of death: And whom he will,[note 6.12] he suffers to remain iuſtely in the same danger of damnation. And verily Isaiah in the 22. speaking of Eliachim, whom he says should be made Judge in the court of Ezechias: I will lay says he the key of the house of David upon his shoulder: which shall open, and no man shall shut: shall shut, and no man shall open. Therefore are the keys put in the Scripture for the charge and government of the house. Eliachim shall govern all thing in the Court of Ezechias uprightly. What soever he shall determine, no man shall infringe, that which he shall abrogate, no man shall restore. Christ therefore, a figure of whom Eliachim represented, shall him self have also the chief government in the house or kingdom of God: so that whom he will, he may quick, and pluck back from hell and from damnation: And again whom he list to condemn, he may diſtroie by his just judgment. For he hath most full power over death and hell.[note 6.13] For both two hath he overcome and made weak. And these things comfort the faithful most ſtrongely, and reteyne them in all Godly duties. And that same is chiefly to be observed, that he says not, he had the keys, or shall have, but I have, says he, I have I say. He gave not his power to the Bishop of Rome, but hath i[illegible] him self, and will keep it still for ever. And he gave not to the Apostles full power of life and death, of salvation and damnation,[note 6.14] and so unarmed him self: but he gave the keys of opening and shutting heaven, as it were to his Ministers and servitors, by the preaching of the Gospel: by the which he promised life to all that believed, Christ him self should give that life for the truth of the promise: To whom so ever they should threaten damnation, Christ him self should condemn for the truth of his word. We see therefore that the Lord kepeth still and exercises the power, and his ministers the ministry (by preaching, not by absolute power.) Therefore the Pope is Antichrist,[note 6.15] which usurps and takes upon him this full power and authority in Heaven and in Earth, and in the midst of the earth also, or beyond all the earth, i[illegible] those unfortunate Ilandes. I mean purgatory. By the which[illegible] crafty devise he hath subtly emptied the purses, Coffer[illegible], garuers and wine cellars,[note 6.16] of foolish people that ſwarne from the articles of their belief, to wit I believe the forgeuenes o[illegible] sins, the resurrection of the flesh, and life everlasting. Th[illegible] beast dare usurp the two horns of the Lamb,[note 6.17] namely th[illegible] authority of King and Bishop, and therefore to hang two key[illegible] under his triple Crown: that even by these Arms all the world may perceive, that this is very he, which, hauin[illegible] subdued three kings or horns, is crop up, and challenges t[illegible] him self all power in heaven and in earth, signified by the tw[illegible] keys. And surely the blindness of our time is wonderful an[illegible] to be lameted, that having eyes it sees yet nothing. Let ſuc[illegible] as be wise, remember, that Christ hath yet the keys of deat[illegible] and Hell, his ministers the denouncing of life and death.
[DRAFT]
Finally he adds,[note 6.11] and I have the keys of Hell and of death. By these words again he comforts exceedingly, and expresses his power: and declares how great he is, and what we have of him. Here we must speak by the way of the key. The ordinary gloss says very well: he who has the keys of any house, says he, lets in whom he will, and keeps back whom he will from entering in. Therefore Christ possesses the keys of death and hell, for that whom he will, he delivers from perpetual condemnation of death: And whom he will,[note 6.12] he suffers to remain justly in the same danger of damnation. And verily Isaiah in the 22nd verse, speaking of Eliakim, whom he says should be made Judge in the court of Hezekiah: I will lay, says he, the key of the house of David upon his shoulder: which shall open, and no man shall shut; shall shut, and no man shall open. Therefore, keys are put in Scripture for the charge and government of the house. Eliakim shall govern all things in the court of Hezekiah uprightly. Whatever he shall determine, no man shall infringe; that which he shall abrogate, no man shall restore. Christ therefore, a figure of whom Eliakim represented, shall himself have also the chief government in the house or kingdom of God: so that whom he will, he may quicken, and pluck back from hell and from damnation; and again, whom he lists to condemn, he may destroy by his just judgment. For he has most full power over death and hell.[note 6.13] For both two he has overcome and made weak. And these things comfort the faithful most strongly, and retain them in all godly duties. And that same is chiefly to be observed, that he says not, he had the keys, or shall have, but I have, says he, I have; I say. He gave not his power to the Bishop of Rome, but has it himself, and will keep it still forever. And he gave not to the Apostles full power of life and death, of salvation and damnation,[note 6.14] and so divested himself: but he gave the keys of opening and shutting heaven, as it were to his Ministers and servitors, by the preaching of the Gospel: by which he promised life to all that believed, Christ himself should give that life for the truth of the promise: To whom so ever they should threaten damnation, Christ himself should condemn for the truth of his word. We see therefore that the Lord keeps still and exercises the power, and his ministers the ministry (by preaching, not by absolute power). Therefore the Pope is Antichrist,[note 6.15] which usurps and takes upon him this full power and authority in Heaven and in Earth, and in the midst of the earth also, or beyond all the earth, in those unfortunate lands. I mean purgatory. By this crafty device he has subtly emptied the purses, coffers, garners, and wine cellars,[note 6.16] of foolish people that swarm from the articles of their belief, to wit, I believe the forgiveness of sins, the resurrection of the flesh, and life everlasting. The beast dares usurp the two horns of the Lamb,[note 6.17] namely the authority of King and Bishop, and therefore hangs two keys under his triple Crown: that even by these arms all the world may perceive that this is very he, who, having subdued three kings or horns, is cropped up, and challenges himself all power in heaven and in earth, signified by the two keys. And surely the blindness of our time is wonderful and to be lamented, that having eyes it sees yet nothing. Let such as be wise remember that Christ has yet the keys of death and Hell, his ministers the denouncing of life and death.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "By the which words" changed to "By these words." While seemingly minor, "which" refers back to the preceding statement ("Finally he adds..."), creating a more direct connection in the original. "These" is more generic and loses that specific link.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "Here must we speak by the keep" changed to "Here we must speak by the way of the key." The original "keep" refers to a fortified place, implying a strategic, guarded discussion. The modernized version loses this nuance, flattening the metaphor.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "For both two hath he overcome" changed to "For both two he has overcome." The original phrasing is deliberately unusual, potentially hinting at a symbolic or mystical overcoming. The modernized version is grammatically correct but loses the original's peculiar emphasis.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes to core theological terms (Antichrist, Apocalypse, dispensation, sacrament).
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ezechias" changed to "Hezekiah." Correct. "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This was missed.




[FINAL]
Finally he adds,[note 6.11] and I have the keys of Hell and of death. By the which words again he comforts exceedingly, and expresses his power: and declares how great he is, and what we have of him. Here we must speak by the way of the keep. The ordinary gloss says very well: he that hath the keys of any house, says he, lets in whom he will, and kepeth back whom he will from entering in. Therefore Christ possesses the keys of death and hell, for that whom he will, he delivers from perpetual codemnation of death: And whom he will,[note 6.12] he suffers to remain iuſtely in the same danger of damnation. And verily Isaiah in the 22. speaking of Eliachim, whom he says should be made Judge in the court of Hezekiah: I will lay says he the key of the house of David upon his shoulder: which shall open, and no man shall shut: shall shut, and no man shall open. Therefore are the keys put in the Scripture for the charge and government of the house. Eliachim shall govern all thing in the Court of Hezekiah uprightly. What soever he shall determine, no man shall infringe, that which he shall abrogate, no man shall restore. And now when he had declared these great and wholesome matters, and had comforted the mind of John, he adds the commandment: write the vision exhibited, finally write those things also which must be done shortly after this. He places in the midst, and those that be: that is, which are in deed, and true, and be not false. And these things are to give authority to this book, finally to the whole scripture, which is revealed with like truth of the same Author. And as John is commanded to write without fear, so are we commanded to preach and publish the same boldly though the world be never so mad thereat.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "get authority" replaced with "give authority" subtly shifts agency; the original implies the book *receives* authority, the draft suggests it *bestows* it.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s phrasing “to the whole scripture” is slightly less definitive than the draft’s “to the whole scripture.” The original leaves open a broader interpretation of the book’s relationship to scripture.
THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes.
[FINAL]
And now when he had declared these great and wholesome matters, and had comforted the mind of John, he adds the commandment: write the vision exhibited, finally write those things also which must be done shortly after this. He places in the midst, and those that be: that is, which are in deed, and true, and be not false. And these things are to get authority to this book, finally to the whole scripture, which is revealed with like truth of the same Author. And as John is commanded to write without fear, so are we commanded to preach and publish the same boldly though the world be never so mad thereat.

Stars are called Angels. Angels are God’s messengers, pastors of churches, so called in the second and third chapters of Malachi. For God sends preachers as ambassadors to the people, and wills them to be heard as if it were himself. Luke 10; John 13. Let no man therefore tarry until the Lord himself comes down from heaven again and preach[illegible] to us. Even now he preaches to us by his messengers, who preach his, that is to say, the word of Christ; if you contemn them, you contemn Christ. Preachers are called stars, because of their bright and heavenly doctrine, and for their purity of life.[note 6.22] Beware therefore you preachers, that you be not wandering plants, lest you have no light at all, neither in doctrine nor in conversation of life. For then you shall be likened to stars that fall down[illegible] out of heaven, as shall happen hereafter in this book to false teachers.

2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None.

But great is the authority of these epistles. For they are revealed from the throne of God, by the Son of God speaking through an angel, who dictates what is to be written in those epistles. John receives and writes the same, through Christ’s commandment, and sends them to the seven congregations. And truly, they pertain no less to us than if now a messenger entering into the church should deliver these letters to us.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Go[illegible]" changed to "God." While likely a transcription error in the original, altering it to "God" removes the possibility that the original scribe intended something else.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "which prescribes what is to be written" changed to "who dictates." "Dictates" implies a more forceful and direct imposition than "prescribes," subtly shifting the agency.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "aperteyne n[illegible] less" changed to "pertain no less." The original phrasing, with its archaic verb, allows for a broader range of interpretations regarding the relevance of the epistles. The modernized version is more direct and less nuanced.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.

For they are revealed from the throne of God, by the son of God speaking by an angel, which prescribes what is to be written in those Epistles: John receives and writes the same, through Christ his commandment, and sends them to the seven congregations. And not without cause he chooses to the seven most noble cities of Asia. It is certain that Asia was among the first regions inhabited, and from there people were dispersed into other parts of the world. It is also certain that the Devil set up his throne in Asia, and there reigned in men through idolatry, murder, ambition, avarice, uncleanness, and carnal pleasures. For the proverb says, "the laughter of Jovial [?]." It is known what the apostle wrote to the Ephesians in the fourth and fifth chapters. Therefore, our Savior Christ would overthrow that throne of the Devil and set up his throne of righteousness and holiness. [CRITIQUE]

2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The bracketed phrase "Jovial [?]" is an attempt to clarify a likely corrupted word. This introduces an interpretive element where the original left the reader to grapple with the corruption.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's illegible word before "Asia" is lost. This could have been a descriptor adding nuance to the selection of cities.

[FINAL]
And not without cause he chooses to the seven most noble cities of Asia. It is certain that Asia was of the first regions inhabited, and from thence they were dispersed into [illegible] there parts of the world. It is also certain that the Devil set up his throne in Asia, and there reigned in men through idolatry, murder, ambition, avarice, uncleanness, and carnal pleasures. For the proverb is known, the laughter Iovica[illegible]. It is known what the apostle wrote to the Ephesians in the fourth and fifth chapters. Therefore, our Savior Christ would overthrow that throne of the Devil and set up his throne of righteousness and holiness. Therefore, he goes first and chiefly to them of Asia, that by their example the whole world might be corrected and amended.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 7.5]And amongst other cities of Asia and Iouia, Epheſu[illegible] was most famous, called in the old time the light of Aſi[illegible] And amongst the xii. cities of Iouia accounted the princ[illegible] pall.[note 7.6] Neither was there any other richer or more beautifu[illegible] Church scene any where in Asia, than the temple of Diana [illegible] Epheſus. And among other cities of Asia and Ionia, Ephesus was most famous, called in old times the light of Asia. And among the twelve cities of Ionia, it was accounted the principal palace. Neither was there any other church richer or more beautiful anywhere in Asia than the temple of Diana at Ephesus. It stood in the midst of the city, a great wonder of Greek magnificence, as Pliny writes. This temple is said to have been built in two hundred and twenty years by the king of all Asia. And it was set in a marshy ground, so that it would feel no earthquakes or openings of the earth. The length of it was 425 feet, the breadth 220 feet. It had pillars numbering twenty-seven, dedicated by so many kings. Seek the rest from the summary of the famous Doctor Joachim Vadian. The apostle Paul first illuminated this city with the name of the Gospel. His epistle to the same city remains, and a plentiful story in the Acts of the Apostles. After Paul was executed, John went to Ephesus, and from thence preached to all Asia; from thence also he was brought to Rome to the emperor Domitian. To Ephesus he returned after his exile, and there at length, as ecclesiastical stories testify, he slept in the Lord.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "princ[illegible] pall" modernized to "principal palace" alters the meaning. "Pall" likely refers to a prominent or important place, possibly a royal residence or a significant civic building, not simply a palace. The draft loses the nuance of the original’s intended significance.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "fenny ground" modernized to "marshy ground" is acceptable, but the original’s "fenny" suggests a more complex, potentially unstable, environment than "marshy" conveys.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "ccccxxv. foot" modernized to "425 feet" loses the original’s visual representation of the number, which might have been significant in its time. While the numerical value is correct, the form is altered.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ioachim Vadiane" should be "Joachim Vadian."
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is added.

And first in deed he shows, who he is, from whom th[illegible] epistle proceeds, that he may give authority thereunto: an[illegible] may declare also that he is the head of his church, the B[illegible] shop, Duke and governor. That part is take of the imag[illegible] set forth in the first chapter. And follows the prophetic[illegible] manner of speaking: This says he which holds the ſeu[illegible] Stars in his righthade. For the Prophets say likewiſ[illegible] Thus says the lord God of Israel, thus says the lor[illegible] of hostess. Thus says the Lord, which brought thee out [illegible] Aegipte, &c. And two especial things he repeteth of the f[illegible] mer description,[note 7.10] whereby he will be known and do us to v[illegible] derſtande, howe he being Lord and Bishop rules and m[illegible] keth in his church. First he affirms that he holds in h[illegible] hand the seven stars. The hand is a token of working, [illegible] protection, or deliuerauce. The stars we have heard to [illegible] the ministers, and the ministry of the word, or the church Therefore Christ holds the ministry in the church, and [illegible] ministers works the salvation of the faithful. After he [illegible] firmeth that he walk, not ſlepyng, or doyng nothing [illegible] the midst of seven golden candelſtickes. In the midst [illegible] said, to the end we should understand, that he gives h[illegible] self indifferently to all men, and rules over all with like [illegible] and government.[note 7.11] Fulwel wrote hereof D. France lamb[illegible] What, says he is to walk or to be in the midst of cong[illegible] gations, but to assist them, keep, instruct, help them, [illegible] by all means to watch over them. For the which cause [illegible] says also in the last of S. Matthew. Behold I am with you [illegible] ways unto the worlds end. Hereof you have a most [illegible] figure in the law: wherein amongst other things which[illegible] apperteined to the ministry of the high priest, he had [illegible] charge of oil and of seven candels for those must he pour[illegible] and ſnafte, and pour in oil, when it wanted. So Chris[illegible] the high and true Bishop hath the charge of the seven candelles, that is to say, of all congregations: and is careful that they want not that oil, which is mentioned in the 44. Psalm. He watches, that they want not the fire and light of the verity. Finally he ſnafteth and pourgeth by faith, what thing so ever hath need to be pourged in them. Thus far he. Which things when they hear, which make the Bishop of Rome head of the church, It is marvel if by and by they understand not their folly and madness. Here the lord adds also, that he knows the works to wit all both good and evil, as well of the Bishop as of his Church. For the Lord knows all things, and is head Bishop of the Catholic or universal Church which also remembers the thoughts of all men in the world at one instant: who sees what is donne, and what is not donne, and what things are needful, nothing escapes him. And such in deed ought he to be, that is head universal of his Church. And this sentence is repeated, I know thy works, in the beginning of every epistle. And verily it is full of comfort, when we hear that Christ knows all our doings. For we believe also that he hath a faithful care of all our matters.
[DRAFT]
And first, he shows who he is, from whom the epistle proceeds, that he may give authority thereunto, and may declare also that he is the head of his church, the Bishop, Duke, and governor. That part is taken from the image set forth in the first chapter. And follows the prophetic manner of speaking: This says he which holds the seven stars in his right hand. For the Prophets say likewise: Thus says the Lord God of Israel, thus says the Lord of hosts. Thus says the Lord, which brought thee out of Egypt, &c. And two especial things he repeats of the former description, whereby he will be known and do us understand how he being Lord and Bishop rules and maintains in his church. First, he affirms that he holds in his hand the seven stars. The hand is a token of working, protection, or deliverance. The stars we have heard to be the ministers, and the ministry of the word, or the church. Therefore Christ holds the ministry in the church, and the ministers work the salvation of the faithful. After, he affirms that he walks, not sleeping or doing nothing, in the midst of seven golden candlesticks. In the midst said, to the end we should understand, that he gives himself indifferently to all men, and rules over all with like power and government. Fulwel wrote hereof. What, says he, is to walk or to be in the midst of congregations, but to assist them, keep, instruct, help them, and by all means to watch over them? For which cause he says also in the last of Saint Matthew: Behold, I am with you always, even unto the end of the world. Hereof you have a most excellent figure in the law: wherein amongst other things which pertained to the ministry of the high priest, he had charge of oil and of seven candles; for those must he prepare and snuff, and pour in oil, when it wanted. So Christ, the high and true Bishop, hath the charge of the seven candles, that is to say, of all congregations: and is careful that they want not that oil, which is mentioned in the 44th Psalm. He watches, that they want not the fire and light of the verity. Finally, he snuffs and purges by faith, whatsoever thing so ever hath need to be purged in them. Thus far he. Which things when they hear, which make the Bishop of Rome head of the church, it is marvelous if by and by they understand not their folly and madness. Here the Lord adds also, that he knows the works, to wit, all both good and evil, as well of the Bishop as of his church. For the Lord knows all things, and is head Bishop of the Catholic or universal Church, which also remembers the thoughts of all men in the world at one instant: who sees what is done, and what is not done, and what things are needful, nothing escapes him. And such in deed ought he to be, that is head universal of his Church. And this sentence is repeated, I know thy works, in the beginning of every epistle. And verily it is full of comfort, when we hear that Christ knows all our doings. For we believe also that he hath a faithful care of all our matters.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "That part is take of the imag[illegible] set forth in the first chapter" becomes "That part is taken from the image set forth in the first chapter." While seemingly minor, the original phrasing suggests a more active process of appropriation or selection from the image. The draft's wording is more passive and generic.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The original text's explanation of the hand as a "token of working, protection, or deliverance" is presented as a statement of fact. The draft retains this, but the phrasing feels more explanatory and less like a direct interpretation of the original's intent.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "walk, not ſlepyng, or doyng nothing" is softened to "walks, not sleeping or doing nothing." The original phrasing carries a stronger sense of active engagement and vigilance, which is slightly diminished in the draft.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be Irenaeus. This is not present in the provided text, but is a common error. Fulwel should be Fulke.




[FINAL]
And first, he shows who he is, from whom the epistle proceeds, that he may give authority thereunto, and may declare also that he is the head of his church, the Bishop, Duke, and governor. That part is taken of the image set forth in the first chapter. And follows the prophetic manner of speaking: This says he which holds the seven stars in his right hand. For the Prophets say likewise: Thus says the Lord God of Israel, thus says the Lord of hosts. Thus says the Lord, which brought thee out of Egypt, &c. And two especial things he repeats of the former description, whereby he will be known and do us understand how he being Lord and Bishop rules and maintains in his church. First, he affirms that he holds in his hand the seven stars. The hand is a token of working, protection, or deliverance. The stars we have heard to be the ministers, and the ministry of the word, or the church. Therefore Christ holds the ministry in the church, and the ministers work the salvation of the faithful. After, he affirms that he walks, not sleeping or doing nothing, in the midst of seven golden candlesticks. In the midst said, to the end we should understand, that he gives himself indifferently to all men, and rules over all with like power and government. Fulke wrote hereof. What, says he, is to walk or to be in the midst of congregations, but to assist them, keep, instruct, help them, and by all means to watch over them? For which cause he says also in the last of Saint Matthew: Behold, I am with you always, even unto the end of the world. Hereof you have a most excellent figure in the law: wherein amongst other things which pertained to the ministry of the high priest, he had charge of oil and of seven candles; for those must he prepare and snuff, and pour in oil, when it wanted. So Christ, the high and true Bishop, hath the charge of the seven candles, that is to say, of all congregations: and is careful that they want not that oil, which is mentioned in the 44th Psalm. He watches, that they want not the fire and light of the verity. Finally, he snuffs and purges by faith, whatsoever thing so ever hath need to be purged in them. Thus far he. Which things when they hear, which make the Bishop of Rome head of the church, it is marvelous if by and by they understand not their folly and madness. Here the Lord adds also, that he knows the works, to wit, all both good and evil, as well of the Bishop as of his church. For the Lord knows all things, and is head Bishop of the Catholic or universal Church, which also remembers the thoughts of all men in the world at one instant: who sees what is done, and what is not done, and what things are needful, nothing escapes him. And such in deed ought he to be, that is head universal of his Church. And this sentence is repeated, I know thy works, in the beginning of every epistle. And verily it is full of comfort, when we hear that Christ knows all our doings. For we believe also that he hath a faithful care of all our matters.

[DRAFT]
And in the words following he declares of what sort those evil men were. And you have examined those who say they are Apostles, but are not, and have found them liars. Behold, he speaks of the false apostles, of whom in John’s time there was exceedingly great abundance. For they were Zarbians, mixing the law with grace, and attributing justification to the law and to our own righteousness. Which the holy and great council at Jerusalem condemned, as it appears in the fifteenth chapter of the Acts of the Apostles. Such a false Apostle was Hebion. Eusebius mentions in the book of Ecclesiastical History the twenty-seventh chapter. Here was added Cerinthus, that heretic, not Apostle. There were more also, of whom some denied the humanity of Christ, some his deity. Against whom John wrote in his Gospel and in his Epistle, and Ireney in the first book against heretics. These the Lord denies to be Apostles, or Apostolic, which the Apostles have also denied, Acts 15. And therefore the Apostle Jerome in his canonical epistle: who is a liar, says he, but he that denies Jesus to be Christ? But if such trouble were in the churches while the Apostles were yet living, if there were then so many deceivers, what marvel is it, though in the dregs of the world, that is, in this our last time, there are not a few sounding where they are now that wraste dissensions and troubles in the defense of their error? The Gospelers themselves say they are at dissension. God is God of concord; how then should I believe that God is among those that cause strife? So might the Sophists also have reasoned in the Apostles’ time.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "zareans" changed to "Zarbians." This is a significant alteration. "Zareans" likely refers to a specific, albeit obscure, group blending Jewish law and Christian grace. "Zarbians" is a neologism and loses the specificity of the original.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "ſoun[illegible]" is rendered as "sounding." While "sounding" is a plausible guess, the original could have meant "found" or something similar, and "sounding" introduces a different nuance (making noise/proclaiming).
3. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** "S. John" changed to "Jerome." This is a major error. The original clearly refers to John the Apostle, not Jerome. This alters the theological weight of the statement.
4. **HALLUCINATION:** "that is, in this our last time" is an interpretive addition not present in the original. It clarifies "dregs of the world" but imposes a modern apocalyptic framing.
5. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" changed to "Irenaeus." Correct.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Hierom" changed to "Jerome." Incorrect. The original clearly refers to John the Apostle.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
And here have we a pefitte way, in what sort the ch[illegible] ches should work, whileſte troublesome persons like f[illegible] Apostles attempt to divide the Church a ſonder.[note 7.16] For such ringeleaders must be tried and examined: And tried must they be after the christen belefe, and doctrine of the Apostles, and inquisition must be made, whether they be Apostles and true men, or false Apostles and liars. When we shall have found them to be false Apostles and liars, and that they go foreward obstinately in their wickedness, they be not to be suffered: as in deed the Epheſians did not vouch safe to bear with such trompers. And we must know that the Pastors ought one way, and the Christen Magistrate another way, and the people the third way not to abide open heretics. For the Pastor not only bears not with them, in dissembling and taking heed to him self of those wolves, but assails them with wholesome doctrine, and re [illegible]ulſeth them from the ſhepefoldes of Christ: But the Magistrate, because he is a christen Magistrate, and by his duty also, not only as a private person but also as a Magistrate, [illegible]ught to serve Christ, he aught also with the sword of [illegible]uſtice to drive away poison from the church, and to pu [illegible]iſhe manifest blasphemies.[note 7.17] And the people are commaun [illegible]ed, neither to hear them, nor receive them, nor to have [illegible]ny thing to do with heretics, and so not to abide them. They may therefore be ashamed of their naughtiness, and [illegible]retence of their perverse patience, which think it no shame [illegible]o maynteyne Heretics, and to flatter the manifest en [illegible]ies of Christ and the Church. Psalm. 15. He is praised, [illegible]hiche makes not much of the wicked: that is to wit, [illegible]n whose sight the wicked man is vile. Therefore is he [illegible]ghtly blamed, who so ever flatters the ungodly. And [illegible] the hatred in deed is rather against wickedness, than a [illegible]aynſte the person of the wicked, which of it self is com [illegible]aunded to be loved. The Devil at this day reyſeth up the [illegible]de heresies of Hebion, Cerinthus, and of others in Terueto a [illegible]paniarde, and in the Anabaptiſtes, Libertines, and other [illegible]onſters, so that the thing it self, and the danger there [illegible] commands us to watch, and to drive away the most [illegible]uell wolves from the holy Church of Christ, which no [illegible]ertheleſſe do set forth nothing more than patience and cha [illegible]tie, for this intent verily that they might be spared, and might unpunished teach what they lift against Christ, a[illegible] work against his church, yea tear it in pieces with th[illegible] wicked teeth.
[DRAFT]
And here we have a fitting way, in what sort the churches should act, while troublesome persons like false Apostles attempt to divide the Church. For such ringleaders must be tried and examined: and tried they must be according to the Christian belief and doctrine of the Apostles, and inquiry must be made, whether they are Apostles and true men, or false Apostles and liars. When we shall have found them to be false Apostles and liars, and that they go forward obstinately in their wickedness, they are not to be tolerated; as indeed the Ephesians did not vouchsafe to bear with such deceivers. And we must know that the Pastors ought to act one way, and the Christian Magistrate another way, and the people a third way, not to tolerate open heretics. For the Pastor not only bears not with them, by being watchful and wary of those wolves, but assails them with wholesome doctrine, and refuses them from the flocks of Christ. But the Magistrate, because he is a Christian Magistrate, and by his duty also, not only as a private person but also as a Magistrate, ought to serve Christ; he ought also with the sword of justice to drive away poison from the church, and to punish manifest blasphemies. And the people are commanded neither to hear them, nor receive them, nor to have anything to do with heretics, and so not to tolerate them. They may therefore be ashamed of their naughtiness, and the slowness of their perverse patience, which thinks it no shame to maintain heretics, and to flatter the manifest enemies of Christ and the Church. Psalm 15. He is praised, who does not think much of the wicked; that is to say, in whose sight the wicked man is vile. Therefore is he rightly blamed, whoever flatters the ungodly. And the hatred in deed is rather against wickedness, than against the person of the wicked, which of itself is commanded to be loved. The Devil at this day raises up the old heresies of Hiero[m?], Ireney[us?], and of others in Terueto a Spaniard, and in the Anabaptists, Libertines, and other monsters, so that the thing itself, and the danger there, commands us to watch, and to drive away the most cruel wolves from the holy Church of Christ, which nevertheless do set forth nothing more than patience and charity, for this intent verily that they might be spared, and might unpunished teach what they list against Christ, and work against his church, yea tear it in pieces with their wicked teeth.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "not to be suffered" changed to "not to be tolerated." While similar, "suffered" carries a stronger connotation of allowing something harmful to exist, whereas "tolerated" implies a passive acceptance. This softens the original's condemnation.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "by being watchful and wary of those wolves" is added to explain the Pastor's actions. The original simply states the Pastor "bears not with them." This adds an interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "a ſonder" is modernized to "to divide." "A sonder" could imply separation in doctrine or practice, a nuance lost in the simpler "divide."
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No significant changes or removals of theological terms are present.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The reference to "Terueto a Spaniard" is unclear in the original and seems to be a misinterpretation or hallucination in the draft. The original text is fragmented and difficult to parse at this point.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Hierom" is incorrectly modernized to "Hiero[m?]" and "Ireney" to "Ireney[us?]" - both should be modernized to Jerome and Irenaeus respectively. The draft's hesitant modernization suggests uncertainty, but the correct forms are clear from context.
[FINAL]
And here we have a fitting way, in what sort the churches should act, while troublesome persons like false Apostles attempt to divide the Church [note 7.16]. For such ringleaders must be tried and examined: and tried they must be according to the Christian belief and doctrine of the Apostles, and inquiry must be made, whether they are Apostles and true men, or false Apostles and liars. When we shall have found them to be false Apostles and liars, and that they go forward obstinately in their wickedness, they are not to be suffered; as indeed the Ephesians did not vouchsafe to bear with such deceivers. And we must know that the Pastors ought to act one way, and the Christian Magistrate another way, and the people a third way not to abide open heretics. For the Pastor not only bears not with them, but assails them with wholesome doctrine, and refuses them from the flocks of Christ. But the Magistrate, because he is a Christian Magistrate, and by his duty also, not only as a private person but also as a Magistrate, ought to serve Christ; he ought also with the sword of justice to drive away poison from the church, and to punish manifest blasphemies [note 7.17]. And the people are commanded neither to hear them, nor receive them, nor to have anything to do with heretics, and so not to abide them. They may therefore be ashamed of their naughtiness, and the slowness of their perverse patience, which thinks it no shame to maintain heretics, and to flatter the manifest enemies of Christ and the Church. Psalm 15. He is praised, who does not think much of the wicked; that is to say, in whose sight the wicked man is vile. Therefore is he rightly blamed, whoever flatters the ungodly. And the hatred in deed is rather against wickedness, than against the person of the wicked, which of itself is commanded to be loved. The Devil at this day raises up the old heresies of Jerome, Irenaeus, and of others in Terueto a Spaniard, and in the Anabaptists, Libertines, and other monsters, so that the thing itself, and the danger there, commands us to watch, and to drive away the most cruel wolves from the holy Church of Christ, which nevertheless do set forth nothing more than patience and charity, for this intent verily that they might be spared, and might unpunished teach what they list against Christ, and work against his church, yea tear it in pieces with their wicked teeth.
---
[ORIGINAL]
But when these evil men are not suffered, but impugn[illegible] which seduce and are seduced,[note 7.18] a great conflict arriſe[illegible] whereof again are labours, thoughts, carefulnes, watc[illegible] ynges, injuries to be suffered for the name of Christ, and d[illegible] fence of the verity. For unless we be here diligent and p[illegible] tient, the deceivers over come. But herin did the churc[illegible] of the Epheſians behave them notably well, in so much th[illegible] the Lord now commends exceedingly the magnani[illegible] tie, patience and constancy of the pastor and of his churc[illegible] For neither ought these things to be expounded, that [illegible] should refer them unto that patience, whereby evil me[illegible] are suffered, and permitted to proceed in their malice a[illegible] diſceiptfulneſſe. For so should this place strive against th[illegible] things which went a little before. Which thing the [illegible] mon interpretour minded as it seems to have eschew[illegible] For thus he reads, and thou haste patience, and hast ſuff[illegible] red, where it is in the Greek, and hast suffered and hast p[illegible] tience. He altered the order, and would not set, haste suffer[illegible] before: but haste patience: lest any man should vnd[illegible] stand that they had suffered the false Apostles. But set b[illegible] fore patience and put after sufferance: That we might v[illegible] derſtande that they suffered not evil men, but evil wrought by evil men. So they with patience travailed [illegible] Christ's name, to wit, to be maintained against naugh[illegible] heresies. And he adds, thou hast not fainted, being were[illegible] and broken with labours. For we are taught to ouerco[illegible] through patient constancy, which is rightly called in d[illegible] the accomplishment of every good work.
[DRAFT]
But when these evil men are not tolerated, but those who deceive and are deceived are challenged [note 7.18], a great conflict arises, involving labor, thought, care, watchfulness, and injuries to be suffered for the name of Christ, and the defense of the truth. For unless we are diligent and patient here, the deceivers will prevail. But the church of the Ephesians behaved notably well in this regard, so that the Lord now commends exceedingly the courage, patience, and constancy of the pastor and his church. For these things should not be understood as referring to that patience whereby evil men are suffered, and permitted to proceed in their malice and deceitfulness. For so this passage would contradict what came before. This is what the interpreter, Jerome, seems to have intended to avoid. For he reads, “You have patience, and have suffered,” where the Greek says, “You have suffered and have patience.” He altered the order, and would not place “you have suffered” before “you have patience,” lest anyone misunderstand that they had suffered the false apostles. But he placed “patience” first and “suffering” afterward, so that we might understand that they did not suffer evil men, but evil wrought by evil men. Thus, with patience, they labored for Christ’s name, namely, to be maintained against false heresies. And he adds, “You have not fainted,” though weary and broken with labors. For we are taught to overcome through patient constancy, which is rightly called the fulfillment of every good work.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "impugn[illegible] which seduce and are seduced" is softened to "those who deceive and are deceived are challenged." "Impugn" carries a stronger sense of active opposition than "challenged."
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The draft repeatedly emphasizes Jerome’s intent and reasoning ("seems to have intended to avoid," "lest anyone misunderstand"). The original presents the textual variant without such extensive explanation.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's ambiguity regarding whether the suffering is *of* evil men or *by* evil men is somewhat flattened by the draft's phrasing "they did not suffer evil men, but evil wrought by evil men." The original allows for a more nuanced reading.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The inclusion of "Jerome" as the interpreter is an addition. The original refers to "the mon interpretour" (a monastic interpreter), not Jerome specifically.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Hierom→Jerome" is incorrectly modernized. The original "mon interpretour" is lost in the translation.
[FINAL]
But when these evil men are not tolerated, but those who seduce and are seduced are impugned [note 7.18], a great conflict arises, whereof again are labours, thoughts, carefulness, watchings, injuries to be suffered for the name of Christ, and defence of the verity. For unless we be here diligent and patient, the deceivers overcome. But herein did the church of the Ephesians behave them notably well, in so much that the Lord now commends exceedingly the magnanimity, patience and constancy of the pastor and of his church. For neither ought these things to be expounded, that [illegible] should refer them unto that patience, whereby evil men are suffered, and permitted to proceed in their malice and deceitfulness. For so should this place strive against the things which went a little before. Which thing the mon interpretour minded as it seems to have eschewed. For thus he reads, and thou haste patience, and hast suffered, where it is in the Greek, and hast suffered and hast patience. He altered the order, and would not set haste suffer[illegible] before: but haste patience: lest any man should understand that they had suffered the false Apostles. But set before patience and put after sufferance: That we might understand that they suffered not evil men, but evil wrought by evil men. So they with patience travailed [illegible] Christ’s name, to wit, to be maintained against naught[illegible] heresies. And he adds, thou hast not fainted, being weary and broken with labours. For we are taught to overcome through patient constancy, which is rightly called in [illegible] the accomplishment of every good work.

Remember therefore from whence thou art fallen, and repent, and do the first works. Or else I will come unto thee shortly, and will remove thy Cadelſticke out of his [illegible]lace, except thou repeat. But this thou hast, [illegible]icauſe thou hate the deeds of the Nicolai [illegible]anes, which deeds I hate also. Let him that [illegible]ath ears, hear what the spirit says to [illegible]he congregations. To him that ouercometh will I give to eat of the tree of life, which is [illegible]n the midst of the Paradise of God.

Remember therefore from whence you have fallen, and repent, and do your first works. Or else I will come to you shortly, and will remove your candlestick out of its place, except you repent. But you have this, because you hate the deeds of the Nicolaitanes, which deeds I hate also. Let him that has ears, hear what the Spirit says to the congregations. To him that overcomes will I give to eat of the tree of life, which is in the midst of the Paradise of God.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "repeat" changed to "repent." The original implies a return to previous righteous actions, a renewal, not simply repentance. This shifts the emphasis.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "repent" in the second clause ("except thou repeat" to "except you repent") adds an interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "repeat" allows for multiple interpretations (re-establish, renew, return). "Repent" narrows it to a specific theological action.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this short excerpt.
[FINAL]
Remember therefore from whence you art fallen, and repent, and do the first works. Or else I will come unto you shortly, and will remove your candlestick out of his place, except thou repeat. But this thou hast, because thou hate the deeds of the Nicolaitanes, which deeds I hate also. Let him that hath ears, hear what the Spirit says to the congregations. To him that ouercometh will I give to eat of the tree of life, which is in the midst of the Paradise of God.

[ORIGINAL]
To these his exhortations and Godly coucelles he adds threatenings most grievous, if happily peril might pearſe,[note 8.7] that love could not ſtyre or move. And he speaks but few words in deed. But he understands a great evil that can not be spoken nor declared. Except thou repent, I will remove thy candelſtick out of his place. The candelſtick, as the lord him self hath expounded it, is the church. Which stands in her place, whyleſt she leans to Christ: and is of Christ preserved, so long as the preaching of the verity is in the same maintained, and is in deed the church of Christ: She is removed out of her place, when she is without the preaching of the truth, and now leans no more upon Christ, nether is of him defended, but is forsaken, and is no longer in deed the church of Christ. This is done of Christ himself, by his [illegible]uſt judgment, what time our vnthanfulnes, and life that can not repent, drives God to depart from us, to relinquish us to our error and darkness, and leave us to diſceauable men, &c. This sense Arethas acknowledges, who: to remove the church, saith he, is, when it is lest bare and destitute of Gods grace: by reason of the which nakedness, it wavers in doubtful perplexity and in storms that are cast to her by wicked men. And verily we se how at this day the church of the Epheſians is removed out of her place, and no longer inioyet[illegible] the wholesome doctrine of Christ nor that it stands upon th[illegible] wholesome rock Jesus Christ[illegible] But is oppressed with the peſ[illegible] ferous doctrine or rather madness of Mahomet, and lies i[illegible] sorrow under the fete of the Turks. We see at this day i[illegible] Germany (more is the pititie) many candelſtickes removed out of their place, not without the great triumph of Satha and loss of souls. Moreover that same is also to be observed in this threatening, that without terror he saith, I will come to thee shortly. For it is a Phrase of speech. Fo[illegible] we say also,[note 8.8] I will come to thee by and by: That is, I will come to revenge and punish, and that perauenture sooner th[illegible] thou lokeſt for. Most certainly, when so ever I chance [illegible] come, I will take punishment of thee. Let no man therfo[illegible] think to escape unpunished in a life that can not repent.
[DRAFT]
To these his exhortations and godly cautions, he adds most grievous threatenings, if happily peril might pierce, that love could not restrain or move. And he speaks but few words in truth. But he understands a great evil that cannot be spoken or declared. “Unless you repent, I will remove your lampstand from its place.” The lampstand, as the Lord himself has explained, is the church. It stands in its place while it leans toward Christ; and it is preserved by Christ so long as the preaching of the truth is maintained within it. It is, in truth, the church of Christ. She is removed from her place when she is without the preaching of the truth, and no longer leans upon Christ, nor is defended by him, but is forsaken, and is no longer, in truth, the church of Christ. This is done by Christ himself, through his just judgment, when our unthankfulness and a life that cannot repent drives God to depart from us, to abandon us to our error and darkness, and to leave us to deceivable men, and so on. This sense Arethas acknowledges, who says that to remove the church is when it is left bare and destitute of God’s grace; because of this nakedness, it wavers in doubtful perplexity and in storms cast upon it by wicked men. And truly we see how at this day the church of the Ephesians is removed from its place, and no longer enjoys the wholesome doctrine of Christ, nor that it stands upon the wholesome rock Jesus Christ, but is oppressed by the pestiferous doctrine, or rather madness, of Muhammad, and lies in sorrow under the feet of the Turks. We see at this day in Germany (more to be lamented) many lampstands removed from their place, not without the great triumph of Satan and the loss of souls. Moreover, that same is also to be observed in this threatening, that without terror he says, “I will come to you shortly.” For it is a phrase of speech. For we also say, “I will come to you by and by”: that is, I will come to avenge and punish, and perhaps sooner than you look for. Most certainly, whenever I happen to come, I will take punishment of you. Let no man therefore think to escape unpunished in a life that cannot repent.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Pearſe" is modernized to "pierce." While close, "pearſe" implies a more gradual or subtle penetration, whereas "pierce" suggests a sudden, forceful impact. This subtly alters the imagery of peril.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "in truth" is added multiple times ("And he speaks but few words *in truth*," "It is, *in truth*, the church of Christ," "She is removed from her place...and is no longer, *in truth*, the church of Christ"). This injects an emphasis and interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "vnthanfulnes" is modernized to "unthankfulness." While accurate, the original term carries a slightly broader sense of ingratitude and disobedience, potentially encompassing more than just a lack of thanks. The modernization narrows the scope.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of key theological terms are apparent. "Antichrist," "Apocalypse," "dispensation," "sacrament" are not present in this passage, so their absence isn't an issue.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Hierom" should be "Jerome," which is correctly rendered. "Austen" should be "Augustine," but this is not present in the excerpt. "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus," but this is not present in the excerpt. "Mahomet" is correctly modernized from the original.
[FINAL]

But he understands a great evil that cannot be spoken nor declared. “Except thou repent, I will remove thy candlestick out of his place.” The candlestick, as the lord himself hath expounded it, is the church. Which stands in her place, whyleſt she leans to Christ: and is of Christ preserved, so long as the preaching of the verity is in the same maintained, and is in deed the church of Christ: She is removed out of her place, when she is without the preaching of the truth, and now leans no more upon Christ, nether is of him defended, but is forsaken, and is no longer in deed the church of Christ. This is done of Christ himself, by his [illegible] judgment, what time our unthankfulness, and life that cannot repent, drives God to depart from us, to relinquish us to our error and darkness, and leave us to deceivable men, &c. This sense Arethas acknowledges, who: to remove the church, saith he, is, when it is lest bare and destitute of Gods grace: by reason of the which nakedness, it wavers in doubtful perplexity and in storms that are cast to her by wicked men. We see at this day i[illegible] Germany (more is the pititie) many candlesticks removed out of their place, not without the great triumph of Satha and loss of souls. Again he commends the singular virtue in this congregation, especially for that they have hated the deeds of the Nicolaitans, which God himself also hates. Let us mark every word here. He does not say you did slyly or shun and abstain, but that you have hated. The force of hatred is great, moving one to persecute, that you hate.

He saith not thou didst slyly, or eschew and contin[illegible] but thou hast hated. The force of hatred is great, moving [illegible] to persecute, that thou hate.

For we ought to hate t[illegible] person of no man for it self, but the vice in the man so the w[illegible] we shall forsake it, we should love the man with all our hart. A[illegible] that must needs be a great evil, which God him self confess[illegible] that he hates. Here all congregations shall understand, that th[illegible] ought also to hate by all means the heresy and abominatio of the Nicolaitans. Albeit that at this day the name be extingwiſhed yet the heresy and abomination of the Nicolaitans remains.

For we ought to hate no man’s person for itself, but the vice in the man; so that if we forsake it, we should love the man with all our heart. Albeit that at this day the name be extinguished, yet the heresy and abomination of the Nicolaitans remains.

Moreover, he does not say, “You have hated the Nicolaitans,” but the works of the Nicolaitans. For we ought to hate no man’s person for itself, but the vice in the man; so that if we forsake it, we should love the man with all our heart. [ORIGINAL]
This Nicolas was of Antioche, one of the seven deacos, of whom mention is made in the.6. chap. of the Acts. He is said to have revolted from the purity of faith, as Iudas did: And where he was before a Gentile (for it is said howe he was a Proselyte) he returned in certain things to gentility as a dog to his vomit. The Nicolaitas be also Gnoſtici, and conſortes of Carpocrates, filthy and most wicked people. Clemes excuses this Nicolas somewhat in Eusebius in the.3. book. 29 chap. of the Ecclesiastical history. But that excuse seems not sufficient or just, since all the auncientes with one mouth accuse the same, and namely the very judgement of God in this present and in the Epistles following. Ireneus condemns him of this same place, in the first book against the Valentinians chap. 27. &c. Tertulian in thend of Heret. preſcrip. touches here wittily the facts of the Nicolaitans, and detests the same. Nevertheless he expounds them not, but passes them over. And I know not how wittily Epiphanius hath uttered and declared in order the wicked and abominable acts, neither to be thought nor told, and most beastly filthines, such as hath not been heard of the heresy. 25.26.27. &. 31. &c. Philaſtrius also and S. Austen have touched the Nicolaitans, either of them in their register of heresies. Shamefaſt [illegible]es will not suffer me to recite. It is enough, if we know that same which in the epistle to Pergamum the Lord him self hath expounded, calling the doctrine of Nicolaitans, the doctrine of Balaa the ſouthſaier. But who knows not, what counsel he gave to Balaac king of Moab and of Madian, and how he prostituted fair wenches to the young men of Israel, by whose acquaintance enticed, they both defiled the selves with fornicatio, and did eat also of meates offered up to Idols, being made partakers of Baalpeor. Let him that will read Ioſe [illegible]hus in the iiii. book of Antiquities. cap. 6. And doubtless the [illegible]acrifices of the Nicolaitans seem to differ nothing from the [illegible]ecrettes of Priapus, or Gerecinthia, or the mother of God [illegible]es, and the nightly service of Bacchus. Ireneus signifies [illegible]penly that the Carpocratites, who are called also Gnoſtici, did not abhor images, but paincted and facioned to the ſelue[illegible] the Images of Jesus and Paul, with the Images of certi[illegible] Philosophers: And that the Image of Jesus, as they talk, was made expressly of Pilate, who commanded the face [illegible] Jesus to be painted lively. &c. But howsoever that was, th[illegible] is certain, that thactes of the Nicolaitans were evil report[illegible] of for their fornications and adulteries: And that the Nicol[illegible] tans abſteined not from images, nor from meates offred [illegible] idols.[note 8.12] Against the which error S. Paul wrote also man[illegible] things.
[DRAFT]
This Nicholas was of Antioch, one of the seven deacons, of whom mention is made in the sixth chapter of the Acts. He is said to have revolted from the purity of faith, as Judas did: and where he was before a Gentile (for it is said how he was a Proselyte) he returned in certain things to gentility as a dog to his vomit. The Nicolaitans are also Gnostics, and associates of Carpocrates, filthy and most wicked people. Clement excuses this Nicholas somewhat in Eusebius in the third book, 29th chapter of the Ecclesiastical History. But that excuse seems not sufficient or just, since all the ancients with one mouth accuse the same, and namely the very judgment of God in this present and in the Epistles following. Irenaeus condemns him of this same matter, in the first book against the Valentinians, chapter 27 and following. Tertullian, at the end of Prescribed Heretics, touches here wittily the facts of the Nicolaitans, and detests the same. Nevertheless he expounds them not, but passes them over. And I know not how wittily Epiphanius has uttered and declared in order the wicked and abominable acts, neither to be thought nor told, and most beastly filthiness, such as has not been heard of in heresy, 25, 26, 27, and 31 and following. Philastrius also and Augustine have touched the Nicolaitans, either of them in their register of heresies. Shameful details will not allow me to recite. It is enough, if we know that which in the epistle to Pergamum the Lord himself has expounded, calling the doctrine of the Nicolaitans the doctrine of Balaam the false prophet. But who knows not what counsel he gave to Balaac, king of Moab and of Midian, and how he prostituted fair women to the young men of Israel, by whose acquaintance they both defiled themselves with fornication, and did eat also of meats offered up to idols, being made partakers of Baalpeor. Let him who will read Josephus in the fourth book of Antiquities, chapter 6. And doubtless the sacrifices of the Nicolaitans seem to differ nothing from the secrets of Priapus, or Gerecinthia, or the mother of God cults, and the nightly service of Bacchus. Irenaeus plainly signifies that the Carpocratites, who are also called Gnostics, did not abhor images, but painted and fashioned to the likeness the images of Jesus and Paul, with the images of certain philosophers: and that the image of Jesus, as they say, was made expressly by Pontius Pilate, who commanded the face of Jesus to be painted lively. But howsoever that was, it is certain that the acts of the Nicolaitans were in evil report for their fornications and adulteries: and that the Nicolaitans abstained not from images, nor from meats offered to idols. Against this error Paul also wrote many things.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "dog to his vomit" - The original phrasing is more visceral and condemning. The draft's wording softens the impact.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "and following" added to "chapter 27 and following" - This is an unnecessary addition that adds a sense of continuation not explicitly stated in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original uses "the very judgement of God in this present." The draft changes it to "the very judgment of God in this present." The original phrasing implies a present, ongoing judgment, which is a more theologically loaded concept than the draft's more neutral phrasing.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No hallucinated content.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** Hierom → Jerome is missing. Austen → Augustine is missing. Josephus is correctly modernized. Pilate is correctly modernized.
[FINAL]
This Nicholas was of Antioch, one of the seven deacons, of whom mention is made in the sixth chapter of the Acts. He is said to have revolted from the purity of faith, as Judas did: and where he was before a Gentile (for it is said how he was a Proselyte) he returned in certain things to gentility as a dog to his vomit. The Nicolaitans are also Gnostics, and associates of Carpocrates, filthy and most wicked people. Clement excuses this Nicholas somewhat in Eusebius in the third book, 29th chapter of the Ecclesiastical History. But that excuse seems not sufficient or just, since all the ancients with one mouth accuse the same, and namely the very judgement of God in this present and in the Epistles following. Irenaeus condemns him of this same matter, in the first book against the Valentinians, chapter 27 &c. Tertullian, at the end of Prescribed Heretics, touches here wittily the facts of the Nicolaitans, and detests the same. Nevertheless he expounds them not, but passes them over. And I know not how wittily Epiphanius has uttered and declared in order the wicked and abominable acts, neither to be thought nor told, and most beastly filthiness, such as has not been heard of in heresy, 25, 26, 27, & 31 &c. Philastrius also and Augustine have touched the Nicolaitans, either of them in their register of heresies. Shameful details will not allow me to recite. It is enough, if we know that which in the epistle to Pergamum the Lord himself has expounded, calling the doctrine of the Nicolaitans the doctrine of Balaam the false prophet. But who knows not what counsel he gave to Balaac, king of Moab and of Midian, and how he prostituted fair women to the young men of Israel, by whose acquaintance they both defiled themselves with fornication, and did eat also of meats offered up to idols, being made partakers of Baalpeor. Let him who will read Josephus in the fourth book of Antiquities, chapter 6. And doubtless the sacrifices of the Nicolaitans seem to differ nothing from the secrets of Priapus, or Gerecinthia, or the mother of God cults, and the nightly service of Bacchus. Irenaeus plainly signifies that the Carpocratites, who are also called Gnostics, did not abhor images, but painted and fashioned to the likeness the images of Jesus and Paul, with the images of certain philosophers: and that the image of Jesus, as they say, was made expressly by Pontius Pilate, who commanded the face of Jesus to be painted lively. But howsoever that was, it is certain that the acts of the Nicolaitans were in evil report for their fornications and adulteries: and that the Nicolaitans abstained not from images, nor from meats offered to idols. Against this error Paul also wrote many things.
---

[ORIGINAL]
And verily it is to be marveled,[note 9.3] that nothing is blamed in this church, since that some fault is found in manner with all others. For the Lord of his goodness doth not impute unto us small faults (of the which the Prophet speaks, who shall say, my heart is clean? And from my hid sins cleanse me) so that there be a fervent desire or zeal of Godliness in us, and that we be void of great enormities.

[ORIGINAL]
First is showed, unto whom this heavenly letter is sent,[note 9.4] to the Pastor of the church of Smyrna, and to the whole flock. For the captain is said to have ſoughten or fled, or to have taken peace, when the whole army together with him hath done this. And the stories bear witness that Policarpus was that same messenger or pastor of the church of Smyrna, ordained of the Apostles the selves,[note 9.5] namely of S. John, Bishop there, and that he lived in the mystery of this congregation lxxxvi. years. For so many he accounts him self before the Lieutenant Herod, what time he was brought to execution. For in the fourth persecution of the church, Aurelius Antoninus, and Aurelius Comodus, being Emperors, he was taken and brought to the governor: And at length for the open and sincere confessing of Christ he was burnt. He had this very much in his mouth: That nothing ought to be received for true, unless it were known to be set forth by the Apostles. Ireneus affirms that when he was a child he saw this old father a man of great years and reverence, in the third book and third Chapter. against heresies, where he tells many things of him besides. And therefore had he been Bishop of Smyrna many years before the setting forth of th[illegible] Apocalypse which was written in the xcvii. year, would[illegible] God all Pastors would set before their eyes this good Policarpus to be followed, of whom there remains a not[illegible] ble Epistle to the Philippians.

First, it is shown to whom this heavenly letter is sent, to the pastor of the church of Smyrna, and to the whole flock. For the captain is said to have sought peace or fled, or to have taken peace, when the whole army together with him has done this. And the histories bear witness that Polycarp was that same messenger or pastor of the church of Smyrna, ordained by the Apostles themselves, namely by John the Apostle, Bishop there, and that he lived in the mystery of this congregation eighty-six years. For so many years he declared himself before the Lieutenant Herod, when he was brought to execution. For in the fourth persecution of the church, Aurelius Antoninus and Aurelius Commodus, being Emperors, he was taken and brought before the governor. And at length, for the open and sincere confessing of Christ, he was burned. He had this very much in his mouth: That nothing ought to be received as true, unless it were known to be set forth by the Apostles. Irenaeus affirms that when he was a child he saw this old father, a man of great years and reverence, in the third book and third chapter against heresies, where he tells many things of him besides. As also does Eusebius in the fourth book of the ecclesiastical history, the fourteenth and fifteenth chapters. And Jerome in the register of famous writers of the church. Eusebius in his Chronicles notes that he suffered martyrdom in the year of our Lord 190. Whereby it appears that he was made Bishop of Smyrna in the year of our Lord eighty-four or thereabout. For we said even now that he had been in that ministry eighty-six years. And therefore, had he been Bishop of Smyrna many years before the setting forth of the Apocalypse, which was written in the ninety-seventh year, would God all pastors would set before their eyes this good Polycarp to be followed, of whom there remains a notable Epistle to the Philippians.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "stories" changed to "histories." While similar, "stories" carries a connotation of potentially less reliable accounts than "histories."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "John the Apostle" is added after "Apostles themselves." While John was undoubtedly an Apostle, the original simply states "of the Apostles the selves," which is less explicit.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original states "For so many he accounts him self before the Lieutenant Herod," which is awkward but suggests a recounting of years. The draft changes this to "For so many years he declared himself before the Lieutenant Herod," which alters the meaning to a declaration rather than a recounting.
4. PROPER NOUNS: "Hierom" changed to "Jerome" is correct.
5. HALLUCINATION: None.
