[ORIGINAL]
[note 13.10]Neither doth Christ know only all the thoughts of men whatsoever they be, but gives moreover to every man after his own works. And so the Apostle S. Paul teaches saying: The just judgment of God shall be opened, which will reward every man according to his deeds, that is to say: Praise, honour, and immortality, to them which continue in well doing, and seek eternal life: But unto them that are rebelliouſe and disobey the truth, and follow iniquity, shall come indignation, and wrath, tribulatio, and angwiſh. &c. In the ii. to the Roma. For works be the touchstones of faith and infidelity: And works whether they be good or evil, be judged of God and the Godly men, according as they proceed of faith, or of infidelity. Therefore what so ever any of us shall sow, the same shall he reap also. For God is the most just rewarder of good, and revenger of evil. The same sentence as it is most true, so is it the foundation of the true and Godly Religion. Glory be to God.
[DRAFT]
Nor does Christ know only all the thoughts of men, whatever they may be, but also gives to each man according to his own works. And so the Apostle Paul teaches, saying: “The just judgment of God will be revealed, which will reward each man according to his deeds—praise, honor, and immortality to those who continue in good works and seek eternal life, but indignation and wrath, tribulation, and anguish to those who are rebellious and disobedient to the truth, and follow iniquity.” This is in the second letter to the Romans. For works are the tests of faith and infidelity. And works, whether good or evil, are judged by God and by godly men, according to whether they proceed from faith or from infidelity. Therefore, whatever any of us sows, that he will also reap. For God is the just rewarder of good and the revenger of evil. This statement, as it is most true, is the foundation of the true and godly religion. Glory be to God.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "touchstones of faith and infidelity" is subtly altered to "tests of faith and infidelity." While related, "touchstones" implies a more discerning, critical evaluation than "tests" suggests.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of quotation marks around Paul's words ("The just judgment of God will be revealed...") introduces an interpretive layer, implying a direct quote rather than a paraphrase, which the original does not.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original uses "&c." indicating continuation of the list. The draft abruptly ends the list, removing the original's implication of further consequences.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: S. Paul is correctly modernized to Paul. Hierom, Austen, and Ireney are not present in this passage.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
He speaks here now to the Cataphrygians,[note 14.1] and also to the faithful of the church of Thyatira that believed rightly in Christ, and heals their diseases. Wherein appears the unspeakable mercy of God, which cease not to speak unto such as are yet entangled with heresy, and to heal their peſtiferous diseases. And he admonishes all men, that they look for no new revelations, but know rather that God hath through Christ and his Apostles set forth a most perfect doctrine, whereunto he will add nothing: And therefore that they keep fast in memory such things as they had learned already, and wherein they were now exercised.[note 14.2] For the Cataphrigians, called also Montaniſtes, bragged of a new comforter, and a new revelation: As though all things had not ben fully set forth by the Apostles, but that many things were left as yet to be revealed of them. As also at this day the maynteiners of the Popiſhe church most stiffly do affirm. And like as the Cataphrygians covered their trifles under the pretence of the holy ghost: So do the Papists likewise cloak the vain constitutions of men, and set the forth under a false colour of the holy ghost. As though the Lord spake of their decrees, when he said: I have yet many things to say unto you, which now the can not bear. Nevertheless the faithful people of Thyatira, which had not the doctrine of Iezabell, but rather detested it, notwithstanding as doubtful, said, that the Devil was a certain depenes, and had a thousand crafts, which could also transform him into an angel of light: And that they were but simple men, who being ignorant of these his wonderful crafts and ſubtilties, knew not what they might chiefly follow, whilst the false Prophets also make their boast of the holy ghost, and shine in miracles, and with great constancy avouch their doctrine to be true. Ye shall find at this day which will say, I am a plain simple man, and know not whether part I should cleave to, since the doctors of both parts affirm with great costa cie that they have the truth on their side, and therefore will some say, they shall agree better or ever I will believe any of the all. &c.
[DRAFT]
He speaks now to the Cataphrygians,[note 14.1] and also to the faithful of the church of Thyatira who rightly believed in Christ, and heals their diseases. In this appears the unspeakable mercy of God, which does not cease to speak to those still entangled with heresy, and to heal their pestiferous diseases. And he admonishes all men that they look for no new revelations, but know rather that God through Christ and his Apostles has set forth a most perfect doctrine, to which he will add nothing; and therefore that they keep fast in memory such things as they had already learned and in which they were now exercised.[note 14.2] For the Cataphrygians, called also Montanists, boasted of a new comforter and a new revelation, as though all things had not been fully set forth by the Apostles, but that many things were left yet to be revealed by them. As also at this day the maintainers of the Popish church most stiffly affirm. And just as the Cataphrygians covered their trifles under the pretense of the Holy Spirit, so do the Papists likewise cloak the vain constitutions of men and set them forth under a false color of the Holy Spirit, as though the Lord spoke of their decrees when he said, “I have yet many things to say unto you, which now you cannot bear.” Nevertheless, the faithful people of Thyatira, who did not have the doctrine of Jezebel but rather detested it as doubtful, said that the Devil was a certain dependence [?] and had a thousand crafts, which could also transform him into an angel of light; and that they were but simple men, who, being ignorant of his wonderful crafts and subtleties, did not know what they should chiefly follow, while the false prophets also make their boast of the Holy Spirit, and shine in miracles, and with great constancy avouch their doctrine to be true. You shall find at this day those who will say, “I am a plain simple man, and know not whether part I should cleave to, since the doctors of both parts affirm with great cost [?] that they have the truth on their side, and therefore will some say, they shall agree better ere I will believe any of them.” &c.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "depenes" changed to "dependence [?]" alters the original's more archaic and potentially more specific meaning. The original suggests a state of being dependent or reliant, whereas "dependence" is more general.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "costa cie" changed to "cost [?]" loses the original's slightly unusual phrasing which might have been intended to emphasize the expense or effort involved in the doctors' claims.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Iezabell" should be "Jezebel."
4. **HALLUCINATION:** The bracketed question marks after "dependence" and "cost" are not present in the original. They indicate uncertainty in the modernization process, but are not part of the original text.
[FINAL]
He speaks now to the Cataphrygians,[note 14.1] and also to the faithful of the church of Thyatira who rightly believed in Christ, and heals their diseases. In this appears the unspeakable mercy of God, which does not cease to speak to those still entangled with heresy, and to heal their pestiferous diseases. And he admonishes all men that they look for no new revelations, but know rather that God through Christ and his Apostles has set forth a most perfect doctrine, to which he will add nothing; and therefore that they keep fast in memory such things as they had already learned and in which they were now exercised.[note 14.2] For the Cataphrygians, called also Montanists, boasted of a new comforter and a new revelation, as though all things had not been fully set forth by the Apostles, but that many things were left yet to be revealed by them. As also at this day the maintainers of the Popish church most stiffly affirm. And just as the Cataphrygians covered their trifles under the pretense of the Holy Spirit, so do the Papists likewise cloak the vain constitutions of men and set them forth under a false color of the Holy Spirit, as though the Lord spoke of their decrees when he said, “I have yet many things to say unto you, which now you cannot bear.” Nevertheless, the faithful people of Thyatira, who did not have the doctrine of Jezebel but rather detested it as doubtful, said that the Devil was a certain depenes and had a thousand crafts, which could also transform him into an angel of light; and that they were but simple men, who, being ignorant of his wonderful crafts and subtleties, did not know what they should chiefly follow, while the false prophets also make their boast of the Holy Spirit, and shine in miracles, and with great constancy avouch their doctrine to be true. You shall find at this day those who will say, “I am a plain simple man, and know not whether part I should cleave to, since the doctors of both parts affirm with great costa cie that they have the truth on their side, and therefore will some say, they shall agree better ere I will believe any of them.” &c.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 14.4]And these words of the Lord must be weighed more diligently, to the end we may perceive the great fruicte that is in the [illegible]: that is I will lay upon you none other weight or burthe, besides this that you have. The Lord affirms that he will add nothing more to the doctrine evangelical set forth by the Apostles, as to that which is most perfect. Certes if the doctrine of Moiſes were so perfect, that the Lord him self did prohibit, that no man should add or take away any thing fro the same, but only should doe that which was commanded, as we read in the.4. and.12. Chap. of Deuteronomy. Who would doubt that there should want any thing in the doctrine of Christ the son of God? He therefore now affirms, that he will lay nothing upon them, more then he had laid, and the which they bear at that time.
[DRAFT]
And these words of the Lord must be weighed more diligently, so that we may perceive the great benefit in them: that is, I will lay upon you no other weight or burden besides what you already have. The Lord affirms that he will add nothing more to the evangelical doctrine set forth by the Apostles, as to that which is most perfect. Certainly, if the doctrine of Moses were so perfect that the Lord himself prohibited anyone from adding or taking away anything from it, but only doing what was commanded, as we read in the fourth and twelfth chapters of Deuteronomy. Who would doubt that the doctrine of Christ, the Son of God, would be lacking in anything? He therefore now affirms that he will lay nothing upon them more than he had already laid, and which they were bearing at that time.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "fruicte" changed to "benefit." While related, "fruicte" implies a more substantial, potentially spiritual reward than "benefit" suggests.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "so that" after "diligently" adds a layer of explanation not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "[illegible]" is removed, eliminating the original's ambiguity. While a modern editor might attempt to reconstruct it, its absence is a loss.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]

The Lord affirms that he will add nothing more to the doctrine evangelical set forth by the Apostles, as to that which is most perfect. Chap. of Deuteronomy. Who would doubt that there should want any thing in the doctrine of Christ the son of God? He therefore now affirms, that he will lay nothing upon them, more then he had laid, and the which they bear at that time.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 14.5]A burden in the ſermons of the Prophets is take for doctrine of grave and weighty matters. The Apostles also call the law a yocke and burden. Where therefore the Lord saith that he will not lay upon the church any other burden, he saith howe he will not reveal any other doctrine, nor further charge the, with other rites or ceremonies, than such as he had ordained and imposed already. And with these words of Christ accord those things very well, which are red in thapoſtles epistle Sinodical Act xv. For by the common consent of the congregation, and after the mind of the holy ghost, they say they will impose nothing moreover upon the church, than such things as they had received already of S. Paul, and a few things that they added for a declaration of the same. Wherupo S. Paul said to the Galath. If an angel from heaven preach unto you an other Gospel, besides that which is preached, let him be accursed.
[DRAFT]
A burden in the sermons of the Prophets is taken as doctrine concerning grave and weighty matters. The Apostles also call the law a yoke and burden. Therefore, when the Lord says that he will not lay any other burden upon the church, he means that he will not reveal any other doctrine, nor further charge them with other rites or ceremonies, than those he had already ordained and imposed. These words of Christ accord well with what is read in the apostles' epistle, the Synodical Act of xv. For by the common consent of the congregation, and after the mind of the Holy Spirit, they say they will impose nothing moreover upon the church, than such things as they had received already from Paul, and a few things that they added for a declaration of the same. Whereupon Paul said to the Galatians: If an angel from heaven preach unto you another gospel besides that which is preached, let him be accursed.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "take for doctrine" changed to "taken as doctrine concerning," subtly shifts from a descriptive observation to an interpretive assertion.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "he means that" inserted after "he will not lay any other burden upon the church" adds an explanatory layer not present in the original.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "Wherupo" is a slightly archaic connector. While the draft's "Whereupon" is acceptable, the original's phrasing allows for a broader range of possible connections that the more direct "Whereupon" eliminates.
PROPER NOUNS: "S. Paul" changed to "Paul." While acceptable, the original's abbreviation is a stylistic choice reflecting the period and its removal homogenizes the text.
HALLUCINATION: None.
[FINAL]
A burden in the sermons of the Prophets is take for doctrine of grave and weighty matters. The Apostles also call the law a yocke and burden. Where therefore the Lord saith that he will not lay upon the church any other burden, he saith howe he will not reveal any other doctrine, nor further charge the, with other rites or ceremonies, than such as he had ordained and imposed already. And with these words of Christ accord those things very well, which are red in thapoſtles epistle Sinodical Act xv. For by the common consent of the congregation, and after the mind of the holy ghost, they say they will impose nothing moreover upon the church, than such things as they had received already of S. Paul, and a few things that they added for a declaration of the same. Wherupo S. Paul said to the Galath. If an angel from heaven preach unto you an other Gospel, besides that which is preached, let him be accursed.
---
[ORIGINAL]
What than? [illegible] hold fast,[note 14.6] namely that which you have received, suffering it not to be plucte out of your hades: Hold fast I say, with touth and nail, till I come: that is to say unto the last judgement. Therefore he testifies expressly, that this doctrine shall be perpetual, and unchangeable, and therefore to be kept most stiffly of all me, and not to be ſhroke fro, though all the world cry out, and persuade the contrary. Arethas Biſh. of Caesarea He required of the nothing else, saith he, but that they wold keep safely the godly pledge of faith until his coming. This if we shall do, we may easily eschew the crafts of the devil, and diſceauable clouds. For whatsoever they shall bring forth, whatsoever they shall forge and fine, or die with the cou terfeited colour of the holy ghost, we shall have always recourse to the simple doctrine of Christ set forth by thapoſtles, wherein alone we shall rest, rejecting althigs that shall not acerd with the same.
[DRAFT]
What then? Hold fast, namely that which you have received, suffering it not to be plucked out of your hands: Hold fast I say, with tooth and nail, until I come; that is to say, unto the last judgment. Therefore he testifies expressly that this doctrine shall be perpetual and unchangeable, and therefore to be kept most steadfastly by all of us, and not to be shaken from, though all the world cry out and persuade the contrary. Arethas, Bishop of Caesarea, required of them nothing else, he says, but that they would keep safely the godly pledge of faith until his coming. This if we shall do, we may easily avoid the crafts of the devil and deceivable clouds. For whatever they shall bring forth, whatever they shall forge and refine, or dye with the counterfeited color of the Holy Spirit, we shall always have recourse to the simple doctrine of Christ set forth by the apostles, wherein alone we shall rest, rejecting all things that shall not accord with the same.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "stiffly of all me" changed to "steadfastly by all of us." The original phrasing, while archaic, emphasizes personal responsibility and adherence. The draft's wording shifts the focus to a collective obligation, potentially diluting the individual commitment implied in the original.
2. PROPER NOUNS: "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This is a consistent error needing correction throughout the modernized text.
3. HALLUCINATION: The phrase "die with the cou terfeited colour of the holy ghost" in the original is rendered as "dye with the counterfeited color of the Holy Spirit" in the draft. The original uses "die," suggesting a deceptive appearance or false representation, while the draft uses "dye," referring to a coloring process. This alters the intended meaning.
[FINAL]
What then? Hold fast, namely that which you have received, suffering it not to be plucked out of your hands: Hold fast I say, with tooth and nail, until I come; that is to say, unto the last judgment. Therefore he testifies expressly that this doctrine shall be perpetual and unchangeable, and therefore to be kept most stiffly of all me, and not to be shaken from, though all the world cry out and persuade the contrary. Arethas, Bishop of Caesarea, required of them nothing else, he says, but that they would keep safely the godly pledge of faith until his coming. This if we shall do, we may easily avoid the crafts of the devil and deceivable clouds. For whatever they shall bring forth, whatever they shall forge and refine, or die with the counterfeited colour of the holy ghost, we shall always have recourse to the simple doctrine of Christ set forth by the apostles, wherein alone we shall rest, rejecting all things that shall not accord with the same.

[ORIGINAL]
And this wholesome doctrine of Christ confounds all traditions,[note 14.7] and subverts all conſtinitios made since the time of the Apostles. The godly may always object this ſayin of Christ to the traditioners. I will lay none other burthe upon you, besides that you have: That same hold fast until the last judgment. They shall allege that same also, that the Apostles deny that they will add nothing more. Act. 15. Christ spake this in the time of S. John, in the year of our Lord lxxxxvii. Therefore what so ever laws, traditios, decrees have been made since that time, we know they were not imposed of Christ, which saith so expressly that he will lay none other burden on the faithful. Where then become the decrees and constitutions of worshipping Images in the church, for the consecration and celebrating of masses. What shall we say to the decretalles of the Bishop of Rome? They are all overthrown and stricken down as it were with a thonderbolte, by this only sentence of Christ. I will impose none other burden, the that you have, keep that until the judgment. Behold, he saith, unto the judgment least any should imagine in the mean season, that another thing had pleased the holy ghost. Let us therefore persevere in the same.
[DRAFT]
This wholesome doctrine of Christ confounds all traditions and subverts all constitutions made since the time of the Apostles. The godly may always object this saying of Christ to the traditioners. I will lay no other burden upon you, besides that which you have: hold fast to that until the last judgment. They shall allege this also, that the Apostles denied that they would add anything more. Acts 15. Christ spoke this in the time of Jerome, in the year of our Lord 97. Therefore, whatever laws, traditions, decrees have been made since that time, we know they were not imposed by Christ, who says so expressly that he will lay no other burden on the faithful. Where then become the decrees and constitutions of worshipping images in the church, for the consecration and celebrating of masses? What shall we say to the decretals of the Bishop of Rome? They are all overthrown and stricken down as it were with a thunderbolt, by this only sentence of Christ: I will impose no other burden than that which you have, keep that until the judgment. Behold, he says, unto the judgment, lest any should imagine in the meantime that another thing had pleased the Holy Spirit. Let us therefore persevere in the same.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "lxxxxvii" changed to "97." This significantly alters the timeline and context. The original likely refers to a specific year within a historical understanding of John's time, which is lost with the simplification.
2. PROPER NOUNS: "S. John" changed to "Jerome." This is a critical error. Jerome is a different figure entirely.
3. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of commas around "Acts 15" disrupts the flow and implies a pause for reflection that isn't present in the original.
4. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "That same hold fast" is simplified to "hold fast to that." While functionally similar, the original phrasing carries a subtle emphasis on maintaining the existing burden.
5. PROPER NOUNS: "Austen" should be "Augustine" (not present in this excerpt, but a common error in modernized texts).
[FINAL]
This wholesome doctrine of Christ confounds all traditions[note 14.7] and subverts all constitutions made since the time of the Apostles. The godly may always object this saying of Christ to the traditioners. I will lay none other burthe upon you, besides that you have: That same hold fast until the last judgment. They shall allege that same also, that the Apostles deny that they will add nothing more. Act. 15. Christ spake this in the time of S. John, in the year of our Lord lxxxxvii. Therefore what so ever laws, traditios, decrees have been made since that time, we know they were not imposed of Christ, which saith so expressly that he will lay none other burden on the faithful. Where then become the decrees and constitutions of worshipping Images in the church, for the consecration and celebrating of masses. What shall we say to the decretalles of the Bishop of Rome? They are all overthrown and stricken down as it were with a thonderbolte, by this only sentence of Christ. I will impose none other burden, the that you have, keep that until the judgment. Behold, he saith, unto the judgment least any should imagine in the mean season, that another thing had pleased the holy ghost. Let us therefore persevere in the same.

[ORIGINAL]
The latter: I will give him the morning star. And he understood the knowledge of Christ increasing daily more and more, and so even Christ himself,[note 14.11] in like case as the day in the rising of the morning star waxes brighter and brighter: In the which sense the Apostle S. Peter is red to have used this allegory in the ii. Epistle first Chapter, or at the least he promised a clerenes most bright. For Daniel saith, howe the faithful in the resurrection shall shine like the firmament. The which thing also the Lord Christ alleges the xiii. of Math. And the Apostle alluding hereunto said, that one star was brighter then an other: So likewise in the resurrection one shall be made brighter then an other. These promises be most great, neither can I think that any greater can be given us. God grant us grace, that we may be made partakers of so great things.
[DRAFT]
The latter: I will give him the morning star. And he understood the knowledge of Christ increasing daily more and more, and so even Christ himself, [note 14.11] in like case as the day in the rising of the morning star waxes brighter and brighter. In that sense, the Apostle Saint Peter is said to have used this allegory in the first chapter of his second Epistle, or at the least he promised a brightness most clear. For Daniel says how the faithful in the resurrection shall shine like the firmament. The same thing also the Lord Christ mentions in Matthew 13. And the Apostle alluding hereunto said that one star was brighter than another: so likewise in the resurrection one shall be made brighter than another. These promises are most great, and I cannot think that any greater can be given us. God grant us grace that we may be made partakers of such great things.
[CRITIQUE]

3. PROPER NOUNS: "S." changed to "Saint." While acceptable, "S." is a common abbreviation for "Saint" in the period and its removal feels like a slight smoothing that loses a touch of the original's style.
4. LOST AMBIGUITY: No significant ambiguity is lost.

Finally he applies this epistle to all churches and ages of the world. Whereof since we have spoken oftener than once, there is no cause that by oft repeating I should be tedious to any man. To the Lord our God be praise and glory.
[DRAFT]
Finally, he applies this epistle to all churches and ages of the world. Concerning which, since we have spoken of this more than once, there is no need for me to be tedious to anyone by repeating it often. To the Lord our God be praise and glory.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "oft repeating I should be tedious to any man" subtly shifts from a concern about the speaker's potential boredom to a statement of unnecessary repetition. The original implies a potential imposition on the reader; the draft simply states it's unnecessary.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "by repeating it often" is an interpretation of "oft repeating." The original is more direct and less explanatory.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "oft repeating" allows for a broader interpretation of the speaker's actions. The draft's "repeating it often" is more specific.
[FINAL]
Finally, he applies this epistle to all churches and ages of the world. Concerning which, since we have spoken of this more than once, there is no cause that by oft repeating I should be tedious to any man. To the Lord our God be praise and glory.

[ORIGINAL]
AND write to the messenger of the congregatio of Sardis, this saith he that hath the spirits of God, and the vii. stars. I know thy works: Thou hast a name that thou lives and thou art dead. Be awake, and strength the things which remain, that are ready to die. For I have not found thy works perfect before God. Remember therefore howe thou hast received and heard, and hold fast and repent. If thou shalt not watch, I will come on thee as a thief, and thou shalt not know what hour I will come upon thee.

If you do not watch, I will come on you as a thief, and you will not know at what hour I will come upon you.

Write to the messenger of the congregation of Sardis, this says he who has the spirits of God and the seven stars. I know your works: you have a reputation that you live, but you are dead. Be alert and strengthen what remains, which is about to die. For I have not found your works perfect before God. Remember therefore how you have received and heard, and hold fast and repent. If you do not watch, I will come on you as a thief, and you will not know at what hour I will come upon you.

[ORIGINAL]
The first part of the Epistle coteineth those things which we have now recited: Nether doth he proceed herein in other order, than we have scene him to have proceeded hitherto. For first he shows to whom it is dedicated, and sent: Namely to the Pastor of the congregation of Sardis,[note 15.2] and therefore also to the whole church. Sardis is said to have ben the head city of Lydia or of Maonia, the metropolitan city of Creſus the most rich king of Lydia, whom Herodotus writes that king Cyrus overcame, a town most famous, and pricked and painted with pride, that it was a water: And addict to voluptuouſnes. For Strabo in the xiii. book of Geography testifies that all the maidens thereof were harlots, who mentions more of the same city. Certes it seems to have kept his old wont, even at such time also as it had received the name of the Lord: And therefore to have been more given to fornication, and all manner of filthy lust. The which thing the Lord seems to have blamed in them, as S. Paul likewyſe persecuted the self same vise in the Corinthians. The world can hardly believe that simple fornication is sin whereupon in that great counsel of the Apostles,[note 15.3] both they and the elders and the whole assembly with one mind decreed, that the ge tiles should abstain from fornication. The devil at this day goes about many times to defile the church again with fornication, to set up stews, and that by authority and openly whordom might be practised. For so being cast out, he takes seven worse spirits, enterprising to possess that place again out of the which he was exiled by the preaching of the Gospel. We must therefore resist him, least the Lord Jesus him self do accuse us, as he doth here accuse them of Sardis most grievously.[note 15.4] Then is the Lord Jesus declared to be author of the Epistle, not without praise. For he is said to have the seven spirits of God, that is to have the seven formed spirit, whom he also powers out upon the faithful, or else he is one only spirit, and not seven: but seven, that is to wit, his graces be many and diverse, as I declared in the first chapter, for the same hath also in his right hand seven stars, to wit, the whole multitude of all preachers and ministers, keeping and instructing the. And this beginning agrees not amiss with this argument, which he treats in this Epistle. For of the spirit of Christ is life: Of the want of the spirit, death. Christ preserves the ministers, how angry so ever me are in the church, with the for accusing their wickedness. Privily therefore he warns them, to crave the spirit, to noriſhe the life spiritual: And to trust in Christ, which will defend the ministers, and advance them.
[DRAFT]
The first part of the Epistle contains those things which we have now recited. Nor does he proceed herein in any other order than we have seen him to proceed hitherto. For first, he shows to whom it is dedicated and sent: namely, to the pastor of the congregation of Sardis, and therefore also to the whole church. Sardis is said to have been the head city of Lydia or of Maonia, the metropolitan city of Croesus, the most rich king of Lydia, whom Herodotus writes that King Cyrus overcame, a town most famous, and marked and stained with pride, that it was a den of vice and addicted to voluptuousness. For Strabo in the thirteenth book of Geography testifies that all the maidens thereof were harlots, who mentions more of the same city. Certainly, it seems to have kept its old ways even at such time as it had received the name of the Lord, and therefore to have been more given to fornication and all manner of filthy lust. The which thing the Lord seems to have blamed in them, as Paul likewise persecuted the same vice in the Corinthians. The world can hardly believe that simple fornication is sin, whereupon in that great counsel of the Apostles, both they and the elders and the whole assembly with one mind decreed that the Gentiles should abstain from fornication. The devil at this day goes about many times to defile the church again with fornication, to set up brothels, and that by authority and openly whoredom might be practiced. For so being cast out, he takes seven worse spirits, enterprising to possess that place again out of which he was exiled by the preaching of the Gospel. We must therefore resist him, lest the Lord Jesus himself do accuse us, as he does here accuse them of Sardis most grievously. Then is the Lord Jesus declared to be author of the Epistle, not without praise. For he is said to have the seven spirits of God, that is to have the seven formed spirit, whom he also pours out upon the faithful, or else he is one only spirit, and not seven: but seven, that is to wit, his graces are many and diverse, as I declared in the first chapter, for he has also in his right hand seven stars, to wit, the whole multitude of all preachers and ministers, keeping and instructing them. And this beginning agrees not amiss with this argument, which he treats in this Epistle. For of the spirit of Christ is life; of the want of the spirit, death. Christ preserves the ministers, however angry some are in the church, for accusing their wickedness. Privily therefore he warns them, to crave the spirit, to nourish the spiritual life, and to trust in Christ, which will defend the ministers and advance them.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "pricked and painted with pride, that it was a water" changed to "marked and stained with pride, that it was a den of vice." The original phrasing is unusual, but the modernization loses the peculiar, almost metaphorical quality of the original, replacing it with a more straightforward (and potentially less accurate) interpretation.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "stews" changed to "brothels." While functionally similar, "stews" carried a specific historical connotation relating to regulated prostitution, which "brothels" lacks. This introduces a modern interpretation.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing regarding the seven spirits is complex and open to multiple interpretations. The draft's phrasing ("that is to have the seven formed spirit") attempts to clarify, but risks narrowing the meaning and potentially imposing a specific theological understanding not explicitly present in the original.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms were identified.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content was added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No errors in proper noun modernization were identified.
[FINAL]
The first part of the Epistle contains those things which we have now recited. Nether doth he proceed herein in other order, than we have seen him to have proceeded hitherto. For first he shows to whom it is dedicated, and sent: Namely to the Pastor of the congregation of Sardis, and therefore also to the whole church. Sardis is said to have ben the head city of Lydia or of Maonia, the metropolitan city of Creſus the most rich king of Lydia, whom Herodotus writes that king Cyrus overcame, a town most famous, and pricked and painted with pride, that it was a water: And addict to voluptuouſnes. For Strabo in the xiii. book of Geography testifies that all the maidens thereof were harlots, who mentions more of the same city. Certes it seems to have kept his old wont, even at such time also as it had received the name of the Lord: And therefore to have been more given to fornication, and all manner of filthy lust. The which thing the Lord seems to have blamed in them, as S. Paul likewyſe persecuted the self same vise in the Corinthians. The world can hardly believe that simple fornication is sin whereupon in that great counsel of the Apostles, both they and the elders and the whole assembly with one mind decreed, that the ge tiles should abstain from fornication. The devil at this day goes about many times to defile the church again with fornication, to set up stews, and that by authority and openly whordom might be practised. For so being cast out, he takes seven worse spirits, enterprising to possess that place again out of the which he was exiled by the preaching of the Gospel. We must therefore resist him, least the Lord Jesus him self do accuse us, as he doth here accuse them of Sardis most grievously. Then is the Lord Jesus declared to be author of the Epistle, not without praise. For he is said to have the seven spirits of God, that is to have the seven formed spirit, whom he also powers out upon the faithful, or else he is one only spirit, and not seven: but seven, that is to wit, his graces be many and diverse, as I declared in the first chapter, for the same hath also in his right hand seven stars, to wit, the whole multitude of all preachers and ministers, keeping and instructing the. And this beginning agrees not amiss with this argument, which he treats in this Epistle. For of the spirit of Christ is life: Of the want of the spirit, death. Christ preserves the ministers, how angry so ever me are in the church, with the for accusing their wickedness. Privily therefore he warns them, to crave the spirit, to noriſhe the life spiritual: And to trust in Christ, which will defend the ministers, and advance them.
---
[ORIGINAL]
After, the same that he testifies in all other Epistles, he repeated here also. I know thy works. Whereof I have spoken before. The Lo[illegible]de is ignorant of nothing that is done in the church, which is also the searcher of hearts. And especially he blames this in this Church,[note 15.5] that she thought her self a live, where she was dead. He speaks not of the corporal, but of the spiritual life and death. For Christ lives by his spirit in his saints, and faithful, and shows lively works by them. Like as the Lord teaches in the vi. of John, and in many other places of the Gospel of S. John. The Apostle said also, that he lived not now,[note 15.6] but that Christ lived in him: The same Apostle said, that wydowes living in watones, being alive were dead. They be dead therefore which have not Christ living in the by faith and spirit. Which have not the virtue of Christ working in the, that is which brings not forth lively works.[note 15.7] For the Lord is red to have said also in the Gospel: Suffer the dead to bury their dead. The Sardenſians therefore had the name of men living that is to say, they were called Christians, spiritual, regenerated and holy worshippers of God: but they were dead, to wit, hipocrites, in whom no spirit nor Christen life appeared. The flesh, the world and corruption, as yet lived in the. But such churches displease Christ: There be many such at this day. But whether doth Christ reject them? Verily he blames such, but not to confound them, (for so the world blames) but that they should repent. For he wills not the death of a sinner, but rather that he should convert and live. And therefore consequently he prepares a medicine for the disease.
[DRAFT]
As he testifies in all his other letters, he repeats it here also: “I know your works.” Of this I have spoken before. The Lord is ignorant of nothing that is done in the church, which is also the searcher of hearts. And especially he condemns this in this Church [note 15.5], that she thought herself alive where she was dead. He speaks not of the physical, but of the spiritual life and death. For Christ lives by his spirit in his saints and faithful, and shows lively works through them, as the Lord teaches in John 6 and many other places in the Gospel of Saint John. The Apostle also said that he did not now live [note 15.6], but that Christ lived in him. The same Apostle said that widows living in luxury, though alive, were dead. They are dead therefore who do not have Christ living in them by faith and spirit; who do not have the virtue of Christ working in them, that is, who do not produce lively works [note 15.7]. For the Lord is reported to have said also in the Gospel: “Let the dead bury their dead.” The Sardinians therefore had the name of living men, that is to say, they were called Christians, spiritual, regenerated, and holy worshippers of God; but they were dead, namely, hypocrites, in whom no spirit nor Christian life appeared. The flesh, the world, and corruption still lived in them. But such churches displease Christ. There are many such today. But how does Christ reject them? Truly he condemns such, but not to destroy them (for so the world condemns), but that they should repent. For he does not will the death of a sinner, but rather that he should turn and live. And therefore, consequently, he prepares a medicine for the disease.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "The Lord is ignorant of nothing" replaces "The Lorde is ignorant of nothing," subtly shifting from a statement about God's knowledge to a declaration about God's lack of ignorance. While functionally similar, the original phrasing carries a slightly different theological weight.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "living in luxury" is added to "living in watones." While "watones" likely implies a similar concept, the draft's explicit addition of "luxury" introduces a specific and potentially anachronistic interpretation.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "living in the" (referring to the Sardinians) is rendered as "living in them." The original phrasing, while potentially awkward, allows for a broader interpretation (living *within* the church, for example). The draft's phrasing is more restrictive.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of key theological terms.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** "Let the dead bury their dead" is placed in quotation marks in the draft, implying a direct quote. The original does not use quotation marks.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "S. John" is changed to "Saint John." While acceptable, the original abbreviation is a stylistic feature of the period and its removal alters the tone slightly.
[FINAL]
As he testifies in all his other letters, he repeats it here also: “I know your works.” Of this I have spoken before. The Lo[illegible]de is ignorant of nothing that is done in the church, which is also the searcher of hearts. And especially he blames this in this Church [note 15.5], that she thought her self a live, where she was dead. He speaks not of the corporal, but of the spiritual life and death. For Christ lives by his spirit in his saints, and faithful, and shows lively works by them. Like as the Lord teaches in the vi. of John, and in many other places of the Gospel of S. John. The Apostle said also, that he lived not now [note 15.6], but that Christ lived in him: The same Apostle said, that wydowes living in watones, being alive were dead. They be dead therefore which have not Christ living in the by faith and spirit. Which have not the virtue of Christ working in the, that is which brings not forth lively works [note 15.7]. For the Lord is red to have said also in the Gospel: Suffer the dead to bury their dead. The Sardenſians therefore had the name of men living that is to say, they were called Christians, spiritual, regenerated and holy worshippers of God: but they were dead, to wit, hipocrites, in whom no spirit nor Christen life appeared. The flesh, the world and corruption, as yet lived in the. But such churches displease Christ: There be many such at this day. But whether doth Christ reject them? Verily he blames such, but not to confound them, (for so the world blames) but that they should repent. For he wills not the death of a sinner, but rather that he should convert and live. And therefore consequently he prepares a medicine for the disease.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 15.9]Now also he adds the reason, why he commands to confirm the flock least they ſlyde in to death. For I have not found thy works full or perfect before God. The Greek copy Complutenſian and Arethas have, my God. By works he vnderſtadeth all things that are done, words works, and the whole conversation of men. The works doubles even of Saints be evermore vnperfit, if we have respect to humane imbecility. For always so long as we live here, the flesh fights against the spirit: In so much that Iob said, how he feared all his works, and therefore fled to the cleme cie of the judge: Notwithstanding they be perfect and full in respect of Christ. For he is our fulness, and in him we are co plete, John. 1. Epheſ. 1. Coloſſ. the 12. And he makes us pertakers of his fulness by faith. They of Sardis were destitute of true faith, wherefore every work of theirs must needs be vnperfit before God: which allows nothing but that is of the son and most pure. Therefore the Lord comaundeth to teach faith diligently, and beat it in, that they may b[illegible] made perfect in Christ. This is the best medicine for the deadly disease of Christ's church.
[DRAFT]
Now he also adds the reason why he commands to confirm the flock, lest they slip into death. For I have not found your works full or perfect before God. The Greek copies Complutensian and Arethas have, “my God.” By works he understands all things that are done, words, deeds, and the entire conversation of men. The works, even of saints, are always imperfect if we have regard to human weakness. For as long as we live here, the flesh fights against the spirit; so that Job said he feared all his works and therefore fled to the clemency of the judge. Nevertheless, they are perfect and full in respect of Christ, as in John 1, Ephesians 1, and Colossians 1:12. And he makes us partakers of his fullness by faith. Those of Sardis were destitute of true faith, wherefore every work of theirs must needs be imperfect before God, which allows nothing but that which is of the Son and most pure. Therefore the Lord commands to teach faith diligently and beat it in, that they may be made perfect in Christ. This is the best medicine for the deadly disease of Christ’s church.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "ſlyde in to death" changed to "slip into death." While semantically close, "ſlyde" carries a sense of deliberate deviation or deception, subtly altering the nuance of the original warning.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "as in John 1, Ephesians 1, and Colossians 1:12" after "perfect and full in respect of Christ" adds an interpretive layer not present in the original. It explicitly points to scriptural references, which the original leaves to the reader.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "the cleme cie of the judge" is somewhat obscure, potentially allowing for multiple interpretations of the divine judgment. The draft's "the clemency of the judge" removes this ambiguity, making it more straightforward but less evocative.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content is present.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Complutensian is correctly modernized. Austen/Augustine, Ireney/Irenaeus are not present in this passage. Hierom/Jerome is not present in this passage.
[FINAL]
Now he also adds the reason why he commands to confirm the flock, lest they fly into death. For I have not found your works full or perfect before God. The Greek copy Complutensian and Arethas have, “my God.” By works he understands all things that are done, words, deeds, and the whole conversation of men. The works even of Saints be evermore vnperfect, if we have respect to humane imbecility. For always so long as we live here, the flesh fights against the spirit: in so much that Iob said, how he feared all his works, and therefore fled to the cleme cie of the judge: Notwithstanding they be perfect and full in respect of Christ. For he is our fulness, and in him we are co plete, John. 1. Epheſ. 1. Coloſſ. the 12. And he makes us pertakers of his fulness by faith. They of Sardis were destitute of true faith, wherefore every work of theirs must needs be vnperfect before God: which allows nothing but that is of the son and most pure. Therefore the Lord comaundeth to teach faith diligently, and beat it in, that they may b[illegible] made perfect in Christ. This is the best medicine for the deadly disease of Christ's church.

[ORIGINAL]
Unto the which repentance now,[note 15.11] after the divine prophetical and apoſtolical manner, he draws the by the threatenings. Which are in deed to be referred as well to the ministers, as to the people, in the congregation. Again the Lord uses parables, which we read that he used in S. Matthew. 24. Where with the same he exhorts to watching and sobriety. Which place sins it is there expounded at large, I need not to use many words about it here. To the Lord be praise and thanks geuyng for evermore.
[DRAFT]
Unto which repentance, now, after the divine, prophetic, and apostolic manner, he draws by means of threats. These threats are indeed to be referred both to the ministers and to the people in the congregation. Again, the Lord uses parables, as we read that he used in Matthew 24. With these, he exhorts to watchfulness and sobriety. That passage is explained at length, so I need not use many words about it here. To the Lord be praise and thanks giving forevermore.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "by the threatenings" changed to "by means of threats" subtly alters the agency. The original suggests the threatenings *themselves* draw people; the draft suggests a more mediated action.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "That passage is explained at length" is interpretive. The original simply states the passage needs no further elaboration.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "Which place sins it is there expounded" is slightly awkward but allows for a reading where the *sins* are expounded, not the passage itself. The draft's "That passage is explained" removes this ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: S. Matthew modernized to Matthew correctly.
[FINAL]

Which are in deed to be referred as well to the ministers, as to the people, in the congregation. Again the Lord uses parables, which we read that he used in Matthew 24. With the same he exhorts to watching and sobriety. Which place sins it is there expounded; I need not to use many words about it here. To the Lord be praise and thanks giving forevermore.

[ORIGINAL]
But thou haste a few names in Sardis, which have not defiled their garments: And they shall walk with me in white, for they are worthy. He that ouercometh shall be clothed in white array, and I will not put out his name out of the book of life: And I will confess his name before my Father, and before his Angles. He that hath ears let him hear what the spirit says to the congregations.
[DRAFT]
But you have a few names in Sardis who have not defiled their garments; and they shall walk with you in white robes, for they are worthy. The one who conquers will be clothed in white array, and I will not blot out his name from the book of life; and I will confess his name before my Father and before the angels. He who has ears let him hear what the Spirit says to the congregations.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "walk with me" changed to "walk with you." This subtly alters the relationship implied – from a direct connection with the divine to a more communal one.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "white" changed to "white robes." While not a drastic change, the addition of "robes" adds a layer of visual specificity not explicitly present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "they are worthy" is more open to interpretation regarding who is worthy. The draft’s phrasing, while similar, loses some of that ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 16.1]The second part of this heavenly epistle is contained in these points, in the which is praised and commended the innocence, holiness and integrity of the faithful in the congregation of Sardis, in true religion. He exhorts them by a promise most large unto perseverance. Last he propounds again unto them most ample rewards: even to the corrupt sort, in case they amend: and to the faithful, if they continue as they be.
[DRAFT]
[note 16.1]The second part of this heavenly epistle is contained in these points, wherein the innocence, holiness, and integrity of the faithful in the congregation of Sardis, in true religion, are praised and commended. He exhorts them by a most generous promise to persevere. Finally, he again proposes to them most ample rewards: even to the corrupt, if they repent; and to the faithful, if they continue as they are.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "large" changed to "generous" subtly alters the sense of the promise. "Large" implies magnitude, potentially referring to the scope or value of the promise. "Generous" focuses on the giver's character.

ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of "repent" to describe the corrupt sort's potential action introduces an interpretation not explicitly present in the original. While repentance is implied, stating it directly adds a layer of explanation.

LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing regarding the rewards is slightly ambiguous – it's not entirely clear *what* is being proposed again. The draft's phrasing, while clearer, loses this ambiguity.
[FINAL]
[note 16.1]The second part of this heavenly epistle is contained in these points, in which the innocence, holiness, and integrity of the faithful in the congregation of Sardis, in true religion, are praised and commended. He exhorts them by a most large promise unto perseverance. Last he propounds again unto them most ample rewards: even to the corrupt sort, in case they amend: and to the faithful, if they continue as they be.

[ORIGINAL]
The complutenſia book hath thus [illegible]: But thou haste a few names in Sardis. Which is as much as if he had said, they be not all corrupt and dead with thee, although in deed those be very few. And so Arethas reads it in Greek, and the common translation in Latin other copies have, which Eraſmus follows, thou haste a few names also at Sardis: that is, even in Sardis hast thou names,[note 16.2] but few. And he put names for notable men. Which manner of speaking is also in our langage. For we say, there is no man of name, for no excellent or noble personage, he signifies therefore, that there be in the same Church noble personages, and that noble in ſoundenes of faith and holynes of life: but very few,[note 16.3] if they should be referred or compared to the number of Hipocrites or dead, which in deed are a great deal more. Neither ought we here at to marvel. For the Lord says also in the Gospel, that many be called, few chosen: And that the greater part of this world walk in that broad and wide way of perdition, Matthew. 20. and 7. The which also S. Peter repeated in the 2. chapter of his latter epistle. That they are rather to be hissed at, than confuted, which seek to defend their error, by a multitude. You shall hear very oft at this day. You are but a few in number, we are innumerable, and therefore our matter is the better.
[DRAFT]
The Complutensian book has it thus: “But you have a few names in Sardis.” This is as much as to say, they are not all corrupt and dead among you, although in truth these are very few. And so Arethas reads it in Greek, and the common translation in Latin, and other copies have, “You have a few names also at Sardis,” that is, even in Sardis you have names, but few. And he puts “names” for notable men. This manner of speaking is also in our language. For we say, “there is no man of name,” meaning no excellent or noble personage. He signifies therefore that there are in the same church noble personages, and that noble in soundness of faith and holiness of life, but very few, if they should be referred or compared to the number of hypocrites or dead, which in truth are a great deal more. Neither ought we here at all to marvel. For the Lord says also in the Gospel, that many are called, few are chosen, and that the greater part of this world walks in that broad and wide way of perdition, Matthew 20:7. Which also Saint Peter repeated in the second chapter of his latter epistle: that they are rather to be hissed at than confuted, who seek to defend their error by a multitude. You shall hear very often at this day: “You are but a few in number, we are innumerable, and therefore our matter is the better.”
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "And so Arethas reads it in Greek, and the common translation in Latin other copies have" is simplified to "And so Arethas reads it in Greek, and the common translation in Latin, and other copies have." The original phrasing implies a comparison between readings, which is lost.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "meaning no excellent or noble personage" is an unnecessary explanation. The original text's explanation is sufficient.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original’s phrasing "he put names for notable men" is softened to "And he puts 'names' for notable men." The original suggests a deliberate substitution, which the draft diminishes.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No hallucinated content is present.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No errors in proper noun modernization.
[FINAL]
The Complutensian book has it thus: “But you have a few names in Sardis.” This is as much as to say, they are not all corrupt and dead among you, although in truth these are very few. And so Arethas reads it in Greek, and the common translation in Latin other copies have, “You have a few names also at Sardis,” that is, even in Sardis you have names,[note 16.2] but few. And he put names for notable men. This manner of speaking is also in our language. For we say, “there is no man of name,” he signifies therefore that there are in the same church noble personages, and that noble in soundness of faith and holiness of life, but very few,[note 16.3] if they should be referred or compared to the number of hypocrites or dead, which in truth are a great deal more. Neither ought we here at all to marvel. For the Lord says also in the Gospel, that many are called, few are chosen: And that the greater part of this world walk in that broad and wide way of perdition, Matthew. 20. and 7. The which also S. Peter repeated in the 2. chapter of his latter epistle. That they are rather to be hissed at, than confuted, which seek to defend their error, by a multitude. You shall hear very oft at this day. You are but a few in number, we are innumerable, and therefore our matter is the better.

[ORIGINAL]
But that same excellent thing is chiefly to be observed,[note 16.4] that although they were but few good, yet nevertheless the Lord comendeth and extols those few, doubtless for the example and imitation of all other churches. The words in deed are short, but the praise most ample and large. That they had not defiled their garments: which is as much as if he had said, you have not polluted your souls with strange opinions or spots of heresy. For you have remained sincere in the true faith: your bodies also, and the whole conversation of your life, you have not defiled with filthy lusts, with fleſhely pleasures and voluptuouſenes. Doubtless this is the greatest praise and most certain sign of perfect godliness: wherewith I would wish that mo of us were marked. But the manner of speech here requires also an exposition.[note 16.5] The allegory of garments is often and much used in holy scripture. The use of apparel invented of God him self, and showed to our forefathers, hath this chief property, to hide the privy parts of our body, to beautife and set forth the body, and keep of heate and cold. And therefore Christ him self is called the garment of Chriſtias, and in the gospel in deed the wedding garment. Whereupon the Apostle advises us to put on the new man, which is made after God even Christ him self. Roman. 13. Epheſ. 4. Coloſſ. 3. For Christ covers not only our privy parts, but all the filthines also of the soul, he adorns and beautifies us, and drives fro us all injury, and all evil. And we defile this garment, when neither in faith nor in holiness of life we do answer to our profeſſio. For Christ is our garment, and Christianity, sincere faith, and holiness of life are our apparel: And even faith and our conversation is our garment. For as much therefore as the Sardenſians were of a sincere faith, and vncorrupte manners, they are said to have kept their garments clean and undefiled. The lord also gives now a reward unto virtue.[note 16.6] And they shall walk with me, says he, in white array. These excellent things verily doth he rehearse to reteyne the Sardenſians in their duty, to nourriſhe them to greater things and to move other also to sincerity and integrity. Saints walk with Christ in white array, that is to say, have fruitio of the same glory, wherein we believe Christ to shine. For he desires his father, that he will grant to the faithful, that where so ever he is, they may be with him, and see his glory, &c. in the 17. of John. And with S. Matthew. in the tranſformatio or clarifiyng, the face of Christ appeared bright like the sun, his apparel and rest of his body as light. So appeared Christ unto John in the first Chapter. of this book, clothed in white array. Now therefore says he, the godly that have not defiled their garment, shall accompany me, having put on light also.[note 16.7] He adds an other thing, for they be worthy. This is the greatest praise, when the Captain says, that the soldier is worthy of honour and glory. The greatest shame or ignomy is, when it is said with us, thou art unworthy. The first kind of speech shows him to be most excellent in all kind of virtue, which is said to be worthy of eternal light, by the later is signified, that he which is accounted unworthy of a good and excellent thing, is maruelouſe negligent and vngraciouſe. But here we need not to reason of the merit and desert of worthiness. God pronou ceth his to be worthy of glory, the godly refer all the goodness that is in them unto grace, and still complain of their unworthiness. Not to reprove God of lying, but to praise and comende the excellent goodness that is in him: acknowledging in deed that he rewards good works, and dignifieth the worthiness of saints: but they are nothing proud hereof, but acknowledge all this to come of grace. This appears in the doctrine of the Gospel, Luke. 17. Matthew. 25. where Saints commended of God, for the works of mercy, seem the acknowledge nothing thereof.
[DRAFT]
But that same excellent thing is chiefly to be observed, that although they were but few good, yet nevertheless the Lord commends and extols those few, doubtless for the example and imitation of all other churches. The words in deed are short, but the praise most ample and large. That they had not defiled their garments: which is as much as if he had said, you have not polluted your souls with strange opinions or spots of heresy. For you have remained sincere in the true faith: your bodies also, and the whole conversation of your life, you have not defiled with filthy lusts, with fleshly pleasures and voluptuousness. Doubtless this is the greatest praise and most certain sign of perfect godliness: wherewith I would wish that more of us were marked. But the manner of speech here requires also an exposition. The allegory of garments is often and much used in holy scripture. The use of apparel invented of God himself, and showed to our forefathers, has this chief property, to hide the privy parts of our body, to beautify and set forth the body, and keep of heat and cold. And therefore Christ himself is called the garment of Christians, and in the gospel in deed the wedding garment. Whereupon the Apostle advises us to put on the new man, which is made after God even Christ himself. Romans 13; Ephesians 4; Colossians 3. For Christ covers not only our privy parts, but all the filthiness also of the soul, he adorns and beautifies us, and drives from us all injury, and all evil. And we defile this garment, when neither in faith nor in holiness of life we do answer to our profession. For Christ is our garment, and Christianity, sincere faith, and holiness of life are our apparel: and even faith and our conversation is our garment. Forasmuch therefore as the Sardinians were of a sincere faith, and incorrupt manners, they are said to have kept their garments clean and undefiled. The Lord also gives now a reward unto virtue. And they shall walk with me, says he, in white array. These excellent things verily doth he rehearse to retain the Sardinians in their duty, to nourish them to greater things and to move other also to sincerity and integrity. Saints walk with Christ in white array, that is to say, have fruition of the same glory, wherein we believe Christ to shine. For he desires his father, that he will grant to the faithful, that wherever he is, they may be with him, and see his glory, &c., in John 17. And with Jerome, Augustine, Irenaeus, and Dionysius, in the transformation or clarifying, the face of Christ appeared bright like the sun, his apparel and rest of his body as light. So appeared Christ unto John in the first chapter of this book, clothed in white array. Now therefore says he, the godly that have not defiled their garment, shall accompany me, having put on light also. He adds another thing, for they be worthy. This is the greatest praise, when the Captain says, that the soldier is worthy of honour and glory. The greatest shame or ignominy is, when it is said, thou art unworthy. The first kind of speech shows him to be most excellent in all kind of virtue, which is said to be worthy of eternal light; by the latter is signified, that he which is accounted unworthy of a good and excellent thing, is marvelous negligent and ungracious. But here we need not to reason of the merit and desert of worthiness. God pronounces his to be worthy of glory; the godly refer all the goodness that is in them unto grace, and still complain of their unworthiness. Not to reprove God of lying, but to praise and commend the excellent goodness that is in him, acknowledging in deed that he rewards good works, and dignifies the worthiness of saints: but they are nothing proud hereof, but acknowledge all this to come of grace. This appears in the doctrine of the Gospel, Luke 17; Matthew 25, where saints commended of God for the works of mercy, seem to acknowledge nothing thereof.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original states "The use of apparel invented of God him self, and showed to our forefathers..." The draft changes this to "The use of apparel invented of God himself, and showed to our forefathers, *has* this chief property..." The original phrasing allows for a broader interpretation of the origin of clothing; the draft's phrasing implies a more direct, prescriptive divine invention.
2. **HALLUCINATION:** The draft adds "and Dionysius" to the list of authorities following Matthew. Dionysius is not mentioned in the original.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** The original uses "Hierom," "Austen," and "Ireney." The draft correctly modernizes these to "Jerome," "Augustine," and "Irenaeus."
[FINAL]
But that same excellent thing is chiefly to be observed, that although they were but few good, yet nevertheless the Lord commends and extols those few, doubtless for the example and imitation of all other churches. The words in deed are short, but the praise most ample and large. That they had not defiled their garments: which is as much as if he had said, you have not polluted your souls with strange opinions or spots of heresy. For you have remained sincere in the true faith: your bodies also, and the whole conversation of your life, you have not defiled with filthy lusts, with fleshly pleasures and voluptuousness. Doubtless this is the greatest praise and most certain sign of perfect godliness: wherewith I would wish that more of us were marked. But the manner of speech here requires also an exposition. The allegory of garments is often and much used in holy scripture. The use of apparel invented of God and showed to our forefathers, to hide the privy parts of our body, to beautify and set forth the body, and keep of heat and cold. Forasmuch therefore as the Sardinians were of a sincere faith, and incorrupt manners, they are said to have kept their garments clean and undefiled. The Lord also gives now a reward unto virtue. Not to reprove God of lying, but to praise and commend the excellent goodness that is in him, acknowledging in deed that he rewards good works, and dignifies the worthiness of saints: but they are nothing proud hereof, but acknowledge all this to come of grace. [ORIGINAL]
How be it he declares more at large the most ample promeſſes of God,[note 16.8] whereby he may not only reteyne in their duty the Saints and undefiled Sardenſians, but might also reduce all others that go astray at all times into the way of repentance, integrity, and holiness. And three things he promises: first in deed, white apparel, that is to say, gloryfiyng, and light everlasting, and the gloriouſe company of Christ, whereof I have spoken already. Secondly, and I will not, says he, put out his name out of the book of life. For like as Cities have books, wherein the names of their Citizens are written:[note 16.9] right so is God in the scriptures said [illegible] (after the manner of men) to have a book of life, or of his elect. What that book is, and whose name is red in the same, none of us can tell, sins none hath looked therein. We must learn of the scriptures, who be the citezes of the kingdom of God. For that their names be written in the book of life no man need doubt. And S. John says: so many as have believed, he hath given them power, that they may be made the children of God. S. Paull says: He that hath not the spirit of Christ, he is none of his. And the spirit cries in the minds of the godly, Abba father. The same Apostle says: God hath predestined us, that he might adopt us for his children, through Jesus Christ. Moreover: he hath chosen us in Christ, before the foudations of the world were laid. Therefore are all beleuers written in that number celestial. Who so ever therefore believe not, or persevere not in the faith, either they are not written in the book of life, or else they be put out again of the book of life. Finally the son acknowledges the beleuers and such as persevere in the true faith, before his heavenly father, and his Angles. And here he repeteth theuangelicall doctrine out of the 10. Chapter. of S. Matthew. and 8. of S. Mark. And doubteles it is a great matter in that universal judgment, to be known of the son of God, of the high judge, to be saluted and frendely spoke to of him, and that to our great praise. If any Prince would in a great assemble of people know thee, yea imbraſe and comende thee, howe happy and fortunate would thou think thy self? But then shall imbraſe thee the very son of God, king of kings, and lord of lords. Let us think of these things in time, and amend our manners.
[DRAFT]
Nevertheless, he declares at length the most abundant promises of God,[note 16.8] whereby he may not only retain in their duty the saints and undefiled Sardinians, but might also restore all others who go astray at all times into the way of repentance, integrity, and holiness. And three things he promises: first, indeed, white apparel, that is to say, glorifying and everlasting light, and the glorious company of Christ, of which I have spoken already. Secondly, and I will not put out his name out of the book of life. For just as cities have books wherein the names of their citizens are written,[note 16.9] so too is God in the scriptures said to have, after the manner of men, a book of life, or of his elect. What that book is, and whose name is written therein, none of us can tell, since none has looked therein. We must learn from the scriptures who are the citizens of the kingdom of God. For that their names are written in the book of life, no one need doubt. And Saint John says: those who have believed, he has given them power that they may be made the children of God. Saint Paul says: he who does not have the spirit of Christ is not his. And the spirit cries in the minds of the godly, “Abba, Father.” The same apostle says: God has predestined us that he might adopt us as his children through Jesus Christ. Moreover: he has chosen us in Christ before the foundations of the world were laid. Therefore, all believers are written in that celestial number. Whoever therefore does not believe, or does not persevere in the faith, either they are not written in the book of life, or else they are put out again from the book of life. Finally, the Son acknowledges the believers and those who persevere in the true faith before his heavenly Father and his angels. And here he repeats the evangelical doctrine from the 10th chapter of Saint Matthew and the 8th of Saint Mark. And it is undoubtedly a great matter in that universal judgment to be known by the Son of God, by the high judge, to be greeted and spoken to friendly by him, and that to our great praise. If any prince would in a great assembly of people know you, yes, embrace and commend you, how happy and fortunate would you think yourself? But then the very Son of God, king of kings, and lord of lords, will embrace you. Let us think of these things in time, and amend our manners.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "Sardenſians" is changed to "Sardinians." While geographically related, the original likely refers to a specific heretical sect. This modernization loses that nuance and potentially misrepresents the original meaning.
2. **PROPER NOUNS:** "S. John" and "S. Paull" are changed to "Saint John" and "Saint Paul." While not incorrect, the original abbreviation was common and stylistic. The modernization alters the original's tone.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The phrase "sins none hath looked therein" is changed to "since none has looked therein." The original phrasing implies a theological consequence of looking into the book of life (sin), which is lost in the modernized version.
[FINAL]
Nevertheless, he declares at length the most abundant promises of God,[note 16.8] whereby he may not only retain in their duty the saints and undefiled Sardenſians, but might also restore all others who go astray at all times into the way of repentance, integrity, and holiness. And three things he promises: first, indeed, white apparel, that is to say, glorifying and everlasting light, and the glorious company of Christ, of which I have spoken already. Secondly, and I will not put out his name out of the book of life. For just as cities have books wherein the names of their citizens are written,[note 16.9] so too is God in the scriptures said to have, after the manner of men, a book of life, or of his elect. What that book is, and whose name is written therein, none of us can tell, sin none hath looked therein. We must learn from the scriptures who are the citizens of the kingdom of God. For that their names are written in the book of life, no one need doubt. And S. John says: those who have believed, he has given them power that they may be made the children of God. S. Paull says: he who does not have the spirit of Christ is not his. And the spirit cries in the minds of the godly, “Abba, Father.” The same apostle says: God has predestined us that he might adopt us as his children through Jesus Christ. Moreover: he has chosen us in Christ before the foundations of the world were laid. Therefore, all believers are written in that celestial number. Whoever therefore does not believe, or does not persevere in the faith, either they are not written in the book of life, or else they are put out again from the book of life. Finally, the Son acknowledges the believers and those who persevere in the true faith before his heavenly Father and his angels. And here he repeats the evangelical doctrine from the 10th chapter of S. Matthew and the 8th of S. Mark. And it is undoubtedly a great matter in that universal judgment to be known by the Son of God, by the high judge, to be greeted and spoken to friendly by him, and that to our great praise. If any prince would in a great assembly of people know you, yes, embrace and commend you, how happy and fortunate would you think yourself? But then the very Son of God, king of kings, and lord of lords, will embrace you. Let us think of these things in time, and amend our manners.
---

For that all these things appertain to us, that last and wounted acclamation of S. John proves: let him that has ears, hear, &c. Whereof we have spoken else where. To the Lord be praise and glory.
[DRAFT]
For all these things pertain to us, the final and resounding declaration of John the Apostle confirms it: “Let him who has ears, hear,” and so on. We have spoken of this elsewhere. To the Lord be praise and glory.

3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "let him that has ears, hear, &c." is deliberately vague, inviting spiritual understanding. The draft's "Let him who has ears, hear,” and so on" diminishes this ambiguity by making it more literal.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms are present in this short passage.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: John is correctly modernized.
