[ORIGINAL]
[note 17.1]In all other congregations the Lord at the least found some fault, in the only church of Philadelphia he blame[illegible]h nothing: not that any man is found in this flesh so perfect, that he hath not need of the grace of God. For David cries out: enter not Lord, into judgment with thy servant, for no man living shall be justified in thy sight. But S. John and S. Paull also make all me subject to sin: which thing also S. Austen discourses learnedly against the Pelagians. Therefore that blames nothing in this congregation, it is not to be understand, as though it were not defiled with daily faults: but therefore he imputeth nothing, for because the sincerity and integrity of faith covers and hides what vice so ever there be. For there is no codemnation to them that are graffed in Christ Jesus. And albeit that other churches have also the right faith, yet this excells especially, &c. It might be referred chiefly to the Bishop of the same Church.
[DRAFT]
In all other congregations the Lord at the least found some fault, but in the only church of Philadelphia he finds nothing to blame: not that any man is found in this flesh so perfect that he does not need the grace of God. For David cries out, “Enter not, Lord, into judgment with your servant, for no living man shall be justified in your sight.” But Saint John and Saint Paul also acknowledge all men to be subject to sin, which thing Saint Augustine also discusses learnedly against the Pelagians. Therefore, that he finds nothing to blame in this congregation is not to be understood as though it were not defiled with daily faults, but rather he imputes nothing because the sincerity and integrity of faith covers and hides whatever vice there may be. For there is no condemnation to those who are grafted in Christ Jesus. And although other churches also have the right faith, this one excels especially, &c. It might be referred chiefly to the Bishop of the same church.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "blame[illegible]h nothing" changed to "he finds nothing to blame." While functionally similar, the original phrasing carries a nuance of active searching and finding fault, which the draft loses.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "acknowledge all men to be subject to sin" is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. The original simply states they "make all me subject to sin."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "it is not to be understand" is somewhat archaic and allows for a broader interpretation of what the lack of blame signifies. The draft's "is not to be understood" is more direct and less open to alternative readings.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals of theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Austen → Augustine is correct. Hierom and Ireney are not present.
[FINAL]

For David cries out: “Enter not, Lord, into judgment with thy servant, for no man living shall be justified in thy sight.” But S. John and S. Paull also make all me subject to sin: which thing also S. Austen discourses learnedly against the Pelagians. [ORIGINAL]
And the lord herin follows the same order, which we see he hath followed in others. For it is one and the same kind of doctrine with all churches and in all times. First therefore is showed, unto whom the epistle is written or dedicated: to the pastor and whole cogregation of Philadelphia.[note 17.2] Philadelphia was a city of Lydia, neither very famous, nor yet obscure. We read how it hath be oft shake with earthquakes, and repared again. Strabo mentions thereof in the 12. book of Geography, and so have other authors also. Yet it made it self famous by virtues. After is the Lord Christ signified to be Author of this epistle, who at other times also hath told S. John what he should write.

[ORIGINAL]
And to Christ are attributed three things or rather Christ attributeth three things to him self, that he is holy, true, and hath the key of David: The which he hath borrowed of the Image of the first Chapter. Christ is holy,[note 17.3] because he is pure and clean from all filthines, and from all vnrightuouſnes, very God, a co ſuming fire, doyng no man any wrong, having nothing at all that may be blamed. For to him the Seraphin ſayng rightly, holy, holy, holy, Lord God of Sabbath: Isaiah. 7.[note 17.4] Christ is also the holy one of the Saints, a sanctification I say that ſanctifieth all, that be sanctified. The same loves holiness in saints. Christ therefore is most truly called. Antichrist the Pope hath taken upon him this title, and so filthy sits on this beast, as if you should call a privy or a Iakes a Rosier. Spit upon that vile and filthy beast, which suffers him self to be called the most holy father, and worship Christ the holy one of all holy, unless you had rather understand by that holiness not every holiness, but pope holiness that is to wit, stinking and swimming full of all abominations. Christ is like wise called true,[note 17.5] because he is eternal and faithful, evermore constant and incorrupt. He can neither diſceiue nor be diſceiued. The same most constantly kepeth his promeſſes. All his words be undoubted and true: Albeit that flesh, that can abide no delay, begin many times to doubt, yet no one point or jot of them falls away. The truth of the Lord endures for ever. Thou stand upon a most sure foundation, if thou lean unto Christ, which in the 14. of John also calls him self the verity.
[DRAFT]
And to Christ are attributed three things, or rather Christ attributes three things to himself: that he is holy, true, and has the key of David. He has borrowed these from the imagery of the first chapter. Christ is holy because he is pure and clean from all defilement and all unrighteousness, very God, a consuming fire, doing no wrong to anyone, having nothing at all that may be blamed. For the Seraphim rightly say, “Holy, holy, holy, Lord God of Hosts,” Isaiah 6:3. Christ is also the holiness of the saints, a sanctification that sanctifies all who are sanctified. He loves holiness in saints. Therefore, Christ is most truly called holy. Antichrist, the Pope, has taken upon himself this title, and sits on this beast so filthily, as if you were to call a privy or a latrine a rose garden. Spit upon that vile and filthy beast, which allows itself to be called the most holy father, and worship Christ, the holy one of all holy, unless you understand by that holiness not every holiness, but papal holiness—that is to say, stinking and swimming full of all abominations. Christ is likewise called true because he is eternal and faithful, evermore constant and incorruptible. He can neither deceive nor be deceived. He steadfastly keeps his promises. All his words are without doubt and true. Although flesh, which cannot endure delay, often begins to doubt, yet not one point or jot of them falls away. The truth of the Lord endures forever. You stand upon a most sure foundation if you lean unto Christ, who in John 14 also calls himself the truth.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **Meaning Drift:** "The which he hath borrowed of the Image of the first Chapter" is rendered as "He has borrowed these from the imagery of the first chapter." This shifts the meaning from a direct textual borrowing to a more general inspiration or representation.
2. **Added Interpretation:** "Christ is also the holiness of the saints" is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. The original says "a sanctification I say that sanctifieth all, that be sanctified," which is a statement about Christ's role in sanctification, not an equation of Christ *with* holiness.
3. **Lost Ambiguity:** The original's phrasing "unless you had rather understand by that holiness not every holiness, but pope holiness" is softened to "unless you understand by that holiness not every holiness, but papal holiness." The original implies a choice ("had rather"), which is lost in the draft.
4. **Hallucination:** The phrase "Lord God of Hosts" replaces "Lord God of Sabbath" (Isaiah 7). This is an error; the reference is to Isaiah 6:3, not Isaiah 7.
5. **Proper Nouns:** "Iakes" is incorrectly modernized to "latrine." "Iakes" is a variant spelling of "Jakes," a derogatory term for a privy or toilet, and should be retained to preserve the original's tone and meaning.
[FINAL]
And to Christ are attributed three things, or rather Christ attributes three things to himself: that he is holy, true, and has the key of David. The which he hath borrowed of the Image of the first Chapter. Christ is holy, because he is pure and clean from all filthiness and from all unrighteousness, very God, a consuming fire, doing no man any wrong, having nothing at all that may be blamed. For the Seraphim rightly say, “Holy, holy, holy, Lord God of Sabbath,” Isaiah 6:3. [note 17.4] Christ is also a sanctification, I say that sanctifieth all that be sanctified. The same loves holiness in saints. Christ therefore is most truly called holy. Antichrist, the Pope, hath taken upon him this title, and so filthy sits on this beast, as if you were to call a privy or a Jakes a Rosier. Spit upon that vile and filthy beast, which suffers him self to be called the most holy father, and worship Christ the holy one of all holy, unless you had rather understand by that holiness not every holiness, but pope holiness that is to wit, stinking and swimming full of all abominations. Christ is like wise called true, because he is eternal and faithful, evermore constant and incorrupt. He can neither deceive nor be deceived. The same most constantly keeps his promises. All his words be undoubted and true. Albeit that flesh, that can abide no delay, begin many times to doubt, yet no one point or jot of them falls away. The truth of the Lord endures for ever. Thou stand upon a most sure foundation, if thou lean unto Christ, which in the 14. of John also calls him self the verity.

[ORIGINAL]
And he commends so the perseverance in faith in this congregation,[note 17.8] that he signifies with all, that the same also did proceed of the grace of Christ. Thou haste, says he, little power, and as it were no force and strength, which this world regards, as power, riches, wordly wisdom, lucky success, plenty of friends, and such other like things. Therefore thou can attribute nothing to thy self, nothing to thine own strength, not so much as this that thou art a church, and that the verity of the gospel is freely preached with thee.[note 17.9] For I set open this door. And by my strength I keep open the same, that no man can shut the same door: to wit the preachinge and grace ones granted, by any means to prohibit, let, or take away. To open the door, is a common phrase of speaking, used of the Apostle, in the 1. to the Corinth. 16. and the 2. Corinth. the 2. He opens the door, which gives an occasion, and prepares the way to enter in. By the word therefore was opened the door of life. The faithful might enter in: the infidels could not stop this way. For the hand of Christ held the door open. And these things in deed do declare,[note 17.10] whereof it is that in cities, towns, and villages not greatly furnished with any force or power, the course of the gospel proceeds with so lucky success: And where many go about by layng waite, crafts and policies, threatenings and persecutions to shut the door, they ca not. These things are not done through our cunning and wisdom, but of the grace of God.
[DRAFT]
And he praises the perseverance in faith within this congregation, so that he indicates to all that it proceeds from the grace of Christ. “You have little power, and seemingly no force or strength, which the world regards as power, riches, worldly wisdom, fortunate success, plenty of friends, and similar things. Therefore, you can attribute nothing to yourself, nothing to your own strength, not even this that you are a church, and that the truth of the gospel is freely preached among you. For I have opened this door. And by my strength I keep the same door open, so that no one can shut it—that is, the preaching and grace once granted, by any means to prohibit, hinder, or take away. To open the door is a common phrase used by the Apostle in 1 Corinthians 16 and 2 Corinthians 2. He opens the door, which provides an opportunity and prepares the way to enter. Therefore, by the word the door of life was opened. The faithful could enter in; the unbelievers could not stop this way. For the hand of Christ held the door open. And these things indeed declare, which is why in cities, towns, and villages not greatly furnished with any force or power, the course of the gospel proceeds with such fortunate success. And where many attempt by waiting, schemes, and policies, threats, and persecutions to shut the door, they cannot. These things are not done through our cunning and wisdom, but by the grace of God.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "lucky success" changed to "fortunate success." While semantically close, "lucky" carries a nuance of chance and divine favor absent in "fortunate," potentially softening the original's theological emphasis.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "let, or take away" is softened to "hinder or take away." "Let" implies permission, a crucial element of the original's argument about divine allowance, now lost.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus."
4. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "Therefore, by the word the door of life was opened" is not present in the original. It is an interpretive addition.
[FINAL]

Therefore, you can attribute nothing to yourself, nothing to your own strength, not even this that you are a church, and that the truth of the gospel is freely preached among you. For I have opened this door. And by my strength I keep the same door open, so that no one can shut it—that is, the preaching and grace once granted, by any means to prohibit or take away. How be it if any man list to understand those things and such as follow herafter peculiarly of the pastor or bishop of the church, I will not be against it. The virtue of Christ kept him.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 18.1]An excellent virtue is commended in the congregation of Philadelphia, that they have kept the word of Christ, not every word, but the word of Christ, and have not denied it. And he hath begone to rehearse most large rewards, which both he hath given to this church, and is also ready to give to any other like in the zeal of godly religion. For we are allured by rewards.
[DRAFT]
A commendable virtue is praised in the congregation of Philadelphia: that they have kept the word of Christ, not every word, but the word of Christ, and have not denied it. And he has begun to recount great rewards, which both he has given to this church, and is also ready to give to any other like in the zeal of godly religion. For we are drawn by rewards.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "Allured" changed to "drawn." While similar, "allured" carries a connotation of temptation or attraction that "drawn" lacks, subtly altering the motivation presented.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "we are allured by rewards" is a somewhat ambiguous statement about human motivation. The draft's "we are drawn by rewards" is less evocative and removes some of the original's potential for multiple interpretations regarding the nature of that attraction.
[FINAL]
A commendable virtue is praised in the congregation of Philadelphia: that they have kept the word of Christ, not every word, but the word of Christ, and have not denied it. And he has begun to recount great rewards, which both he has given to this church, and is also ready to give to any other like in the zeal of godly religion. For we are allured by rewards.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 18.3]Than follows an other benefit of God. Thenemies of God shall know and find, that the church, and every mebre of the same be the well beloved children of God. Thenemies of the church suppose, the faithful to be wicked, gods enemies, heretics, churchrobbers, hated of god, and unworthy to live. But they shall understand that nothing is derer to God than the church, as for the which he gave his son, which he chose also for his spouse, and hath made partaker of his kingdom.
[DRAFT]
Then follows another benefit of God. God’s enemies shall know and find that the church, and every member of it, are God’s well-beloved children. God’s enemies suppose the faithful to be wicked, enemies of God, heretics, church robbers, hated by God, and unworthy to live. But they shall understand that nothing is dearer to God than the church, for whom he gave his son, whom he also chose as his spouse, and has made partaker of his kingdom.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Than" to "Then" is a minor change, but "Than follows" is slightly more formal and emphasizes sequence. The draft loses a touch of the original's slightly archaic tone.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None apparent.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None apparent.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]
Then follows another benefit of God. God’s enemies shall know and find that the church, and every member of the same, are the well-beloved children of God. God’s enemies suppose the faithful to be wicked, enemies of God, heretics, church robbers, hated of God, and unworthy to live. But they shall understand that nothing is dearer to God than the church, for the which he gave his son, whom he also chose as his spouse, and hath made partaker of his kingdom.

[ORIGINAL]
Again he comendeth the perseverance of the faithful in the true religion. Thou hast kept, says he,[note 18.5] the word of my patience. The word of patience is the Gospel of eternal salvation: which is otherwise called of S. Paull the word of the cross, and that for two considerations. First for because he describes the cross and patience of Christ whereby we are saved. And again he persuades us also to bear the cross, and patiently to suffer with Christ, Matthew. 16.2. Timoth. 2. Neither must any man look for any perseverance of him that is impatient. The Lord says in the 12. of Luke, in your patience you shall possess your souls. Therefore hath either the pastor or the church of Philadelphia kept the word of patience, to wit in retaining in their hearts the patience of Christ through faith, and in showing patience in words or sayings, and ſuſteyning much travel in body. Which in deed is the best way to keep churches safe and sound, and every one of the faithful. Let them keep, I say, the word of Christ his patience, and the rest commit to the Lord.
[DRAFT]
Again he commends the perseverance of the faithful in the true religion. “You have kept,” he says, [note 18.5], “the word of my patience.” The word of patience is the Gospel of eternal salvation, which Paul also calls the word of the cross, and that for two considerations. First, because he describes the cross and patience of Christ by which we are saved. And again, he urges us also to bear the cross and patiently to suffer with Christ, Matthew 16:24; 2 Timothy 2:3. Neither must any man expect perseverance from one who is impatient. The Lord says in Luke 12:36, “In your patience you shall possess your souls.” Therefore, either the pastor or the church of Philadelphia has kept the word of patience, namely, by retaining in their hearts the patience of Christ through faith, and by showing patience in words and sayings, and sustaining much hardship in body. This indeed is the best way to keep churches safe and sound, and every one of the faithful. Let them keep, I say, the word of Christ’s patience, and the rest commit to the Lord.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "ſuſteyning much travel in body" changed to "sustaining much hardship in body." "Travel" implies journey or labor, which could have a broader spiritual connotation. "Hardship" is more narrowly physical.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of semicolons and rephrasing of "Matthew. 16.2. Timoth. 2" to "Matthew 16:24; 2 Timothy 2:3" adds a layer of interpretation, suggesting a direct connection between the verses that might not have been explicitly intended in the original. Verse 16:24 is a more specific reference than simply "Matthew 16."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "the rest commit to the Lord" is open to interpretation regarding what "the rest" refers to. The draft maintains this, which is appropriate.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Hierom→Jerome, Austen→Augustine, Ireney→Irenaeus are not present in this passage. The modernization of "Paull" to "Paul" is correct.
[FINAL]
Again he commends the perseverance of the faithful in the true religion. “You have kept,” he says, [note 18.5], “the word of my patience.” The word of patience is the Gospel of eternal salvation, which Paul also calls the word of the cross, and that for two considerations. First, because he describes the cross and patience of Christ whereby we are saved. And again, he urges us also to bear the cross and patiently to suffer with Christ, Matthew 16:24; 2 Timothy 2:3. Neither must any man expect perseverance from one who is impatient. The Lord says in Luke 12:36, “In your patience you shall possess your souls.” Therefore, either the pastor or the church of Philadelphia has kept the word of patience, namely, by retaining in their hearts the patience of Christ through faith, and by showing patience in words and sayings, and sustaining much travel in body. This indeed is the best way to keep churches safe and sound, and every one of the faithful. Let them keep, I say, the word of Christ’s patience, and the rest commit to the Lord.

[ORIGINAL]
For it follows: And I will keep thee again from the hour of temptation, &c.[note 18.6] The hour of temptation is expou ded two ways. For either he speaks of heresies and of heretics, by whose talk, and crafty iuggelynge, leudenes, and diſceiptfulnes is tempted the faith, simplicity, and integrity of the faytheful. Whereof the Lord treats much in the 13. Chapter of Deuteronomie. Or else he speaks verily of the persecutions, which the emperors of Rome have inflicted, among whom Traiane a most mighty Prince set forth sore proclamations against the Christians. Whereof Plinie also made mention in the 10. book of Epistles, the ho dreth and one. But Christ preserved the church of Philadelphia, and kepeth also at this day the faithful by his word and power in the perils of heretics and heresies, and finally of persecutions also: so that the faithful may stand sure in all controversies and receive nothing of heretics that is strange from gods word, and also give no place in persecutions. Christ causes many times that the burden of persecution presses not so heavily. Therefore let us always be constant in gods word, and permit the defence to our Lord God. He will not neglect us, &c.
[DRAFT]
For it follows: And I will keep you again from the hour of temptation, &c.[note 18.6] The hour of temptation is explained in two ways. For either he speaks of heresies and heretics, by whose talk, and crafty juggling, lewdness, and deceptiveness tempts the faith, simplicity, and integrity of the faithful. Of this, the Lord treats much in the 13th Chapter of Deuteronomy. Or else he speaks truly of the persecutions which the Roman emperors have inflicted, among whom Trajan, a most mighty prince, issued severe proclamations against the Christians. Of this, Pliny also made mention in the 10th book of Epistles, the hundred and one. But Christ preserved the church of Philadelphia, and keeps also at this day the faithful by his word and power in the perils of heretics and heresies, and finally of persecutions also: so that the faithful may stand firm in all controversies and receive nothing of heretics that is strange from God’s word, and also give no place in persecutions. Christ causes many times that the burden of persecution presses not so heavily. Therefore let us always be steadfast in God’s word, and permit the defense to our Lord God. He will not neglect us, &c.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "iuggelynge" modernized to "juggling" loses some of the original's sense of deceptive trickery and subtle manipulation. "leudenes" modernized to "lewdness" is acceptable but less nuanced.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None apparent.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None apparent.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals.
5. HALLUCINATION: None apparent.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "Austen" should be "Augustine" (not present in this passage, but a general point). "Plinie" modernized to "Pliny" is correct. "Traiane" modernized to "Trajan" is correct.
[FINAL]
For it follows: And I will keep you again from the hour of temptation, &c.[note 18.6] The hour of temptation is explained in two ways. For either he speaks of heresies and heretics, by whose talk, and crafty jugglery, lewdness, and deceptiveness tempts the faith, simplicity, and integrity of the faithful. Of this, the Lord treats much in the 13th Chapter of Deuteronomy. Or else he speaks truly of the persecutions which the Roman emperors have inflicted, among whom Trajan, a most mighty prince, issued severe proclamations against the Christians. Of this, Pliny also made mention in the 10th book of Epistles, the hundred and one. But Christ preserved the church of Philadelphia, and keeps also at this day the faithful by his word and power in the perils of heretics and heresies, and finally of persecutions also: so that the faithful may stand firm in all controversies and receive nothing of heretics that is strange from God’s word, and also give no place in persecutions. Christ causes many times that the burden of persecution presses not so heavily. Therefore let us always be steadfast in God’s word, and permit the defense to our Lord God. He will not neglect us, &c.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 18.7]But for as much as in temptations and afflictions the Lord seems many times to our flesh to tarry overlong, and in manner to neglect his: for we say the Lord prevents, and adds, behold I come shortly. Shortly, I say, that is to say in time: not to late nor to hastily. The which we say, neither to soon nor to late, but in dew time and season. If the lord therefore shall seem to be over slow, despair not: for he will come timely enough, when he shall see it good. Doe not thou prescribe unto him the manner and mean of deliverance, but abide the Lords leisure. Read what goodly and wholesome things S. Paull hath written concerning this matter in the end of the 10. chapter to the Hebrews, where a place also out of the 2. Chapter. of Abachuc is alleged.
[DRAFT]
But since in trials and afflictions the Lord often appears to our flesh to delay, and to seem to neglect his own work: for we say the Lord anticipates, and adds, “Behold, I come shortly.” Shortly, I say, that is to say, in time: neither too late nor too hastily. This we say, neither too soon nor too late, but in due time and season. If the Lord therefore seems to be slow, do not despair: for he will come in due time, when he sees it fitting. Do not you prescribe to him the manner and means of deliverance, but wait for the Lord’s appointed time. Read what excellent and wholesome things Saint Paul has written concerning this matter in the end of the tenth chapter to the Hebrews, where a passage also from the second chapter of Habakkuk is cited.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "prevents" is altered to "anticipates." While related, "prevents" in this context implies foreknowledge and proactive action, a nuance lost in "anticipates."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "wait for the Lord’s appointed time" is an interpretation not explicitly present in the original "abide the Lords leisure." The original emphasizes patience; the draft adds a sense of a preordained schedule.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "in dew time and season" is softened to "in due time and season," losing some of the poetic and slightly archaic quality.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "S. Paull" is correctly modernized to "Saint Paul," "Abachuc" to "Habakkuk."
[FINAL]
But since in trials and afflictions the Lord often appears to our flesh to delay, and to seem to neglect his: for we say the Lord prevents, and adds, “Behold, I come shortly.” Shortly, I say, that is to say in time: not too late nor too hastily. The which we say, neither too soon nor too late, but in dew time and season. If the Lord therefore shall seem to be over slow, despair not: for he will come timely enough, when he shall see it good. Do not thou prescribe unto him the manner and mean of deliverance, but abide the Lords leisure. Read what goodly and wholesome things Saint Paul has written concerning this matter in the end of the tenth chapter to the Hebrews, where a place also out of the second chapter of Habakkuk is alleged.

[ORIGINAL]
Conquerors and worthy of the Empire, are crowned. The virgin loses her crown, that is defiled. Therefore heretics, false prophets, and seducers take away the crown, what time they seduce and corrupt: therefore says the Lord: Thou haste gotten honour and glory, see that no man take it from thee. So we read that S. Paull spake in the 2. to the Coloſs. Let no man take from you the victory. In the 18. of Ezekiel the Lord testifies, that he will not impute rightuouſnes to the just, in case he forsake and leave his rightouſenes let us pray therefore, that we may evermore persevere in the word of the Lord.
[DRAFT]
Conquerors and those worthy of the Empire are crowned. The virgin loses her crown when she is defiled. Therefore, heretics, false prophets, and seducers take away the crown when they seduce and corrupt. Thus says the Lord: “You have gained honor and glory; see that no one takes it from you.” So we read that Paul spoke in the second letter to the Colossians: “Let no one take away the victory from you.” In the eighteenth chapter of Ezekiel, the Lord testifies that he will not impute righteousness to the just if he forsakes and abandons his righteousness. Let us pray, therefore, that we may always persevere in the word of the Lord.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "Forsake and leave" changed to "forsakes and abandons" subtly alters the tone; "leave" implies a more passive abandonment than "abandons."
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "rightuousnes" is plural, suggesting a broader concept than the singular "righteousness" in the draft. This removes a potential nuance regarding multiple aspects of righteousness.
PROPER NOUNS: "S. Paull" modernized to "Paul" is acceptable, but the lack of a full stop after "Paull" in the original is lost.
[FINAL]
Conquerors and those worthy of the Empire are crowned. The virgin loses her crown when she is defiled. Therefore, heretics, false prophets, and seducers take away the crown when they seduce and corrupt. Thus says the Lord: “You have gained honor and glory; see that no one takes it from you.” So we read that S. Paull spake in the second letter to the Colossians: “Let no one take away the victory from you.” In the eighteenth chapter of Ezekiel, the Lord testifies that he will not impute righteousness to the just, in case he forsake and leave his rightuousnes. Let us pray, therefore, that we may always persevere in the word of the Lord.

[ORIGINAL]
Our Lord proceeds in raccomptynge much more ample rewards, which he would give to them that overcome: And so he tempers his words, that we may easily perceive, this promise not only to appertain to the congregation of Philadelphia, but to all the Churches in the whole world, yea and to every of the faythefull. And as we have oftentimes repeated already (for I am not aſſhamed to repeat, saying that the Lord him self so greatly urges the victory) again we say, that those things are promised not to such as fight lightly or negligently (for diverse fight,[note 19.1] and by and by run away) but to those that overcome and persevere to the end. For our life is a warfare upon earth: which Iob also hath confessed. The soldier hath a sure purpose to overcome his enemies. Our enemies are, the devil, the world, and the flesh. Against these we must earnestly fight: of none other intent, but that we may overcome. The Apostle in the 6. to the Epheſ. described the armour of the faithful. In victory the Saints consider ſinceries and integrity, that we lose nothing of the verity known: but let us reteyne the pure word of God, and sincere faith, and let us keep our bodies and our souls clean from all pollution, and that to our lives end. He propounds most ample rewards by promise.[note 19.2] Whereby he alludeth to the manner of Greeks and Romans, who decreed Images to such as deserved well of the common welthe in the which also they wrote their virtues, for whose cause they were set up either in the court or market place or else where. They seemed by this mean to deliver to their posterity as it were by hand the glory of their elders, which they made also as it were everlasting.[note 19.3] Otherwise the use of this vocable Column, or pillar is diverse. Ieremie is called of God a pillar for his constancy. The Apostles are called of S. Paull in the 2. to the Galath. chief pillars, for their excellency, and that the churches leaned upon them, for the preaching of the verity. The church it self also is called the pillar and base of verity, for as much as it is grouded upon the sure rock Christ. In the Temple of Salomo stood two columns or pillars, figures of the everlasting kingdom of Christ,[note 19.4] and of the holy church. In this place a pillar is taken for a man, in glory and beauty excellent. For he says not that he will erecte a pillar for a godly man: but I will, says he, make him a pillar, that is to say, I will beautify him with honours and glory everlasting.
[DRAFT]
Our Lord proceeds in recounting much more ample rewards, which he would give to those who overcome: and so he tempers his words, that we may easily perceive that this promise not only pertains to the congregation of Philadelphia, but to all the churches in the whole world, yes, and to every of the faithful. And as we have oftentimes repeated already (for I am not ashamed to repeat, saying that the Lord himself so greatly urges the victory), again we say, that those things are promised not to such as fight lightly or negligently (for diverse fight, and by and by run away) but to those that overcome and persevere to the end. For our life is a warfare upon earth: which Job also has confessed. The soldier has a sure purpose to overcome his enemies. Our enemies are the devil, the world, and the flesh. Against these we must earnestly fight: of none other intent, but that we may overcome. The Apostle in the 6th to the Ephesians described the armor of the faithful. In victory the Saints consider sincerity and integrity, that we lose nothing of the verity known: but let us retain the pure word of God, and sincere faith, and let us keep our bodies and our souls clean from all pollution, and that to our lives’ end. He propounds most ample rewards by promise. Whereby he alludes to the manner of Greeks and Romans, who decreed images to those who deserved well of the common wealth, in which also they wrote their virtues, for whose cause they were set up either in the court or marketplace or else where. They seemed by this means to deliver to their posterity as it were by hand the glory of their elders, which they made also as it were everlasting. Otherwise the use of this vocable Column, or pillar is diverse. Jeremiah is called by God a pillar for his constancy. The Apostles are called by Paul in the 2nd to the Galatians chief pillars, for their excellency, and that the churches leaned upon them, for the preaching of the verity. The church itself also is called the pillar and base of verity, for as much as it is grounded upon the sure rock Christ. In the Temple of Solomon stood two columns or pillars, figures of the everlasting kingdom of Christ, and of the holy church. In this place a pillar is taken for a man, in glory and beauty excellent. For he says not that he will erect a pillar for a godly man: but I will, says he, make him a pillar, that is to say, I will beautify him with honors and glory everlasting.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "raccomptynge" is modernized to "recounting." While functionally similar, "raccomptynge" carries a sense of careful accounting or detailing, which "recounting" lacks. This subtle nuance is lost.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The phrase "to our lives end" is changed to "to our lives’ end.” The original’s phrasing is slightly ambiguous – does it refer to the end of one's life, or the end of a specific period? The addition of the possessive apostrophe removes this ambiguity, making it definitively about the end of one's life, which might not have been the original intent.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Austen" should be "Augustine."
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus."
[FINAL]
Our Lord proceeds in raccomptynge much more ample rewards, which he would give to those who overcome: and so he tempers his words, that we may easily perceive that this promise not only pertains to the congregation of Philadelphia, but to all the churches in the whole world, yes, and to every of the faithful. And as we have oftentimes repeated already (for I am not ashamed to repeat, saying that the Lord himself so greatly urges the victory), again we say, that those things are promised not to such as fight lightly or negligently (for diverse fight, and by and by run away) but to those that overcome and persevere to the end. For our life is a warfare upon earth: which Job also has confessed. The soldier has a sure purpose to overcome his enemies. Our enemies are the devil, the world, and the flesh. Against these we must earnestly fight: of none other intent, but that we may overcome. The Apostle in the 6th to the Ephesians described the armor of the faithful. In victory the Saints consider sincerity and integrity, that we lose nothing of the verity known: but let us retain the pure word of God, and sincere faith, and let us keep our bodies and our souls clean from all pollution, and that to our lives end. He propounds most ample rewards by promise. Whereby he alludes to the manner of Greeks and Romans, who decreed images to those who deserved well of the common wealth, in which also they wrote their virtues, for whose cause they were set up either in the court or marketplace or else where. They seemed by this means to deliver to their posterity as it were by hand the glory of their elders, which they made also as it were everlasting. Otherwise the use of this vocable Column, or pillar is diverse. Jeremiah is called by God a pillar for his constancy. The Apostles are called by Paul in the 2nd to the Galatians chief pillars, for their excellency, and that the churches leaned upon them, for the preaching of the verity. The church itself also is called the pillar and base of verity, for as much as it is grounded upon the sure rock Christ. In the Temple of Solomon stood two columns or pillars, figures of the everlasting kingdom of Christ, and of the holy church. In this place a pillar is taken for a man, in glory and beauty excellent. For he says not that he will erect a pillar for a godly man: but I will, says he, make him a pillar, that is to say, I will beautify him with honors and glory everlasting.

[ORIGINAL]
1. Petri. 1. And S. Paul the first to the Corinthians the iii. and iiii. There shall be more said of the new Jerusalem in thend of this book. But of these the understand, what is the church of Christ, the fellowship of the faithful, regenerated by the word of God. &c.
[DRAFT]
1 Peter 1:3. And Paul, in his first letter to the Corinthians, chapters 3 and 4, speaks further of the new Jerusalem toward the end of this book. But understand this: what is the church of Christ, the fellowship of the faithful, regenerated by the word of God. &c.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "There shall be more said" changed to "speaks further" subtly alters the tone; the original implies a future promise, while the draft suggests a recounting of what already exists.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: The addition of "in his first letter" is unnecessary and adds a layer of explanation not present in the original.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "in thend of this book" is somewhat vague, potentially referring to a specific section or a general conclusion. The draft's "toward the end of this book" is more precise and loses the original's ambiguity.
[FINAL]
1 Peter 1:3. And Paul, to the Corinthians, the first, the third and fourth. There shall be more said of the new Jerusalem in the end of this book. But understand, what is the church of Christ, the fellowship of the faithful, regenerated by the word of God. &c.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 19.11]Finally in them that overcome is written a new name, and that in deed the new name of Christ: Not only that they should be called Christians of Christ, but because the name is a brief description of every thing and nature, and a new name is promised: It follows that we should understand that men shall be renewed, chiefly by glorifiyng. He promises therefore a glorifiyng to the Godly. Whereof is spoke else where in the xvii. of S. Math. 1. Cor. xv. Phil. iii. And the first of John the iii. These most ample rewards the saints may verily look for, if they fight that they may overcome.
[DRAFT]
Finally, in those who overcome, it is written of a new name, and indeed the new name of Christ. Not only that they should be called Christians of Christ, but because the name is a brief description of every thing and nature, and a new name is promised: it follows that we should understand that men shall be renewed, chiefly by glorifying. He promises therefore a glorifying to the godly. Of this, there is spoken elsewhere in Matthew 17:1, 1 Corinthians 15, Philippians 3, and 1 John 3. These most ample rewards the saints may truly look for, if they fight that they may overcome.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "glorifiyng" to "glorifying" is a minor shift but subtly alters the archaic tone and potentially the intended emphasis on the act of glorifying itself.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "indeed the new name of Christ" is an interpretation not explicitly present in the original. The original simply states "a new name of Christ."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "a brief description of every thing and nature" is quite broad and potentially metaphorical. The draft, while not *incorrect*, loses some of this expansive ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
Finally, in those who overcome, it is written of a new name, and the new name of Christ. Not only that they should be called Christians of Christ, but because the name is a brief description of every thing and nature, and a new name is promised: it follows that we should understand that men shall be renewed, chiefly by glorifiyng. He promises therefore a glorifiyng to the godly. Of this, there is spoken elsewhere in Matthew 17:1, 1 Corinthians 15, Philippians 3, and 1 John 3. These most ample rewards the saints may truly look for, if they fight that they may overcome.

[ORIGINAL]
Hereunto is annexed the wonted acclamation, by the which both this doctrine is applied and communicated to all churches through out the world. And is declared that it came not of men as vain, but of the very spirit of God most true. This spirit the Lord grant us.
[DRAFT]
Hereunto is annexed the customary affirmation, by which both this doctrine is applied and communicated to all churches throughout the world. And it is declared that it did not come from men as a frivolous thing, but from the very spirit of God, most true. This spirit the Lord grant us.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "wonted acclamation" to "customary affirmation" subtly shifts the sense. "Wonted" implies a familiar, traditional greeting, while "customary" is more neutral and could suggest a formal declaration rather than a heartfelt expression.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "vain" to "frivolous thing" adds a layer of interpretation. "Vain" in 1561 could imply empty, deceptive, or lacking substance – a broader critique than simply "frivolous." The draft's choice narrows the potential meaning.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "came not of men as vain" leaves open the possibility that human involvement, however flawed, was present. The draft's "did not come from men as a frivolous thing" more strongly asserts a purely divine origin, removing a potential nuance.
[FINAL]
Hereunto is annexed the wonted acclamation, by which both this doctrine is applied and communicated to all churches throughout the world. And it is declared that it came not of men as vain, but of the very spirit of God most true. This spirit the Lord grant us.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 20.5]You shall find at this day many like, to whom this is common and ever in their mouth: I have learned both to be a goſpeller, and to be a soldier, to drink, to play the whoremonger, and live at pleasure. You shall find like churches, serving both Christ and Mammon, or merchandise, Bacchus, Venus, and God of battle. Both they and all these here are confuted, and are called to repentance. Which argues, that the mercy of God is greatest, not forsaking nor rejecting so corrupt churches, and men full of so great filthines.
[DRAFT]
You will find today many people who say this is common and is always on their lips: “I have learned to be both a Gospel follower and a soldier, to drink, to play the whoremonger, and to live at pleasure.” You will find churches like these, serving both Christ and Mammon, or commerce, Bacchus, Venus, and the god of battle. Both they and all these here are refuted, and are called to repentance. This demonstrates that God’s mercy is greatest, not abandoning or rejecting churches so corrupt, and people full of such great filthiness.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "goſpeller" is softened to "Gospel follower." While not entirely inaccurate, "goſpeller" implies a zealous advocate, a nuance lost in the draft.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of quotation marks around the list of behaviors ("I have learned to be both...pleasure") implies a direct quote, which isn't explicitly indicated in the original. It adds a layer of interpretation suggesting a specific, recited creed.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "merchandise" is replaced with "commerce." "Merchandise" in the 16th century could refer to a wider range of activities, including exploitative trade practices, not just standard commerce. The draft narrows the meaning.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms are changed or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.

For he rebukes them severely, and says, “You do not know that you are what you are.” That ignorance is a great evil, and the beginning of desperate blindness, when a man thinks he has something that he does not. For such persevere in their error, and admit no counselor. Therefore the Lord says, “You do not know that you are poor, wretched, weary, and worn with evils.” For they are toiled with many labors that serve this world. They are miserable. You do not see your own misery; others who see it are deeply sorry. You do not see in what condition you are. This kind of speech signifies a man very wretched and desperate, whose misery others see, but he himself sees nothing, a poor man or a beggar. 4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "ix. of John" changed to "John 9." While functionally equivalent, the original’s Roman numeral is more consistent with the period’s citation style.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No new content is added.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 21.5]For no man shall imagine, that there is bargayning before God, as there is with men: As though the spiritual gifts of God mought be bought for money. Where amongst other things come, saith he, bye without money, and without price, or exchange. And by and by: I[illegible] hearing hear me, incline your care, &c. Therefore the Romish Chananite hath no hold hereof, I mean the Pope that great merchant, which sells all things in the church, even those things which he hath not, the greatest diſceiuer in the world. Hereunto is added moreover, that like as it is in Essay plainly expressed, of whom such graces or gifts are to be bought: So Christ also here saith expressly, I counsel thee to by of me. For no man shall imagine that there is bargaining before God, as there is with men: as though the spiritual gifts of God might be bought for money. This is repugnant to the whole scripture, and especially against the determination of Saint Peter pronounced against Simon Magus. But this our exposition the Prophet Isaiah approves in the fifty-fifth chapter, where among other things comes, “Say, ‘Without money, without price, or exchange.’” And immediately following: “If you hear, give heed; incline your ear.” Therefore, the Roman Canaanite has no hold hereof, I mean the Pope, that great merchant, who sells all things in the church, even those things which he does not have, the greatest deceiver in the world. And therefore he says in the Gospel of Saint John: “Let him who has hunger or thirst, come to me.” To me, I say, let him come. John 4:6 and 7. And Saint Peter says, “Lord, to whom shall we go? [FINAL]
For all these things the Lord counsels the Laodicians, to seek God’s word, and believe it verily. For so it should come to pass, that being enriched with all spiritual gifts, they might live a pure conversation in the church, might possess Christ, and judge rightly of all matters of salvation. And in these things also consists true repentance: forgiveness of sins, and amendment of life. &c.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Upon these things he infers the some of the matter,[note 21.12] and saith: Where thou see God so earnestly seek thy salvation, I pray thee continue not always to be thus in a mammering nether hot nor cold. [illegible], be zelouſe, take unto thee a fervent zeal to follow and apprehend thy salvation. For now he sets the feruentnes of faith conceived of the word and spirit of God, against this newtralitie or warmnes. After he adds, and repent, in ſorſaking thine evil conversation, and being of Christ tried gold: That is purefied and pourged, whereby thou may be rich, be arrayed in white, and may have a medicine wherewith to annointe thine eyes, that thou may see. To God be glory.
[DRAFT]
Upon these things he infers some of the matter. And he says: Where you see God so earnestly seeking your salvation, I pray you do not always remain in a hesitant state, neither lukewarm nor fervent. Be zealous, take to yourself a fervent zeal to follow and grasp your salvation. For now he sets the fervor of faith, conceived by the word and spirit of God, against this neutrality or lukewarmness. After this, he adds, “Repent, in forsaking your evil ways, and be like gold tried by Christ: that is, purified and cleansed, so that you may be rich, arrayed in white, and may have a medicine with which to anoint your eyes, that you may see.” To God be glory.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "mammering nether hot nor cold" altered to "hesitant state, neither lukewarm nor fervent" loses the original's specific nuance of indecision and tepidity. "Mammering" suggests a stammering, halting approach, which is more than just hesitancy.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "be like gold tried by Christ" after "being of Christ tried gold" adds an interpretive layer not present in the original.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "being of Christ tried gold" is somewhat metaphorical and open to interpretation. The draft's clarification "be like gold tried by Christ" removes this ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were changed or removed.

[ORIGINAL]
Behold I stand at the door and knock: If any man hear my voice and open the door, I will come in unto him, and will sup with him, and he with me: To him that ouercometh will I grant to sit with me on my seat even as I overcame, and have sat with my father on his seat. Let him that hath ears, hear, what the spirit saith to the congregations.

Hereby also the Lord allures the Laodiceans to repentance, showing that every time is mete for conversion, and that God is evermore ready to receive sinners, and provokes them always that they should amede and live. And this matter he expounds in an allegorical and goodly speech,[note 22.1] taken out of the fifth Chapter. of the book of Canticles. For he feigns the Lord to stand at the door and knock, yea and to promise to them that open the greatest familiarity and joys unspeakable.

Hereby the Lord also draws the Laodiceans to repentance, showing that every time is suitable for conversion, and that God is always ready to receive sinners, and constantly urges them to repent and live. And this matter he explains in an allegorical and beautiful speech [note 22.1], taken from the fifth chapter of the Song of Songs. For he portrays the Lord as standing at the door and knocking, indeed promising to those who open it the greatest intimacy and unspeakable joys.

3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "familiarity" changed to "intimacy." "Familiarity" in 1561 could encompass a range of closeness, including a spiritual connection. "Intimacy" is more specifically romantic or deeply personal, narrowing the potential meaning.

[ORIGINAL]
First therefore is declared the benevolence of God towards sinners, and his most ready will always to receive the same, yea and his infinite study to move men to repentance, that they might live. For the Lord stands at the door, and knocks. The word of standing doth signify, that God is always prepared, always watches over our salvation. For he sits not ſtyll, nor lies not on the one side like a sluggard: He stands buſely to his work. And I stand saith he, not I stood, or shall stand: But I stand evermore ready, evermore loving and gentle. What doth he? He knocks, and that in deed at the door, desiring to be let in. For like as he that knocks at the door seriously, covets to be let in: so God desires earnestly to be of us received. And God uses sundry kinds of knocking. For he warns, and exciteth with his word by the Prophets, again by signs and wonders, and also by sundry chauces and mouings.[note 22.2] These things may be scene in the city of Ieruſale. He sends to them his Prophets and Apostles. He shows divers wonders. He brings on the sorrowful chances, that they might admonish the: Such as are reported, Luke. 13. of the Galileans, and of those whom the tour of Siloe had overwhelmed. We may se the like at this day, how the Lord knocks. Therefore he said truly, Hieruſale, Hieruſale. &c. Math. 23. These are doubtless the parts and doings of god which will not that a sinner should die, but rather convert and live.
[DRAFT]
First, therefore, is declared the benevolence of God toward sinners, and his most ready will always to receive them, yes, and his infinite study to move men to repentance, that they might live. For the Lord stands at the door, and knocks. The word "standing" signifies that God is always prepared, always watches over our salvation. For he does not sit still, nor lie on one side like a sluggard; he stands busily to his work. And "I stand," he says, not "I stood" or "I shall stand," but "I stand" evermore ready, evermore loving and gentle. What does he do? He knocks, and that indeed at the door, desiring to be let in. For just as he who knocks at the door seriously covets to be let in, so God desires earnestly to be received by us. And God uses sundry kinds of knocking. For he warns and excites with his word by the Prophets, again by signs and wonders, and also by sundry chances and movements. [note 22.2] These things may be seen in the city of Jerusalem. He sends to them his Prophets and Apostles. He shows divers wonders. He brings on the sorrowful chances, that they might admonish them—such as are reported in Luke 13, of the Galileans and of those whom the tower of Siloam had overwhelmed. We may see the like at this day, how the Lord knocks. Therefore he said truly, “Jerusalem, Jerusalem.” &c. Matthew 23. These are doubtless the parts and doings of God, which will not that a sinner should die, but rather convert and live.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** The insertion of "by us" after "received" in "God desires earnestly to be of us received" alters the original's more general appeal for acceptance.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The explanation "The word 'standing' signifies that God is always prepared..." is an interpretation, not a modernization. It imposes a specific meaning that isn't explicitly stated in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "mouings" is softened to "movements." "Mouings" carries a stronger sense of divine prompting or influence, a nuance lost in the more neutral "movements."
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ieruſale" should be "Jerusalem," not "Jeruſale."
5. **HALLUCINATION:** None.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
Than must we se, what is required of us,[note 22.3] verily that we should hear the knocking and noise of the knocker, and that also we open and receive such as desire to come in. Here are they confuted, which speak of man as though he were a block, and image I know not what manner of drawing, saying: It is neither in that runner nor in the willer. &c. Certain altogether abstain from well doing, saying, if I be chosen, it is enough. But the scripture requires every where hearing and obedience. We know that thelect are only saved, and that thelect in Christ: In Christ to be they that believe: That faith is of hearing, hearing by the word of god. Therefore saith the Prophet, this day if you hear his voice. &c. This same is recited of the Apostle. Heb. 4. The Apostle also. 2. Tim. 2. In a great house there be not only vessels of gold, but of earth also. If any pourge him self. &c. And therefore the Lord saith, I knock: It shall be thy part, not to despise him that knocks, but to open unto him. And he recites in deed two things, to hear, which both in the.8. and.9. of John is required of the children of God, and of the true sheep: And to open, that is to receive the Lord, or believe, to obey, and to frame them selves after the will of God, and to do penance. Notwithstanding we must here beware, that we think not that man hath power of him self to receive the Lord. The Lord illumines his elect, and by him we can do all things, without who we can do nothing. Other places must be coferred with this as John. 15.2. Corinth. 3. Philip. 2. They therefore that open, do open by the grace of God. They that open not being wrapped in their sins, through their own fault open not, and not through any fault of God.
[DRAFT]
Then we must see what is required of us, truly that we should hear the knocking and noise of the knocker, and also open and receive those who desire to come in. Here are those refuted, who speak of humans as though they were a block and an image of some unknown drawing, saying: It is neither in the runner nor in the willer. &c. Some entirely abstain from doing good, saying, if I am chosen, it is enough. But Scripture requires everywhere hearing and obedience. We know that people are only saved, and that in Christ: in Christ to be those who believe: that faith is of hearing, hearing by the word of God. Therefore, the prophet says, “This day if you hear his voice.” &c. This is recited by the Apostle in Hebrews 4. The Apostle also, in 2 Timothy 2, says, “In a great house there are not only vessels of gold, but of earth also. If anyone cleanses himself.” &c. And therefore the Lord says, “I knock.” It shall be your part, not to despise him who knocks, but to open to him. And he recounts indeed two things, to hear, which is required both in the 8th and 9th verses of John, of the children of God and of the true sheep: and to open, that is to receive the Lord, or believe, to obey, and to shape ourselves after the will of God, and to do penance. Nevertheless, we must be on our guard here, so that we do not think that humans have power of themselves to receive the Lord. The Lord illuminates his elect, and by him we can do all things, without whom we can do nothing. Other passages must be consulted with this, such as John 15:2, Corinthians 3, and Philippians 2. Those therefore who open, do open by the grace of God. Those who do not open are wrapped in their sins, not through their own fault, but through a fault of God [?]
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Than" to "Then" is a minor change, but "Than must we se" is slightly more archaic and conveys a sense of urgency that "Then we must see" lacks.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "Here are they confuted, which speak of man as though he were a block" becomes "Here are those refuted, who speak of humans as though they were a block." The original implies a specific group being refuted; the draft generalizes.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "Thelect" to "people" loses the theological specificity of the original. "Thelect" refers to the elect, a distinct group within Christian theology. Replacing it with "people" dilutes the meaning.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** "penance" is a reasonable modernization, but the original’s use of "thelect" is significantly altered.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** "through a fault of God [?]" is not present in the original. The original states "not through any fault of God." The addition introduces a contradictory and inaccurate interpretation.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Corinth" should be "Corinthians."
[FINAL]

Therefore this place expounds that.[note 22.6] For Christ after his deity gives that, which after his humanity he denies that he ca give. This place then proves, that Christ is very God, geuer of eternal life, &c.
[DRAFT]

This passage then proves that Christ is very God, giver of eternal life, and so forth.

And here he declares with a godly voice,[note 23.4] what John should do, and how he should behave him self. Christ bids John ascend into ſuperceleſtial places, not in body, but in mind. Therefore must our mind be lifted up into the contemplation of Heavenly things, and be pourged as much as may be from earthely affections, that we may behold heavenly things with an heavenly contemplation. [ORIGINAL]
[note 23.6]And first of all before the Seals and tropettes is set forth a figure or tip of God, and his most righteous judgment and government in all things: and that through out the 4. and 5. Chapter. wholly, that it might prepare us to the reading or hearing of those things which shall follow in the 6. 7. and 8. Chapter. And seem to others, and to mans judgment to be grievous, harde, and unjust. And the Tip or vision was after this sort.[note 23.7] In heaven it self appeared a seat or throne of Majesty. He that sits therein, holds in his right hand a book, closed with Seals. By him that sate stood a lamb, which takes the book, and opens the Seals thereof. And out of this Throne also proceeds a ſeuenfolde spirit, wonderfully uttering his virtues. Before the seat appears a glassy Sea, bright, and even like Crystal. The throne it self rests like a wagon upon four beasts full of eyes and wings, beneath appering round about, and inuirunnyng or compaſing the throne. A rainbow like a ſmaragde goes roude about the same. About the Throne by a circle appear xxiiii. Seats, and so many elders sitting in them, crowned, and in white array. This is the order of this second vision. In their place shall be declared what the Labe, what the beasts, what the Elders and the other parts did. It suffices now to have touched the chiefest points of the vision, and a shadowing some of the same.[note 23.8]
[DRAFT]
And first of all, before the Seals and symbols are presented, there is shown a representation or glimpse of God, and his most righteous judgment and governance in all things; and this is done throughout chapters 4 and 5, so that it might prepare us for the things that will follow in chapters 6, 7, and 8. And it may seem to others, and to human judgment, to be grievous, difficult, and unjust. And the representation or vision was as follows.

In heaven itself appeared a seat or throne of Majesty. He who sits therein holds in his right hand a book, sealed with seven seals. Standing beside him who sits is a Lamb, who takes the book and opens the seals. And from this throne also proceeds a sevenfold spirit, wonderfully displaying his virtues. Before the throne appears a sea of glass, bright and smooth like crystal. The throne itself rests like a wagon upon four beasts full of eyes, with wings beneath, appearing all around and encircling the throne. A rainbow like an emerald goes around it. About the throne, by a circle, appear twenty-four seats, and so many elders sitting in them, crowned and in white array. This is the order of this second vision. Their roles will be explained: what the Lamb, what the beasts, what the Elders, and the other parts did. It suffices now to have touched the chiefest points of the vision, and to have given a shadowing of some of it.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Tip" (23.7) is modernized to "representation or glimpse." While not entirely inaccurate, "tip" in the original carries a sense of foreshadowing or a preliminary indication, a nuance potentially lost.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "so that it might prepare us" (23.6) is added. The original simply states the purpose is "that it might prepare us." The addition implies a deliberate pedagogical intent not explicitly stated in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "ſeuenfolde spirit" (23.7) is modernized to "a sevenfold spirit." While the spelling is corrected, the original phrasing is intentionally unusual and potentially suggestive of a complex, multifaceted divine presence. The modernized version loses some of that potential ambiguity.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms are altered or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The added phrase "so that it might prepare us" (23.6) constitutes hallucinated content.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 23.13]About the Throne are scene set xxiiii. seats, and in them sit xxiiii. Elders, as Senators of the most mighty kingdom of God, and fathers of the Hierarchy celestial: This number is made of xii. and xii. But xii. Patriarches signify the whole people of Israel, and the old church before Christ. The Christen church was planted and sprang up of the twelve Apostles, after the incarnation of Christ, whereupon that xii. number comprehends the whole church of the new people. Therefore is the whole universality of Saints assembled in heaven, and triumphing with Christ their king. And therefore be they clothed in white raiment, to wit pourged by Christ, and pure and clean from all corruption. Crowned also, because they have overcome and now reign in eternal glory, verily kings and priests through Christ. The description also of their behauour admoniſſheth, that in them is nothing wauntinge, but to be truly blessed: and therefore they be showed sitting, not that they are judges, or judge for Christ, but because they rest from their labours, and be of most quiet and pure affections, sitting with the high judge. But what thing doe these? They give God no counsel, what he should do, or by what mean or way he may doe this or that, but they allow his judgements. For they know all his works to be just and holy. The which shall immediately follow. What shall we do than? shall it be mete for us to inquire of the judgements of God, or prescribe what he should do or not do? I think not you have in this universality of saints, all patriarches, all judges and kings, all princes, and the whole people of God: you have among these, king Solomon him self, and the most excellent and wittiest Princes of the world: you have the Apostles, and men Apoſtolical, Martyrs, and the wise men of the whole universal world. Wilt thou condemn their judgements? following therefore their example, busy not thy self to move curious questions: praise the just judgements of God, and know that the Lord is just in all his ways, and holy in all his works. To whom be glory.
[DRAFT]
[note 23.13]Around the Throne are seen twenty-four seats, and in them sit twenty-four elders, as senators of the most mighty kingdom of God, and fathers of the heavenly hierarchy. This number is made of twelve and twelve. But twelve patriarchs signify the entire people of Israel, and the old church before Christ. The Christian church was planted and sprang up from the twelve Apostles, after the incarnation of Christ, so that this twelve encompasses the whole church of the new people. Therefore, the entire universality of saints is assembled in heaven, triumphing with Christ their king. And therefore they are clothed in white raiment, namely, purged by Christ, and pure and clean from all corruption. Crowned also, because they have overcome and now reign in eternal glory, truly kings and priests through Christ. The description also of their behavior admonishes that in them is nothing lacking, but that they are truly blessed; and therefore they are shown sitting, not that they are judges or judge for Christ, but because they rest from their labors and are of most quiet and pure affections, sitting with the high judge. But what do these do? They give God no counsel as to what he should do, or by what means or way he may do this or that, but they approve his judgments. For they know all his works to be just and holy, which will immediately follow. What then shall we do? Shall it be fitting for us to inquire about the judgments of God, or prescribe what he should do or not do? I think not. You do not have in this universality of saints, all patriarchs, all judges and kings, all princes, and the whole people of God; you have among these, King Solomon himself, and the most excellent and wisest princes of the world; you have the Apostles, and men apostolic, martyrs, and the wise men of the whole universal world. Will you condemn their judgments? Following therefore their example, do not busy yourself with curious questions; praise the just judgments of God, and know that the Lord is just in all his ways, and holy in all his works. To whom be glory.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "allow his judgements" (original) is softened to "approve his judgments" (draft). While not a drastic change, "allow" carries a sense of acceptance and perhaps even a degree of restraint that "approve" lacks, subtly altering the nuance of the original's understanding of the elders’ role.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "wauntinge" is rendered as "lacking." While functionally similar, "wauntinge" carries a connotation of deficiency or something being incomplete, a more specific and potentially richer meaning than the more general "lacking."
3. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes were made to terms like "Antichrist," "Apocalypse," "dispensation," "sacrament," etc., as they are absent from the original passage.
4. **HALLUCINATION:** No new content is present.
5. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Austen" should be "Augustine."
[FINAL]
[note 23.13]Around the Throne are seen twenty-four seats, and in them sit twenty-four elders, as senators of the most mighty kingdom of God, and fathers of the heavenly hierarchy. This number is made of twelve and twelve. But twelve patriarchs signify the entire people of Israel, and the old church before Christ. The Christian church was planted and sprang up from the twelve Apostles, after the incarnation of Christ, so that this twelve encompasses the whole church of the new people. Therefore, the entire universality of saints is assembled in heaven, triumphing with Christ their king. And therefore they are clothed in white raiment, namely, purged by Christ, and pure and clean from all corruption. Crowned also, because they have overcome and now reign in eternal glory, truly kings and priests through Christ. The description also of their behavior admonishes that in them is nothing wanting, but that they are truly blessed; and therefore they are shown sitting, not that they are judges or judge for Christ, but because they rest from their labors and are of most quiet and pure affections, sitting with the high judge. But what do these do? They give God no counsel as to what he should do, or by what means or way he may do this or that, but they allow his judgments. For they know all his works to be just and holy, which will immediately follow. What then shall we do? Shall it be fitting for us to inquire about the judgments of God, or prescribe what he should do or not do? I think not. You do not have in this universality of saints, all patriarchs, all judges and kings, all princes, and the whole people of God; you have among these, King Solomon himself, and the most excellent and wisest princes of the world; you have the Apostles, and men apostolic, martyrs, and the wise men of the whole universal world. Will you condemn their judgments? Following therefore their example, do not busy yourself with curious questions; praise the just judgments of God, and know that the Lord is just in all his ways, and holy in all his works. To whom be glory.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 24.1]Our Lord Jesus Christ, as the faithefull pastor of his church, will utter the destinies and woderful calamities that will come upon the church. Therefore to the intent he might stop the mouths of such as mutiue and be inquisitive of the judgements of God, and might persuade all me to have patience in these storms of evils, he sets forth a treatise before, wherein he shows that all things are done or permitted to be done of God by his most just providence, and are governed or ordered by the Lamb, with a judgment most righteous and holy. For who so believes and remembers this, in what chauces so ever he happen, he submits him self humbly and lowly and obediently to his God, and cries always, the Lord is righteous in all his mayes and holy in all his works. And this is the most true state of the first part of this vision, which is done in the 4. and 5. Chapter. And is more over most elegaunt, most pleasant, and most full of consolation. Althinges are more lively set forth and perceived in such fit and heavenly representations, than they can be understand in bare words.
[DRAFT]
Our Lord Jesus Christ, as the faithful pastor of his church, will reveal the destinies and wonderful calamities that will come upon the church. Therefore, to the intent he might stop the mouths of those who question and are inquisitive about the judgments of God, and might persuade all men to have patience in these storms of evils, he sets forth a treatise beforehand, wherein he shows that all things are done or permitted to be done by God through his most just providence, and are governed or ordered by the Lamb, with a judgment most righteous and holy. For whoever believes and remembers this, whatever circumstances he encounters, he submits himself humbly and lowly and obediently to his God, and cries always, “The Lord is righteous in all his ways and holy in all his works.” And this is the most true state of the first part of this vision, which is done in the 4th and 5th chapters. It is moreover most elegant, most pleasant, and most full of consolation. All things are more vividly set forth and perceived in such fitting and heavenly representations than they can be understood in bare words.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "utter the destinies" (original) is softened to "reveal the destinies" (draft). While not a massive shift, "utter" implies a more authoritative declaration.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "through his most just providence" is added to "of his most just providence." This subtly shifts the agency, implying God *actively* uses providence rather than simply allowing it.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "in what chauces so ever he happen" (original) is rendered as "whatever circumstances he encounters" (draft). The original phrasing, while archaic, allows for a broader range of possibilities than the more specific "circumstances."
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals of theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present to check.
[FINAL]
Our Lord Jesus Christ, as the faithful pastor of his church, will utter the destinies and wonderful calamities that will come upon the church. Therefore, to the intent he might stop the mouths of those who mutiny and be inquisitive of the judgments of God, and might persuade all men to have patience in these storms of evils, he sets forth a treatise beforehand, wherein he shows that all things are done or permitted to be done of God by his most just providence, and are governed or ordered by the Lamb, with a judgment most righteous and holy. For who so believes and remembers this, in what chances so ever he happen, he submits him self humbly and lowly and obediently to his God, and cries always, “The Lord is righteous in all his ways and holy in all his works.” And this is the most true state of the first part of this vision, which is done in the 4. and 5. Chapter. It is moreover most elegant, most pleasant, and most full of consolation. All things are more lively set forth and perceived in such fit and heavenly representations, than they can be understand in bare words.
