[ORIGINAL]
Secodly Christ is called the rote of David, wherein he appears to have alluded to that saying of Isaiah in the.11.[note 27.7] chap. Than shall a bud come forth of the stock of Ieſſe, and a flour shall ascend out of the roots thereof. Namely Marie the daughter of David, of whom that most sacred flower Christ sprang and came, was the stock of Ieſſe. And of the very rotes of David, or of the virgin, I mean of the most true humane nature, Jesus Christ was borne very man into the world. For he toke no where the Angles nature, but the ſede of Abraham. He is therefore our brother,[note 27.8] of the same substance with us, after his humanity. These things do comfort us exceedingly, and confute heretics most strongly: which fine that Christ hath not a very humane body. We have more hereof in the.1. of Matthew. and.1.2.3. of Luke. [note 27.9] After it is expressly spoken of the same our lord, that he is in the midst of the Throne, in the midst of the iiii. beasts, and in the midst of the xxiiii. Elders: and is therefore exepted out of the nobre of creatures, out of the number of Angles, and out of the number of Saints. For he is greater than these, to wit of the same substance with the father, in glory and power equal. For the father is in the midst of the Throne, from thence proceeds the holy ghost: even there is found also now the lamb Christ, not only very man, but also very God.[note 27.10] And is a distinct person. For the blessed Trinity knows not any cofuſio. The father is God, the son is God, the holy ghost is God: yet are all three but one God, the father in his subsistence, the son in his, and the holy ghost in his, not making three Gods, but three proprieties and persons in one indiuiſible and eternal essence. And where as Christ is mentioned to be in the midst of the beasts, and in the midst of Elders: he is doubtless signified after the divine nature to be every where, to be the life and preservation of all creatures, also in the midst of his chosen, and of his Church. Therefore like as we believe Jesus Christ to be very man, so let us also believe him to be very God, of the same substance with God the father. Therefore let Seruiet perish with Arrius and Mahomet, and as many as deny Christ to be the son of God, coequal with the father in all things. Furthermore he is now also called a lamb,[note 27.11] not that he is a sheep of nature, but for that by a lamb is prefigured the innocent redemer of the world, and the only wholesome sacrifice of all faithful. A lamb is a token of innocence, and from the beginning appointed for sacrifices. Abel offered up a Lamb, after the law was offered a daily sacrifice, in the morning a lamb, and at evening a lamb. For christ is the expiation of them, which were in the beginning of the world, and which in the end shall be. The Paſchall lamb in the.12. of Exodus. Whose blood prohibited the Angel diſtroier from the houses and tents, represented the figure of Christ, by whose precious blood we are reconciled to God. This expoſitio of the Paschal lamb S. Peter him self in the.1. Pet. 1. and S. Paull in the.1. to the Corinth. 5. have brought. Isaiah accords with them in the.53. chapter. And so expounded by the Apostle S. Philip in the 8. of the Acts. Finally S. John Baptist, which with the fingar stretched out and poinctyng to Christ, exclamed: behold the Lamb of God, which takes away the sins of the world. Let us therefore believe, that the same Jesus Christ, unto whom all power is give of the father, to be our deliverer, our expiation, reconciliation, innocence, sanctification, justification and everlasting salvation: as he whom we shall hear in the xiii. Chapter. to have be slain from the beginning of the world, for so much as his only death, and one oblation made from the beginning of the world, and continually to the worlds end, doth sanctify all those that are sanctified Which the Apostle also affirms in the.10. to the Hebrews.
[DRAFT]
Secondly, Christ is called the root of David, wherein he appears to have alluded to that saying of Isaiah in the 11th chapter. Then shall a bud come forth of the stock of Jesse, and a flower shall ascend out of the roots thereof. Namely, Mary, the daughter of David, of whom that most sacred flower, Christ, sprang and came, was the stock of Jesse. And of the very roots of David, or of the virgin, I mean of the most true human nature, Jesus Christ was born very man into the world. For he took not the angelic nature, but the seed of Abraham. He is therefore our brother, of the same substance with us, after his humanity. These things do comfort us exceedingly and confute heretics most strongly, who deny that Christ has a very human body. We have more hereof in Matthew 1 and Luke 1:2-3. After it is expressly spoken of the same our Lord, that he is in the midst of the throne, in the midst of the four beasts, and in the midst of the twenty-four Elders: and is therefore excepted out of the number of creatures, out of the number of angels, and out of the number of saints. For he is greater than these, to wit, of the same substance with the Father, in glory and power equal. For the Father is in the midst of the throne, from thence proceeds the Holy Ghost: even there is found also now the Lamb, Christ, not only very man, but also very God. And is a distinct person. For the blessed Trinity knows no confusion. The Father is God, the Son is God, the Holy Ghost is God: yet are all three but one God, the Father in his subsistence, the Son in his, and the Holy Ghost in his, not making three Gods, but three properties and persons in one indivisible and eternal essence. And whereas Christ is mentioned to be in the midst of the beasts and in the midst of the Elders, he is doubtless signified after the divine nature to be everywhere, to be the life and preservation of all creatures, also in the midst of his chosen and of his Church. Therefore, like as we believe Jesus Christ to be very man, so let us also believe him to be very God, of the same substance with God the Father. Therefore let Servetus perish with Arius and Mahomet, and as many as deny Christ to be the Son of God, coequal with the Father in all things. Furthermore, he is now also called a Lamb, not that he is a sheep of nature, but for that by a lamb is prefigured the innocent redeemer of the world and the only wholesome sacrifice of all the faithful. A lamb is a token of innocence, and from the beginning appointed for sacrifices. Abel offered up a lamb; after the law was offered a daily sacrifice, in the morning a lamb, and at evening a lamb. For Christ is the expiation of them which were in the beginning of the world, and which in the end shall be. The Paschal lamb in Exodus 12 whose blood prohibited the destroying angel from the houses and tents, represented the figure of Christ, by whose precious blood we are reconciled to God. This exposition of the Paschal lamb, Jerome, Peter, and Paul have brought in 1 Peter 1 and 1 Corinthians 5. Isaiah accords with them in the 53rd chapter. And so expounded by the Apostle Philip in Acts 8. Finally, John the Baptist, which with the finger stretched out and pointing to Christ, exclaimed: “Behold the Lamb of God, which takes away the sins of the world.” Let us therefore believe that the same Jesus Christ, unto whom all power is given of the Father, is our deliverer, our expiation, reconciliation, innocence, sanctification, justification, and everlasting salvation: as he whom we shall hear in the 13th Chapter to have been slain from the beginning of the world, for so much as his only death and one oblation made from the beginning of the world, and continually to the world’s end, doth sanctify all those that are sanctified. Which the Apostle also affirms in Hebrews 10.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "He toke no where the Angles nature, but the ſede of Abraham" is modernized to "For he took not the angelic nature, but the seed of Abraham." While technically accurate, the original phrasing ("no where") emphasizes a deliberate absence, a lack of angelic lineage. The modernized version is less emphatic.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "Jerome, Peter, and Paul have brought in 1 Peter 1 and 1 Corinthians 5" is an interpretive addition. The original simply states "S. Peter him self in the.1. Pet. 1. and S. Paull in the.1. to the Corinth. 5." The draft explicitly attributes the interpretation to those figures, which isn't present in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "of the very rotes of David, or of the virgin" is ambiguous, potentially referring to both David's lineage and Mary's virginity as sources of Christ's humanity. The draft's "And of the very roots of David, or of the virgin" retains the ambiguity, but the modernization subtly shifts the emphasis, making the "virgin" feel more like a clarification of the "roots of David" rather than a separate, potentially equally valid interpretation.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is present in the draft not present in the original.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Hierom→Jerome" is missing. The original "Hierom" should be modernized to "Jerome." Austen should be Augustine, and Ireney should be Irenaeus.
[FINAL]
Secondly, Christ is called the root of David, wherein he appears to have alluded to that saying of Isaiah in the 11th chapter. Then shall a bud come forth of the stock of Jesse, and a flower shall ascend out of the roots thereof. Namely, Mary, the daughter of David, of whom that most sacred flower, Christ, sprang and came, was the stock of Jesse. And of the very roots of David, or of the virgin, I mean of the most true human nature, Jesus Christ was born very man into the world. For he took nowhere the angelic nature, but the seed of Abraham. He is therefore our brother, of the same substance with us, after his humanity. These things do comfort us exceedingly and confute heretics most strongly, who deny that Christ has a very human body. We have more hereof in Matthew 1 and Luke 1:2-3. After it is expressly spoken of the same our Lord, that he is in the midst of the throne, in the midst of the four beasts, and in the midst of the twenty-four Elders: and is therefore excepted out of the number of creatures, out of the number of angels, and out of the number of saints. For he is greater than these, to wit, of the same substance with the Father, in glory and power equal. For the Father is in the midst of the throne, from thence proceeds the Holy Ghost: even there is found also now the Lamb, Christ, not only very man, but also very God. And is a distinct person. For the blessed Trinity knows no confusion. The Father is God, the Son is God, the Holy Ghost is God: yet are all three but one God, the Father in his subsistence, the Son in his, and the Holy Ghost in his, not making three Gods, but three properties and persons in one indivisible and eternal essence. And whereas Christ is mentioned to be in the midst of the beasts and in the midst of the Elders, he is doubtless signified after the divine nature to be everywhere, to be the life and preservation of all creatures, also in the midst of his chosen and of his Church. Therefore, like as we believe Jesus Christ to be very man, so let us also believe him to be very God, of the same substance with God the Father. Therefore let Servetus perish with Arius and Mahomet, and as many as deny Christ to be the Son of God, coequal with the Father in all things. Furthermore, he is now also called a Lamb, not that he is a sheep of nature, but for that by a lamb is prefigured the innocent redeemer of the world and the only wholesome sacrifice of all the faithful. A lamb is a token of innocence, and from the beginning appointed for sacrifices. Abel offered up a lamb; after the law was offered a daily sacrifice, in the morning a lamb, and at evening a lamb. For Christ is the expiation of them which were in the beginning of the world, and which in the end shall be. The Paschal lamb in Exodus 12 whose blood prohibited the destroying angel from the houses and tents, represented the figure of Christ, by whose precious blood we are reconciled to God. This exposition of the Paschal lamb, Jerome, Peter, and Paul have brought in 1 Peter 1 and 1 Corinthians 5. Isaiah accords with them in the 53rd chapter. And so expounded by the Apostle Philip in Acts 8. Finally, John the Baptist, which with the finger stretched out and pointing to Christ, exclaimed: “Behold the Lamb of God, which takes away the sins of the world.” Let us therefore believe that the same Jesus Christ, unto whom all power is given of the Father, is our deliverer, our expiation, reconciliation, innocence, sanctification, justification, and everlasting salvation: as he whom we shall hear in the 13th Chapter to have been slain from the beginning of the world, for so much as his only death and one oblation made from the beginning of the world, and continually to the world’s end, doth sanctify all those that are sanctified. Which the Apostle also affirms in Hebrews 10.
---
[ORIGINAL]
How be it this lamb or saviour of the world is said to stand in the midst of the throne, verily for that now he executes thoffice of a catholic king, and priest and governor, being always ready and prepared to save. So S. Stephen also in the.7. of the Acts sees him standing. Or else in other places we read that Christ sits on the right hand of the father. To the which this place gain says not, considering that to sit, is both to rest and to reign.
[DRAFT]
Nevertheless, this Lamb or Savior of the world is said to stand in the midst of the throne, truly because now he executes the office of a universal king, priest, and governor, always ready and prepared to save. Thus, Stephen also in Acts 7 sees him standing. Or else in other places we read that Christ sits on the right hand of the Father. This passage does not contradict that, considering that to sit signifies both rest and reign.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "catholic king" altered to "universal king." While related, "catholic" in 1561 carried a theological weight signifying the universal Church, not simply a broad descriptor of rulership. This shifts the nuance.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "This passage does not contradict that" is an interpretive addition not present in the original. The original simply states the observation.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "gain says not" is awkward but preserves a slight ambiguity about the relationship between the two statements. The draft's phrasing eliminates this ambiguity, presenting a more definitive (and potentially unwarranted) reconciliation.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were changed or removed.
[FINAL]
Nevertheless, this Lamb or Savior of the world is said to stand in the midst of the throne, verily for that now he executes the office of a catholic king, priest and governor, being always ready and prepared to save. So S. Stephen also in the.7. of the Acts sees him standing. Or else in other places we read that Christ sits on the right hand of the Father. To the which this place gain says not, considering that to sit is both to rest and to reign.

[ORIGINAL]
Furthermore this lamb Christ Jesus our lord hath vii.[note 27.13] horns, not that in deed he carries so many horns like a goat of Iude. An horne, as appears by Daniel, and by the song of Zacharie in the 1. of Luke, signifies power and kingdom. The vii. number is the number of fulness. It is therefore signified that Christ is endued with all kind of power, divine, humane, imperial, pontifical, royal, briefly most absolute. In the 13. Chapter. we shall hear that the beast hath taken to him two horns, as it were of the lamb, whereof I shall speak in his place. Daniel in the 7. Chapter. And rule, says he, was given him, and honour and kyngedome, that all nations and tounges might worship him, whose rule is an everlasting rule, which shall not perish nor decay at any time. Now hath he seven eyes also. These he expounds, and says,[note 27.14] which are the vii. spirits of God, sent into the whole world. I showed you before, that the vii. spirits are called a ſeuenfold spirit. Here therefore is signified the fulness of the spirit, which the lord powereth out upon all flesh. Here is signified the universal knowledge of the son, in whose sight are present, what things so ever are done in heaven and in earth, openly and privily. For the spirit of Christ, that unmeasurable force, incomprehensible and most divine, searches and pearſeth all things, nothing is hid from his eyes, which view the whole world.
[DRAFT]
Furthermore, this Lamb, Christ Jesus our Lord, has seven horns, not that in truth he carries so many horns like a goat of Judea. An horn, as appears by Daniel and by the song of Zacharias in the first chapter of Luke, signifies power and kingdom. The number seven is the number of fullness. It is therefore signified that Christ is endowed with every kind of power, divine, human, imperial, pontifical, royal, briefly, most absolute. In the thirteenth chapter, we shall hear that the beast has taken to him two horns, as it were of the lamb, about which I shall speak in its place. Daniel in the seventh chapter says, “And rule was given him, and honor and kingdom, that all nations and tongues might worship him, whose rule is an everlasting rule, which shall not perish nor decay at any time.” Now he has seven eyes also. He expounds these, and says [note 27.14], which are the seven spirits of God, sent into the whole world. I showed you before that the seven spirits are called a sevenfold spirit. Here therefore is signified the fullness of the Spirit, which the Lord pours out upon all flesh. Here is signified the universal knowledge of the Son, in whose sight are present whatever things are done in heaven and on earth, openly and privately. For the spirit of Christ, that unmeasurable force, incomprehensible and most divine, searches and pierces all things; nothing is hidden from his eyes, which view the whole world.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "kind of power" changed to "every kind of power." While seemingly minor, "kind" implies a variety, whereas "every" suggests totality, potentially altering the nuance of the original's description of Christ's power.
2. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." (Not present in this passage, but a general note for future review.)
3. **HALLUCINATION:** No new content is present.
[FINAL]

An horn, as appears by Daniel, and by the song of Zacharie in the 1. of Luke, signifies power and kingdom. The seven number is the number of fulness. ANd when he had taken the book, the four beasts, and xxiiii. Elders fell down before the lamb, having Harps and golden Vialles full of odours (which are the prayers of Saints) and they sang a new song, saying: Thou art worthy to take the book, and to open the Seals thereof: for thou waste killed, and haste redeemed us by thy blood, out of all kinredes and tongues, and people and natios, and haste made us unto our God, Kings and Priests, and we shall reign on the earth.

[ORIGINAL]
For this example is verily manifold, and even of six parts, such as you shall hardly find propounded in any other matter.[note 28.2] And in this matter is of very great force. First in deed we have heard in the iiii. Chapter. that the four beasts cried out before the Throne of him that sate: holy, holy, holy, lord God omnipotent. Second we understand that the xxiiii. Elders fell down, worshipped, cast away their crowns, and sang an Himne. Now follows the third degree of this example. For as first the beasts and Elders did these things severally, so now jointly with one accorde the beasts and Elders fall down together before the labe. Let us therefore fall down also in all the judgements and works of God, before the lamb governor of all, and let us worship. For although it be not here added, and they worshipped: yet are they to be understand for this intent to have fallen down, that they might worship. For to fall down, is to worship. Which thing is also perceived by this that follows.[note 28.3] For they offer prayers to the lamb, that is to wit, singe an Himne, which is a part of godly worshipping. Morouer it follows immediately, that every creature sang an Himne to him that sets in the Throne, and to the labe, &c. And verily two things especially and diligently S. John treats in this example. For first he paints out gallantly the behaviour of the beasts and elders. After he annexeth the Himne, praise geuyng, or song. And so much as appertains their behaviour. Before all things they fall down before the lamb: as even now I said.
[DRAFT]
For this example is truly manifold, and even consists of six parts, such as you will hardly find presented in any other subject. And this matter is of very great force. First, we have heard in the fourth chapter that the four beasts cried out before the throne of him who sat: holy, holy, holy, Lord God Almighty. Second, we understand that the twenty-four elders fell down, worshipped, cast away their crowns, and sang a hymn. Now follows the third degree of this example. For as first the beasts and elders did these things separately, so now jointly with one accord, the beasts and elders fall down together before the Lamb. Let us therefore fall down also in all the judgments and works of God, before the Lamb, governor of all, and let us worship. For although it is not here added, “and they worshipped,” they are to be understood to have fallen down for that purpose, because to fall down is to worship. This is also evident from what follows. For they offer prayers to the Lamb, that is to say, sing a hymn, which is a part of godly worship. Moreover, it follows immediately that every creature sang a hymn to him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb, and so on. And truly, two things especially and diligently Jerome treats in this example. First, he depicts brilliantly the behavior of the beasts and elders. After that, he adds the hymn, praise-giving, or song. And so much as pertains to their behavior. Above all, they fall down before the Lamb, as I said just now.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "manifold, and even of six parts" changed to "manifold, and even consists of six parts." While seemingly minor, "consists of" imposes a structure not explicitly stated.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "because to fall down is to worship" is an interpretation, not a direct translation of the original's implication. The original leaves the connection more suggestive.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s phrasing regarding the falling and worship is more suggestive and less definitive. The draft’s explanation reduces this ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: "S. John" changed to "Jerome." This is a significant error; the original refers to John, the author of Revelation.
5. HALLUCINATION: "praise geuyng" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "Hierom→Jerome" is incorrect. The original text uses "S. John," which should be maintained as "St. John" in a modernized version. Austen should be Augustine, Ireney should be Irenaeus, but these are not present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
[note 28.4]And this place is of efficacitie enough to prove the deity of our Saviour Christ. For these things ought to be conferred with those which are written upon the same words in the iiii. Chapter. The xxiiii. Elders fell down before him that sits in the Throne, and worship him that lives for ever and ever: And now it is said, that the self same elders have fallen down before the lamb: whereupon it follows that he that lives for ever and the lamb be worshipped with like glory, culte, and honour: And that the son is coequal with the father, to be worshipped for ever. Whereby now is openly perceived the abominable and detestable error of Arius and Seruetus,[note 28.5] confuted at this present not only of the beasts, but also of the whole cogregation of Saints in heave. Idle men reason subtly, and peruerte and wraſte gods word after their wonted giantly boldenes, at their pleasure: we will rather follow the examples of all saints and creatures in the world, and will worship the lamb with him that sits in the throne blessed for evermore.
[DRAFT]
And this passage is sufficiently powerful to demonstrate the deity of our Savior Christ. For these things should be compared with what is written on the same words in chapter four. The twenty-four elders fell down before him who sits on the throne and worship him who lives forever and ever. And now it is said that the very same elders have fallen down before the Lamb; whereupon it follows that he who lives forever and the Lamb are worshipped with like glory, cult, and honor: and that the Son is coequal with the Father, to be worshipped forever. By this, now, the abominable and detestable error of Arius and Servetus is openly perceived, refuted at this time not only by the beasts but also by the entire congregation of saints in heaven. Idle men reason subtly, perverting and twisting God’s word according to their usual, brazen boldness, at their pleasure. We will rather follow the examples of all saints and creatures in the world and will worship the Lamb with him who sits on the throne, blessed forevermore.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "efficacitie enough to prove" changed to "sufficiently powerful to demonstrate." While similar, "efficacity" implies a specific, inherent power, whereas "sufficiently powerful" is more general and weakens the original's assertion of inherent divine power.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The draft's phrasing "should be compared" replaces "ought to be conferred," subtly shifting from a suggestion of reasoned discussion to a prescriptive instruction.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "giantly boldenes" is rendered as "brazen boldness." "Giantly" suggests a boldness born of size or scale, a nuance lost in "brazen."
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Servetus is correctly modernized from Seruetus.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
Again there are object to us the Elders lying prostrate on the pavement, holding in their hades harps and vialles.[note 28.6] An harp in the Pſalmes and holy history is an instrument of music, coſecrate to praises divine. Of the vialle, of what shape or fashion the cup was, the writers of vessels treat much: I understand it to be simply a cup or a bolle, such as we read there were many in the tabernacle and temple, appointed both for drink offerings, and also for sweet odours and incense. Nevertheless these things in the holy heavenly dwellers be not to be taken corporally, but spiritually, after a ſuffiguration. For what the spirit of God understood, the revealer of ſecrettes, S. John him self adds which be the prayers of Saints. Therefore is signified, that Saints offer prayers to God: which are much more acceptable to him,[note 28.7] than the sweet melody of Musical instruments is to man, or pleasant savour of sweet gumes or of incense. Arethas the expoſitour, in that they have Harps, says he, it shows a concorde and agreement in geuynge God thanks. And hereof we learn again, what we should do in the contemplation and understanding of the judgements and works of God. The lord is to be praised and blessed, because he is good, and his mercy endures for ever. But if thanks must be given to God, if his works and iudgmentes are to be praised, why do certain men expostulate with God, blaming, or bringing in suspicion his iudgemetes? let us learn moreover, that Organes and those corporal incenses do no longer become the church of God.
[DRAFT]
Again, the elders are presented to us, lying prostrate on the pavement, holding harps and vials. [note 28.6] A harp in the Psalms and holy history is an instrument of music, consecrated to divine praises. Concerning the vial, writers about vessels discuss much about its shape and fashion; I understand it to be simply a cup or a bowl, such as we read there were many in the tabernacle and temple, appointed both for drink offerings and also for sweet odors and incense. Nevertheless, these things in the holy heavenly dwellers are not to be taken corporally, but spiritually, by figuration. For what the Spirit of God understood, the revealer of secrets, Saint John himself adds, which are the prayers of the saints. Therefore, it is signified that saints offer prayers to God, which are much more acceptable to him [note 28.7] than the sweet melody of musical instruments is to man, or the pleasant savor of sweet gums or of incense. Arethas, the expositor, says that because they have harps, it shows a concord and agreement in giving God thanks. And from this we learn again what we should do in the contemplation and understanding of the judgments and works of God. The Lord is to be praised and blessed because he is good, and his mercy endures forever. But if thanks must be given to God, if his works and judgments are to be praised, why do certain men expostulate with God, blaming or bringing into suspicion his judgments? Let us learn moreover that organs and those corporal incenses do no longer become the church of God.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "holding in their hades harps" becomes "holding harps and vials." "Hades" is a specific, albeit archaic, term with theological connotations (the underworld) that is lost in the simplification.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The original uses "ſuffiguration" which implies a symbolic representation. The draft changes this to "by figuration," which is less precise and loses the nuance of the original’s theological context.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "the holy heavenly dwellers" is unusual and slightly ambiguous, potentially referring to angels or a heavenly realm. The draft's "the holy heavenly dwellers" removes this ambiguity, flattening the language.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of significant theological terms are present.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No new content is added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** All proper nouns are modernized correctly.
[FINAL]
Again, the elders are presented to us, lying prostrate on the pavement, holding harps and vials. [note 28.6] A harp in the Psalms and holy history is an instrument of music, consecrated to divine praises. Of the vial, writers about vessels discuss much about its shape and fashion; I understand it to be simply a cup or a bowl, such as we read there were many in the tabernacle and temple, appointed both for drink offerings and also for sweet odors and incense. Nevertheless, these things in the holy heavenly dwellers are not to be taken corporally, but spiritually, after a ſuffiguration. For what the Spirit of God understood, the revealer of secrets, Saint John himself adds, which are the prayers of the saints. Therefore, it is signified that saints offer prayers to God, which are much more acceptable to him [note 28.7] than the sweet melody of musical instruments is to man, or the pleasant savor of sweet gums or of incense. Arethas, the expositor, says that because they have harps, it shows a concord and agreement in giving God thanks. And from this we learn again what we should do in the contemplation and understanding of the judgments and works of God. The Lord is to be praised and blessed because he is good, and his mercy endures forever. But if thanks must be given to God, if his works and judgments are to be praised, why do certain men expostulate with God, blaming or bringing into suspicion his judgments? Let us learn moreover that organs and those corporal incenses do no longer become the church of God.

[ORIGINAL]
Of this place S. Ireney in the 4. book against heresies,[note 28.8] in the.33. and.34. Chapter. shows, that the prayers and thanks geuyng of Saints be the same oblation which Malachie prophesied to be offered up through out the whole world. And shortly after Tertullian followed the same exposition agyynſt the Jews, and in the iiii. book against Marcion: whom other doctors of the Church have followed. But those pleasant sophistical triflers, I mean the popiſſhe divines, do as it were triumph in those things, yet lead they in the mean time a shadowing and a most vain triumph. For they apply these things to their sacrifice, wherein they fain them selves under the some of bread and wine to offer up to God the father the body and blood of Christ, a propiciatory sacrifice for the quick and the dead. But Ireney and Tertullian speak not of such a sacrifice, but of the oblation of prayers, which the maſſemonging Priest offers not up alone, but the whole cogregation of Christ sanctified in his blood, geuynge thanks in the lords supper to God the father for their free redemption. These holy fathers never knew the sale Masses of these Chananites.
[DRAFT]
Saint Irenaeus, in the fourth book against heresies, chapters 33 and 34, demonstrates that the prayers and thanksgiving of the saints are the same offering that Malachi prophesied would be offered throughout the world. Shortly afterward, Tertullian followed the same explanation against the Jews and in the fourth book against Marcion, and other church doctors have followed suit. But those pleasant, sophistical triflers—I mean the Roman Catholic theologians—seem to triumph in these matters, yet they lead a shadowy and utterly empty triumph. For they apply these things to their sacrifice, in which they claim to offer to God the Father the body and blood of Christ, a propitiatory sacrifice for the living and the dead, under the guise of bread and wine. But Irenaeus and Tertullian do not speak of such a sacrifice, but of the offering of prayers, which the Mass-priest does not offer alone, but the entire congregation of Christ, sanctified in his blood, giving thanks to God the Father for their free redemption in the Lord’s Supper. These holy fathers never knew the sale Masses of these Canaanites.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** "Mass" is introduced as a descriptor. The original uses "maſſemonging Priest" which is a pejorative, but doesn't explicitly name "Mass" as a concept. The draft's capitalization and framing subtly shifts the meaning.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "followed suit" is added after Tertullian. This implies a direct continuation of thought that isn't present in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's "Chananites" is rendered as "Canaanites." While the original is likely a scribal error, changing it removes the ambiguity and potential for a deliberate, albeit flawed, reference.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "popiſſhe divines" is modernized to "Roman Catholic theologians." While technically accurate, it loses the specific, and somewhat derogatory, tone of the original.
5. **MEANING DRIFT:** The original’s phrasing "lead they in the mean time a shadowing and a most vain triumph" is softened to "lead a shadowy and utterly empty triumph." The original is more forceful and condemnatory.
[FINAL]
Saint Irenaeus, in the fourth book against heresies, chapters 33 and 34, demonstrates that the prayers and thanksgiving of the saints are the same offering that Malachi prophesied would be offered throughout the world. Shortly afterward, Tertullian followed the same exposition against the Jews and in the fourth book against Marcion: whom other doctors of the Church have followed. But those pleasant, sophistical triflers—I mean the popiſſhe divines—do as it were triumph in those things, yet lead they in the mean time a shadowing and a most vain triumph. For they apply these things to their sacrifice, wherein they fain them selves under the some of bread and wine to offer up to God the father the body and blood of Christ, a propiciatory sacrifice for the quick and the dead. But Ireney and Tertullian speak not of such a sacrifice, but of the oblation of prayers, which the maſſemonging Priest offers not up alone, but the whole cogregation of Christ sanctified in his blood, geuynge thanks in the lords supper to God the father for their free redemption. These holy fathers never knew the sale Masses of these Chananites.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 28.9]Of this same place of S. John the self same Papists go about to prove and to establish the praying to Saints in heaven. Behold, say they, the Saints are said to pray openly in heave. But they need not to pray for them selves, and therefore as intercessors and Patrones they pray for their clients and worshippers in Earth. I answer, that the Saints in deed pray in heaven, but that you adding the kind and manner of praying, do expound it to be intercession, patchyng to a piece of your own, do forge and coutrefet the same, and maliciously and falsely do belie it. S. John here expounds him self, so that he needs not your patchyng. For he adds, and they singe a new song. Yea and he recites the whole form of this song, lest any man should corrupt that which he had said of prayers. And that same form conteyneth praise and blessing or thanks geuyng, and not intercession, or invocation. For certain it is even by the doctrine of the Apostle. 1. Timoth. 2. and to the Philipp. 4. that there be two chief kinds of prayer: invocation and praise or thanks geuyng. But the thing it self plainly proves, that S. John speaks here of the latter, and not of the first.[note 28.10] And where as this place expounds certain Tips, shadows or mysteries of Gods law, by the same we may rather confute the intercession of Saints in Heaven, for their worshippers. For in the law is permitted one only golden Altar of incense. And the same represented a figure of Christ. For one Christ is the mediator and intercessor betwixt God and man. It was not laweful for the people of God, to burn incense, but upon this Altar only. It was not laweful for any man, to prepare or make for him self an odour of those kind of gumes, whereof the divine incense consisted, and to smell to it: as appears in the.30. of Exodus. Why than doe not these understand, that prayers belong to God alone, and that the Saints in Heaven would not smell of such incense? David in the.141. psalm. says, let my prayer be directed as incense in thy sight, the liſtynge up of my hands an evening sacrifice. The Devil desires to have such manner of incenses to be made unto him: As appears in the.4. of Matthew. and in S. Austen of the City of God. But our heavenly Saints, are not Deuilles. Why vnderſtonde they not that this Altar of incense stands now in Heaven on the right hand of the father, and there makes intercession for us: and that for his sake the father is reconciled to us, and we are accepted of God, and that by him alone we must offer up our prayers unto God, which are else abominable? Why see they not the heavenly Saints at this present to attribute all things to the only lamb alone, and to challenge nothing to them selves? Finally that they make no mention of their worshippers: but plainly testify, that the only lamb was and is worthy, which should take the book, &c.
[DRAFT]
[note 28.9] Regarding this same passage from the writings of John, the Roman Catholics attempt to use it to prove and establish the practice of praying to saints in heaven. “Behold,” they say, “the saints are depicted as praying openly in heaven.” But they argue that they do not pray for themselves, and therefore, as intercessors and patrons, they pray for their clients and worshippers on earth. I reply that the saints indeed pray in heaven, but you, by adding the kind and manner of praying, do misinterpret it as intercession, adding a piece of your own invention, and maliciously and falsely distort its meaning. John explains himself here, so that your additions are unnecessary. For he adds, “and they sing a new song.” Indeed, he recites the entire form of this song, lest anyone corrupt what he had said about prayers. And that same form contains praise and blessing or thanksgiving, and not intercession or invocation. It is certain, even according to the Apostle’s doctrine, as found in 1 Timothy 2 and Philippians 4, that there are two chief kinds of prayer: invocation and praise or thanksgiving. But the thing itself plainly shows that John speaks here of the latter, not the former. [note 28.10] And whereas this passage explains certain types, shadows, or mysteries of God’s law, by the same reasoning we may better refute the intercession of saints in heaven for their worshippers. For in the law, only one golden altar of incense was permitted. And this altar represented a figure of Christ. For one Christ is the mediator and intercessor between God and man. It was not lawful for the people of God to burn incense, but only upon this altar. It was not lawful for any man to prepare or make for himself an odor of those kinds of gums from which the divine incense consisted, and to offer it to God, as appears in Exodus 30. Why, then, do these people not understand that prayers belong to God alone, and that the saints in heaven would not offer such incense? David in Psalm 141 says, “Let my prayer be directed as incense in your sight, the lifting up of my hands as an evening sacrifice.” The Devil desires to have such incense made to him, as appears in Matthew 4 and in Augustine’s *City of God*. But our heavenly saints are not devils. Why do they not understand that this altar of incense now stands in heaven on the right hand of the Father, and there makes intercession for us? And that for his sake the Father is reconciled to us, and we are accepted of God, and that through him alone we must offer up our prayers to God, which are otherwise abominable? Why do they not see the heavenly saints at this present attributing all things to the only Lamb alone, and claiming nothing for themselves? Finally, that they make no mention of their worshippers, but plainly testify that the only Lamb was and is worthy, who should take the book, and so on?
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "patchyng to a piece of your own, do forge and coutrefet the same, and maliciously and falsely do belie it" is softened to "adding a piece of your own invention, and maliciously and falsely distort its meaning." The original conveys a stronger accusation of deliberate deception.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "the Roman Catholics attempt to use it" in note 28.9 is an interpretive addition not present in the original. It frames the argument in a modern context.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original’s phrasing "the liſtynge up of my hands an evening sacrifice" is rendered as "the lifting up of my hands as an evening sacrifice." While functionally similar, the original's slightly awkward phrasing contributes to its archaic tone and a certain level of ambiguity that is lost.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "the writings of John" in note 28.9 is an addition. The original simply states "Of this same place of S. John."
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Austen" is correctly modernized to "Augustine."
[FINAL]
[note 28.9] Of this same place of S. John the self same Papists go about to prove and to establish the praying to Saints in heaven. Behold, say they, the Saints are said to pray openly in heave. But they need not to pray for them selves, and therefore as intercessors and Patrones they pray for their clients and worshippers in Earth. I answer, that the Saints in deed pray in heaven, but that you adding the kind and manner of praying, do expound it to be intercession, patchyng to a piece of your own, do forge and counterfeit the same, and maliciously and falsely do belie it. S. John here expounds him self, so that he needs not your patchyng. For he adds, and they singe a new song. Yea and he recites the whole form of this song, lest any man should corrupt that which he had said of prayers. And that same form conteyneth praise and blessing or thanks geuyng, and not intercession, or invocation. For certain it is even by the doctrine of the Apostle. 1. Timoth. 2. and to the Philipp. 4. that there be two chief kinds of prayer: invocation and praise or thanks geuyng. But the thing it self plainly proves, that S. John speaks here of the latter, and not of the first. [note 28.10] And where as this place expounds certain Tips, shadows or mysteries of Gods law, by the same we may rather confute the intercession of Saints in Heaven, for their worshippers. For in the law is permitted one only golden Altar of incense. And the same represented a figure of Christ. For one Christ is the mediator and intercessor betwixt God and man. It was not laweful for the people of God, to burn incense, but upon this Altar only. It was not laweful for any man, to prepare or make for him self an odour of those kind of gumes, whereof the divine incense consisted, and to smell to it: as appears in the.30. of Exodus. Why than doe not these understand, that prayers belong to God alone, and that the Saints in Heaven would not smell of such incense? David in the.141. psalm. says, let my prayer be directed as incense in thy sight, the liſtynge up of my hands an evening sacrifice. The Devil desires to have such manner of incenses to be made unto him: As appears in the.4. of Matthew. and in S. Austen of the City of God. But our heavenly Saints, are not Deuilles. Why vnderſtonde they not that this Altar of incense stands now in Heaven on the right hand of the father, and there makes intercession for us: and that for his sake the father is reconciled to us, and we are accepted of God, and that by him alone we must offer up our prayers unto God, which are else abominable? Why see they not the heavenly Saints at this present to attribute all things to the only lamb alone, and to challenge nothing to them selves? Finally that they make no mention of their worshippers: but plainly testify, that the only lamb was and is worthy, which should take the book, &c.

[ORIGINAL]
And the praise or thanks giving of the heavenly saints he hath called a new song,[note 28.11] which in the Scriptures is no new thing. For the Saints say, that they will singe in Earth unto God a new song, Psalm xxxiii.xcvi xcviii.cxliiii. Isaiah.xlij. And new songs be called these new balades or verses in meter, which are made of some new benefit or noble act done. And because the mind of man is delighted greatly with new benefits, they singe a new song, which with a ioyefull mind praise GOD, and give him thanks with their inward affections. Finallye they singe a new song, which with purified minds and renewed with the Spirit doe laud GOD: which thing was chiefly given to those heavenly Saints. Whereof we learn again howe it behooves our minds to be affected and furniſſhed in the prayers and praises of GOD. This same, says Arethas, I cal a new song, by whose benefit we, which being lighted in all parts of the earth, departing from the antiquity of the law written, and walking in the newnes of life, are taught by the holy ghost to singe a giving of thanks.
[DRAFT]
And the praise or thanksgiving of the heavenly saints he has called a new song, which in the Scriptures is no new thing. For the saints say that they will sing on Earth unto God a new song, Psalm 33, 96, 98, 149. Isaiah 42. New songs are called these new ballads or verses in meter, which are made of some new benefit or noble act done. And because the mind of man is delighted greatly with new benefits, they sing a new song, which with a joyful mind praise God, and give him thanks with their inward affections. Finally, they sing a new song, which with purified minds and renewed by the Spirit do laud God: which thing was chiefly given to those heavenly saints. Whereof we learn again how it behooves our minds to be affected and furnished in the prayers and praises of God. This same, says Arethas, I call a new song, by whose benefit we, which being enlightened in all parts of the earth, departing from the antiquity of the law written, and walking in the newness of life, are taught by the Holy Spirit to sing a giving of thanks.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "singe" changed to "sing" multiple times. While a minor lexical change, "singe" can imply a burning or passionate quality, potentially softening the original's intensity.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The explanation of "new songs" as "balades or verses in meter, which are made of some new benefit or noble act done" feels like an added interpretation. The original doesn't explicitly define the *reason* for new songs beyond human delight in new benefits.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "with the Spirit doe laud GOD" changed to "renewed by the Spirit do laud God." The original phrasing allows for a broader understanding of spiritual agency; the draft's revision is more specific, implying a direct renewal *by* the Spirit.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "Arethas" is correct. "Hierom" should be "Jerome" (not present in this passage). "Austen" should be "Augustine" (not present in this passage). "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus" (not present in this passage).
[FINAL]
And the praise or thanksgiving of the heavenly saints he has called a new song, which in the Scriptures is no new thing. For the saints say that they will sing on Earth unto God a new song, Psalm 33, 96, 98, 149. Isaiah 42. New songs are called these new ballads or verses in meter, which are made of some new benefit or noble act done. And because the mind of man is delighted greatly with new benefits, they sing a new song, which with a joyful mind praise God, and give him thanks with their inward affections. Finally, they sing a new song, which with purified minds and renewed with the Spirit do laud God: which thing was chiefly given to those heavenly saints. Whereof we learn again how it behooves our minds to be affected and furnished in the prayers and praises of God. This same, says Arethas, I call a new song, by whose benefit we, which being enlightened in all parts of the earth, departing from the antiquity of the law written, and walking in the newness of life, are taught by the Holy Spirit to sing a giving of thanks.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 28.14]In the mean time they comende most highly the virtue or effect of Christ's death. For this being rightly understand we are more ready to submit our selves to that governor, whom we know to be our saviour, which loves us dearly, and would have all saved. And the chiefest effect of Christ's death is redemption,[note 28.15] redeminge includes captivity. We were prisoners and servants of sin, of death, and very bondeſlaues of the devil and hell. And the son of God came and toke flesh, and shed his blood (for so also is the manner of redemyng us expressed of the elders) and he hath washed us from our sins, and being purified he hath ransomed us from the power of death, hell, sin and Satan, that now we be of God. Therefore they say expressly, thou hast redeemed us to God. We be therefore of God: the devil hath no more power over us: we are the freemen of Christ, delivered through his blood. 1. Peter. 1. Hebrews. 9. And for as much as we be now of God, to wit justified frely, by his grace, through the blood of Christ, as the apostle says also in the 3. Chapter. to the Romans, we ought to serve God verily in the newnes of spirit, not the flesh and the devil, in the oldenes of the letter and of our flesh. Which the same Apostle discourses more at large in the 6. to the Romans.
[DRAFT]
In the meantime, they commend highly the power or effect of Christ’s death. For this, being rightly understood, we are more ready to submit ourselves to that governor whom we know to be our savior, who loves us dearly and would have all saved. And the chiefest effect of Christ’s death is redemption; redemption includes deliverance from captivity. We were prisoners and servants of sin, of death, and very bondslaves of the devil and hell. And the Son of God came and took flesh, and shed his blood (for so also is the manner of redeeming us expressed by the ancients) and he has washed us from our sins, and being purified, he has ransomed us from the power of death, hell, sin, and Satan, that now we belong to God. Therefore, they say expressly, “You have redeemed us to God.” We belong therefore to God; the devil has no more power over us; we are the freemen of Christ, delivered through his blood. 1 Peter, 1; Hebrews, 9. And inasmuch as we now belong to God, namely, justified freely by his grace through the blood of Christ, as the apostle also says in the 3rd chapter to the Romans, we ought to serve God truly in the newness of spirit, not the flesh and the devil, in the oldness of the letter and of our flesh. Which the same apostle discusses more at length in the 6th chapter to the Romans.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "bondeſlaues" is softened to "bondslaves." While semantically close, "bondeſlaues" carries a stronger sense of being bound and enslaved, a nuance potentially lost.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "expressed by the ancients" replaces "of the elders." This adds a layer of interpretation; the original simply references a tradition without explicitly categorizing it.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "We be therefore of God" is changed to "We belong therefore to God." While functionally similar, the original phrasing has a slightly more profound theological implication of *being* part of God's essence, which "belonging" diminishes.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "elders" should be "ancients" to match the original.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is added.
[FINAL]
In the meantime, they commend highly the power or effect of Christ’s death. For this, being rightly understood, we are more ready to submit ourselves to that governor whom we know to be our savior, who loves us dearly and would have all saved. And the chiefest effect of Christ’s death is redemption; redemption includes deliverance from captivity. We were prisoners and servants of sin, of death, and very bondeſlaues of the devil and hell. And the Son of God came and took flesh, and shed his blood (for so also is the manner of redeeming us expressed of the ancients) and he has washed us from our sins, and being purified, he has ransomed us from the power of death, hell, sin, and Satan, that now we be of God. Therefore, they say expressly, “You have redeemed us to God.” We be therefore of God; the devil has no more power over us; we are the freemen of Christ, delivered through his blood. 1 Peter, 1; Hebrews, 9. And inasmuch as we be now of God, namely, justified freely by his grace through the blood of Christ, as the apostle also says in the 3rd chapter to the Romans, we ought to serve God truly in the newness of spirit, not the flesh and the devil, in the oldness of the letter and of our flesh. Which the same apostle discusses more at length in the 6th chapter to the Romans.

[ORIGINAL]
Of redeption follows an other effect of Christ's death,[note 28.17] for that it makes men justified to God kings and priests. For they that be justified, work rightuouſnes. I have expounded this place concerning the priesthood and kingdom of Christians in the first chapter where you may have it.
[DRAFT]
Following redemption is another effect of Christ's death,[note 28.17] for it makes people righteous before God, kings, and priests. For those who are righteous are workers of righteousness. I have explained this passage concerning the priesthood and kingdom of Christians in the first chapter, where you may find it.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Kings and priests" in the original likely refers to the spiritual roles adopted by believers, not a literal designation. "Kings and priests" in the draft reads as a more literal, potentially hierarchical, interpretation.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The draft's phrasing "makes people righteous before God" adds a layer of explanation not explicitly present in the original. The original simply states "makes men justified to God kings and priests."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original’s phrasing is slightly more open to multiple interpretations regarding the nature of justification. The draft’s more direct wording reduces this ambiguity.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]
Following redemption is another effect of Christ's death,[note 28.17] for it makes men justified to God, kings, and priests. For they that are justified work righteousness. I have explained this passage concerning the priesthood and kingdom of Christians in the first chapter, where you may find it.

[ORIGINAL]
In the fourth place now come the Angels of God also to the Elders and to the beasts,[note 29.1] I mean to the most excellent creatures of God, and together with them praise with an hymn God and the lamb: doubtless for an example to us, that, as I ofttimes say and repeat, we might understand what thing becomes us also.
[DRAFT]
Fourthly, the Angels of God now come to the elders and to the beasts, [note 29.1] that is, to the most excellent creatures of God, and together with them praise God and the Lamb in a hymn; certainly, this is for our example, as I often say and repeat, so that we might understand what is fitting for us as well.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "doubtless" is softened to "certainly," diminishing the original's emphasis.
ADDED INTERPRETATION: "that is" is unnecessary explanatory filler. The original relies on the reader to understand the connection.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "what thing becomes us also" is more open to interpretation regarding duty and appropriate behavior than the draft's "what is fitting for us as well."
[FINAL]
Fourthly, the Angels of God now come to the elders and to the beasts, [note 29.1] I mean to the most excellent creatures of God, and together with them praise God and the Lamb in an hymn; doubtless for an example to us, as I ofttimes say and repeat, we might understand what thing becomes us also.

[ORIGINAL]
Of Angels David in c.iiii. Psalm. speaking emogs other things: which makes, says he, his Angels spirits,[note 29.2] and his ministers a flame of fire. He testifies therefore that the Angels were made or created of God. By their substance he calls them spirits, and by a parable a flame of fire, which is pure, bright, most swift, piercing, and burning. Therefore after their sort and mean the Angelical spirits be altogether such: whom by their office he calls ministers,[note 29.3] to wit of God and man. For S. Paull also to the Hebr. brynging this same place of David, be they not all, says he, ministering spirits, which are sent forth into ministry, for them which be heirs of Salvation? verily understanding men. These things teach us to judge rightly of Angels, and that no man should worship ministers, or any be they never so excellent creatures, for their godly gifts. Neither in deed can the Angels or Saints abide them selves to be worshipped. Here doubtless they attribute all glory to God and to the Lamb, to God three and one, that all we should doe the like. Here is also declared the place, wherein the Angles were, about the Throne, about the beasts, and about the Elders. Therefore they guarded all these places round about as it were a guard. Daniel in times paste saw things not much unlike these in the.7. Chapter. Certainly they stand like ministers and servitors, ready to do service. Angels are said also to be about the godly upon earth, and to attend upon the salvation and ministry of men. In the.34. Psalm. David sings, he being afflicted called upon the lord, and the Lord heard him, and from all his troubles he delivered him. The Angel of the lord pitches his tents about them which fear him (the lord) and he delivers the. And not much unlike things you may read in the.91. Psalm. And thou shalt here note, that those that be afflicted do cal upon the lord, and not the Angels: And that the lord doth hear, and deliver, and for the working thereof uses the ministry of Angels, as his ministers. And like as no man that is well in his wit doth reverence, cal upon, and worship the son, for that God by the same gives great benefits to men. So no man honours, calls upon, and worſhippeth Angels for that God uses their ministry in deliueryng of men.
[DRAFT]
Of Angels David in Psalm 4 speaking among other things: which makes, says he, his Angels spirits, and his ministers a flame of fire. He testifies therefore that the Angels were made or created of God. By their substance he calls them spirits, and by a parable a flame of fire, which is pure, bright, most swift, piercing, and burning. Therefore after their sort and manner the Angelical spirits be altogether such: whom by their office he calls ministers, to wit of God and man. For Paul also to the Hebrews, bringing this same place of David, says, “Are they not all ministering spirits, sent forth to minister for those who will inherit salvation?” truly understanding men. These things teach us to judge rightly of Angels, and that no man should worship ministers, or any, however excellent creatures they may be, for their godly gifts. Neither in deed can the Angels or Saints abide themselves to be worshipped. Here doubtless they attribute all glory to God and to the Lamb, to God three and one, that all we should do the like. Here is also declared the place wherein the Angels were, about the throne, about the beasts, and about the Elders. Therefore they guarded all these places round about as it were a guard. Daniel in times past saw things not much unlike these in chapter 7. Certainly they stand like ministers and servants, ready to do service. Angels are said also to be about the godly upon earth, and to attend upon the salvation and ministry of men. In Psalm 34, David sings, he being afflicted called upon the Lord, and the Lord heard him, and from all his troubles he delivered him. The Angel of the Lord pitches his tents about them which fear him, and he delivers them. And not much unlike things you may read in Psalm 91. And you shall here note that those that are afflicted do call upon the Lord, and not the Angels: And that the Lord doth hear, and deliver, and for the working thereof uses the ministry of Angels, as his ministers. And like as no man that is well in his wit doth reverence, call upon, and worship the son, for that God by the same gives great benefits to men, so no man honors, calls upon, and worships Angels for that God uses their ministry in delivering of men.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "emogs other things" changed to "among other things." While semantically close, "emogs" suggests a more direct engagement or inclusion, potentially softening the original's tone.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "be they never so excellent creatures" is softened to "however excellent creatures they may be." The original phrasing carries a stronger emphasis on the potential for idolatry, even with exceptionally impressive beings.
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "S. Paull" changed to "Paul." While acceptable, the abbreviation "S." (for Saint) was common and its removal subtly alters the original's style and potentially its theological context.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This is a significant error.
5. **MEANING DRIFT:** "verily understanding men" changed to "truly understanding men." While subtle, "verily" carries a stronger sense of certainty and conviction, which is lost in the modernization.
6. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "And not much unlike things you may read in the.91. Psalm" changed to "And not much unlike things you may read in Psalm 91." The original includes the period, which could imply a specific verse or passage, whereas the draft removes it, making it a general reference.
[FINAL]
Of Angels David in Psalm 4 speaking among other things: which makes, says he, his Angels spirits,[note 29.2] and his ministers a flame of fire. He testifies therefore that the Angels were made or created of God. By their substance he calls them spirits, and by a parable a flame of fire, which is pure, bright, most swift, piercing, and burning. Therefore after their sort and manner the Angelical spirits be altogether such: whom by their office he calls ministers,[note 29.3] to wit of God and man. For Paul also to the Hebrews, bringing this same place of David, says, “Are they not all ministering spirits, sent forth to minister for those who will inherit salvation?” verily understanding men. These things teach us to judge rightly of Angels, and that no man should worship ministers, or any be they never so excellent creatures, for their godly gifts. Neither in deed can the Angels or Saints abide themselves to be worshipped. Here doubtless they attribute all glory to God and to the Lamb, to God three and one, that all we should do the like. Here is also declared the place wherein the Angels were, about the throne, about the beasts, and about the Elders. Therefore they guarded all these places round about as it were a guard. Daniel in times past saw things not much unlike these in chapter 7. Certainly they stand like ministers and servants, ready to do service. Angels are said also to be about the godly upon earth, and to attend upon the salvation and ministry of men. [ORIGINAL]
For after this, the proper duty or office of Angles is touched: They singe praises to God, and comende the labe of God, and that with a load voice. It skills not greatly wheter with a lowe or an high voice thou ſingeſt praises to the Lord: but for as much as they that cry with a load voice, are for the most part sore moved, as overwhelmed with great sorrow, or with great gladness rejoicing, therefore shall we praise God with a load voice, in case with a stout spirit, and with the inward affection of the heart we shall praise God.
[DRAFT]
Following this, the proper duty of the angels is addressed: they sing praises to God and commend God’s work, and that with a loud voice. It matters little whether you sing praises to the Lord with a low or a high voice; but because those who cry with a loud voice are often deeply moved, whether overwhelmed with great sorrow or rejoicing with great gladness, therefore we shall praise God with a loud voice, if with a steadfast spirit and with the inward affection of the heart we praise God.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "It skills not greatly wheter with a lowe or an high voice thou ſingeſt praises to the Lord" is softened to "It matters little whether you sing praises to the Lord with a low or a high voice." While functionally similar, the original carries a slightly more dismissive tone, suggesting the difference is unimportant. The draft's phrasing is more neutral.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None apparent.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None apparent.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: None present in this excerpt.
5. HALLUCINATION: None apparent.
6. PROPER NOUNS: None present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
Following this, the proper duty of the angels is addressed: they sing praises to God and commend God’s work, and that with a loud voice. It skills not greatly whether with a low or an high voice thou singest praises to the Lord; but for as much as they that cry with a loud voice are for the most part sore moved, as overwhelmed with great sorrow, or with great gladness rejoicing, therefore shall we praise God with a loud voice, in case with a stout spirit, and with the inward affection of the heart we shall praise God.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 29.8]You marvel, I wot well, howe all creatures, ſyns many of them be void of reason, and insensible can praise God. How be it this figure proſopopeia that is the feigning of persons is very common with all the prophets, and chiefly with David, praise the him Sun and Moon, says he, praise him the bright stars. Praise the Lord from the earth the Dragons and all deep places, fire, hayle, snow, Iſe, &c. And by such manners of speaking the Prophets would encourage and ſtire up men to praise God: saying that creatures which have no life, do after their manner praise God, see that you after your manner doe praise God in Hymns and spiritual Pſalmes. And in deed David shows a plain reason, why he commands bodies that have no life to praise God, let them praise, says he, the name of the lord, why? because he commanded, and they were created. As though he should say: they be his creatures, and in that they remain yet, they have it of him: therefore let them make the name of God gloriouſe, as of their maker and preserver. And he signified also the manner of praising, where he adds, he hath ordained them, that they should endure for ever: he gave them an ordenaunce, neither is it transgressed. As if he should have said. Where they neglect no part of those things, whereunto they are made, but are ready in their place, order, and time, and do their duty exceedingly well, do they not preach unto men the wonderful wisdom and power of God? For in an other Pſalme also david says, the heavens show forth the glory of God, and the firmament declares the works of his hands. &c. Thus I say the creatures with out life do praise and comende the name of God unto men, what time they are moved, work wonderfully, and obediently do the thing were unto they are appointed.
[DRAFT]
You marvel, I well know, how all creatures, even many that lack reason and feeling, can praise God. However, this figure of personification—the feigning of persons—is very common among all the prophets, and especially with David, who says, “Praise him, sun and moon; praise him, bright stars.” “Praise the Lord from the earth, sea monsters and all deep places, fire, hail, snow, and ice.” And by such ways of speaking, the prophets would encourage and stir up people to praise God, saying that creatures which have no life, after their manner, praise God; see that you, after your manner, do praise God in hymns and spiritual psalms. Indeed, David shows a plain reason why he commands bodies that have no life to praise God: “Let them praise the name of the Lord,” he says, “because he commanded, and they were created.” As though he should say: they are his creatures, and in that they remain, they have it of him; therefore let them make the name of God glorious, as of their maker and preserver. And he signified also the manner of praising, where he adds, “He hath ordained them that they should endure forever; he gave them an ordinance, neither is it transgressed.” As if he should have said: Where they neglect no part of those things for which they were made, but are ready in their place, order, and time, and do their duty exceedingly well, do they not preach unto men the wonderful wisdom and power of God? For in another psalm also, David says, “The heavens show forth the glory of God, and the firmament declares the works of his hands.” Thus I say the creatures without life do praise and commend the name of God unto men, when they are moved, work wonderfully, and obediently do the thing unto which they are appointed.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "Dragons and all deep places" changed to "sea monsters and all deep places." While "sea monsters" isn't inherently incorrect, "dragons" held specific cultural and theological connotations in 1561 that "sea monsters" lacks, potentially softening the original's imagery.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The insertion of em-dashes around "the feigning of persons" and the subsequent explanation ("the feigning of persons—the feigning of persons—") adds a layer of analysis not present in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original’s phrasing "whereunto they are made" is altered to "for which they were made." While semantically similar, the original phrasing allowed for a broader interpretation of purpose, while the draft’s phrasing is slightly more restrictive.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes to theological terms.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No hallucinated content.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** No errors in proper noun modernization.
[FINAL]
You marvel, I well know, how all creatures, even many that lack reason and feeling, can praise God. However, this figure of prosopopeia—the feigning of persons—is very common with all the prophets, and especially with David, who says, “Praise him, sun and moon; praise him, bright stars.” “Praise the Lord from the earth, dragons and all deep places, fire, hail, snow, and ice.” And by such manners of speaking, the prophets would encourage and stir up people to praise God, saying that creatures which have no life, after their manner, praise God; see that you, after your manner, do praise God in hymns and spiritual psalms. Indeed, David shows a plain reason why he commands bodies that have no life to praise God: “Let them praise the name of the Lord,” he says, “because he commanded, and they were created.” As though he should say: they are his creatures, and in that they remain, they have it of him; therefore let them make the name of God gloriouſe, as of their maker and preserver. And he signified also the manner of praising, where he adds, “He hath ordained them that they should endure forever; he gave them an ordenaunce, neither is it transgressed.” As if he should have said: Where they neglect no part of those things whereunto they are made, but are ready in their place, order, and time, and do their duty exceedingly well, do they not preach unto men the wonderful wisdom and power of God? For in another Pſalme also, David says, “The heavens show forth the glory of God, and the firmament declares the works of his hands.” Thus I say the creatures with out life do praise and comende the name of God unto men, when they are moved, work wonderfully, and obediently do the thing were unto they are appointed.

[ORIGINAL]
The four beasts singe to it Amen,[note 29.10] either so confirmyng the Hymn of the creatures, or thus declaring their consent with them. To the intent we should with one mind pray together and praise God blessed for evermore. With these are moreover confuted the deſſenſions of men. The Lord allows the concorde and agreement of men, and requires utterly, especially in prayers and godly praises. For he co maunds in the Gospel to lay down thine oblation, which thou would offer, in case thou doest remebre any discord betwixt thee and thy brother, to go unto him, and to renew amity, and than to return to thine offering: which in the prophets is called an abomination, in case it be offered of minds possessed with rancour and malice. &c.
[DRAFT]
The four beasts sing to it Amen,[note 29.10], either confirming the hymn of the creatures, or thus declaring their consent with them. To the intent that we should with one mind pray together and praise God blessed forevermore. With these are moreover refuted the dispensations of men. The Lord allows the concord and agreement of men, and requires utterly, especially in prayers and godly praises. For he commands in the Gospel to lay down thine offering, which thou wouldest offer, in case thou dost remember any discord between thee and thy brother, to go unto him, and to renew amity, and then to return to thine offering: which in the prophets is called an abomination, in case it be offered of minds possessed with rancor and malice. &c.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "deſſenſions" changed to "dispensations." This alters the meaning from arguments or disagreements to divine arrangements, a significant shift.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "co maunds" is archaic but understandable. "commands" loses the archaic flavor and subtly alters the tone.
THEOLOGICAL TERMS: "dispensations" is a loaded term that wasn't necessarily present in the original's intended meaning.
[FINAL]
The four beasts sing to it Amen,[note 29.10], either confirming the hymn of the creatures, or thus declaring their consent with them. To the intent that we should with one mind pray together and praise God blessed forevermore. With these are moreover refuted the defenses of men. The Lord allows the concord and agreement of men, and requires utterly, especially in prayers and godly praises. For he co maunds in the Gospel to lay down thine offering, which thou wouldest offer, in case thou dost remember any discord between thee and thy brother, to go unto him, and to renew amity, and then to return to thine offering: which in the prophets is called an abomination, in case it be offered of minds possessed with rancor and malice. &c.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 30.3]But before all things S. John is excited (and in him all we) to be attentife. And one, that is to wit the first of the beasts doth excite him. One of the Sabbat is set for the first day in the weke, that same is verily the sunday. The voice of the beast is like unto thonder. Whereby is signified that here is treated of great and most weighty matters. For most great and terrible things follow, which shake the whole world. Therefore let us not play the ſlepy sluggards, let us not be blind and deaf. Doubtless the ſloughtfulnes of our time is such, that we little consider the works of God and what is done in our time. The storks, swallows and Turtels, and the rest of living things pass us, which full well observe their time. Therefore are we here well stirred up, that we should not be slothful, but should mark what things are declared and showed us of the Lord.
[DRAFT]
But above all, Saint John is stirred and eager (and in him, all of us should be) to be attentive. And one, that is to say, the first of the beasts, excites him. One of the Sabbath is set for the first day of the week, which is truly Sunday. The voice of the beast is like unto thunder, signifying that great and weighty matters are being treated. For most great and terrible things follow, which shake the whole world. Therefore, let us not play the sleepy sluggards, let us not be blind and deaf. Doubtless, the slothfulness of our time is such that we little consider the works of God and what is done in our time. The storks, swallows, and turtles, and the rest of living things pass us by, which carefully observe their time. Therefore, let us be stirred up here, that we should not be slothful, but should mark what things are declared and shown to us by the Lord.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "excited (and in him all we)" changed to "stirred and eager (and in him, all of us should be)" subtly shifts the tone from a sense of divine prompting to a more generalized exhortation.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "signifying that great and weighty matters are being treated" is an interpretation not present in the original. The original simply states the voice is "whereby is signified," leaving the signification open.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "One, that is to wit the first of the beasts doth excite him" is somewhat enigmatic. The draft's "that is to say, the first of the beasts, excites him" clarifies it too directly, potentially losing the original's intended mystification.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
But above all, Saint John is excited (and in him all we) to be attentive. And one, that is to wit the first of the beasts doth excite him. One of the Sabbath is set for the first day in the week, that same is verily the sunday. The voice of the beast is like unto thunder, whereby is signified that here is treated of great and most weighty matters. For most great and terrible things follow, which shake the whole world. Therefore let us not play the sleepy sluggards, let us not be blind and deaf. Doubtless the slothfulnes of our time is such, that we little consider the works of God and what is done in our time. The storks, swallows and Turtels, and the rest of living things pass us, which full well observe their time. Therefore are we here well stirred up, that we should not be slothful, but should mark what things are declared and showed us of the Lord.
