[note 30.4]And when S. John had diligently marked what was don, he sees the lamb, Christ I mean our redemer, open one seal, that is to say the first: And straight ways came forth a white horse, on whom he that sate, had a bow bent, and an Arrow in it. To him was given a crown, and he wet forth conqueryng, that he might overcome. This is the vision: the exposition whereof is easy. For the Lord says, that he will declare the destinies of the church.

[ORIGINAL]
To the same is given a Crown,[note 30.8] to wit a kingdom and all power of ruling. For David propheciyng before said, the Lord shall send forth the rod of his power out of Zion, to rule among thine enemies. Moreover there is given him a crown, that he may crown such as serve him faithfully. And it is a phrase of speaking, and he went forth conqueryng, that he might overcome: for that which is, he that went forth is a conqueror, and to this end went forth, that he might overcome. For it signifies that Christ will advance the preaching of his word through out the world, no man being able to resist, and even in despite also of Hell gates. For the word of the Lord endures for ever.
[DRAFT]
To him is given a crown, namely a kingdom and all power of ruling. For David prophesied beforehand, saying, “The Lord will send forth the rod of his power out of Zion, to rule among your enemies.” Moreover, a crown is given to him, that he may crown those who serve him faithfully. And it is a figure of speech, and he went forth conquering, that he might overcome: for he who went forth is a conqueror, and for this purpose went forth, that he might overcome. For it signifies that Christ will advance the preaching of his word throughout the world, no one being able to resist, and even in defiance of the gates of Hell. For the word of the Lord endures forever.

[ORIGINAL]
And this place teaches,[note 30.9] that the Church shall be always in the world, and likewise always the truth preached, though the enemies bowels burst. But if we read over the story of the Church, we shall better understand all things, and shall perceive that this Prognostication hath always ben most certain. Christ was ones through the ministry of the word showed to the world by the Apostles, and the matter proceeded most luckily, howe much some ever the most mighty of this world resisted the same. The thing is wonderful, in case those five hundred years be considered, which immediately after the incarnation of our Lord are accounted. In them went forth the conqueror that he might overcome: And overcame in deed, the whole world receyuyng Christ, and worſhippyng him. Sins those years, as before also, certain ſedes of errors began to be sown abroad. The Bishops began to contend for the supremacy, and who should be the universal head of the Church in earth: They began to reason of the use of Images in the Church, and brought them into Churches in deed, as also they called the Biſſhoppe of Rome the supreme and general head of the Church in earth. And mighty Princes, and in a manner the whole state of learned men conspired in these opinions: but he hath vanquished, which went forth, that he might vanquish. He had in his church innumerable, which bowed not their knees before this Baal. A thousand years after the incarnation of Christ, the Biſhoppes began to profanely to pollute the lords supper: and other undefiled doctrines of faith: but what I pray you did they prevail by so many counsels, determinations, and endeavours most ernest? he that went forth to overcome, hath overcome. That white Horse hath stoutly invaded to the salvation of many. For how great battles in these last five hodreth years the godly and learned me have sustained against the Popes and Bishops, stories bear witness. At this day also appears through out the whole world, howe luckily yet that white horse goes forward, which hath pierced even until our time. The Gospel is believed, neither can that faith be extinguished with any waters or fires.
[DRAFT]
And this place teaches that the Church shall be always in the world, and likewise always the truth preached, though the enemies’ hearts burst. But if we read over the story of the Church, we shall better understand all things, and shall perceive that this prophecy has always been most certain. Christ was once through the ministry of the word showed to the world by the Apostles, and the matter proceeded most successfully, however much some of the most powerful in this world resisted it. The thing is wonderful, if those five hundred years are considered, which immediately after the incarnation of our Lord are accounted. In them went forth the conqueror that he might overcome: and overcame in deed, the whole world receiving Christ and worshipping him. Since those years, as before also, certain seeds of errors began to be sown abroad. The bishops began to contend for supremacy, and who should be the universal head of the Church on earth: they began to reason of the use of images in the Church, and brought them into churches in deed, as also they called the bishop of Rome the supreme and general head of the Church on earth. And mighty princes, and in a manner the whole state of learned men conspired in these opinions: but he has vanquished, which went forth that he might vanquish. He had in his church innumerable, who bowed not their knees before this Baal. A thousand years after the incarnation of Christ, the bishops began to profanely pollute the Lord’s Supper and other undefiled doctrines of faith: but what, I pray you, did they prevail by so many councils, determinations, and earnest endeavors? He that went forth to overcome has overcome. That white horse has stoutly advanced to the salvation of many. For how great battles the godly and learned have sustained against the Popes and bishops in these last five hundred years, stories bear witness. At this day also appears throughout the whole world, how successfully yet that white horse goes forward, which has pierced even until our time. The Gospel is believed, neither can that faith be extinguished with any waters or fires.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "bowels burst" changed to "hearts burst." While conveying a similar sentiment, "bowels burst" carries a more visceral, potentially hyperbolic, and scripturally resonant meaning absent in "hearts burst."
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "worſhippyng him" changed to "worshipping him." The original spelling, while archaic, retains a degree of ambiguity regarding the intensity or nature of the worship. The modernized spelling removes this nuance.
3. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** "lords supper" changed to "Lord’s Supper." While capitalization is a modernization choice, it subtly shifts the perceived importance and formality of the sacrament.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Biſſhoppe" changed to "bishop." While a standard modernization, it loses the original's specific spelling which might have had regional or idiosyncratic significance.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content is added.
[FINAL]
And this place teaches[note 30.9] that the Church shall be always in the world, and likewise always the truth preached, though the enemies’ bowels burst. But if we read over the story of the Church, we shall better understand all things, and shall perceive that this prophecy has always been most certain. Christ was once through the ministry of the word showed to the world by the Apostles, and the matter proceeded most successfully, however much some of the most powerful in this world resisted it. The thing is wonderful, if those five hundred years are considered, which immediately after the incarnation of our Lord are accounted. In them went forth the conqueror that he might overcome: and overcame in deed, the whole world receiving Christ and worshipping him. Since those years, as before also, certain seeds of errors began to be sown abroad. The bishops began to contend for supremacy, and who should be the universal head of the Church on earth: they began to reason of the use of images in the Church, and brought them into churches in deed, as also they called the Biſſhoppe of Rome the supreme and general head of the Church on earth. And mighty princes, and in a manner the whole state of learned men conspired in these opinions: but he has vanquished, which went forth that he might vanquish. He had in his church innumerable, who bowed not their knees before this Baal. A thousand years after the incarnation of Christ, the bishops began to profanely pollute the Lord’s Supper and other undefiled doctrines of faith: but what, I pray you, did they prevail by so many councils, determinations, and earnest endeavors? He that went forth to overcome has overcome. That white horse has stoutly advanced to the salvation of many. For how great battles the godly and learned have sustained against the Popes and bishops in these last five hundred years, stories bear witness. At this day also appears throughout the whole world, how successfully yet that white horse goes forward, which has pierced even until our time. The Gospel is believed, neither can that faith be extinguished with any waters or fires.

[ORIGINAL]
Thou make exception, that they were heretics which resisted the bishop and See of Rome in these.500. last years, as Bertram, John Scot ſurnamed Dunſe, Berengarius, Arnoldus Brixianus, Waldo Wicleffe, and Huſſe, Luther, and Zwinglius, and such other men of the same sort, moreover certain of these were overcome also, and put to death by the Pope. I answer, that as men they might err in many things: but in those things wherein with the Scripture they consent against the See of Rome, I affirm that they erred not, but said the truth. Whereupon it is certain that Christ overcame by them. What time Micheas, Helias, Zacharias, Amos, Ieremias and others preached by the word of God against Idols and worshippers of Idols, they were also condemned for ſeditioufe and heretics: yea and certain of them were taken out of the way: but was the verity vanquished? Antichrist is said that he should have good fortune, and that he should punish and afflict the strong, and the people of God: but men being ministers may be oppressed, the ministry never decays. S. Paull says that he is bounden for the Gospels sake, but the Gospel not to be bouden. Therefore hath he overcome hitherto, and shall overcome still, which went forth that he might conquer. They stumble upon this conqueror as at the stone of offence, who so ever, and what so ever they be, which seek to interrupt the plain course of the Gospel.
[DRAFT]
You make exception that they were heretics who resisted the bishop and See of Rome in these last 500 years, as Bertram, John Scot, surnamed Duns, Berengarius, Arnoldus Brixianus, Waldo, Wycliffe, and Hus, Luther, and Zwinglius, and such other men of the same sort; moreover, certain of these were also overcome and put to death by the Pope. I answer that, as men, they might err in many things; but in those things wherein with the Scripture they consent against the See of Rome, I affirm that they erred not, but said the truth. Whereupon it is certain that Christ overcame by them. What time Micah, Elijah, Zechariah, Amos, Jeremiah, and others preached by the word of God against idols and worshippers of idols, they also were condemned as seditious and heretics; yea, and certain of them were taken out of the way. But was the truth vanquished? Antichrist is said to have good fortune, and to punish and afflict the strong and the people of God; but men being ministers may be oppressed, the ministry never decays. Saint Paul says that he is bound for the Gospel’s sake, but the Gospel is not to be bound. Therefore he has overcome hitherto, and shall overcome still, which went forth that he might conquer. They stumble upon this conqueror as at the stone of offense, whoever and whatever they be, which seek to interrupt the plain course of the Gospel.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **Meaning Drift:** "bounden" changed to "bound." While functionally similar, "bounden" carries a sense of obligation and duty that "bound" lacks, subtly altering the theological weight of Paul's situation.
2. **Hallucination:** "Saint Paul" added. The original uses "S. Paull."
3. **Proper Nouns:** "Micheas" to "Micah," "Helias" to "Elijah," "Zacharias" to "Zechariah," "Ieremias" to "Jeremiah." These are all correct.
4. **Theological Terms:** No changes or removals detected.
[FINAL]
You make exception that they were heretics who resisted the bishop and See of Rome in these last 500 years, as Bertram, John Scot, surnamed Duns, Berengarius, Arnoldus Brixianus, Waldo, Wycliffe, and Hus, Luther, and Zwinglius, and such other men of the same sort; moreover, certain of these were also overcome and put to death by the Pope. I answer that, as men, they might err in many things; but in those things wherein with the Scripture they consent against the See of Rome, I affirm that they erred not, but said the truth. Whereupon it is certain that Christ overcame by them. What time Micah, Elijah, Zechariah, Amos, Jeremiah, and others preached by the word of God against idols and worshippers of idols, they also were condemned as seditious and heretics; yea, and certain of them were taken out of the way. But was the truth vanquished? Antichrist is said to have good fortune, and to punish and afflict the strong and the people of God; but men being ministers may be oppressed, the ministry never decays. S. Paull says that he is bounden for the Gospels sake, but the Gospel is not to be bouden. Therefore he has overcome hitherto, and shall overcome still, which went forth that he might conquer. They stumble upon this conqueror as at the stone of offense, whoever and whatever they be, which seek to interrupt the plain course of the Gospel.

[ORIGINAL]
Moreover what time the second seal should be opened the second beast,[note 30.10] to wit the Ore or Calfe exhorts again S. John to attentiuenes, and that we should consider what is propouded unto us. And now comes forth the red horse, whose colour is somewhat like fire: there sits also on him a rider, to whom power is given to disturb peace in earth, and that men should kill one an other. For there is given him a great sword. The red horse signifies the state of wars,[note 30.11] full of fire and blood. He that sits on this horse is Mars, or rather the father of lies, I mean the Devil, which was a murderer from the beginning. He gathers to him the dregs of men to make civil commotions, for the wars, destruction, firyng, slaughter, and desolation. You see from whence the breaking of peace is, which God hates. And we hear howe it is given him: Mark given, by the just judgment of God to be permitted, that troublyng all peace, he should take it away, and set men together by the ears, that one may wound and kill an other. For so we read in the.1. of Iob, howe Satan had power given him of God against Iob. Unto bloody soldiers is given a great sword, great power to hurt, a wonderful force of fighting: As also Nahum expounds it. Neither is it a rare thing in the scriptures for Monarchs, tirannes, and mighty men of war to be called, a sword. For so Ezekiel called Nabuchodonoſor: and Isaiah called Sinacherib king of Aſſyrians a whetstone.
[DRAFT]
Moreover, concerning the time when the second seal should be opened, the second beast,[note 30.10] namely, the Ore or Calf, exhorts again Saint John to attentiveness, and that we should consider what is proposed to us. And now comes forth the red horse, whose color is somewhat like fire; there sits also on him a rider, to whom power is given to disturb peace on earth, and that men should kill one another. For there is given him a great sword. The red horse signifies the state of wars, full of fire and blood. He who sits on this horse is Mars, or rather the father of lies, that is, the Devil, who was a murderer from the beginning. He gathers to him the dregs of men to make civil commotions, for the wars, destruction, burning, slaughter, and desolation. You see from whence the breaking of peace is, which God hates. And we hear how it is given him: Mark given, by the just judgment of God to be permitted, that troubling all peace, he should take it away, and set men together by the ears, that one may wound and kill another. For so we read in the first chapter of Job, how Satan had power given him of God against Job. To bloody soldiers is given a great sword, great power to hurt, a wonderful force of fighting, as also Nahum explains it. Neither is it a rare thing in the scriptures for monarchs, tyrants, and mighty men of war to be called a sword. For so Ezekiel called Nebuchadnezzar; and Isaiah called Sennacherib, king of Assyria, a whetstone.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Ore or Calfe" is rendered as "the Ore or Calf" - the original phrasing suggests a question or uncertainty about the identity, which is lost in the definite article.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "that is, the Devil" is an unnecessary addition after "the father of lies." The original text's association is clear without this explanatory phrase.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "Mark given" remains unchanged, but the original's ambiguity regarding whether "Mark" is a person or a reference to the Gospel of Mark is preserved. The draft does nothing to clarify, which is appropriate, but the lack of change should be noted.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: Nabuchodonoſor → Nebuchadnezzar is a correct modernization. Sinacherib → Sennacherib is a correct modernization. Hieronymus is missing from the original, so no correction needed. Austen, Ireney, etc. are also absent.
[FINAL]
Moreover what time the second seal should be opened the second beast,[note 30.10] to wit the Ore or Calfe exhorts again S. John to attentivenes, and that we should consider what is propouded unto us. And now comes forth the red horse, whose colour is somewhat like fire: there sits also on him a rider, to whom power is given to disturb peace in earth, and that men should kill one an other. For there is given him a great sword. The red horse signifies the state of wars,[note 30.11] full of fire and blood. He that sits on this horse is Mars, or rather the father of lies, I mean the Devil, which was a murderer from the beginning. He gathers to him the dregs of men to make civil commotions, for the wars, destruction, firyng, slaughter, and desolation. You see from whence the breaking of peace is, which God hates. And we hear howe it is given him: Mark given, by the just judgment of God to be permitted, that troublyng all peace, he should take it away, and set men together by the ears, that one may wound and kill an other. For so we read in the.1. of Iob, howe Satan had power given him of God against Iob. Unto bloody soldiers is given a great sword, great power to hurt, a wonderful force of fighting: As also Nahum expounds it. Neither is it a rare thing in the scriptures for Monarchs, tirannes, and mighty men of war to be called, a sword. For so Ezekiel called Nabuchodonoſor: and Isaiah called Sinacherib king of Aſſyrians a whetstone.
---
[ORIGINAL]
Let us peruse over stories, and see and such wars be not found, wherein men have slain themselves with mutual wounds, and have killed one an other like beasts. If you will read Herodiane, Oroſius, and other good Hiſtoriographers, you may find that the Roman Emperors have ben troubled with most grievous wars, for none other cause, than that they refused peace offered to them by the gospel. For none other cause was Rome it self at the last taken by the Weſtgothians, of the Eaſtgothians burnt and destroyed. The lord had give them Christen Princes: but they loved more Idols. For Simmachus governor of the city was so bold to require a restitution of Idolatry. I speak nothing now of Athila, nothing of the Persian and Affricane wars. And what time there was a wonderful strife emogs the Bishops about the supremacy, the Saracenes sprang up and became mighty. After the Thousand year began the holy war, which as it was most bloody, so was it of longest continuance. Never any such war was made in all the world. Boniface the.8. instituted first the year of Iubiley, a most wicked man, who also did exhibit him self to be scene of the people both Pope and Emperor. But the same year of a thousand and three hundred, wherein he did these things, arroſe up in Asia the whip or scourge of God Ottoman, the original of themperours of Turks which reign at this day. For so when Solomon builded places of Idolatry, his enemies sprang up, which wonderfully vexed and afflicted the kingdom of Solomon. What wars are made now a days, and what be the causes of wars, all wise men do see. We will not receive the peaceable gospel: It is reason therefore, that the Turkiſſh Armies should invade us, that we may both feel Antichrist to be a stout warrior, and may all abhor and deteſte him.
[DRAFT]
Let us peruse stories and see if such wars can be found, in which men have slain themselves with mutual wounds, and have killed one another like beasts. If you will read Herodotus, Orosius, and other good historians, you may find that the Roman Emperors were troubled with most grievous wars, for no other cause than that they refused peace offered to them by the gospel. For no other cause was Rome itself at the last taken by the Visigoths, and burnt and destroyed by the Ostrogoths. The Lord had given them Christian princes, but they loved idols more. For Simmachus, governor of the city, was so bold as to require a restoration of idolatry. I speak nothing now of Attila, nothing of the Persian and African wars. And when there was a wonderful strife among the bishops about supremacy, the Saracens sprang up and became mighty. After the thousand years began the holy war, which was as bloody as it was long. Never was such a war made in all the world. Boniface VIII instituted first the year of Jubilee, a most wicked man, who also exhibited himself to be seen by the people, both as Pope and Emperor. But in the same year of one thousand three hundred, in Asia arose Ottoman, the scourge of God, the origin of the emperors of Turks who reign at this day. For so when Solomon built places of idolatry, his enemies sprang up, which wonderfully vexed and afflicted the kingdom of Solomon. What wars are made nowadays, and what are the causes of wars, all wise men see. We will not receive the peaceable gospel; it is reason therefore that Turkish armies should invade us, that we may both feel Antichrist to be a stout warrior, and may all abhor and detest him.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **Meaning Drift:** "Let us peruse over stories" changed to "Let us peruse stories." While seemingly minor, "over" implies a careful and thorough examination, a nuance lost in the draft.
2. **Added Interpretation:** "The lord had give them Christen Princes" changed to "The Lord had given them Christian princes." The original phrasing carries a slightly more active, providential tone, suggesting divine intervention. The draft's wording is more passive.
3. **Lost Ambiguity:** "Weſtgothians" changed to "Visigoths" and "Eaſtgothians" changed to "Ostrogoths." While the modern spellings are correct, the original’s slightly archaic spelling may have been intentional, reflecting a specific historical context or source. The draft removes this potential ambiguity.
4. **Hallucination:** No content is added.
5. **Proper Nouns:** "Oroſius" changed to "Orosius." Correct. "Athila" changed to "Attila." Correct. "Simmachus" changed to "Simmachus." Correct.
6. **Theological Terms:** "Antichrist" remains unchanged. "Apocalypse," "dispensation," "sacrament" are not present in the original and therefore not altered.
[FINAL]
Let us peruse stories and see if such wars can be found, in which men have slain themselves with mutual wounds, and have killed one another like beasts. If you will read Herodotus, Orosius, and other good historians, you may find that the Roman Emperors were troubled with most grievous wars, for no other cause than that they refused peace offered to them by the gospel. For no other cause was Rome itself at the last taken by the Visigoths, of the Ostrogothians burnt and destroyed. The lord had give them Christen Princes, but they loved Idols more. For Simmachus, governor of the city, was so bold to require a restitution of Idolatry. I speak nothing now of Attila, nothing of the Persian and Affricane wars. And what time there was a wonderful strife emogs the Bishops about the supremacy, the Saracenes sprang up and became mighty. After the Thousand year began the holy war, which as it was most bloody, so was it of longest continuance. Never any such war was made in all the world. Boniface the.8. instituted first the year of Iubiley, a most wicked man, who also did exhibit him self to be scene of the people both Pope and Emperor. But the same year of a thousand and three hundred, wherein he did these things, arroſe up in Asia the whip or scourge of God Ottoman, the original of themperours of Turks which reign at this day. [ORIGINAL]
Christ exalted aboue all things,[note 31.1] and Lord of all in heaven and in earth, opens the seals of the book divine, that is to wit disposes and governs with great rightuouſnes the ordenaunces and judgements of God: and first in deed he gives a proſperouſe course to the preaching of the gospel, sending always faythefull ministers preaching the Gospel of the kingdom of God, peace, and concorde. But for as much as evil men do contemn the Evangelical peace, they are certainly worthy to be molested with cruel wars. Therefore the lamb opens the second seal, and there rush out cruel wars, slaughters, ſeditions and robberies.
[DRAFT]
Christ, exalted above all things, and Lord of all in heaven and on earth, opens the seals of the divine book, that is to say, he disposes and governs with great righteousness the ordinances and judgments of God. First, indeed, he gives a prosperous course to the preaching of the gospel, sending always faithful ministers preaching the Gospel of the kingdom of God, peace, and concord. But because evil men despise the Evangelical peace, they are certainly worthy to be afflicted with cruel wars. Therefore, the Lamb opens the second seal, and there rush out cruel wars, slaughters, seditions, and robberies.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "molested" changed to "afflicted." While related, "molested" carries a sense of unwarranted interference or harassment, which might be subtly different from the more general "afflicted."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: None altered.
5. HALLUCINATION: None.
6. PROPER NOUNS: None present.
[FINAL]
Christ, exalted above all things,[note 31.1] and Lord of all in heaven and on earth, opens the seals of the divine book, that is to wit he disposes and governs with great righteousness the ordinances and judgments of God: and first in deed he gives a prosperous course to the preaching of the gospel, sending always faithful ministers preaching the Gospel of the kingdom of God, peace, and concord. But for as much as evil men do contemn the Evangelical peace, they are certainly worthy to be molested with cruel wars. Therefore, the Lamb opens the second seal, and there rush out cruel wars, slaughters, seditions, and robberies.

[ORIGINAL]
The rider of this horse holds in his hand a balance? [illegible],[note 31.5] with two scales hanging at either end of the beam, which we cal a pair of weightes. Arethas says that a balance is a token of right and equity. For thou hast sat, says David upon thy Throne which judge rightuouſnes: therefore is a balance the judgment of the just judgment of God. Arethas hath not alleged these things amiss, how be it we ought rather to prefer the exposition of S. John him self. For a voice is heard from the midst of the beasts, which expounds to us the balance. For it sounds, a measure[note 31.6] of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny. And this measured called Choinix, signifies a diet or daily meat: as Eraſmus hath in his proverb, sit not upon thy measure. The same in his annotations upon this place: Choinix, says he, is a measure of wheat, or other breadcorne, which is sufficient for one days meat. Budaeus thinks that it weighs iiii. pound, Pollux. iii. The word therefore signifies, that a very little meat shall cost a great price, and yet not to be gotten for money. Which chau ceth in the time of famine. What the Roman penny is worth Budaeus shows: we understand by it plainly a great price. Therefore two things are signified, ſcarſitie or derth of corn, and famine. Dearth reiſeth the price beyond reason. Famine hath nothing to by, though he hath never so much money lying by him: but hongreth, waunteth, pines, and at the last miserably, consumes to naught: wherein verily dearth and famine do differ. The Germanes discern them by several words calling dearth ſcarſetie, and famine, honger. Yet are they for the most part indiuiſible.
[DRAFT]
The rider of this horse holds in his hand a balance, with two scales hanging at either end of the beam, which we call a pair of weights. Arethas says that a balance is a token of right and equity. For you have sat upon your throne which judges righteousness, therefore a balance is the judgment of the just judgment of God. Arethas has not alleged these things amiss, but we ought rather to prefer the exposition of Saint John himself. For a voice is heard from the midst of the beasts, which expounds to us the balance. For it sounds, a measure of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny. And this measure, called Choinix, signifies a diet or daily meat, as Erasmus has in his proverb, “Sit not upon your measure.” The same in his annotations upon this place: Choinix, he says, is a measure of wheat, or other breadcorn, which is sufficient for one day’s meat. Budaeus thinks that it weighs four pounds, Pollux three. The word therefore signifies that a very little meat shall cost a great price, and yet not be gotten for money, which happens in the time of famine. What the Roman penny is worth Budaeus shows: we understand by it plainly a great price. Therefore two things are signified, scarcity of corn, and famine. Scarcity raises the price beyond reason. Famine has nothing to buy, though he has never so much money lying by him, but hungers, wants, pines, and at the last miserably consumes to naught, wherein verily scarcity and famine do differ. The Germans discern them by several words, calling scarcity “scarsetie,” and famine, “honger.” Yet they are for the most part indivisible.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **Meaning Drift:** "Arethas says that a balance is a token of right and equity. For thou hast sat, says David upon thy Throne which judge rightuouſnes" is changed to "Arethas says that a balance is a token of right and equity. For you have sat upon your throne which judges righteousness." The original phrasing, "thou hast sat, says David," is more evocative of a direct quotation and maintains the original's slightly archaic tone. The draft's phrasing is less specific and loses the direct attribution to David.
2. **Added Interpretation:** The phrase "we ought rather to prefer the exposition of S. John him self" is changed to "but we ought rather to prefer the exposition of Saint John himself." While seemingly minor, the addition of "but" introduces a subtle shift in argumentative flow that wasn't present in the original.
3. **Lost Ambiguity:** The original's "[illegible],[note 31.5]" is simply removed. This eliminates a potential source of information for a scholar, and the removal itself is an interpretive act.
4. **Hallucination:** The original includes "What the Roman penny is worth Budaeus shows: we understand by it plainly a great price." The draft retains this, but the original’s phrasing is slightly different.
5. **Proper Nouns:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." This is not present in the provided text, but is a common error.
[FINAL]
The rider of this horse holds in his hand a balance,[note 31.5] with two scales hanging at either end of the beam, which we call a pair of weights. Arethas says that a balance is a token of right and equity. For thou hast sat, says David upon thy Throne which judge rightuouſnes: therefore is a balance the judgment of the just judgment of God. Arethas hath not alleged these things amiss, how be it we ought rather to prefer the exposition of S. John him self. For a voice is heard from the midst of the beasts, which expounds to us the balance. For it sounds, a measure[note 31.6] of wheat for a penny, and three measures of barley for a penny. And this measured called Choinix, signifies a diet or daily meat: as Eraſmus hath in his proverb, sit not upon thy measure. The same in his annotations upon this place: Choinix, says he, is a measure of wheat, or other breadcorne, which is sufficient for one days meat. Budaeus thinks that it weighs iiii. pound, Pollux. iii. The word therefore signifies, that a very little meat shall cost a great price, and yet not to be gotten for money. Which chau ceth in the time of famine. What the Roman penny is worth Budaeus shows: we understand by it plainly a great price. Therefore two things are signified, ſcarſitie or derth of corn, and famine. Dearth reiſeth the price beyond reason. Famine hath nothing to by, though he hath never so much money lying by him: but hongreth, waunteth, pines, and at the last miserably, consumes to naught: wherein verily dearth and famine do differ. The Germanes discern them by several words calling dearth ſcarſetie, and famine, honger. Yet are they for the most part indiuiſible.

[ORIGINAL]
And we read in the old story of the Bible,[note 31.7] that for the contempt of the preaching of Gods law, and the bringing in of a strange kind of worshipping God, the Iſraelites in the times of Helias and Heliſaeus were most greuouſely punished with honger and penury. These things be plentifully declared in the.3. book of Kings the.17. and.18. Chapter. Also in the.4. of Kings, the.6. and.7. Chapter. Moreover in the time of the Emperor Claudius, whilst the Apostles preached the Gospel faithfully, and the Jews and Gentiles stoutly repulsing it, famine most greuouſely afflicted the Roman Empire: which thing S. Luke rehearses in the Acts of the Apostles.11. Chapter. Which things were done in deed before this reuelatio was to S. John exhibited. Sins that time the Hiſtoriographers recite sundry and innumerable famines, dearthes and penuries, in diverse countries, sent of God for contempt of the truth. Nauclerus mentions of a famine in the year of our lord. D xxxix. wherein mothers also devoured their own children. What hath chanced in our memory in those wars of Millan and else where, it is no need to rehearse. They be yet freſſhe in memory, and written in the stories of Galeacius Capella. We felt some part hereof also in the year of our Lord. M D.XXIX. and the years following. The just Lord puniſſheth, and more will puniſſhe the great ingratitude, and contempt of his Godly word: as he did in the destruction of Jerusalem, would God it would please the world most blind, through repentance to convert unto God when he punishes and with free and willing minds embrace the word of verity: for so should there be more felicity and less misery.
[DRAFT]
And we read in the old story of the Bible,[note 31.7] that for the contempt of the preaching of God’s law, and the bringing in of a strange kind of worshiping God, the Israelites in the times of Elijah and Elisha were most grievously punished with hunger and poverty. These things are plentifully declared in the 3rd book of Kings, chapters 17 and 18. Also in the 4th of Kings, chapters 6 and 7. Moreover, in the time of Emperor Claudius, while the Apostles preached the Gospel faithfully, and the Jews and Gentiles stoutly repulsed it, famine most grievously afflicted the Roman Empire: which thing Luke rehearses in the Acts of the Apostles, chapter 11. Which things were done indeed before this revelation was exhibited to John. Since that time, historians recount sundry and innumerable famines, dearths, and penuries in diverse countries, sent of God for contempt of the truth. Nauclerus mentions a famine in the year of our Lord 1339, wherein mothers also devoured their own children. What has chanced in our memory in those wars of Milan and elsewhere, it is no need to rehearse. They are yet fresh in memory, and written in the histories of Galeazzo Capella. We felt some part hereof also in the year of our Lord 1529 and the years following. The just Lord punishes, and more will punish the great ingratitude and contempt of his godly word, as he did in the destruction of Jerusalem. Would God it would please the world, so blind, through repentance to convert unto God when he punishes, and with free and willing minds embrace the word of truth: for so should there be more felicity and less misery.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "penury" changed to "poverty." While related, "penury" implies a more extreme and desperate state than "poverty." This softens the original's severity.
2. PROPER NOUNS: "Galeacius Capella" changed to "Galeazzo Capella." This is an error; the original name is correct.
3. PROPER NOUNS: "D xxxix" changed to "1339." While the modernization is technically correct, it loses the original's Roman numeral style, which was common and deliberate in the 1561 text. A note explaining the conversion might be preferable.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes detected.
[FINAL]
And we read in the old story of the Bible,[note 31.7] that for the contempt of the preaching of God’s law, and the bringing in of a strange kind of worshipping God, the Israelites in the times of Elijah and Elisha were most grievously punished with hunger and penury. These things be plentifully declared in the 3rd book of Kings the.17. and.18. Chapter. Also in the.4. of Kings, the.6. and.7. Chapter. Moreover in the time of the Emperor Claudius, whilst the Apostles preached the Gospel faithfully, and the Jews and Gentiles stoutly repulsing it, famine most greuouſely afflicted the Roman Empire: which thing S. Luke rehearses in the Acts of the Apostles.11. Chapter. Which things were done in deed before this reuelatio was to S. John exhibited. Since that time the Hiſtoriographers recite sundry and innumerable famines, dearthes and penuries, in diverse countries, sent of God for contempt of the truth. Nauclerus mentions of a famine in the year of our lord. D xxxix [note: Roman numeral for 1339], wherein mothers also devoured their own children. What hath chanced in our memory in those wars of Millan and else where, it is no need to rehearse. They be yet freſſhe in memory, and written in the stories of Galeacius Capella. We felt some part hereof also in the year of our Lord. M D.XXIX. and the years following. The just Lord puniſſheth, and more will puniſſhe the great ingratitude, and contempt of his Godly word: as he did in the destruction of Jerusalem, would God it would please the world most blind, through repentance to convert unto God when he punishes and with free and willing minds embrace the word of verity: for so should there be more felicity and less misery.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 31.8]How be it for a comfort at the end of this Seal is added, and Oil and Wine see thou hurt not. He names the kinds most necessary for the use of man, and means, that God doth mercifully reserve some things, that be chiefly necessary for mans use, especially for the electes sake, that all should not perish and pine in general. Whereby we understand that the lord forgets not his mercy, even in the midst of afflictio and plagues that he sends. Thus in times paste minding to punish Aegipte and other nations with famine, he sent before Ioſeph, by whom he might preserve the house of Iacob, and other people innumerable. You see herein most clearly, that it is of God, that sometime the corn is blasted, and the vines and olives perish: and to be of him, that the corn increases, and wine also. So hath he also before protested in the law, Leuitic. 26. And Deuteron. 28.
[DRAFT]
Nevertheless, for comfort at the end of this seal, it is added, “See that you hurt not the oil and the wine.” He names the things most necessary for human use, and means that God mercifully reserves some things that are chiefly necessary for humanity, especially for the sake of the elect, so that all do not perish and suffer. By this, we understand that the Lord does not forget his mercy, even in the midst of affliction and plagues that he sends. Thus, in times past, intending to punish Egypt and other nations with famine, he sent Joseph beforehand, through whom he might preserve the house of Jacob and other people innumerable. You see clearly herein that it is from God that sometimes the grain is blighted, and the vines and olive trees perish; and that it is from him that the grain increases, and the wine also. So he has also previously protested in the law, Leviticus 26, and Deuteronomy 28.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "pine" is replaced with "suffer." "Pine" carries a connotation of wasting away, a more specific and evocative image of suffering than the general term "suffer."
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "intending to punish Egypt" is an interpretation not explicitly stated in the original. The original simply states "minding to punish."
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "of God" is rendered as "from God." While similar, "from God" implies a more direct causation than the original's phrasing, which allows for a broader range of divine agency.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: All proper nouns are correctly modernized.
[FINAL]
Nevertheless, for comfort at the end of this seal, it is added, “See that you hurt not the oil and the wine.” He names the things most necessary for human use, and means that God mercifully reserves some things that are chiefly necessary for humanity, especially for the sake of the elect, so that all do not perish and pine. By this, we understand that the Lord does not forget his mercy, even in the midst of affliction and plagues that he sends. Thus, in times past, minding to punish Egypt and other nations with famine, he sent Joseph beforehand, through whom he might preserve the house of Jacob and other people innumerable. You see clearly herein that it is of God that sometimes the grain is blighted, and the vines and olive trees perish; and that it is of him that the grain increases, and the wine also. So he has also previously protested in the law, Leviticus 26, and Deuteronomy 28.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 31.9]We are come now to the fourth Seal, at the opening whereof, and to behold the operation, we are excited of the Egle, the fourth beast. Of whom we have spoken before ones or twice. And the pale Horse[note 31.10] comes forth, in Greek [illegible], which colour resembles withered grass and Herbs. Solomon in the.12. Chapter. of Eccleſ. calls the colour apperyng in dead bodies, and their countenances, Golden liquor. All Poets call death Pale. And the rider in deed is expressly called Death. We understand the course of the plague and of all diseases, and even of death it self: whom Hell follows, that is to say a pit or a grave. For Scheol in Hebrewe signifies a pit or a grave. But if you will needs understand it of the place of them that be damned, doubtless they be carried headlong into Hell, so many as here with sickness consumed, die with out faith and repentance. Therefore hell follows death rightly. But if thou had rather by hell understand, a grave: it signifies that all shall be full of coarſes and sepulchres.
[DRAFT]
We have now come to the fourth Seal, at the opening of which, and to observe its effect, we are stirred up by the Eagle, the fourth beast. Of whom we have spoken before on one or two occasions. And the pale Horse comes forth, in Greek *chloros*, which color resembles withered grass and herbs. Solomon in chapter 12 of Ecclesiastes calls the color appearing in dead bodies and their faces a golden liquid. All poets call death pale. And the rider is indeed expressly called Death. We understand the course of plague and all diseases, and even death itself; and Hell follows, that is to say, a pit or a grave. For *Sheol* in Hebrew signifies a pit or a grave. But if you will necessarily understand it of the place of the damned, undoubtedly they are cast headlong into Hell, so many as here, consumed by sickness, die without faith and repentance. Therefore Hell follows death rightly. But if you would rather understand Hell as a grave, it signifies that it will be full of corpses and sepulchers.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "excited of the Egle" changed to "stirred up by the Eagle." While not drastically different, "excited" carries a sense of awe or prompting that "stirred up" lacks, potentially diminishing the original's tone.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "*chloros*" in brackets, while providing a Greek term, introduces an element of scholarly explanation not present in the original. It's a gloss, not a modernization.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "But if you will needs understand it of the place of them that be damned..." allows for a degree of interpretive wiggle room. The draft's "But if you will necessarily understand it of the place of the damned..." is more forceful and less open to alternative readings.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No errors in proper noun modernization.
[FINAL]
We have now come to the fourth Seal, at the opening whereof, and to behold the operation, we are excited of the Eagle, the fourth beast. Of whom we have spoken before ones or twice. And the pale Horse comes forth, in Greek [illegible], which colour resembles withered grass and Herbs. Solomon in the.12. Chapter. of Eccleſ. calls the colour apperyng in dead bodies, and their countenances, Golden liquor. All Poets call death Pale. And the rider in deed is expressly called Death. We understand the course of the plague and of all diseases, and even of death it self: whom Hell follows, that is to say a pit or a grave. For Scheol in Hebrewe signifies a pit or a grave. But if you will needs understand it of the place of them that be damned, doubtless they be carried headlong into Hell, so many as here with sickness consumed, die with out faith and repentance. Therefore hell follows death rightly. But if thou had rather by hell understand, a grave: it signifies that all shall be full of coarſes and sepulchres.

Whereupon he powers out his fury upon the third part of the world. For he knows, whom he should punish, and whom he should nurriſhe tenderly.

Therefore if we covet to be quit of so great evils, let us serve God in truth, annd make much of his word, which he hath sent to heal us. And reason it is that such as reject soud doctrine, should be vexed with sundry diseases of soul and body, &c.

[CRITIQUE]

3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The "and so forth" is a flattening of the original's ellipsis. The original's trailing off suggests a list not fully enumerated, hinting at a wider range of consequences than the draft's explicit "various diseases."

[ORIGINAL]
And first in expounding the same,[note 32.1] we shall speak generally of the persecutions, wherewith as well the ministers, as all the faithful church also, is diversely exercised. The lord Christ hath showed us before in the Gospel many things touching the persecutions to come verily that he might prepare the minds of all the faithful to battle and patience. The places be in the.10. and.24. of Matthew: In the.12. and.21. of Luke: in the.14.15. and.16. of John. And also the acts of the Apostles tel of many things, which the godly suffered in that most holy primitiue Church, should he have ben thought to have ben well in his wits, if any man than should have said: he[illegible] of it appears, that thapoſtolical church, is not the church, for that it is subject to all the mockeries, injuries and slaughters of all men? why than doe we not acknowledge at this day, that they are fowly deceived, which measure the church by the outward peace and tranquillity of things? Paulus Oroſius in the.7. book of histories raccompteth ten grievous persecutions, raised against the church fro the time of the Apostles until the emperor Coſtantine: which time did not fully accomplish the space of ccc. years. The first was stirred up by Nero, a monſtrouſe man, whereof also Tacitus mentions in his Chronicles. This same rid out of the way Peter and Paul, the most holy? Apostles of Christ. The second destruction of the church brought in Domitian, which in the same his perſecutio most grievously afflicted both this our S. John, and the whole church also: and when he was brought to Rome, banished him into the Isle of Pathmos. The third raised Traiane, whereof Plinie governor of Asia makes mentio in the 10. book of Epistles. In this was Ignatius an holy Bishop cast and devoured of wilde beasts. And M. Antoninus verut molested the church with the fourth persecution, and consumed with fire Polycarpus a bishop most worthy. Septimus Seuerus moved the fifte persecution, which Eusebius pourſeweth in the.6. book of the Ecclesiastical story. Iulius Maximinus killed Pamphilus martyr, and Sextus raged cruelly against the church. And Decius Traianus began the seventh persecution, and executed very many that professed Christ. And Licinius Valerian Emperor beheaded S. Cyprian the good Bishop of Carthage, and was the eight persecutor of the church, Aurelianus verius began the ix. persecution, which he but little advanced, for God most just toke him away immediately. But Diocletian and Maximian shed more christen blood, than any other of the Roman Emperors. Read I pray you the beginning of the.8. book of the Eccleſ. story of Eusebius. Compare those things with our time, and judge and conjecture what will shortly come to pass, and what our state will be. Persecutions are again renewed after Constantine, under Conſtantius and Iulian. But the most terrible and grievous of all have boiled up under Antichrist, and have endured now by the space of five hundred years and more. What is done at this day, all the world sees. The ground is wet with the blood of Martyrs. Which things S. John foresaw.
[DRAFT]
And first in expounding the same,[note 32.1] we shall speak generally of the persecutions, by which as well the ministers as all the faithful church also, is diversely exercised. The Lord Christ has shown us before in the Gospel many things concerning the persecutions to come, truly that he might prepare the minds of all the faithful to battle and patience. The passages are in Matthew 10:24, 12:21, and 14:15, 16; and also the Acts of the Apostles tell of many things which the godly suffered in that most holy primitive Church. Should anyone have been thought to be well in his wits, if any man should have said: it appears that the apostolic church is not the church, for that it is subject to all the mockeries, injuries, and slaughters of all men? Why then do we not acknowledge at this day that they are greatly deceived, who measure the church by the outward peace and tranquility of things? Paulus Orosius in the 7th book of histories recounts ten grievous persecutions raised against the church from the time of the Apostles until the emperor Constantine, which time did not fully accomplish the space of 300 years. The first was stirred up by Nero, a monstrous man, of whom also Tacitus mentions in his Chronicles. This same rid out of the way Peter and Paul, the most holy Apostles of Christ. The second destruction of the church brought in Domitian, who in the same persecution most grievously afflicted both this our Saint John, and the whole church also; and when he was brought to Rome, banished him into the Isle of Patmos. The third raised Trajan, of whom Pliny, governor of Asia, makes mention in the 10th book of Epistles. In this was Ignatius, a holy bishop, cast and devoured of wild beasts. And Marcus Antoninus verius molested the church with the fourth persecution, and consumed with fire Polycarp, a bishop most worthy. Septimus Severus moved the fifth persecution, which Eusebius pursues in the 6th book of the Ecclesiastical history. Julius Maximinus killed Pamphilus, a martyr, and Sextus raged cruelly against the church. And Decius Trajanus began the seventh persecution, and executed very many that professed Christ. And Licinius Valerian, Emperor, beheaded Saint Cyprian, the good bishop of Carthage, and was the eighth persecutor of the church. Aurelianus verius began the ninth persecution, which he but little advanced, for God most just took him away immediately. But Diocletian and Maximian shed more Christian blood than any other of the Roman Emperors. Read, I pray you, the beginning of the 8th book of the Ecclesiastical history of Eusebius. Compare those things with our time, and judge and conjecture what will shortly come to pass, and what our state will be. Persecutions are again renewed after Constantine, under Constantius and Julian. But the most terrible and grievous of all have boiled up under Antichrist, and have endured now by the space of five hundred years and more. What is done at this day, all the world sees. The ground is wet with the blood of martyrs, which things Saint John foresaw.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "should he have ben thought to have ben well in his wits" changed to "Should anyone have been thought to be well in his wits." While the gist is the same, the original phrasing is more emphatic and rhetorical, characteristic of the period. The draft's version is less impactful.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "Paulus Oroſius" changed to "Paulus Orosius." The original's spelling, while unusual, might reflect a regional or idiosyncratic rendering. Removing the "u" eliminates a potential clue to the text's provenance.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "Austen" changed to "Augustine." Austen is a common surname and could be a scribal error for Augustine. The draft's correction is likely correct, but it removes a potential point of investigation regarding the original text's transmission.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms were detected.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No content was added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" changed to "Irenaeus." Correct. "M. Antoninus verut molested" changed to "Marcus Antoninus verius molested". The original is unclear and the draft's correction is likely, but the original's ambiguity is lost. "Septimus Seuerus" changed to "Septimus Severus." Correct. "Iulius Maximinus" changed to "Julius Maximinus." Correct. "Decius Traianus" changed to "Decius Trajanus." Correct. "Aurelianus verius" changed to "Aurelianus verius." Correct. "Conſtantius" changed to "Constantius." Correct.
[FINAL]
And first in expounding the same,[note 32.1] we shall speak generally of the persecutions, wherewith as well the ministers as all the faithful church also, is diversely exercised. The Lord Christ has shown us before in the Gospel many things concerning the persecutions to come, truly that he might prepare the minds of all the faithful to battle and patience. The passages are in Matthew 10:24, 12:21, and 14:15, 16; and also the Acts of the Apostles tell of many things which the godly suffered in that most holy primitive Church. Should anyone have been thought to have been well in his wits, if any man should have said: it appears that the apostolic church is not the church, for that it is subject to all the mockeries, injuries, and slaughters of all men? Why then do we not acknowledge at this day that they are greatly deceived, who measure the church by the outward peace and tranquility of things? Paulus Orosius in the 7th book of histories recounts ten grievous persecutions raised against the church from the time of the Apostles until the emperor Constantine, which time did not fully accomplish the space of 300 years. The first was stirred up by Nero, a monstrous man, of whom also Tacitus mentions in his Chronicles. This same rid out of the way Peter and Paul, the most holy Apostles of Christ. The second destruction of the church brought in Domitian, who in the same persecution most grievously afflicted both this our Saint John, and the whole church also; and when he was brought to Rome, banished him into the Isle of Patmos. The third raised Trajan, of whom Pliny, governor of Asia, makes mention in the 10th book of Epistles. In this was Ignatius, a holy bishop, cast and devoured of wild beasts. And Marcus Antoninus verius molested the church with the fourth persecution, and consumed with fire Polycarp, a bishop most worthy. Septimus Severus moved the fifth persecution, which Eusebius pursues in the 6th book of the Ecclesiastical history. Julius Maximinus killed Pamphilus, a martyr, and Sextus raged cruelly against the church. And Decius Trajanus began the seventh persecution, and executed very many that professed Christ. And Licinius Valerian, Emperor, beheaded Saint Cyprian, the good bishop of Carthage, and was the eighth persecutor of the church. Aurelianus verius began the ninth persecution, which he but little advanced, for God most just took him away immediately. But Diocletian and Maximian shed more Christian blood than any other of the Roman Emperors. Read, I pray you, the beginning of the 8th book of the Ecclesiastical history of Eusebius. Compare those things with our time, and judge and conjecture what will shortly come to pass, and what our state will be. Persecutions are again renewed after Constantine, under Constantius and Julian. But the most terrible and grievous of all have boiled up under Antichrist, and have endured now by the space of five hundred years and more. What is done at this day, all the world sees. The ground is wet with the blood of martyrs, which things Saint John foresaw.

[ORIGINAL]
But where are the souls of them that are slain for the word of God showed unto us? under the Altar,[note 32.7] the Altar is after in the.8. Chapter. set in heave, before the throne of God. Therefore the souls of all Saints are in Heaven, before the Throne of God, which was also signified before in the Tip of the xxiiii. Elders. The Lord hath said also, where I am, there shall be also my servant. But the lord is in heave: therefore the souls of the faithful, whose bodies have ben slain,[note 32.8] or buried without slaughter, be no where else but in Heaven. Nevertheless it waunteth not a singular mystery, that they are laid under the Altar, as under a shadow, through whose benefit the souls may be well at ease. I told you before, and here again repeat, that the altar signifies Christ. For he is also the golden altar, intercessor, and propiciation for our sins. For the propiciation and mediation of Christ we are received into the Joys celestial. And Christ is our life and salvation. Under him we lie hid, as under a cover or a shadow. Thomas of Aquine expounding this place of S. John: by the altar, says he, is signified Christ, in whom and by whom we should offer to the father, what good so ever we doe: and through him is made acceptable, what so ever is pleasant to God. Under this Altar, namely under christ, be the souls, not only in the state of life, (to wit whilst we live here in earth) but also in the state of our country (to wit in heave) as under him of whom they are covered, as under a shadow against all evil. Thus says Thomas. But I suppose that there is an other thing also signified, that martyrs are made confourmable to the Altar, that is to the passion of Christ, and therefore to rest now under the Altar Christ. For they that are pertakers with him in passion, doe communicate also with him in glory.[note 32.9] For like as the bosom of Abraha, is called a receptacle, and that port and hauon of Salvation, into the which the souls of the are received, which had the faith of Abraham: so do we understand the altar to be a place of blessedness in heaven, wherein they rest, which with true faith have acknowledged Christ the altar, propiciation, sanctification and satisfaction: and have moreover in suffering offered them selves to God in Christ, through patience, an acceptable sacrifice to God. Under this Altar was gathered the first martyr Abel: and after as many as have died for religio, and shall begathered, who so ever in bearing the cross: through tribulation enter with Christ into glory.
[DRAFT]
But where are the souls of those who are slain for the word of God? It was shown to us under the Altar,[note 32.7] the Altar being, as described in the eighth chapter, set in heaven before the throne of God. Therefore, the souls of all the saints are in heaven, before the throne of God, which was also signified earlier in the vision of the twenty-four elders. The Lord has said also, “Where I am, there shall my servant be.” But the Lord is in heaven; therefore, the souls of the faithful, whose bodies have been slain,[note 32.8] or buried without slaughter, are nowhere else but in heaven. Nevertheless, it does not lack a singular mystery that they are laid under the Altar, as under a shadow, through whose benefit the souls may be well at ease. I told you before, and here again repeat, that the altar signifies Christ. For he is also the golden altar, intercessor, and propitiation for our sins. Through the propitiation and mediation of Christ, we are received into celestial joys. And Christ is our life and salvation. Under him we lie hidden, as under a cover or a shadow. Thomas Aquinas, expounding this passage of John, says that by the altar is signified Christ, in whom and by whom we should offer to the Father whatever good we do; and through him, whatever is pleasing to God is made acceptable. Under this Altar, namely under Christ, are the souls, not only in the state of life (that is, while we live here on earth) but also in the state of our homeland (that is, in heaven), as under him of whom they are covered, as under a shadow against all evil. Thus says Thomas. But I suppose that there is another thing also signified, that martyrs are made conformable to the Altar, that is, to the passion of Christ, and therefore to rest now under the Altar, Christ. For those who are partakers with him in suffering do also communicate with him in glory.[note 32.9] For just as the bosom of Abraham is called a receptacle, and that port and haven of salvation, into which the souls of those who had the faith of Abraham are received, so we understand the altar to be a place of blessedness in heaven, wherein they rest who, with true faith, have acknowledged Christ as the altar, propitiation, sanctification, and satisfaction; and who moreover, in suffering, have offered themselves to God in Christ, through patience, an acceptable sacrifice to God. Under this Altar was gathered the first martyr, Abel; and after him, as many as have died for the faith, and shall be gathered, whoever in bearing the cross, through tribulation, enter with Christ into glory.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "waunteth not a singular mystery" changed to "it does not lack a singular mystery." While seemingly minor, "waunteth" carries a slightly archaic and more emphatic tone, suggesting a deliberate lack rather than a simple absence. The draft's phrasing softens the original's assertion.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The phrase "as described in the eighth chapter" added after "the Altar being" introduces an interpretive element not present in the original. The original simply states the altar's location.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "country" changed to "homeland" (referring to heaven). "Country" in the original could have a more ambiguous meaning, potentially referring to one's earthly nation. The draft's translation removes this ambiguity, forcing a specifically heavenly interpretation.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "as described in the eighth chapter" is not present in the original.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Thomas of Aquine" changed to "Thomas Aquinas" - Correct. "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus" - Not present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]

Therefore the souls of all Saints are in Heaven, before the Throne of God, which was also signified before in the Tip of the xxiiii. Elders. The Lord hath said also, where I am, there shall be also my servant. But the lord is in heave: therefore the souls of the faithful, whose bodies have ben slain,[note 32.8] or buried without slaughter, be no where else but in Heaven. Nevertheless it waunteth not a singular mystery, that they are laid under the Altar, as under a shadow, through whose benefit the souls may be well at ease. I told you before, and here again repeat, that the altar signifies Christ. For he is also the golden altar, intercessor, and propiciation for our sins. For the propiciation and mediation of Christ we are received into the Joys celestial. And Christ is our life and salvation. Under him we lie hid, as under a cover or a shadow. [ORIGINAL]
[note 32.10]Now is also declared what they do under the Altar. The very martyrs I say, cry, not the beasts, as they have done hitherto: and they cry out with a load voice. No man shall Imagine, that the blessed souls in Heaven doe complain, be sorrowful, doe accuse and be troubled. These things are feigned to an other end, to the intent we should gather thereof, that God forgets not his, that he puts not out all revengement, that he sees, feleth, and regards the injuries and deaths of his servants. Where the vengeance follows not immediately, God is thought of many to sleep, and to have no respect unto his. We hear therefore, that the holy Martyrs cry, and that with a load voice. He appears to have alluded to that same in the.4. of Genesis. The voice of thy brothers blood cries unto me, to wit for vengeance.[note 32.11] For the Divines call certain sins crying, as those which are red in the Scriptures to cry unto God, as is at this present the ſhedyng of blood: the sin of Sodome, in the.9. of Genes. the oppression of widows and orphans, in the.22. of Exodus, wages for work deteyned, Deuteron. 24. and James the.5. How long so ever therefore God defers vengeance be it never so many years, yet is not the blood of the just forgotten before God. S. Paul in the.12. to the Hebrews cries out and says, that the blood of Able speaks. In the.18. of Luke the Lord says, that the afflicted do cry both day and night for deliverance. Would God they would wayegh these things, whose feet are swift to shed blood. God would not in times paste be merciful to his people, for that much innocent blood was shed among them by the mean of Manaſſes their king: as appears in the.4. book of Kings. Therefore dere brethren let us consider well at this day, what we doe, and let us not shed rashly innocent blood.
[DRAFT]
Now it is also declared what they do under the altar. I say, the very martyrs cry out, not the beasts, as they have done until now, and they cry out with a loud voice. Let no one imagine that the blessed souls in heaven are complaining, sorrowful, accusing, or troubled. These things are feigned for another purpose, so that we may understand that God does not forget his people, that he does not withhold vengeance, that he sees, feels, and regards the injuries and deaths of his servants. For when vengeance does not follow immediately, many think that God is asleep and has no regard for them. Therefore, we hear that the holy martyrs cry out, and with a loud voice. He appears to have alluded to that same in Genesis 4: “The voice of your brother’s blood cries to me, for vengeance.”

For the theologians call certain sins “crying,” as those which are red in the Scriptures are said to cry to God, as is presently the shedding of blood; the sin of Sodom in Genesis 9; the oppression of widows and orphans in Exodus 22; wages for work detained, Deuteronomy 24, and James 5. How long so ever therefore God defers vengeance, even if it is for many years, the blood of the just is not forgotten before God. Paul in Hebrews 12 cries out and says that the blood of Abel speaks. In Luke 18, the Lord says that the afflicted cry out both day and night for deliverance. Would God they would weigh these things, whose feet are swift to shed blood. God did not show mercy to his people in times past because much innocent blood was shed among them through the means of Manasseh, their king, as appears in 2 Kings 4. Therefore, dear brethren, let us consider well at this day what we do, and let us not shed innocent blood rashly.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** "Dispensation" and "sacrament" are absent from the original and would be expected in a modernized version of a 1561 text, particularly one dealing with theological concepts.
2. **PROPER NOUNS:** "2 Kings 4" should be "1 Kings 4" to reflect the original numbering.
3. **MEANING DRIFT:** The phrase "so that we may understand" (draft) is an added interpretation not present in "that we should gather thereof" (original).
4. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "God is thought of many to sleep" is softened to "many think that God is asleep" in the draft, reducing the original's potentially more pointed critique of popular opinion.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "For the theologians call certain sins 'crying'" is a slight expansion of the original's phrasing. While the concept is present, the specific wording is not.
[FINAL]
Now it is also declared what they do under the altar. I say, the very martyrs cry out, not the beasts, as they have done until now, and they cry out with a loud voice. Let no one imagine that the blessed souls in heaven do complain, be sorrowful, do accuse and be troubled. These things are feigned to another end, to the intent we should gather thereof, that God forgets not his, that he puts not out all revengement, that he sees, feleth, and regards the injuries and deaths of his servants. Where the vengeance follows not immediately, God is thought of many to sleep, and to have no respect unto his. We hear therefore, that the holy Martyrs cry, and that with a loud voice. He appears to have alluded to that same in Genesis 4: “The voice of thy brothers blood cries unto me, to wit for vengeance.”

For the Divines call certain sins crying, as those which are red in the Scriptures to cry unto God, as is at this present the shedding of blood: the sin of Sodome, in Genesis 9; the oppression of widows and orphans, in Exodus 22; wages for work deteyned, Deuteronomy 24, and James 5. How long so ever therefore God defers vengeance be it never so many years, yet is not the blood of the just forgotten before God. Paul in Hebrews 12 cries out and says, that the blood of Able speaks. In Luke 18 the Lord says, that the afflicted do cry both day and night for deliverance. Would God they would wayegh these things, whose feet are swift to shed blood. God would not in times paste be merciful to his people, for that much innocent blood was shed among them by the mean of Manasseh their king: as appears in 1 Kings 4. Therefore dere brethren let us consider well at this day, what we doe, and let us not shed rashly innocent blood.

[ORIGINAL]
Certainly the words are expressed of S. John,[note 32.12] which the Martyrs cried to the Lord: howe lunge, say they, Lord, which art holy and true, &c. They put God in remembrance, not as ignorant, or inconstant, but as knowing, and most steadfastly mindful of holiness and truth. For in as much as the Lord is holy, he hates all profane and unclean persons, and spares them not. For as much as he is true, he maintains and defends his chosen, and punishes and oppresses his enemies as he hath promised by his word. Sins therefore thou art such, say they, O God, why doest thou not judge, and avenge our blood, of them which in earth, as in their kingdom exercise tyranny, and oppress every good man? All this signifies none other thing, than that God for his own sake, which is holy and true, will never forget the injuries of his servants. Therefore we understand these things to be spoken by a figure called Proſopopeia: that is the feigning of a person: not that the Saints in Heaven do expostulate with God, but that we by such a figure might understand that God hath care of Martyrs, because he is holy and true. S. Austen in the.68. question upon the new Testament: Saying the Lord, says he, hath taught us to pray for our enemies, what is the cause that the souls of those that are slain cry out as doth the blood of Able, and require that they may be avenged? And he makes answer: Saints be not impatient, that they should urge that thing to be done now, which they know shall come to pass in the time prefixed, which neither can be prevented, nor yet delayed: but by this saying he would show, howe God will avenge the blood of his servants, least because he seems now so patient, that wicked war should be thought unpunished, which is made against the Saints: that both he might drive a fear into them that persecute the servants of God, and might also exhort the sufferers unto patience. Thus says he. And this in deed seems the plainest sense of all others, especially if we consider the things that follow in the lords auſwer, and it was said unto them that they should rest, &c.
[DRAFT]
Certainly the words are expressed of Saint John,[note 32.12] which the martyrs cried to the Lord: how long, say they, Lord, which art holy and true, &c. They put God in remembrance, not as ignorant or inconstant, but as knowing and steadfastly mindful of holiness and truth. For in as much as the Lord is holy, he hates all profane and unclean persons, and spares them not. For as much as he is true, he maintains and defends his chosen, and punishes and oppresses his enemies as he has promised by his word. Sins therefore thou art such, say they, O God, why doest thou not judge and avenge our blood, of them which in earth, as in their kingdom, exercise tyranny and oppress every good man? All this signifies no other thing than that God, for his own sake, which is holy and true, will never forget the injuries of his servants. Therefore we understand these things to be spoken by a figure called Prosopopeia: that is, the feigning of a person; not that the saints in Heaven do expostulate with God, but that we by such a figure might understand that God has care of martyrs, because he is holy and true. Saint Augustine in the 68th question upon the New Testament: Saying the Lord has taught us to pray for our enemies, what is the cause that the souls of those that are slain cry out as does the blood of Abel, and require that they may be avenged? And he makes answer: Saints be not impatient, that they should urge that thing to be done now, which they know shall come to pass in the time prefixed, which neither can be prevented, nor yet delayed; but by this saying he would show, how God will avenge the blood of his servants, least because he seems now so patient, that wicked war should be thought unpunished, which is made against the saints: that both he might drive fear into them that persecute the servants of God, and might also exhort the sufferers unto patience. Thus says he. And this in deed seems the plainest sense of all others, especially if we consider the things that follow in the Lord’s answer, and it was said unto them that they should rest, &c.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus."
2. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "lords auſwer" in the original is rendered as "Lord’s answer" in the draft. While a minor change, it introduces a possessive where it wasn't explicitly present.
[FINAL]
Certainly the words are expressed of Saint John,[note 32.12] which the martyrs cried to the Lord: how long, say they, Lord, which art holy and true, &c. They put God in remembrance, not as ignorant or inconstant, but as knowing and steadfastly mindful of holiness and truth. For in as much as the Lord is holy, he hates all profane and unclean persons, and spares them not. Sins therefore thou art such, say they, O God, why doest thou not judge and avenge our blood, of them which in earth, as in their kingdom, exercise tyranny and oppress every good man? All this signifies no other thing than that God, for his own sake, which is holy and true, will never forget the injuries of his servants. Therefore we understand these things to be spoken by a figure called Prosopopeia: that is, the feigning of a person; not that the saints in Heaven do expostulate with God, but that we by such a figure might understand that God has care of martyrs, because he is holy and true. Saint Augustine in the 68th question upon the New Testament: Saying the Lord has taught us to pray for our enemies, what is the cause that the souls of those that are slain cry out as does the blood of Abel, and require that they may be avenged?
