[ORIGINAL]
Primasius Bishop of Utica expounding this place of S. John,[note 32.13] it is not to be thought, says he, that the Saints are incensed with a carnal understanding and stoutness to be avenged, sins we know that through the abundance of charity the very enemies are of the also in this case beloved: but it is evident that they prayed against the kingdom of sin, and to have earnestly desired the other things of that kingdom, whereof we say, thy kingdom come. For it is not lawful, to think that they would covet any thing against the pleasure of God, sins their desires depend upon his will, &c. And S. Gregory: what is it, says he, that the souls make request of revengement, but that they desire the last day of judgment, and the resurrection of bodies slain? Arethas notes here also out of the commentaries of S. Andrew bishop of Caesarea: moreover the Saints appear hereby to wish for the end of the world. Wherefore they are commanded patiently to abide, until the accomplishment of their brethren,[note 32.14] lest they should be fulfilled with out them, after the holy Apostle.
[DRAFT]
Primasius, Bishop of Utica, explaining this passage from the writings of Saint John, states that it should not be understood as the saints seeking vengeance with a carnal understanding and stubbornness; for we know that through the abundance of charity, even enemies are beloved in this case. Rather, it is evident that they prayed against the kingdom of sin, and earnestly desired the things of that kingdom, namely, “thy kingdom come.” It is not lawful to think that they would desire anything against the pleasure of God, since their desires depend upon his will, and so forth. And Saint Gregory asks, what is it that the souls request when they ask for vengeance, but that they desire the final day of judgment and the resurrection of slain bodies? Arethas also notes this from the commentaries of Saint Andrew, Bishop of Caesarea: furthermore, the saints appear here to wish for the end of the world. Therefore, they are commanded to patiently await the fulfillment of their brethren, [?lest] they should be completed without them, according to the holy Apostle.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "stoutness" is softened to "stubbornness," subtly altering the nuance of the original's description of the Saints' potential reaction.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "namely, 'thy kingdom come'" is an unnecessary explanatory addition. The original relies on the reader's familiarity with the Lord's Prayer.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The bracketed "[?lest]" removes the original's ambiguity regarding the conditional clause. The original's phrasing allows for a more complex understanding of the potential consequence.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes or removals of theological terms detected.
5. HALLUCINATION: The phrase "and so forth" is not present in the original.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No errors detected.
[FINAL]
Primasius, Bishop of Utica, explaining this passage from the writings of Saint John, states that it should not be understood as the saints seeking vengeance with a carnal understanding and stoutness; for we know that through the abundance of charity, even enemies are beloved in this case. Rather, it is evident that they prayed against the kingdom of sin, and earnestly desired the things of that kingdom, whereof we say, “thy kingdom come.” It is not lawful to think that they would desire anything against the pleasure of God, since their desires depend upon his will, &c. And Saint Gregory asks, what is it that the souls make request of revengement, but that they desire the final day of judgment and the resurrection of slain bodies? Arethas also notes this from the commentaries of Saint Andrew, Bishop of Caesarea: furthermore, the saints appear here to wish for the end of the world. Therefore, they are commanded to patiently abide until the accomplishment of their brethren, lest they should be fulfilled without them, after the holy Apostle.

[ORIGINAL]
First doubtless the state of souls in heaven is in all things most fortunate. The which is figured by the white garments. For the glory of the blessed is signified, that are now in light, and feel nothing of darkness, of this garment I have spoken before. And it is said expressly, and white garments are given to every one of them. For every soul receives his rewards: And the body also at thend of the world shall receive his own garment. S. Gregory? Saints as yet, says he, have the fruition only of the bless of the souls: but in thend of the world, they shall receive two stoles or garments, for with the perfect joy of the souls they shall be clothed also with the incorruption of bodies. Hereof shall be reasoned more diligently about the end of the.vii Chapter. where this place shall be declared more at large. After it was said to the blessed souls ([illegible]) that they should rest. Therefore they be altogether in quiet, and feel no perturbation, which in the xii. Chapter. shall more plainly be declared. Notwithstanding that it may be referred to delay and breathing, as though he should have said. It was signified to the souls, that they should yet differ and abide. For it follows: yet a little while. Therefore God ſignifyeth, that after a little time he will deliver his, and punniſhe the aduerſaryes.
[DRAFT]
Doubtless, the state of souls in heaven is in all things most fortunate. This is represented by the white garments. For the glory of the blessed, who are now in light and feel nothing of darkness, is signified by this garment, of which I have spoken before. And it is said expressly, that white garments are given to every one of them. For every soul receives his reward; and the body also at the end of the world shall receive its own garment. Saint Gregory says that saints as yet have only the blessedness of souls; but at the end of the world, they shall receive two stoles or garments, for they shall be clothed also with the incorruption of bodies. This will be reasoned about more diligently toward the end of the seventh chapter, where this place shall be declared more at large. After it was said to the blessed souls that they should rest, therefore they are altogether in quiet and feel no perturbation, which will be declared more plainly in the twelfth chapter. Nevertheless, this may be referred to delay and breathing, as though he should have said, “It was signified to the souls that they should yet differ and abide.” For it follows: “Yet a little while.” Therefore, God signifies that after a little time he will deliver his own and punish the adversaries.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "feel nothing of darkness" replaces "feel nothing *of* darkness," subtly shifting the emphasis. The original implies darkness is a quality they are free *from*, the draft implies they experience no darkness at all.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "This is represented by the white garments" is interpretive. The original states the garments *figure* or symbolize, which is a less assertive claim.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's "delay and breathing" is softened to "this may be referred to delay and breathing," diminishing the speculative nature of the original's suggestion.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes detected.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." "Austen" should be "Augustine." "Hierom" should be "Jerome." (Not present in this passage, but a general instruction was given.)
[FINAL]
Doubtless, the state of souls in heaven is in all things most fortunate. The which is figured by the white garments. For the glory of the blessed, who are now in light and feel nothing *of* darkness, is signified by this garment, of which I have spoken before. And it is said expressly, that white garments are given to every one of them. For every soul receives his reward; and the body also at the end of the world shall receive its own garment. Hereof shall be reasoned more diligently about the end of the.vii Chapter. where this place shall be declared more at large. After it was said to the blessed souls that they should rest, therefore they be altogether in quiet and feel no perturbation, which in the xii. Chapter. shall more plainly be declared. Notwithstanding that it may be referred to delay and breathing, as though he should have said. It was signified to the souls, that they should yet differ and abide. [ORIGINAL]
[note 32.18]And the noting of the time seems to be taken of the.2. Chapter. of Abacuk, which place is also alleged of the Apostle in the.11. to the Hebrew. For yet a little while, for because he that shall come, will come, and will not tarry. And the just shall live of his faith, &c. In the.26. of Isaiah we read these words (after he hath showed the resurrection to come, and the last end of the world) go therefore my people, and enter into thine inner chabers, and shut thy doors after thee, hide thee a little while, even for a moment, till the indignation be past. And likewise S. Peter called all this time of affliction unto the judgment, a short time, that we might take comfort therein.1. Peter.1. And.2. Peter.3.
[DRAFT]
And the indication of the time appears to be drawn from the second chapter of Habakkuk, which passage the Apostle also cites in Hebrews 11. For yet a little while, because he who is coming will come, and will not delay. And the righteous shall live by faith, and so forth. In Isaiah 26, we read these words (after he has shown the future resurrection and the final end of the world): “Go, therefore, my people, and enter into your chambers, and shut your doors after you; hide yourselves a little while, even for a moment, until the indignation passes.” And likewise Saint Peter called this entire time of affliction, which is a judgment, a short time, so that we might find comfort in it. 1 Peter 1 and 2 Peter 3.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "And the just shall live of his faith, &c." changed to "And the righteous shall live by faith, and so forth." The "&c." is a standard abbreviation for "et cetera," indicating omitted text. Replacing it with "and so forth" introduces a modern idiom that slightly diminishes the original's directness and formality.

ADDED INTERPRETATION: "which is a judgment" added to "this entire time of affliction." The original simply states it's a "time of affliction." The draft's addition imposes a specific interpretive lens (judgment) not explicitly present.

LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "even for a moment" retains a degree of ambiguity regarding the duration of the hiding. The draft's rendering is functionally equivalent but loses some of that subtle uncertainty.

PROPER NOUNS: "S. Peter" changed to "Saint Peter." While acceptable, "S." was a common abbreviation in 1561 and its removal feels like a slight smoothing that erodes some of the original texture.




[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
Nevertheless these things must religiouſely be expounded. For it is not to be thought,[note 33.2] for that the lamb opens the ſixte seal, and the Son by and by waxes black, that Christ is author of the corrupt and evil doctrine. For Christ it is, that sows in the field good ſede, the hostile man sows darnel: As the Lord him self expounds it Matthew. the.13. For Christ teaches ſownde doctrine by the Apostles and sincere preachers: which when it seems vile to the world and can not please, of his just judgment he leaves the contemners to their affections, and as the Apostle S. Paull says,[note 33.3] he sends upon them the efficacitie of illusion, that they may believe lies, and so may be judged all which had rather believe a lie than the verity. And the seducing through corrupt doctrine, is a more hurteful evil, than are the bloody persecutions. Yea and the seducers and false prophets have done more hurt to the church, than have cruel tirauntes. Finally men are more greuouſely punished what time they are relinquiſſhed to be seduced of deceivers, than when they are objected to be torn in pecs of their murderers. Therefore is it a most grievous plague of god, and utterly to be abhorred, for the simple verity despised, to be delivered to lying deceivers, which after the demerits may, with reverence be it spoken, all to be ſhite, and be peace thee. For where the gospel is purely preached unto many, these men say, I understand not what these men teach us out of the Gospel: but this I can see that the old have all to be rayde us, and these new both to be peace and beſhite us. Therefore shalt thou have teachers, which shall perform to thee in deed the same that thou talk. Would God we waunted examples: and did not see certain nations, which have here tofore had the free and pure preaching of the gospel, and now spoiled of all verity, to sigh under the pleasure and boldenes of most wicked deceivers, which tread Gods word under foot, and condemn it for heresy, and stop the mouths of the wretched people full of mans dung. This is the punishment of the verity despised.
[DRAFT]
Nevertheless, these things must be explained with religious care. For it is not to be thought, [note 33.2] that because the Lamb opens the sixth seal, and the Son immediately becomes dark, Christ is the author of corrupt and evil doctrine. For Christ is the one who sows good seed in the field; the enemy sows darnel, as the Lord himself explains in Matthew 13. Christ teaches sound doctrine through the Apostles and sincere preachers. When this seems vile to the world and cannot please, by his just judgment he leaves the contemners to their affections, and as the Apostle Saint Paul says, [note 33.3] he sends upon them the power of delusion, that they may believe lies, and so be judged—all those who would rather believe a lie than the truth. And the seduction through corrupt doctrine is a more harmful evil than the bloody persecutions. Indeed, the seducers and false prophets have done more harm to the church than cruel tyrants. Finally, people are more grievously punished when they are abandoned to be seduced by deceivers than when they are subjected to being torn into pieces by their murderers. Therefore, it is a most grievous plague of God, and utterly to be abhorred, when simple truth is despised and delivered to lying deceivers, who, it may be spoken with reverence after the fact, seem to all to be [illegible] and to be peace. For where the gospel is purely preached to many, these men say, "I do not understand what these men teach us from the Gospel; but this I can see that the old [illegible] have all to be raided from us, and these new both to be peace and to deceive us." Therefore, you shall have teachers who will perform to you in deed the same that you talk. Would God we lacked examples, and did not see certain nations that formerly had the free and pure preaching of the gospel, and now stripped of all truth, sigh under the pleasure and boldness of most wicked deceivers, who trample God’s word under foot, condemn it as heresy, and stop the mouths of the wretched people full of human dung. This is the punishment of truth despised.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "waxes black" changed to "immediately becomes dark." While conveying a similar visual, "waxes black" carries a nuance of gradual corruption absent in the draft's more abrupt phrasing.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** "Indeed, the seducers and false prophets have done more harm to the church than cruel tyrants" adds emphasis not explicitly present in the original's phrasing, which is more measured.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "all to be ſhite" is rendered "[illegible]" and then attempted to be rephrased. The original's bluntness, while coarse, is a deliberate rhetorical choice, and the draft's sanitization loses the force of the original’s condemnation.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No changes or removals of theological terms are apparent.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** No new content is present.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Ireney" should be "Irenaeus." Austen should be Augustine.
[FINAL]
Nevertheless, these things must be explained with religious care. For it is not to be thought, [note 33.2] that because the Lamb opens the sixth seal, and the Son immediately waxes black, Christ is the author of corrupt and evil doctrine. For Christ it is, that sows in the field good seed, the hostile man sows darnel: As the Lord himself expounds it Matthew. the.13. For Christ teaches sound doctrine by the Apostles and sincere preachers: which when it seems vile to the world and can not please, of his just judgment he leaves the contemners to their affections, and as the Apostle S. Paull says, [note 33.3] he sends upon them the efficacy of illusion, that they may believe lies, and so may be judged all which had rather believe a lie than the verity. And the seducing through corrupt doctrine, is a more hurtful evil, than are the bloody persecutions. Yea and the seducers and false prophets have done more hurt to the church than cruel tyrants. Finally men are more grievously punished what time they are relinquished to be seduced of deceivers, than when they are objected to be torn in pieces of their murderers. Therefore is it a most grievous plague of god, and utterly to be abhorred, for the simple verity despised, to be delivered to lying deceivers, which after the demerits may, with reverence be it spoken, all to be ſhite, and be peace thee. Therefore shalt thou have teachers, which shall perform to thee in deed the same that thou talk. This is the punishment of the verity despised.

[ORIGINAL]
But what time the lamb opened the ſixte seal, there was not heard now as before the voice of the Beasts, Elders, or Martyrs, but a terrible earthquake. An earthquake[note 33.5] in the Scriptures doth signify a wonderful commotion of all things, troubles tumults, and great alterations. And verily greater darkness arriſeth of nothing else, than by altering of Godly religion, and receyuyng of wicked doctrine. For so arriſe ſectes, ſeditions, wars. You may see many examples hereof in the story of the ancient people, which are red to have ben grievously shaken, so oft as they have changed their religion and kind of doctrine. By this Earthequake therefore is signified that exceadynge great trouble shall arriſe hereof, for that a new and a strange kind of doctrine should be brought into the world of men amiss incensed.
[DRAFT]
But when the Lamb opened the sixth seal, there was not heard now as before the voice of the Beasts, Elders, or Martyrs, but a terrible earthquake. An earthquake in the Scriptures signifies a wonderful commotion of all things, troubles, tumults, and great alterations. And truly, greater darkness arises from nothing else than by altering godly religion and receiving wicked doctrine. For so arise sects, seditions, and wars. You may see many examples of this in the history of the ancient people, who are recorded to have been grievously shaken whenever they have changed their religion and kind of doctrine. By this earthquake, therefore, is signified that exceedingly great trouble shall arise from this, because a new and strange kind of doctrine should be brought into the world, amiss inciting men.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Verily" replaced with "And truly" subtly shifts the tone from a more formal, emphatic declaration to a slightly less assertive statement.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The phrase "amiss inciting men" is an interpretation not explicitly present in "amiss incensed." While the meaning is similar, it adds a layer of agency and intent not directly stated.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "incensed" leaves room for multiple interpretations regarding the cause of the disturbance. "Amiss inciting men" narrows it to a deliberate action.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No theological terms were altered or removed.
5. HALLUCINATION: No content is added.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns are present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]
But when the Lamb opened the sixth seal, there was not heard now as before the voice of the Beasts, Elders, or Martyrs, but a terrible earthquake. An earthquake in the Scriptures doth signify a wonderful commotion of all things, troubles, tumults, and great alterations. And verily greater darkness arriſeth of nothing else, than by altering of Godly religion, and receyuyng of wicked doctrine. For so arriſe ſectes, ſeditions, wars. You may see many examples hereof in the story of the ancient people, which are red to have ben grievously shaken, so oft as they have changed their religion and kind of doctrine. By this Earthequake therefore is signified that exceedingly great trouble shall arriſe hereof, for that a new and a strange kind of doctrine should be brought into the world of men amiss incensed.
---
[ORIGINAL]
[note 33.6]Here have you, that you may answer unto them, which impute to the Gospel and to the Preachers thereof what so ever troubles, ſeditions, and commotions be at this day in the world. Helias hath ones made answer for us, which may serve for all times, the place is in the third book of Kings.18. Chapter. I have not troubled Israel, but thou and thy fathers house, which haste forsaken God, &c. Hereunto appertains also the story of Ieremie in the.44. Chapter. Where all the evils that than vexed the wicked are imputed to the ſinſere doctrine and to the Prophet Ieremie without cause. Learn here morouer what to answer them, which say: it is like, that God hath permitted his Church so many years to lie and rot in errors, &c.
[DRAFT]
Here is what you have, so that you may respond to those who attribute to the Gospel and to its preachers whatever troubles, seditions, and commotions exist in the world today. Elijah once made an answer for us, which will suffice for all times; the passage is in the third book of Kings, 18th chapter. “I have not troubled Israel, but you and your fathers’ house, who have forsaken God, &c.” Also relevant is the account of Jeremiah in the 44th chapter, where all the evils that then afflicted the wicked were wrongly imputed to the sincere doctrine and to the prophet Jeremiah. Learn further what to say to those who claim that it seems God permitted his Church to lie and rot in error for so many years, &c.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "Helias hath ones made answer" changed to "Elijah once made an answer" loses the emphatic, almost authoritative tone of the original. "Ones" implies a singular, definitive response.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "Also relevant is the account of Jeremiah" is an interpretive addition. The original simply presents the Jeremiah passage.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: The original's phrasing "it is like, that God hath permitted his Church so many years to lie and rot in errors" is softened to "those who claim that it seems God permitted his Church to lie and rot in error." The original allows for a more direct questioning of God's actions; the draft introduces a layer of distance and qualification.
4. PROPER NOUNS: "Ieremie" should be "Jeremiah."

[FINAL]
Here is what you have, so that you may respond to those who attribute to the Gospel and to its preachers whatever troubles, seditions, and commotions exist in the world today. Elijah once made answer for us, which will suffice for all times; the passage is in the third book of Kings, 18th chapter. “I have not troubled Israel, but you and your fathers’ house, who have forsaken God, &c.” Hereunto appertains also the story of Jeremiah in the 44th chapter. Where all the evils that then vexed the wicked are wrongly imputed to the sincere doctrine and to the prophet Jeremiah. Learn further what to say to those who say: it is like that God hath permitted his Church so many years to lie and rot in errors, &c.

[ORIGINAL]
To these is added an other thing, which helps these things that are spoken: stars fall from Heaven[note 33.11] unto the earth. Daniel called stars preachers in the.12. Chapter. As also S. Peter.2. Peter.2. Therefore do the preachers of churches revolt from the heavenly doctrine of Christ, brought and revealed from heaven, and reducyng men to Heaven, and keeping them in heavenly conversation: And receive earthly, that is the doctrine of men. By the which thing it comes to pass that both the sun is obscured and the moon is made bloody. Stars shine, preachers should set forth to the whole world Christ the true light: but this have they neglected being addict to their own traditions. To these is also added an Image,[note 33.12] [illegible], as the fig tree casts of her figs being shaken of a vehement wind. Here is signified the corruption of preachers, and that a great number of them. For the fig tree was made, to bring forth sweet fruits: so was the ministry of the word ordained for the salvation of men. How be it the figs did ripe, Therefore they remain grene or untimely fruits. Whereby is signified that the preachers were not ripe in true knowledge of Christ: and therefore to be shaken down with every wind of doctrine, that both they have admitted and set forth earthly things. The plenty of false teachers is signified to come in that the untimely figs fall down in great plenty.
[DRAFT]
To these is added another thing, which helps these things that are spoken: stars fall from Heaven[note 33.11] unto the earth. Daniel called stars preachers in the twelfth chapter. As also Saint Peter, in 2 Peter 2. Therefore, the preachers of churches revolt from the heavenly doctrine of Christ, brought and revealed from heaven, and raising men to heaven, and keeping them in heavenly conversation; and they receive earthly doctrine, that is, the doctrine of men. By this thing it comes to pass that both the sun is obscured and the moon is made bloody. Stars shine; preachers should set forth to the whole world Christ, the true light; but this they have neglected, being addicted to their own traditions.

To these is also added an image,[note 33.12] a likeness, as the fig tree casts off her figs being shaken by a vehement wind. Here is signified the corruption of preachers, and that a great number of them. For the fig tree was made to bring forth sweet fruits; so was the ministry of the word ordained for the salvation of men. However, the figs were unripe, therefore they remain green or untimely fruits. Whereby is signified that the preachers were not ripe in true knowledge of Christ; and therefore to be shaken down with every wind of doctrine, that both they have admitted and set forth earthly things. The abundance of false teachers is signified to come, in that the untimely figs fall down in great plenty.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "reducyng men to Heaven" is changed to "raising men to heaven." The original implies a spiritual ascent, a return to grace; the draft loses this nuance, suggesting a more literal elevation.
2. **ADDED INTERPRETATION:** The insertion of "a likeness" before "as the fig tree..." is an interpretive addition not present in the original.
3. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original "figs did ripe, Therefore they remain grene or untimely fruits" is softened to "figs were unripe, therefore they remain green or untimely fruits." While the meaning is similar, the original phrasing is more evocative of a process of decay and premature failure.
4. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** No theological terms were changed or removed.
5. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "a likeness" is added.
6. **PROPER NOUNS:** "S. Peter" is correctly modernized to "Saint Peter."
[FINAL]
To these is added another thing, which helps these things that are spoken: stars fall from Heaven[note 33.11] unto the earth. Daniel called stars preachers in the twelfth chapter. As also Saint Peter, in 2 Peter 2. Therefore do the preachers of churches revolt from the heavenly doctrine of Christ, brought and revealed from heaven, and reducing men to Heaven, and keeping them in heavenly conversation; and receive earthly, that is, the doctrine of men. By the which thing it comes to pass that both the sun is obscured and the moon is made bloody. Stars shine; preachers should set forth to the whole world Christ, the true light; but this they have neglected being addicted to their own traditions.

To these is also added an Image,[note 33.12], as the fig tree casts of her figs being shaken of a vehement wind. Here is signified the corruption of preachers, and that a great number of them. For the fig tree was made, to bring forth sweet fruits: so was the ministry of the word ordained for the salvation of men. How be it the figs did ripe, Therefore they remain grene or untimely fruits. [ORIGINAL]
Of these things now follows an other,[note 33.13] and Heaven went away, [illegible], as it were fled out of men's sight and vaniſſhed away. Again is added an Image or a ſimilitude [illegible] like a scroll folden up or rolled together. Heaven in the Gospel signifies many times the kingdom of God. Therefore the kingdom winds up it self in earth, and the church doth as it were hide her self, not that at the last there should be no church at all (for the church shall be always unto the worlds end) but for as much as in the end of the world the church shall lie hid, neither shall it be thought to be the true church, which is the true church in deed. The letters and words are not wipte out of the book, but are not scene, yea rather are hid, when it is rolled up. It is manifest at this day, what S. John meant by this parable. For all in a manner judge, that new start up Roman church, to be the true church, which in very deed is not the church of Christ: and the church which is the spouse of Christ is judged to be heretical: therefore is the church wrapped up and as rolled together. The Lord unfold and preserve the same: Am.
[DRAFT]

[ORIGINAL]
[note 34.2]Now follows the effect of the corrupt doctrine in men. And hills and Ilondes are moved out of their place: wherein is also a respect had to the earthquake, as though by the earthquake they were removed from their place. And mountains and Isles do betoken, realms, nations and people, so steadfast in faith, that as moutaines and Isles be immovable, and are not shaken with the storms of the Sea, so these might seem to be immutable. Nevertheless at the alteration and corrupting of doctrine, they are now also removed out of their place, and quite overthrown. And such as read Histories shall find every where, that such have ben diſceiued by crafts of heretics, by the power of Mahomet, and by the hypocrisy of the pope, whom you would not have thought should have be abused, in so much that whole Cities and Realms have clean revolted. For ſeducinge is of efficacitie namely in such as now begin to slip and slide from the rock of the church.
[DRAFT]
Now follows the effect of the corrupt doctrine in men. And hills and islands are moved out of their place: wherein is also a respect had to the earthquake, as though by the earthquake they were removed from their place. And mountains and islands do betoken realms, nations, and people, so steadfast in faith, that as mountains and islands are immovable, and are not shaken by the storms of the sea, so these might seem to be immutable. [ORIGINAL]
[note 34.3]And they that being shaken, are removed from the sure foundation, get them into caves and rocks of Hills. For it is unpossible, for him that holds not Christ with a sure faith, to be quiet. For like a raging Sea he is tossed hither and thither. For where as he hath not the sure and certain manner of life, neither commits him self to be only ruled by the Scriptures, that he might hold the certentie, he is content to be led of every one that he meteth with. Wherefore we see them, unto whom Christ alone is not all, to seek salvation in Pilgrimages, in heremitages, in Monkerie, in more severe discipline, in satisfactions, and I know not in what other follies, or rather blasphemies. And these in very deed are said to hide themselves in dens and caves of stone. And think they may lie hid safely in them, make satisfaction for their sins, and please the Lord.
[DRAFT]
And those who, being unsettled, are removed from the firm foundation, flee to caves and rocks in hills. For it is impossible for someone who does not hold fast to Christ with steadfast faith to find rest. Like a raging sea, he is tossed about. For lacking a sure and certain way of life, and not committing himself solely to be governed by Scripture, so that he might grasp certainty, he is content to be led by anyone he encounters. Therefore, we see those to whom Christ alone is not everything seeking salvation in pilgrimages, in hermitages, in monasticism, in stricter discipline, in satisfactions, and I know not what other follies, or rather blasphemies. And these are truly said to hide themselves in dens and caves of stone, thinking they may lie hidden safely within them, make satisfaction for their sins, and please the Lord.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "get them into" changed to "flee to." While functionally similar, "flee to" introduces a sense of panic absent in the original.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "hold fast to Christ" replaces "holds not Christ." The original implies a lack of faith; the draft emphasizes active holding. This shifts the focus slightly.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "certentie" changed to "certainty" and "grasp certainty." The original's "certentie" has a more nuanced sense of assuredness, not merely grasping. The draft's phrasing is overly explicit.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present.
[FINAL]

But in reherſing many kinds of men,[note 34.4] he comprises all states in the world. [ORIGINAL]
[note 34.6]Morouer the causes of this fear, despair and hiding are, the face of him that sits on the Throne, the wrath of the Lamb, and for that they perceive how they can not abide to stand before God in the day of wrath and gods vengeance. Therefore they flee from the face of God, they flee from the Lamb, that they might eschew the vegeaunce, if they could escape it. The fear of God is commended to us in the scriptures, and they which fear not God are condemned: but the scripture speaks of a fear joined with true faith and love. For S. John says, love casts out fear. Even so the same Scripture preaches to us God as just, and shows him to be angry with sin: nevertheless declares him to be benign and merciful to such as acknowledge their sins, and axe forgeuenes, that his only begotten son is give of God to mankind, by whose mediation we may come to the Throne of God, which otherwise no man may attain to. It preaches Christ the son of God to be the Lamb, that is a propiciation for the sins of the whole world: and that the same calls all unto him, excludes no man, but promises and offers unto all, all things of life and salvation. But where as corrupt Preachers, Friars and popiſhe Priests have forsaken this simple and most pure doctrine, wholesome and full of consolation, show that God is like to Rhadamantus a judge inexorable, and set forth Christ rather as one angry than favourable, they doe alienate doubtless the minds of men from God: that now they may say expressly, who is worthy to come into the sight of God? no man shall be saved before this God most severe, and his son a judge most righteous. They turn them therefore to sundry means of salvation they chose them mediators and intercessors by whose mediation, mean and merits they may redeem to themselves the favour of the angry deity. But sins that with God the only mediation and intercession of the son is of force these wretches are diſapointed of their pourpos, and at the length fall into that same desperation. When they perceive that the monaſtical life, and their merits, ca not stand before God, they flee from the face of God: and tourmented with the pricks of their conscience know not what they may do, whither they may turn them, where is the true salvation. Therefore we judge the rightly to be most blessed, which through Christ acknowledge the father as a father: and through Christ have access to the father, as fauouryng them and loving them: acknoweledging verily in the fear of God their sins, but yet with a true faith hoping for remission of sins, knowing that they are through Christ reconciled to God the father. The monaſtical, heremiticall, ſatiſfactoriouſe, and phariſaical faction acknowledges fully this doctrine, therefore are they tourmented with sorrows that can not be uttered. If speak not here of the monasteries or Monks of this our time, in whom we see almost no conscience at all, nor other intent, than to be addict to Idleness, voluptuouſnes, and to bear rule. In times past were found men full of conscience, entering into celles and woods, for non other cause, than that they might so be saved. Of such spake the Lord in the gospel: when they shall say, says he, Christ is in the wilderness, go not forth, &c. And I doubt not, but that some simple also at this day for this intent take upon the the monaſtical life: but they shall find also, the same that S. John here said they should prove and try by experience.
[DRAFT]
Moreover, the causes of this fear, despair, and hiding are the appearance of the one who sits on the throne, the wrath of the Lamb, and because they perceive that they cannot stand before God on the day of wrath and God’s vengeance. Therefore, they flee from the face of God; they flee from the Lamb, that they might avoid the vengeance, if they could escape it. The fear of God is commended to us in the scriptures, and those who do not fear God are condemned; but the scripture speaks of a fear joined with true faith and love. For John says, “Love casts out fear.” Even so, the same scripture preaches to us God as just, and shows him to be angry with sin; nevertheless, it declares him to be benign and merciful to those who acknowledge their sins and ask for forgiveness, that his only begotten Son was given by God to humankind, by whose mediation we may come to the throne of God, which otherwise no one may attain. It preaches Christ, the Son of God, to be the Lamb, a propitiation for the sins of the whole world; and that the same calls all to him, excludes no one, but promises and offers to all, all things of life and salvation. But whereas corrupt preachers, friars, and popish priests have forsaken this simple and most pure doctrine, wholesome and full of consolation, and show that God is like Rhadamantus, a judge inexorable, and set forth Christ rather as one angry than favorable, they do alienate, without doubt, the minds of men from God; that now they may say expressly, “Who is worthy to come into the sight of God?” No one shall be saved before this God most severe, and his Son a judge most righteous. They turn them therefore to sundry means of salvation; they choose mediators and intercessors by whose mediation and merits they may redeem to themselves the favor of the angry deity. But those who, with God, consider the only mediation and intercession of the Son to be of force, these wretches are disappointed of their purpose, and at length fall into that same desperation. When they perceive that the monastic life and their merits cannot stand before God, they flee from the face of God; and tormented with the pricks of their conscience, they know not what they may do, whither they may turn, where is the true salvation. Therefore, we judge those rightly to be most blessed who, through Christ, acknowledge the Father as a Father; and through Christ have access to the Father, as favoring them and loving them; acknowledging truly in the fear of God their sins, but yet with a true faith hoping for remission of sins, knowing that they are through Christ reconciled to God the Father. The monastic, heremitic, expiatory, and Pharisaical faction acknowledges fully this doctrine; therefore, they are tormented with sorrows that cannot be uttered. If I speak not here of the monasteries or monks of this our time, in whom we see almost no conscience at all, nor other intent than to be addicted to idleness, voluptuousness, and to bear rule. In times past were found men full of conscience, entering into cells and woods, for no other cause than that they might so be saved. Of such spoke the Lord in the gospel: “When they shall say,” he says, “Christ is in the wilderness, go not forth,” and so forth. And I doubt not, but that some simple also at this day for this intent take upon the monastic life; but they shall find also the same that John here said they should prove and try by experience.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** "axe forgeuenes" is rendered as "ask for forgiveness." The original phrasing, while archaic, implies a more active and perhaps repentant seeking of forgiveness. The draft's wording is more passive.
2. **THEOLOGICAL TERMS:** "ſatiſfactoriouſe" is rendered as "expiatory." While functionally similar, "satisfactory" carries a slightly different nuance relating to fulfilling a debt or requirement, which is a key concept in some theological interpretations. "Expiatory" focuses more on atonement.
3. **HALLUCINATION:** The draft adds quotation marks around "Who is worthy to come into the sight of God?" in the second paragraph. This is not present in the original.
4. **PROPER NOUNS:** "Austen" should be "Augustine."
[FINAL]
Moreover, the causes of this fear, despair, and hiding are the appearance of the one who sits on the throne, the wrath of the Lamb, and because they perceive that they cannot stand before God on the day of wrath and God’s vengeance. Therefore, they flee from the face of God; they flee from the Lamb, that they might avoid the vengeance, if they could escape it. The fear of God is commended to us in the scriptures, and those who do not fear God are condemned; but the scripture speaks of a fear joined with true faith and love. For John says, “Love casts out fear.” Even so, the same scripture preaches to us God as just, and shows him to be angry with sin; nevertheless, it declares him to be benign and merciful to those who acknowledge their sins and ask for forgiveness, that his only begotten Son was given by God to humankind, by whose mediation we may come to the throne of God, which otherwise no one may attain. It preaches Christ, the Son of God, to be the Lamb, a propitiation for the sins of the whole world; and that the same calls all to him, excludes no one, but promises and offers to all, all things of life and salvation. But whereas corrupt preachers, friars, and popish priests have forsaken this simple and most pure doctrine, wholesome and full of consolation, and show that God is like Rhadamantus, a judge inexorable, and set forth Christ rather as one angry than favorable, they do alienate, without doubt, the minds of men from God; that now they may say, “Who is worthy to come into the sight of God?” No one shall be saved before this God most severe, and his Son a judge most righteous. They turn them therefore to sundry means of salvation; they choose mediators and intercessors by whose mediation and merits they may redeem to themselves the favor of the angry deity. But those who, with God, consider the only mediation and intercession of the Son to be of force, these wretches are disappointed of their purpose, and at length fall into that same desperation. When they perceive that the monastic life and their merits cannot stand before God, they flee from the face of God; and tormented with the pricks of their conscience, they know not what they may do, whither they may turn, where is the true salvation. Therefore, we judge those rightly to be most blessed who, through Christ, acknowledge the Father as a Father; and through Christ have access to the Father, as favoring them and loving them; acknowledging truly in the fear of God their sins, but yet with a true faith hoping for remission of sins, knowing that they are through Christ reconciled to God the Father. The monastic, heremitic, satisfactory, and Pharisaical faction acknowledges fully this doctrine; therefore, they are tormented with sorrows that cannot be uttered. If I speak not here of the monasteries or monks of this our time, in whom we see almost no conscience at all, nor other intent than to be addicted to idleness, voluptuousness, and to bear rule. In times past were found men full of conscience, entering into cells and woods, for no other cause than that they might so be saved. Of such spoke the Lord in the gospel: “When they shall say,” he says, “Christ is in the wilderness, go not forth,” and so forth. And I doubt not, but that some simple also at this day for this intent take upon the monastic life; but they shall find also the same that John here said they should prove and try by experience.
---
[ORIGINAL]
And the things that follow in the 7. Chapter. appertain to the exposition of the ſixte seal, or unto the treatise thereof. And three things chiefly it recites, howe the Angles let the winds that they should not blow: an innumerable company to be sealed in the midst of the corrupt doctrine, which should not perish. And what the state of them is, which are departed out of this world either by martirdom, or else being either undefiled with the corruption so full of enormity, or delivered and pourged from the same: which are annexed because of consolation. For this book of Apocalypse is woderful Evangelical, most full not only of prophecies, but also of admonitions, exhortations, and most cofortable consolations.
[DRAFT]
The things that follow in the seventh chapter pertain to the explanation of the sixth seal, or to the discussion of it. And it chiefly recounts three things: how the angels restrain the winds so they do not blow; an innumerable company to be sealed in the midst of corrupt doctrine, who should not perish. And what the state is of those who have departed from this world either by martyrdom, or else being either undefiled by the corruption so full of enormity, or delivered and purged from the same; which are included for consolation. For this book of Revelation is wonderfully evangelical, most full not only of prophecies, but also of admonitions, exhortations, and most comforting consolations.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "appertain to the exposition of" changed to "pertain to the explanation of or to the discussion of it." The original phrasing implies a more formal, detailed unpacking of the seal; the draft's phrasing is slightly less weighty.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: "Apocalypse" changed to "Revelation." While functionally equivalent for many modern readers, "Apocalypse" retains a more specific theological connotation.
5. HALLUCINATION: None.
6. PROPER NOUNS: None.
[FINAL]
The things that follow in the seventh chapter appertain to the exposition of the sixth seal, or unto the treatise thereof. And three things chiefly it recites, how the angels let the winds that they should not blow: an innumerable company to be sealed in the midst of the corrupt doctrine, which should not perish. And what the state of them is, which are departed out of this world either by martirdom, or else being either undefiled with the corruption so full of enormity, or delivered and pourged from the same: which are annexed because of consolation. For this book of Apocalypse is wonderfully evangelical, most full not only of prophecies, but also of admonitions, exhortations, and most cofortable consolations.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 34.8]First is to be expounded, that which is spoken of the reſtreinte of the winds by the Angels, that they should not blow. Wind as also leave in the scriptures, is used both in good and evil part. For wind is called vain and false doctrine, and an hope conceived of erroneouſe doctrine: As in Oſee the.12. and the.5. and.22. of Ieremie. So is leaven called the Phariſaicall doctrine, and hypocrisy springing thereof. S. Paul in the.4. to the Epheſ. forbids, that we be not carried about with every wind of doctrine. And the holy ghost is shadowed by wind in the.3. Chapter. of S. John. And in the 2. Chapter. of the Acts. Wind is subtle, it pearſeth, is felt, and is not scene: great is the force thereof, it doth refrigerate, it dries, gathers clouds, which rain and make the earth fertile. Full rightly therefore by wind is signified the spirit of God, and the sound doctrine, which is of the spirit of God. Therefore is it one wind, the spirit of God which inspires: and there be four winds, that is to wit many by the corners of Heaven and parts of the earth, that is to wit preachers dispersed through out the whole world. Therefore the doctrine of the Gospel inspired from all parts of the world blows, or is preached: so that there be many winds, yet all proceeding of one. For there is one and the same spirit, which speaks by the ministers, and gives them sundry graces.1. Corinth. 12. Briefly, by the blast of winds we understand the free preaching taken out of the holy Scriptures.
[DRAFT]
First, it must be explained what is spoken of the restraint of the winds by the angels, that they should not blow. Wind, as also appears in the scriptures, is used both in a good and an evil sense. For wind is called vain and false doctrine, and a hope conceived of erroneous doctrine, as in Hosea 12 and 3:5 and 3:22 of Jeremiah. So leaven is called the Pharisaical doctrine, and the hypocrisy springing from it. Paul in 4 Ephesians forbids that we be carried about with every wind of doctrine. And the Holy Spirit is signified by wind in chapter 3 of John. And in chapter 2 of Acts, wind is subtle, it pierces, is felt, and is not seen; great is its force, it cools, it dries, gathers clouds, which bring rain and make the earth fertile. Therefore, rightly, by wind is signified the spirit of God, and the sound doctrine which is of the spirit of God. Therefore it is one wind, the spirit of God which inspires; and there are four winds, that is to say, many from the corners of heaven and parts of the earth, that is to say, preachers dispersed throughout the whole world. Therefore, the doctrine of the Gospel inspired from all parts of the world blows, or is preached, so that there are many winds, yet all proceeding from one. For there is one and the same spirit which speaks by the ministers, and gives them sundry graces, 1 Corinthians 12. Briefly, by the blast of winds we understand the free preaching taken from the holy scriptures.
[CRITIQUE]
1. **MEANING DRIFT:** "leave" is modernized to "leaven," subtly shifting the meaning from a physical substance to a theological concept. While related, it's a change.
2. **LOST AMBIGUITY:** The original's phrasing "wind is subtle, it pearſeth, is felt, and is not scene" is weakened to "wind is subtle, it pierces, is felt, and is not seen." "Pearſeth" implies a penetrating, almost invasive quality lost in "pierces."
3. **PROPER NOUNS:** "4 Ephesians" should be "4 to the Ephesians."
4. **HALLUCINATION:** The phrase "as also appears in the scriptures" is not present in the original.
[FINAL]
First is to be expounded, that which is spoken of the restraint of the winds by the Angels, that they should not blow. Wind, as also leave in the scriptures, is used both in good and evil part. For wind is called vain and false doctrine, and an hope conceived of erroneouſe doctrine: As in Oſee the.12. and the.5. and.22. of Ieremie. So leaven is called the Phariſaicall doctrine, and hypocrisy springing thereof. S. Paul in the.4. to the Epheſ. forbids, that we be not carried about with every wind of doctrine. And the holy ghost is shadowed by wind in the.3. Chapter. of S. John. And in the 2. Chapter. of the Acts. Full rightly therefore by wind is signified the spirit of God, and the sound doctrine, which is of the spirit of God. Therefore is it one wind, the spirit of God which inspires: and there be four winds, that is to wit many by the corners of Heaven and parts of the earth, that is to wit preachers dispersed through out the whole world. For there is one and the same spirit, which speaks by the ministers, and gives them sundry graces.1. Corinth. 12. [ORIGINAL]
ANd I saw an other Angel aſcede from the rising of the son: which had the seal of the living God, and he cried with a load voice to the four Angles (to whom power was given to hurt the earth and the See) saying: hurt not the earth, neither the See, neither the trees, till we have sealed the Servants of our God in their forheades. And I heard the number of them which were sealed, and there were sealed an C. and xliiii. thousand of all the tribes of the Children of Israel. Of the tribe of Iuda were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Ruben were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Gad were sealed xii M. of the tribe of Aſſer were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Neptali were sealed xii.M. Of the tribe of Manaſſes were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Simeon were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Leui were sealed xii.M. of the tribe of Iſachar were sealed xii.M. And I saw another angel ascend from the rising of the sun, who had the seal of the living God, and he cried with a loud voice to the four angels (to whom power was given to hurt the earth and the sea), saying, “Hurt not the earth, neither the sea, neither the trees, until we have sealed the servants of our God in their foreheads.” And I heard the number of those who were sealed, and there were sealed one hundred and forty-four thousand of all the tribes of the children of Israel. Of the tribe of Judah were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Reuben were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Gad were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Asher were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Naphtali were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Manasseh were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Simeon were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Levi were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Issachar were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Zebulun were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Joseph were sealed twelve thousand; of the tribe of Benjamin were sealed twelve thousand.
[CRITIQUE]
MEANING DRIFT: "an C. and xliiii." modernized to "one hundred and forty-four thousand" is acceptable, though the original's numeral form contributes to its archaic feel.
LOST AMBIGUITY: The original "See" is ambiguous (sea or see/vision). The draft's "sea" is a reasonable choice, but eliminates the ambiguity present in the original.
PROPER NOUNS: "Neptali" should be "Naphtali." "Manaſſes" should be "Manasseh." "Ioſeph" should be "Joseph." "Iſachar" should be "Issachar." "Zabulon" should be "Zebulun."

[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
After this I beheld, and lo, a great multitude (which no man could number) of all nations and people, and tunges, stood before the seat, and before the Lamb, clothed with log white garments and Palms in their hands, and cried with a load voice saying: Salvation be ascribed to him that sits upon the seat of our God, and unto the lamb. And all the Angels stood in the compass of the seat, and of the elders, and of the four beasts, and fell down before the seat on their faces, and worshipped God, saying: Am. Blessing, and glory, wisdom and thanks, and honour and power, and might, be unto our God for ever more, Amen.
[DRAFT]
After this I beheld, and lo, a great multitude (which no man could number) of all nations and people, and tongues, stood before the throne, and before the Lamb, clothed with long white garments and palms in their hands, and cried with a loud voice saying: Salvation be ascribed to him that sits upon the throne of our God, and unto the Lamb. And all the angels stood in the circle of the throne, and of the elders, and of the four living creatures, and fell down before the throne on their faces, and worshipped God, saying: Amen. Blessing, and glory, wisdom and thanksgiving, and honour and power, and might, be unto our God for ever and ever. Amen.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "compass" changed to "circle." While similar, "compass" implies a more encompassing, almost protective, boundary, which subtly alters the image.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: "four beasts" changed to "four living creatures." This adds a layer of interpretation, suggesting animation where the original simply describes figures.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: "thanks" changed to "thanksgiving." The original "thanks" is a noun, a general expression of gratitude. "Thanksgiving" implies a ritual or act of giving thanks, introducing a specific practice not present in the original.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes to theological terms.
5. HALLUCINATION: No hallucinated content.
6. PROPER NOUNS: No proper nouns present in this excerpt.
[FINAL]

[ORIGINAL]
We have heard, brethren, the ſixte seal being opened,[note 35.1] that the Sun was made black, the Moon bloody, the Stars to have fallen from Heaven to the Earth, and the rest that we have rehearsed: by all the which was signified the corruption of doctrine. A sorrowful and a fearful matter was shadowed with most sorrowful and most terrible parables. We heard how there followed in the world a most great tourmoile of things, and with many a most grievous despair: and that the winds also were restrained, that they should not blow. But we have experienced, howe great a grief it is, yea and destruction also, to waunte the ayre or wind, in so much that with out breathing and cooling, men must needs wither and be queſomed and choked up. But with so great an evil are they vexed which are destitute of the preaching of Gods word.
[DRAFT]
Brothers, we have heard of the sixth seal being opened,[note 35.1] that the sun was made black, the moon bloody, and the stars fell from heaven to the earth, and the rest that we have rehearsed. By all of this was signified the corruption of doctrine. A sorrowful and fearful matter was shadowed with most sorrowful and terrible parables. We have heard how a great turmoil followed in the world, with much grievous despair, and that the winds also were restrained, so that they should not blow. But we have experienced how great a grief, indeed a destruction, it is to lack the air or wind, so much that without breathing and cooling, men must needs wither, be suffocated, and choked. But with so great an evil are those vexed who are destitute of the preaching of God’s word.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "waunte the ayre or wind" changed to "lack the air or wind" subtly shifts the nuance. "Waunte" implies a deprivation, a loss, whereas "lack" is more neutral.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: None.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: None altered.
5. HALLUCINATION: None.
6. PROPER NOUNS: None present.
[FINAL]
Brothers, we have heard of the sixth seal being opened,[note 35.1] that the sun was made black, the moon bloody, and the stars fell from heaven to the earth, and the rest that we have rehearsed. By all of this was signified the corruption of doctrine. A sorrowful and fearful matter was shadowed with most sorrowful and terrible parables. We have heard how a great turmoil followed in the world, with much grievous despair, and that the winds also were restrained, so that they should not blow. But we have experienced how great a grief, indeed a destruction, it is to want the air or wind, so much that without breathing and cooling, men must needs wither, be suffocated, and choked. But with so great an evil are those vexed who are destitute of the preaching of God’s word.

[ORIGINAL]
[note 35.6]And the Author of this salvation and preſeruatio is first declared to be an Angel aſcendyng from the rising of the sun: to wit the Lord Christ that sun of rightuouſenes, rising up in those most thick Antichristian darkness, to those that seek God, and lighting them the darkness drive away. For Christ is the true light of all times, lighting all, so many as be illumined. He gives his people also preachers, which by the word may defend Gods people, that they be not destroyed with that common destruction.
[DRAFT]
[note 35.6]And the Author of this salvation and preservation is first declared to be an angel ascending from the rising of the sun: namely, the Lord Christ, that sun of righteousness, rising up in those most intense Antichristian darkness, to those who seek God, and driving away the darkness for them. For Christ is the true light of all times, illuminating all who are illuminated. He gives his people also preachers, who by the word may defend God’s people, that they be not destroyed with that common destruction.
[CRITIQUE]
1. MEANING DRIFT: "lighting them the darkness drive away" changed to "driving away the darkness for them." The original phrasing implies a reciprocal action; the light drives away the darkness *as it illuminates*. The draft's phrasing subtly shifts the agency, making it solely Christ's action *for* the seekers, potentially diminishing the role of faith/action on the part of the believers.
2. ADDED INTERPRETATION: The insertion of "namely" before "the Lord Christ" adds a layer of explanation that isn't present in the original. While the connection is implied, the original leaves it slightly more open to interpretation.
3. LOST AMBIGUITY: None.
4. THEOLOGICAL TERMS: No changes. "Antichristian" is retained.
5. HALLUCINATION: None.
6. PROPER NOUNS: None.
[FINAL]
[note 35.6]And the Author of this salvation and preservation is first declared to be an angel ascending from the rising of the sun: to wit the Lord Christ, that sun of righteousness, rising up in those most intense Antichristian darkness, to those who seek God, and lighting them the darkness drive away. For Christ is the true light of all times, illuminating all who are illuminated. He gives his people also preachers, who by the word may defend God’s people, that they be not destroyed with that common destruction.
